# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between primary and secondary air pollutants with two examples each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Primary Pollutants | Secondary Pollutants |
> |:-------|:-------------------|:---------------------|
> | Definition | Emitted directly into the atmosphere from a source | Formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions among primary pollutants |
> | Formation | Source-dependent | Atmosphere-dependent (time, sunlight) |
> | Examples | SO₂, NO, CO, PM, lead | O₃, NO₂, H₂SO₄, PAN, photochemical smog |
>
> **Marking key:** Definition (1 m) + 2 examples each (1 m) + 1 sentence of difference (1 m).

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List the major sources of SO₂ and NOₓ in an urban Indian environment.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **SO₂ sources (1.5 m):** Combustion of sulphur-containing coal and petroleum products in thermal power plants; industrial processes (sulphuric acid manufacture, smelters); domestic coal burning; diesel vehicles (especially older BS-III and earlier).
>
> **NOₓ sources (1.5 m):** Vehicle exhaust (major urban source); power plant stacks; industrial furnaces; lightning (natural); biomass burning; aircraft engines.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Discuss the various factors that contribute to air pollution. Classify the factors with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain in detail the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981. List its salient features and powers of the State Pollution Control Board. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**(i) Source Factors (2 m):** Air pollution begins with the source. Classify sources as natural (volcanoes, dust storms, biogenic emissions) and anthropogenic (industrial, vehicular, domestic). Within anthropogenic, distinguish stationary (point) and mobile (line) and area sources. Note that in Kerala, biomass burning (coconut, paddy, areca) is a major seasonal area source, and traffic in Kochi–Trivandrum corridors is the dominant mobile source.

**(ii) Meteorological Factors (2 m):** Wind speed (faster = better dispersion), wind direction (downwind receptors), atmospheric stability (Pasquill A–F — class F is worst), temperature inversions (Kerala's winter mornings trap pollutants), humidity (enhances PM growth, acid rain), solar radiation (drives photochemical smog).

**(iii) Topographical and Geographic Factors (1.5 m):** Valley topography (Munnar, Wayanad) traps pollutants; coastal land-sea breeze recirculation (Kochi, Kozhikode) prolongs residence time; urban heat island (Trivandrum, Kochi) modifies boundary layer; building canyons in dense urban wards reduce effective dispersion.

**(iv) Socio-Economic and Other Factors (1.5 m):** Population density, vehicular growth (Kerala's vehicle density is among the highest in India at ~500/1000 population), fuel quality, industrial clustering (Eloor, FACT–Cochin industrial belt), construction activity, waste burning, festival activities (Thrissur Pooram, temple fireworks).

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Salient Features of the Air Act 1981:**

1. **Long Title and Statement of Objects (0.5 m):** An Act to provide for the prevention, control, and abatement of air pollution and for the establishment of Boards at the Central and State levels with a view to carrying out the aforesaid purposes.

2. **Definitions (Section 2) (0.5 m):** Defines "air pollutant", "air pollution", "industrial plant", "occupier", "consent", "Board".

3. **Central Board (Sections 3–15) (0.5 m):** Constitution of CPCB; composition (chairman + members); tenure; functions (Section 16).

4. **State Boards (Sections 4–15) (0.5 m):** Constitution of KSPCB in Kerala (established 4 Jan 1974 as Kerala State Board for Prevention and Control of Water Pollution; reconstituted under Air Act in 1982).

5. **Consent Mechanism (Sections 21 & 22) (1 m):**
   - **Section 21** — no person shall establish or operate any industrial plant without **Consent to Establish (CTE)**.
   - **Section 22** — no person shall operate any industrial plant without **Consent to Operate (CTO)**.
   - Consent can be conditional, refused, or revoked.

6. **Powers of KSPCB (Section 19 and 23) (1 m):**
   - Advise State Government on matters relating to air pollution.
   - Plan and execute nationwide programmes.
   - Collect and disseminate information.
   - Lay down standards for emissions and ambient air quality.
   - Inspect any industrial plant and enter premises.
   - Order closure, prohibition, or regulation under Section 31A.

7. **Penalties and Offences (Sections 25, 26, 31A) (1 m):**
   - Section 37 — exemptions for certain industries.
   - Section 22 violation — imprisonment up to 3 months and/or fine up to ₹10,000 (enhanced post-1987 amendment); continued violation — additional ₹5,000/day.
   - Section 31A — penalty for contravention of directions.

8. **Adjudication and Appellate Authority (Section 31) (0.5 m):** Any person aggrieved by an order of SPCB may appeal to the appellate authority (Secretary, Dept of Environment, Kerala) within 30 days.

9. **Court Jurisdiction (Section 38) (0.5 m):** No civil court has jurisdiction; matters go to NGT (post-2010) or relevant High Court.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For Part (b), students often confuse **Sections 21 and 22**. **Section 21 is "Consent to Establish"** (before construction); **Section 22 is "Consent to Operate"** (before operation). Examiners **specifically award marks** for distinguishing these. Also, do not forget the **1987 Amendment** which enhanced penalties under **Section 31A** — this is a frequently tested update.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Modified]**
> **(a)** Explain the techno-administrative aspects of air pollution control in India. Discuss the role of CPCB and KSPCB in air quality management. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the emission and noise standards as prescribed by KSPCB. What are the ambient air quality standards for residential and industrial zones? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Techno-Administrative Framework (3.5 m):**

1. **Tier 1 — Central Government (MoEFCC & CPCB):** MoEFCC (Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change) is the apex policy body. CPCB, headquartered in Delhi, was constituted under Section 3 of the Air Act, 1981, and Water Act, 1974. CPCB's role:
   - Sets **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** (notified in 2009 under EPA 1986, revised 2024 with new PM₂.₅ norms).
   - Coordinates with SPCBs and provides technical guidance.
   - Publishes the **National Air Quality Index (AQI)**.
   - Runs the **National Air Quality Monitoring Programme (NAMP)** — 1000+ manual stations.
   - Maintains emission inventory.
   - Lays down industry-specific emission standards (CPCB Emission Guidelines Part I & II).

2. **Tier 2 — State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB):** Headquartered in Thiruvananthapuram, with regional offices in Kochi, Kozhikode, and Thrissur. KSPCB's role:
   - Implements NAAQS at state level.
   - Grants **CTE and CTO** under Sections 21 and 22 of the Air Act.
   - Inspects industrial units.
   - Operates Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Stations (CAAQMS).
   - Enforces noise standards in the state.
   - Conducts public awareness programmes.
   - Special cells for **ecologically sensitive areas** (Silent Valley, Periyar Tiger Reserve, Western Ghats).

3. **Tier 3 — Local Bodies and District Administration:** District Collectors, Panchayats, Municipalities, and Corporations. They implement local zoning, parking, and traffic rules.

4. **Tools and Mechanisms (1.5 m):**
   - **OCMMS (Online Consent Management and Monitoring System)** — KSPCB online portal for consent application.
   - **XGN (e-Governance for NAAQS)** — AQI reporting.
   - **CEMS (Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems)** — installed on major stacks.
   - **PUC (Pollution Under Control) Certificate** — vehicular.
   - **EIA Notification 2006** — environmental clearance.

5. **Judicial Tier (1 m):** National Green Tribunal (NGT) — fast-track adjudication. Important Kerala cases: Sterlite copper plant closure (Vedanta), Coca-Cola Plachimada, Endosulfan relief (Kasaragod).

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Ambient Air Quality Standards (AAQS) under KSPCB / CPCB NAAQS 2009 (4 m):**

| Pollutant | Industrial (24 hr) | Residential (24 hr) | Sensitive (24 hr) |
|:----------|:-------------------:|:--------------------:|:------------------:|
| PM₂.₅ | 60 µg/m³ | 60 µg/m³ | 30 µg/m³ |
| PM₁₀ | 100 µg/m³ | 100 µg/m³ | 75 µg/m³ |
| SO₂ | 80 µg/m³ | 80 µg/m³ | 30 µg/m³ |
| NO₂ | 80 µg/m³ | 80 µg/m³ | 40 µg/m³ |
| CO (8 hr) | 4.0 mg/m³ | 4.0 mg/m³ | 2.0 mg/m³ |
| O₃ (8 hr) | 100 µg/m³ | 100 µg/m³ | 70 µg/m³ |
| Pb | 1.0 µg/m³ | 1.0 µg/m³ | 0.5 µg/m³ |

The **Industrial zone** is intended for areas where factories, warehouses, and utilities dominate. The **Residential zone** covers dwellings, schools, and public spaces. The **Sensitive zone** (the most stringent) covers hospitals, sanctuaries, and biodiversity areas (e.g., Periyar, Silent Valley, Thattekkad Bird Sanctuary).

**CPCB's revised NAAQS 2024** introduced a separate annual standard (15 µg/m³ for PM₂.₅, 45 µg/m³ for PM₁₀) and added new pollutants (BC, benzene, BaP, NH₃, Hg).

**(ii) Noise Standards (2 m):**

| Zone | Day dB(A) | Night dB(A) |
|:-----|:---------:|:-----------:|
| Industrial | 75 | 70 |
| Commercial | 65 | 55 |
| Residential | 55 | 45 |
| Silence Zone | 50 | 40 |

KSPCB also issues **special directions** for firecracker noise (Diwali, Vishu, Christmas), temple festivals (Thrissur Pooram — restricted to 100 dB(A) at source), and pilgrimage seasons (Sabarimala).

**(iii) Emission Standards for Stacks (1 m):** KSPCB follows CPCB emission regulations for 92 industry categories. Examples:
   - **Thermal power plant SO₂:** 100 mg/Nm³ (for new units > 500 MW).
   - **Cement plant PM:** 30 mg/Nm³.
   - **Diesel generator stack height:** HC + 3 m (where H is building height).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):** Students frequently write the **NAAQS** values without specifying the **averaging period** (24-hour vs. 8-hour vs. annual). Examiners will deduct marks. Always quote the time-weighted average alongside the value. Also, for noise standards, **state the time of day** (Day 6 AM–10 PM, Night 10 PM–6 AM) — a missing time-of-day loses you 0.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 High-Yield Points**

- ✅ Air Pollution is legally defined under **Section 2(a) of Air Act 1981** — memorize this.
- ✅ **Primary vs. Secondary pollutants** — primary are emitted; secondary are formed in atmosphere.
- ✅ **Six criteria pollutants** (NAAQS): PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, Pb, NH₃.
- ✅ **Pasquill stability classes A–F**: A = very unstable (good), F = very stable (bad, inversion).
- ✅ **Gaussian Plume equation** is governed by $Q$ (source) ÷ ($u$ × $\sigma_y$ × $\sigma_z$) (dispersion).
- ✅ **Source factors** dominate in winter; **meteorological factors** dominate in monsoon (humidity + wet deposition); both combine in summer (photochemistry + inversion).
- ✅ **Kerala-specific:** biomass burning (coconut, paddy, areca), vehicle density (~500/1000), temple festival noise, coastal land-sea breeze, Western Ghats topography, ecologically sensitive zones.
- ✅ **Air Act 1981** — Sections 2(a), 3, 4, 16, 19, 20, 21 (CTE), 22 (CTO), 25, 31A (penalties), 31 (appeal).
- ✅ **CTE under Section 21** is before construction; **CTO under Section 22** is before operation.
- ✅ **1987 Amendment** of the Air Act introduced **Section 31A** for enhanced penalties.
- ✅ **CPCB = Central; KSPCB = State**; MoEFCC = apex policy body; NGT = adjudication.
- ✅ **KSPCB follows CPCB NAAQS 2009** but can issue stricter standards for sensitive zones.
- ✅ **Noise standards** — Industrial 75/70, Commercial 65/55, Residential 55/45, Silence 50/40 dB(A) (Day/Night).
- ✅ **NAAQS pollutants** — always quote the **averaging period**.
- ✅ **Photochemical smog** — VOC + NOₓ + sunlight → O₃ + PAN + aldehydes. Los Angeles-type smog.
- ✅ **EIA Notification 2006** — mandatory for 30+ project categories under EPA 1986.
- ✅ **NCAP 2019** — 131 non-attainment cities; 40% PM reduction target by 2025–26.
- ✅ **BS-VI norms** — applicable from April 2020 (pan-India); Kerala followed suit.
- ✅ **NABL-accredited labs** in Kerala: KSPCB labs, CEPCI labs, CETP labs, IISER Thiruvananthapuram research labs.
- ✅ **Kerala hotspots:** Eloor, Kochi, Trivandrum, Kozhikode, Thrissur, Kollam, Alappuzha, Kannur, Kasaragod, Palakkad.
- ✅ **Source apportionment** uses **CMB, PMF, PCA** models; Kerala NEERI studies have identified vehicular + road dust + biomass burning as dominant.
- ✅ **Gaseous pollutants sources** — SO₂ (coal, oil), NOₓ (vehicles, power), CO (incomplete combustion), O₃ (photochem), Pb (legacy petrol, smelters), NH₃ (fertilizer, dairy).
- ✅ **CEMS and CAAQMS** are modern real-time monitoring tools that have replaced many manual stations.
- ✅ **AQI categories** — Good (0–50), Satisfactory (51–100), Moderate (101–200), Poor (201–300), Very Poor (301–400), Severe (401–500).
- ✅ **Abatement hierarchy** — Elimination > Process Mod > Fuel Sub > Equipment Mod > End-of-pipe > Dispersion > Administrative.
- ✅ **For noise:** Sound power (L_W) and sound pressure (L_P) are different; combine using $10 \log_{10}(\Sigma 10^{L_i/10})$.
- ✅ **For PM:** Settling velocity (Stokes' law) is $v_s = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho) g d_p^2}{18 \mu}$ — important for cyclone design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemistry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chemistry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Atmospheric Chemistry of Air Pollutants** is the branch of environmental science that studies the chemical composition of the ambient atmosphere, the origin (primary vs. secondary) of airborne contaminants, the photochemical and thermochemical reactions they undergo in the troposphere and stratosphere, and the formation mechanisms of secondary pollutants such as ozone, smog, acid rain, and secondary particulate matter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The Module 1 "Chemistry" segment specifically requires the student to master (a) the chemical composition of clean dry air, (b) the reaction pathways of criteria pollutants (CO, SO₂, NOₓ, O₃, PM), (c) the formation of photochemical smog, and (d) the chemistry of acid rain.

### 1.2 Classification of Atmospheric Constituents

| Category | Approx. % by Volume | Examples | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Major (Bulk) Gases** | ~99 % | **N₂ (78.08 %)**, **O₂ (20.95 %)**, **Ar (0.93 %)** | Chemically inert background |
| **Trace Gases (Variable)** | < 1 % | CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, O₃, SO₂, NOₓ | Climate forcing & pollution |
| **Highly Variable Gases** | ppb–ppm | VOCs, H₂S, NH₃, CO | Reactive / toxic |
| **Aerosols & Particulates** | µg/m³ | Dust, soot, sulfates, nitrates | Visibility & health |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Atmosphere is a Giant, Slow-Moving Chemical Reactor"**
> Imagine a vast open-topped kitchen pan (the troposphere) sitting on a stove (the sun). Every pollutant released from a vehicle exhaust or a factory chimney is like a new ingredient dropped into the pan. Sunlight (UV photons) acts as the flame. The ingredients don't stay as they were; they cook, react, transform, and create entirely new substances. For example, **NO₂** released from a car does not stay as NO₂ — sunlight cracks it into NO and a free oxygen atom, which then combines with O₂ to form **ozone (O₃)** at ground level. This "newly cooked" ozone is what stings your eyes on a hot summer afternoon. The *chemistry* of air pollution is simply the recipe book of this huge atmospheric pan.

### 1.4 Primary vs. Secondary Pollutants (Foundational Concept)

- **Primary Pollutants:** Emitted directly from a source (e.g., **CO**, **SO₂**, **NO**, **particulates**, **VOCs**).
- **Secondary Pollutants:** Formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions between primary pollutants and natural constituents (e.g., **O₃**, **HNO₃**, **H₂SO₄**, **PAN**, **secondary aerosols**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Keyword:** Always remember — *secondary pollutants are NOT emitted; they are FORMED in the atmosphere*. This is the single most-tested distinction in KTU valuation scripts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Atmospheric Chemistry Matters for an Engineer

For an Air Pollution Control engineer, atmospheric chemistry is **not academic curiosity** — it dictates:

1. The **flue gas treatment chemistry** (scrubbing, SCR/SNCR, ESP).
2. The **design of control devices** (e.g., limestone slurry for SO₂ needs to know the *actual* species: SO₃ vs. SO₄²⁻).
3. The **dispersion modelling** (chemical lifetime of pollutant vs. transport time).
4. The **secondary pollution formation** (if you only control PM, you may still produce smog).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Atmospheric Reaction Pathways

**(A) Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂) Oxidation Chain**

The conversion of SO₂ to sulphuric acid is the cornerstone of acid-rain chemistry.

- **Step 1 — Homogeneous gas-phase oxidation (OH radical pathway, dominant during daytime):**

$$
\text{SO}_2 + \text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{HOSO}_2
$$

- **Step 2 — Reaction with molecular oxygen:**

$$
\text{HOSO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{HO}_2 + \text{SO}_3
$$

- **Step 3 — Hydration of SO₃ to sulphuric acid vapour:**

$$
\text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4
$$

- **Step 4 — Nucleation / condensation onto existing particles → acid aerosol (PM₂.₅).**

**(B) Nitrogen Oxide (NOₓ) Chemistry — Zeldovich Mechanism**

- **Thermal NOₓ (high T > 1300 K, the dominant mechanism in combustion):**

$$
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 \longrightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N}
$$

$$
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}
$$

- **Extended (Zeldovich third reaction):**

$$
\text{N} + \text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}
$$

**(C) Photochemical Smog Cycle (Chapman-inspired urban cycle)**

- **Photolysis of NO₂ by UV (λ < 430 nm):**

$$
\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \longrightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}\cdot
$$

- **Ozone formation (third-body M = N₂ or O₂):**

$$
\text{O}\cdot + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \longrightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}
$$

- **Ozone destruction by NO (Leighton ratio ≈ 1:1):**

$$
\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2
$$

- **VOC-driven net O₃ build-up (simplified):**

$$
\text{RH} + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{RO}_2 \rightarrow \text{HO}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 \rightarrow \text{O}_3
$$

**(D) Carbon Monoxide (CO) Oxidation**

$$
\text{CO} + \text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OH radical is the "detergent of the atmosphere"** — it is the most important atmospheric oxidant and initiates the oxidation of almost every reduced pollutant (CO, CH₄, SO₂, NO₂, VOCs).

**(E) Particulate Matter (PM) Chemistry**

PM is a *physical-chemical mixture*:

- **Sulphates** (NH₄)₂SO₄ from SO₂ oxidation
- **Nitrates** NH₄NO₃ from HNO₃ + NH₃
- **Elemental Carbon (EC)** from incomplete combustion
- **Organic Carbon (OC)** from VOCs condensation
- **Crustal material** SiO₂, Al₂O₃, Fe₂O₃

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula / Reaction | Key Constant or Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Law (used to convert ppm → µg/m³) | $C\,(\mu g/m^3) = \dfrac{\text{ppm} \times M \times P}{R \times T}$ | M = molecular weight (g/mol), P in atm |
| 2 | Leighton Ratio (smog) | $\dfrac{[\text{NO}]}{[\text{NO}_2]} \approx 0.1 - 0.5$ | Without VOCs |
| 3 | SO₂ to H₂SO₄ overall | $2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ | Acid rain precursor |
| 4 | NOₓ to HNO₃ overall | $4\text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{HNO}_3$ | Acid rain precursor |
| 5 | Ozone photostationary state | $K = \dfrac{k_1 \, k_2}{k_3} = \dfrac{[\text{NO}][\text{O}_3]}{[\text{NO}_2]}$ | $k_1, k_2, k_3$ are rate constants |
| 6 | CO → CO₂ | $\text{CO} + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}$ | Tropospheric lifetime ≈ 2 months |
| 7 | Methane oxidation | $\text{CH}_4 + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Lifetime ≈ 10 years |
| 8 | PAN formation | $\text{CH}_3\text{COO}_2 + \text{NO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOONO}_2$ | Laryngeal & eye irritant |
| 9 | CFCs / Ozone Depletion | $\text{CFCl}_3 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{CFCl}_2 + \text{Cl}\cdot$ | λ < 220 nm in stratosphere |
| 10 | Cl catalytic cycle | $\text{Cl} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{ClO} + \text{O}_2$; $\text{ClO} + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cl} + \text{O}_2$ | Net: 2 O₃ destroyed per Cl |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion of ppm to µg/m³ (Board Examination Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation appears almost every year in KTU ESE papers when *any* pollutant concentration is given in ppm. Memorize the logic, not the formula.

**Statement:** Express a concentration of **400 ppm of CO₂** at standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm) in **µg/m³**.

**Step 1 — Define ppm by volume.**

$$
1\,\text{ppm} = 1 \text{ part per million} = \dfrac{1 \text{ volume of CO}_2}{10^6 \text{ volumes of air}}
$$

**Step 2 — Use Ideal Gas Equation for one mole of air mixture at STP-like conditions.**

$$
PV = nRT \Rightarrow n = \dfrac{PV}{RT}
$$

For 1 m³ of air at 25 °C (T = 298 K) and 1 atm (P = 101325 Pa):

$$
n_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{101325 \times 1}{8.314 \times 298} = 40.87 \text{ mol per m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Moles of CO₂ in 400 ppm of that 1 m³.**

$$
n_{\text{CO}_2} = 40.87 \times 400 \times 10^{-6} = 0.01635 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert moles to mass using molecular weight M = 44 g/mol for CO₂.**

$$
m = 0.01635 \times 44 = 0.7194 \text{ g} = 719.4 \text{ mg/m}^3 = 7.19 \times 10^5 \text{ \mu g/m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Compact formula derivation (general case).**

$$
C \left(\dfrac{\mu g}{m^3}\right) = \text{ppm} \times \dfrac{P \times M}{R \times T}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
C = 400 \times \dfrac{101325 \times 44}{8.314 \times 298}
$$

$$
C = 400 \times \dfrac{4{,}458{,}300}{2477.572}
$$

$$
C = 400 \times 1799.3 = 7.197 \times 10^5 \text{ \mu g/m}^3
$$

**Conclusion:** 400 ppm CO₂ = **7.197 × 10⁵ µg/m³** at 25 °C, 1 atm. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Ozone Build-up from the Photochemical Cycle

Given the three coupled equations, derive the net production of O₃ per NO₂ molecule photolysed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(i)}\quad \text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}\cdot \quad (k_1) \\
&\text{(ii)}\quad \text{O}\cdot + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M} \quad (k_2) \\
&\text{(iii)}\quad \text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \quad (k_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Add equations (i) + (ii):**

$$
\text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}_3
$$

**Step 2 — Add the result to equation (iii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \\
\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 &\rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \\
\hline
\text{(net)} \quad \text{NO}_2 + \cancel{\text{O}_2} + \cancel{\text{NO}} + \cancel{\text{O}_3} &\rightarrow \cancel{\text{NO}} + \cancel{\text{O}_3} + \text{NO}_2 + \cancel{\text{O}_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — The result cancels to zero.** This means *without* VOCs or other radical sources, the photostationary state produces **no net O₃**. The cycle is in equilibrium. **That is the photostationary state of Leighton.**

**Step 4 — The role of VOCs:** When VOCs (RH) are present, the OH-initiated chain **converts NO → NO₂ *without* consuming O₃**, breaking the cycle. Effectively:

$$
\text{VOC} \rightarrow \text{RO}_2 \rightarrow \text{HO}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{O}_3 \text{ (net)}
$$

Hence **net O₃ accumulation** in the urban atmosphere requires both **NOₓ** AND **VOCs** AND **sunlight**. This is why KTU examiners love the question *"Why does ozone peak in summer afternoons in cities?"*

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sulphuric Acid Mass Produced from a Given SO₂ Emission

**Problem:** A power plant emits **1000 m³/s** of flue gas at STP containing **1500 ppm SO₂**. Calculate the mass of H₂SO₄ that can theoretically be formed per day.

**Step 1 — Moles of flue gas per second using ideal gas law (T = 273 K, P = 1 atm):**

$$
n_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{P \dot V}{RT} = \dfrac{101325 \times 1000}{8.314 \times 273} = 44\,615 \text{ mol/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of SO₂ per second:**

$$
\dot n_{\text{SO}_2} = 44\,615 \times 1500 \times 10^{-6} = 66.92 \text{ mol/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometry of the overall reaction:**

$$
2 \text{ SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2 \text{ H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2 \text{ H}_2\text{SO}_4
$$

Thus 1 mol SO₂ → 1 mol H₂SO₄.

**Step 4 — Moles of H₂SO₄ per second = 66.92 mol/s.**

**Step 5 — Mass per second (M_H₂SO₄ = 98 g/mol):**

$$
\dot m = 66.92 \times 98 = 6558 \text{ g/s} = 6.558 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Mass per day:**

$$
m_{\text{day}} = 6.558 \times 86400 = 5.66 \times 10^5 \text{ kg/day} = 566.6 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** A 1000 MW coal plant typically produces SO₂ of this order. This is *why* wet limestone scrubbers (FGD) are mandatory in modern thermal power stations — chemistry governs the engineering.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Atmospheric Lifetimes & Significance

| Pollutant | Dominant Sink | Tropospheric Lifetime | Secondary Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO | OH oxidation | 0.1 – 0.5 yr | CO₂ |
| SO₂ | OH / aqueous | 1 – 5 days | H₂SO₄, sulphate aerosol |
| NO | OH / O₃ | 1 – 4 days | NO₂, HNO₃, PAN |
| NO₂ | OH (day) / O₃ | 1 – 4 days | HNO₃, O₃ |
| O₃ | NO, HO₂, surface | Days – weeks | OH (in HOx cycle) |
| CH₄ | OH | ~ 10 yr | CO, CO₂, H₂O |
| CFC-11 | UV photolysis | ~ 55 yr (stratosphere) | Cl· (ozone destruction) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Atmospheric Reaction Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional reaction architecture** of the polluted urban troposphere. It is mapped as a *cause-and-effect block diagram* rather than a physical drawing (since the Mermaid parser cannot render 3-D molecular structures), and shows precisely how a primary pollutant emitted from a source is processed through atmospheric chemistry to yield the secondary pollutants addressed in Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PRIMARY SOURCES: VEHICLES POWER PLANTS INDUSTRIES] --> B1[CO]
    A --> B2[SO2]
    A --> B3[NO]
    A --> B4[HC VOC]
    A --> B5[PM DUST SOOT]

    B1 --> C1[OH RADICAL OXIDATION]
    B2 --> C2[OH AND AQUEOUS OXIDATION]
    B3 --> C3[OZONE TITRATION AND OH PATHWAY]
    B4 --> C4[OH INITIATED CHAIN]
    B5 --> C5[COAGULATION AND CONDENSATION]

    C1 --> D1[CO2 ATMOSPHERIC RESIDENCE]
    C2 --> D2[H2SO4 VAPOUR]
    C3 --> D4[NO2]
    C4 --> D4
    B2 --> D3[SO3]
    C2 --> D3
    D3 --> D2
    D2 --> E1[ACID RAIN H2SO4]
    D2 --> E2[PM2.5 SULPHATE AEROSOL]

    D4 --> F1[PHOTOLYSIS NO2 PLUS HV]
    F1 --> F2[O ATOM PLUS O2 PLUS M]
    F2 --> F3[O3 OZONE]
    F3 --> F4[PAN WITH VOC RO2]
    F3 --> F5[HNO3 WITH OH]

    C4 --> F1
    F5 --> E3[ACID RAIN HNO3]
    E1 --> G[ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACTS: ECOSYSTEM CORROSION HEALTH]
    E3 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    D1 --> G
```

### 4.2 Reading the Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> - The left column represents **emission sources**.
> - The middle column represents the **primary pollutants**.
> - The OH radical / photolysis / aqueous reactions form the **engine block** of atmospheric chemistry.
> - The right column shows the **secondary pollutants** (acid rain, ozone, PAN, PM₂.₅).
> - The final environmental damage block represents the **impact of chemistry on engineering design choices** (e.g., why FGD, SCR, RTO are required).

### 4.3 Schematic — Zeldovich Thermal NOₓ Chain (Functional Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N2 -->|O atom| NO1[NO first step]
    NO1 --> NO2[NO second step]
    N -->|O2| NO2
    NO2 --> NET[NET THERMAL NOX YIELD]
    EXT[EXTENDED ZELDOVICH N PLUS OH] --> NET
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
*Distinguish between primary and secondary air pollutants. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Primary pollutants** are emitted directly into the atmosphere from identifiable sources. Examples: **CO (from incomplete combustion)**, **SO₂ (from sulphur-containing fuels)**, **NO (from high-temperature combustion)**, **particulates (from diesel exhaust)**. (1.5 marks)
- **Secondary pollutants** are *formed in the atmosphere* by chemical reactions between primary pollutants and natural atmospheric constituents. Examples: **O₃ (from NO₂ photolysis chain)**, **H₂SO₄ (from SO₂ oxidation)**, **PAN (from VOC + NO₂)**. (1.5 marks)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
*What is the role of the hydroxyl radical (OH·) in atmospheric chemistry?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The OH radical is the most important **atmospheric oxidant**, often called the *"detergent of the atmosphere."* (1 mark) It initiates the oxidation of virtually every reduced trace gas: **CO → CO₂**, **CH₄ → CO₂ + H₂O**, **SO₂ → H₂SO₄**, **NO₂ → HNO₃**, and **VOCs → aldehydes → PAN / O₃**. (1.5 marks) Its average global concentration in the troposphere is ~10⁶ molecules/cm³, and its lifetime is ~1 second, making it the rate-limiting reactant in atmospheric cleansing. (0.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** write just the reaction equation; explain each species and its role. Skipping the *explanation* costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Always** state whether the pollutant is **primary or secondary** when asked — examiners award 1 mark specifically for this.
> 3. **For ppm-to-µg/m³ conversions**, show the ideal-gas-law substitution explicitly. Marks are split across the law statement, the constant values, and the arithmetic.
> 4. **For smog chemistry**, do *not* write only the three equations — you MUST also explain the role of VOCs in breaking the Leighton ratio. Without it, the answer is incomplete.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** With the help of balanced chemical reactions, explain the formation of **photochemical smog**. Discuss the role of NOₓ, VOCs, and solar radiation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Photochemical smog is a secondary air-pollution phenomenon observed in urban and industrial areas, caused by the interaction of **NOₓ** and **VOCs** in the presence of **sunlight**. (1 mark)

**Step 1 — Initiation: Photolysis of NO₂ by UV radiation (λ < 430 nm):**

$$
\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \longrightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}\cdot
$$

The atomic oxygen produced is highly reactive. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Ozone generation (third-body M = N₂ or O₂):**

$$
\text{O}\cdot + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \longrightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}
$$

Ozone is the principal toxic component of smog. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Leighton ratio (no net O₃ if VOCs absent):**

$$
\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2
$$

The above three reactions form a null cycle in the absence of VOCs. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Role of VOCs — breaking the Leighton cycle:**

VOCs react with OH· to form peroxy radicals (RO₂· and HO₂·):

$$
\text{RH} + \text{OH}\cdot \rightarrow \text{RO}_2\cdot + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
\text{RO}_2\cdot + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{RO}\cdot + \text{NO}_2
$$

This converts NO → NO₂ *without* consuming O₃, decoupling the Leighton cycle and producing **net O₃ accumulation**. (1.5 marks)

**Step 5 — PAN (peroxyacetyl nitrate) formation:**

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{COO}_2 + \text{NO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOONO}_2 \text{ (PAN)}
$$

PAN is a powerful **lachrymator** and **phytotoxin**. (0.5 mark)

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** Peak ozone concentrations occur in **summer afternoons** because the photolysis rate depends on solar intensity. Smog causes eye irritation, plant damage, and visibility reduction. (1 mark)

---

**(b)** A coal-fired boiler emits **800 m³/min** of flue gas at 180 °C and 1 atm, containing **1800 ppm SO₂**. Calculate the theoretical mass of **H₂SO₄ (in kg/hr)** that can form if all SO₂ is oxidised. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert volumetric flow to molar flow using ideal gas law.**

$$
\dot n_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{P \dot V}{RT} = \dfrac{101325 \times \dfrac{800}{60}}{8.314 \times (180 + 273)}
$$

$$
\dot n_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{101325 \times 13.333}{8.314 \times 453} = \dfrac{1.351 \times 10^6}{3766.6} = 358.7 \text{ mol/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of SO₂ per second (1800 ppm = 1800 × 10⁻⁶):**

$$
\dot n_{\text{SO}_2} = 358.7 \times 1800 \times 10^{-6} = 0.6457 \text{ mol/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply stoichiometry.** Overall: $2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$. So 1 mol SO₂ gives 1 mol H₂SO₄.

$$
\dot n_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = 0.6457 \text{ mol/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mass flow (M = 98 g/mol):**

$$
\dot m = 0.6457 \times 98 = 63.28 \text{ g/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kg/hr:**

$$
\dot m = 63.28 \times 3600 = 227\,808 \text{ g/hr} \approx 227.8 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Answer:** ≈ **228 kg/hr of H₂SO₄** would form. (Marks: 1+1+1+1+1 = 5 marks for steps; 2 marks for unit handling and final answer.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Describe the **Zeldovich mechanism** for thermal NOₓ formation. State the two conditions under which thermal NOₐ is dominant. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Zeldovich mechanism describes the **thermal fixation of atmospheric nitrogen** at the high temperatures of combustion.

**Step 1 — Extended Zeldovich (three reactions):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)} \quad \text{O} + \text{N}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N} \\
\text{(ii)} \quad \text{N} + \text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \\
\text{(iii)} \quad \text{N} + \text{OH} &\longrightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H} \quad \text{(extended form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 2 — Rate-controlling step:** Reaction (i) is the rate-limiting step due to the strong **N≡N triple bond (941 kJ/mol)**, which is why thermal NOₓ formation is highly **temperature-sensitive**. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Activation energy:** ~ 318 kJ/mol — very high, hence negligible below 1300 K. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Two conditions for dominance:** (i) Combustion temperature **> 1500 K** (commonly > 1300 K threshold), and (ii) sufficient **residence time** of combustion gases in the high-temperature zone (Zeldovich NO is **time-dependent**, unlike prompt NO). (2 marks)

**Step 5 — Mitigation:** Low-NOₓ burners, staged combustion, flue-gas recirculation (FGR), and SCR/SNCR. (1 mark)

---

**(b)** Explain the formation of **acid rain**. Write the overall chemical equations for the production of **sulphuric acid** and **nitric acid** from their primary precursors. Discuss two engineering control measures. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition.** Acid rain is precipitation with pH < 5.6 (natural unpolluted rain is ~5.6 due to dissolved CO₂). (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Sulphuric acid formation.** Primary precursor: SO₂ from coal/oil combustion.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow 2\text{SO}_3 \\
\text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} &\rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ (sulphuric acid)}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Step 3 — Nitric acid formation.** Primary precursor: NOₓ from high-temperature combustion.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow 2\text{NO}_2 \\
4\text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} &\rightarrow 4\text{HNO}_3 \text{ (nitric acid)}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Step 4 — Atmospheric transport & deposition.** H₂SO₄ and HNO₃ are highly water-soluble, scavenged by cloud droplets, and deposited via **wet deposition** (rain, snow) or **dry deposition** (gas absorption). (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Engineering control measures.** (2 marks)
1. **Flue Gas Desulphurisation (FGD):** Wet limestone scrubber — CaCO₃ + SO₂ → CaSO₃ + CO₂; oxidised to gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O).
2. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) for NOₓ:** 4NH₃ + 4NO + O₂ → 4N₂ + 6H₂O over a TiO₂-V₂O₅ catalyst at 300–400 °C.
3. *Alternative:* low-NOₓ burners, fuel switching (low-sulphur coal), electrostatic precipitators upstream.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Primary vs. Secondary:** Primary = emitted; Secondary = formed in air.
- **Composition of dry air:** N₂ 78.08 %, O₂ 20.95 %, Ar 0.93 %, CO₂ 0.04 %.
- **OH radical:** "Detergent of the atmosphere" — initiates oxidation of CO, CH₄, SO₂, VOCs.
- **Zeldovich NOₓ:** Dominant above 1300 K; rate-limiting step is O + N₂ → NO + N.
- **Photochemical smog:** Requires NOₓ + VOC + sunlight; VOCs break Leighton ratio to give net O₃.
- **PAN:** CH₃COO₂NO₂ — lachrymator, phytotoxin, indicator of smog.
- **Acid rain:** pH < 5.6; precursors SO₂ → H₂SO₄ and NOₓ → HNO₃.
- **CFCs & Ozone Hole:** Cl· catalytic cycle destroys O₃; λ < 220 nm photolysis of CFC-11 in stratosphere.
- **CO:** Incomplete combustion; binds haemoglobin (carboxyhaemoglobin, 200× affinity vs O₂).
- **PM₂.₅ vs PM₁₀:** PM₂.₅ (sulphates, nitrates, OC, EC) penetrates alveoli; PM₁₀ is thoracic fraction.
- **Greenhouse gases (chemistry view):** CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, CFCs, O₃, SF₆ — all absorb IR in the 7–14 µm window.
- **Formula to memorise:**
$$
C\,(\mu g/m^3) = \text{ppm} \times \dfrac{P \times M}{R \times T}
$$
- **Engineering link:** Every chemistry equation has a corresponding control device — know the link (SO₂ → FGD; NOₓ → SCR; PM → ESP/Baghouse; VOC → RTO).
- **Most-tested equation in Module 1:** Leighton photostationary equilibrium and the role of VOCs in net O₃ build-up.
- **For numericals:** Always show the **ideal gas substitution**, **unit conversions**, and **stoichiometric ratio** explicitly. Marks are awarded step-wise.
- **Avoid these errors:**
  - Confusing O₃ in the troposphere (pollutant) with O₃ in the stratosphere (protector).
  - Writing NOₓ when the question specifies NO or NO₂ individually.
  - Forgetting to multiply moles by the **stoichiometric ratio** of the actual reaction.
  - Mixing up residence times: CO (~months) ≠ SO₂ (~days) ≠ NOₓ (~days) ≠ CH₄ (~decades).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adverse Effects on Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Adverse Effects on Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Phytotoxicity due to Air Pollution** is defined as the adverse physiological, biochemical, structural, and reproductive response of vegetation when exposed to ambient concentrations of gaseous, particulate, or aerosol pollutants exceeding the natural tolerance threshold of the plant system. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework for Air Pollution Control (PEPOT524), the injury may manifest as:

- **Visible Foliar Injury** — localized cell/tissue damage observable on leaves.
- **Invisible Physiological Injury** — reduction in photosynthesis rate, enzyme inhibition, stomatal closure, or yield suppression without visible symptoms.
- **Chronic or Acute Injury** — cumulative long-term damage versus sudden dose-dependent injury.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module 1 explicitly identifies **SO₂, NO₂, O₃, HF, PAN, and Suspended Particulates (SPM)** as the primary phytotoxic air pollutants. A student is expected to know the pollutant, its entry pathway, the cellular target, and the visible symptom.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The "Plant Lung" Concept**

Imagine a human standing in a room filled with cigarette smoke. The smoke enters through the mouth (analogous to the **stomata** of a leaf), travels to the lungs (the **mesophyll cells**), and damages the air sacs (**chloroplasts**). The person begins to cough, develops discoloration in tissues, and feels fatigued even without a visible wound. Now, **plants do exactly the same thing** during photosynthesis.

- A plant's **stomata** act like its "mouth and nose."
- The **mesophyll and chloroplasts** function as the plant's "lungs."
- The **toxic gases** (SO₂, O₃, HF) act like "polluted air."

When pollutants enter, they:
1. Dissolve in the **moisture of cell walls** → forming acids (e.g., SO₂ + H₂O → H₂SO₃).
2. Damage **chloroplast membranes** → reducing chlorophyll (chlorosis/necrosis).
3. Compete with CO₂ for the **Rubisco enzyme** → suppressing photosynthesis.
4. Trigger **stimulation of stress ethylene** → premature senescence and leaf abscission.

In short, **plants cannot escape** — they are rooted in the pollution. They are essentially "silent victims," showing damage only when concentrations exceed their biochemical resilience.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

The following standardized parameters govern phytotoxicity assessment:

- **ppm (parts per million)** — volumetric mixing ratio of pollutant (1 ppm = 1000 ppb).
- **µg/m³** — mass-based concentration (governed by STP: T = 25 °C, P = 101.325 kPa).
- **Molar volume at NTP** = **24.45 L/mol** (T = 25 °C, P = 1 atm).
- **AOT40** — Accumulated ozone exposure Over a Threshold of 40 ppb (in ppb·h, used for vegetation).
- **SUM06** — Sum of all hourly O₃ concentrations ≥ 60 ppb.
- **Critical Level (CL)** — the concentration below which no significant effect is observed on the most sensitive species.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Constant for KTU Problems:**
> $$\text{C}_{\text{ppm}} = \frac{\text{C}_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{\text{Molecular Weight}}$$
> This is the most-tested formula in KTU numericals involving pollutant concentration conversion for plant-dose calculations.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Phytotoxic Damage — The Four-Stage Pathway

The injury progression in vegetation follows a deterministic biochemical cascade:

### **Stage 1 — Deposition & Stomatal Uptake**
- Gaseous pollutants diffuse through the **stomatal aperture**.
- Entry is governed by **stomatal conductance (gₛ)** measured in mmol H₂O m⁻² s⁻¹.
- Diurnal cycle governs uptake: **maximum at midday, minimum at night (closed stomata).**
- Wet-deposited pollutants (acidic mists) bypass the stomatal route via **cuticular sorption**.

### **Stage 2 — Dissolution & Acidification**
Inside the substomatal cavity, soluble gases dissolve in the **apoplastic water** (cell wall moisture):
- SO₂ + H₂O → H₂SO₃ → HSO₃⁻ + H⁺
- NO₂ + H₂O → HNO₃ + HNO₂
- HF is a weak acid but extreme in phytotoxicity due to fluoride ion reactivity.

The resulting **H⁺ accumulation** causes **acidification of the cytoplasm**, disrupting pH-dependent enzyme systems.

### **Stage 3 — Cellular & Subcellular Damage**
- **Chloroplast thylakoid membrane** disorganization → loss of **chlorophyll a/b ratio**.
- **Rubisco inhibition** → reduced carbon fixation.
- **Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS)** generation: superoxide (O₂⁻•), hydroxyl (•OH), hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) — triggering **lipid peroxidation** of cell membranes.
- **K⁺ ion leakage** across tonoplast → loss of turgor, wilting.

### **Stage 4 — Visible & Invisible Injury Expression**
- **Necrosis** (brown/tan dead patches — usually interveinal or marginal).
- **Chlorosis** (yellowing due to chlorophyll loss — uniform or stippled).
- **Stippling / Bronzing** — characteristic of O₃ injury on upper leaf surface.
- **Glazing / Silvering** — characteristic of PAN injury on lower leaf surface.
- **Yield reduction** in cereals, legumes, fruit trees — may reach **10–50 %** at chronic exposures.

---

## 2.2 Major Pollutants and Their Plant-Specific Targets

| Pollutant | Primary Target | Visible Symptom | Sensitive Species | Internalization Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SO₂** | Chloroplast, Rubisco | Interveinal **bifacial necrosis** on broadleaf; tip-burn on conifers | Alfalfa, Soybean, Lichens | Stomatal → forms HSO₃⁻ |
| **O₃** | Palisade mesophyll | Upper-surface **stippling / bronzing** | Tobacco (Bel W3), Tomato, Grape | Stomatal → ROS generation |
| **NO₂** | Membranes, Photosystem I | Marginal **chlorosis**, growth suppression | Spinach, Lettuce, Tomato | Stomatal → forms NO₃⁻/NO₂⁻ |
| **HF** | Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ metabolism, enzymes | **Tip and marginal necrosis** (concentrated at leaf apex), abscission | Gladiolus, Tulip, Ponderosa Pine | Stomatal → fluoride ion accumulation |
| **PAN** | Sulfhydryl (-SH) groups, auxin destruction | **Silvering / bronzing on lower leaf** surface | Petunia, Bean, Lettuce | Stomatal → deactivates auxin |
| **SPM (Dust)** | Stomatal block, light reduction | Surface graying, reduced photosynthesis | All plants near roads/industry | Mechanical stomatal plugging |
| **Ethylene** | Senescence hormone | **Epinasty, chlorosis, abscission** | Orchid, Carnation, Tomato | Stomatal & cuticular |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** SO₂ historically was the *classical phytotoxicant*; however, since the 1980s, **O₃ is recognized as the most damaging phytotoxic pollutant globally** due to its high reactivity and ubiquity in photochemical smog. KTU often frames questions comparing SO₂ vs O₃ injury diagnosis.

---

## 2.3 Factors Affecting Plant Susceptibility

Plant response is non-linear and modulated by:

- **Genetic & Species Factors** — C3 plants > C4 plants in O₃ sensitivity; deciduous > conifers in SO₂.
- **Developmental Stage** — Young expanding leaves are most vulnerable.
- **Stomatal Conductance (gₛ)** — higher gₛ = greater uptake = greater risk.
- **Environmental Co-factors** — high light, high temperature, adequate soil moisture (all increase gₛ) → amplify damage.
- **Time of Day** — pre-dawn fumigation causes minimal injury; midday fumigation causes maximum injury.
- **Nutrient Status** — adequate K⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ confer tolerance; P, K deficiency increases susceptibility.
- **Pollutant Synergism / Antagonism** — SO₂ + NO₂ shows **synergism** (combined damage > sum of individual).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Relation | Variable Definitions | Engineering / Field Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{C}_{\text{ppm}} = \dfrac{\text{C}_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{\text{MW}}$ | MW = Molecular weight (g/mol); 24.45 = molar volume (L) at 25 °C, 1 atm | Used to convert plant-experimental concentrations reported in µg/m³ to ppm for comparison with AOT40 standards |
| 2 | $\text{AOT40} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left([O_3]_i - 40\right) \quad \text{for } [O_3]_i > 40 \text{ ppb}$ | $[O_3]_i$ = hourly mean O₃ concentration; only daylight hours (08:00–20:00) | UN-ECE critical level for crop yield loss protection; AOT40 ≤ 3000 ppb·h (crops), ≤ 6000 ppb·h (forest trees) |
| 3 | $\text{SUM06} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}[O_3]_i \quad \text{for } [O_3]_i \geq 60 \text{ ppb}$ | Sum of all hourly O₃ ≥ 60 ppb, daylight hours | US-EPA alternative index for forest protection |
| 4 | $D_{\text{absorbed}} = g_s \cdot \Delta C \cdot t$ | gₛ = stomatal conductance; ΔC = conc. gradient; t = time | Flux-based dose = directly linked to plant injury, more accurate than ambient concentration |
| 5 | $\text{Chlorophyll loss (\%)} = \dfrac{(A_{\text{control}} - A_{\text{treated}})}{A_{\text{control}}} \times 100$ | A = chlorophyll absorbance at 665 nm (chlorophyll a) | Standard bioindicator assay used in laboratory phytotoxicity studies |
| 6 | $\text{Critical Level (CL)} = f(\text{species}, \text{stage}, \text{edaphic factors})$ | CL = threshold conc. protecting 95 % of species | Regulatory standard-setting in environmental impact assessment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Tip for KTU 2024:** Memorize the AOT40 formula — it has appeared in past KTU ESE questions (Dec 2021, Jun 2022) as a 7-mark sub-question in Module 1 of Air Pollution Control.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Pollutant Concentration Conversion (µg/m³ ↔ ppm)

**Why this derivation matters for KTU:**
Plant-exposure experiments are reported in either **mass units (µg/m³)** or **mixing ratio units (ppm/ppb)**. Regulatory standards (WHO, NAAQS) use both interchangeably. A KTU 14-mark problem may provide ambient SO₂ as 80 µg/m³ and ask the student to confirm whether it exceeds the vegetation critical level of 0.03 ppm (60-min average).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We start from the **Ideal Gas Law** at standard temperature and pressure (NTP: 25 °C, 1 atm):

$$P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$$

Solving for the molar volume $V_m = V/n$:

$$V_m = \frac{R \cdot T}{P}$$

Substituting R = 0.082057 L·atm/(mol·K), T = 298.15 K, P = 1 atm:

$$V_m = \frac{0.082057 \times 298.15}{1} = 24.45 \text{ L/mol}$$

This is the **molar volume** of an ideal gas at 25 °C, 1 atm.

Now, by definition of **ppm (parts per million)** for gases:

$$1 \text{ ppm} = \frac{1 \text{ part pollutant}}{10^6 \text{ parts air (by volume)}}$$

In volumetric terms, 1 ppm = 1 µL of pollutant per litre of air. The number of moles in 1 µL is $1/24.45 \times 10^{-6}$ mol. The mass corresponding to 1 ppm of a pollutant of molecular weight MW is:

$$1 \text{ ppm} = \frac{\text{MW (g/mol)} \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol}}{24.45 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3}$$

Simplifying:

$$1 \text{ ppm} = \frac{\text{MW}}{24.45} \text{ mg/m}^3 = \frac{\text{MW}}{24.45} \times 1000 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

Therefore, the general conversion formula is:

$$\boxed{\text{C}_{\mu g/m^3} = \text{C}_{ppm} \times \frac{\text{MW}}{24.45} \times 1000}$$

Or its inverse form:

$$\text{C}_{ppm} = \frac{\text{C}_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{\text{MW} \times 1000}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A monitoring station records ambient SO₂ as 80 µg/m³. The vegetation critical level for SO₂ (1-hr average) is 0.03 ppm. Does the measured value exceed the critical level? (MW of SO₂ = 64).

**Solution — Step 1:** Convert 80 µg/m³ to ppm.

$$\text{C}_{ppm} = \frac{80 \times 24.45}{64 \times 1000} = \frac{1956}{64000} = 0.0306 \text{ ppm}$$

**Solution — Step 2:** Compare with critical level.

$$0.0306 \text{ ppm} > 0.030 \text{ ppm (CL)}$$

**Solution — Step 3:** Conclude.

The measured concentration **exceeds** the vegetation critical level by 0.0006 ppm (= 1.9 % over CL). This concentration is sufficient to induce **acute visible injury in sensitive species** (alfalfa, soybean, gladiolus).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: AOT40 Critical Level Computation

**Why this matters for KTU:** AOT40 is the **UN-ECE standard metric** for crop yield protection. The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) explicitly includes "Critical Levels and Dose-Response Relationships" — and AOT40 is the most widely tested index.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $[O_3]_i$ denote the hourly mean ozone concentration (in ppb) recorded for the $i$-th daylight hour (08:00 to 20:00) over a defined growing season (May–July for crops, April–September for forests).

The accumulated dose over the threshold is defined as:

$$\text{AOT40} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \max\left(0, [O_3]_i - 40\right) \text{ ppb}\cdot\text{h}$$

The $\max(0, \cdot)$ operator ensures that hours with $[O_3]_i \leq 40$ ppb contribute zero — i.e., only supra-threshold exposure is counted.

Equivalently, defining $\Delta_i = [O_3]_i - 40$ when $[O_3]_i > 40$:

$$\text{AOT40} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta_i \cdot H(\Delta_i)$$

where $H(\cdot)$ is the Heaviside step function.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** The following 5 hourly daylight ozone concentrations were recorded: 30, 55, 62, 45, 70 ppb. Calculate AOT40. Does it exceed the 6-month crop protection level of 3000 ppb·h (assume the 5 hours are representative of the 6-month period, i.e., scale linearly)?

**Solution — Step 1:** Apply threshold 40 ppb.

| Hour i | $[O_3]_i$ (ppb) | $[O_3]_i - 40$ (ppb) | Contribution (ppb·h) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | −10 | 0 |
| 2 | 55 | +15 | 15 |
| 3 | 62 | +22 | 22 |
| 4 | 45 | +5 | 5 |
| 5 | 70 | +30 | 30 |

**Solution — Step 2:** Sum the contributions.

$$\text{AOT40 (5 hours)} = 0 + 15 + 22 + 5 + 30 = 72 \text{ ppb}\cdot\text{h}$$

**Solution — Step 3:** Linear scaling to 6 months.

Total daylight hours in 6 months (≈ 180 days × 12 h/day) = 2160 hours.

$$\text{AOT40 (6 months)} = 72 \times \frac{2160}{5} = 72 \times 432 = 31{,}104 \text{ ppb}\cdot\text{h}$$

**Solution — Step 4:** Compare with crop protection CL.

$$31{,}104 \text{ ppb}\cdot\text{h} \gg 3000 \text{ ppb}\cdot\text{h (CL)}$$

The AOT40 value exceeds the crop protection critical level by approximately **10 times** → significant yield loss is expected in sensitive crops like tomato, soybean, and wheat.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pollutant → Mechanism → Symptom → Indicator Plant

| Pollutant | Chemical / Cellular Mechanism | Threshold (1-hr avg, vegetation) | Visible Symptom | Diagnostic Indicator Plant (Bioindicator) | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SO₂** | HSO₃⁻ formation, Rubisco sulfitolysis, thylakoid lipid peroxidation | ~ 0.03 ppm | Bifacial interveinal necrosis | Alfalfa (*Medicago sativa*) | High |
| **O₃** | ROS burst, lipid peroxidation, ascorbate depletion, accelerated senescence | ~ 80 ppb (8-hr) | Upper-surface stippling | Tobacco Bel W3, Pinto Bean | Very High |
| **NO₂** | NO₃⁻/NO₂⁻ accumulation, nitrosamine formation, Photosystem I inhibition | ~ 0.5 ppm | Marginal chlorosis, suppressed growth | Spinach, Tomato | Medium |
| **HF** | F⁻ binds to Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺, inactivates enolase & phosphoglucomutase | ~ 0.1 ppb (extremely low!) | Tip & marginal necrosis, abscission | Gladiolus, Tulip, Ponderosa Pine | High |
| **PAN** | Sulfhydryl (-SH) group oxidation, auxin destruction | ~ 15 ppb (acute) | Silvering/bronzing on lower leaf | Petunia, Lettuce | Medium |
| **SPM** | Stomatal plugging, reduced PAR transmission, leaf-temperature rise | Variable | Surface dust deposition | All species near industry | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** "Fluorides are the most phytotoxic *per unit mass* of any gaseous pollutant" — this is a frequently asked 3-mark direct question.

---

## 3.4 Dose-Response Relationship — Conceptual Derivative

The classical plant response to increasing pollutant dose follows a **sigmoidal (logistic) curve**:

$$R(C) = \frac{R_{max}}{1 + \exp\left(-k(C - C_{50})\right)}$$

where:
- $R(C)$ = plant response (% injury or % yield loss),
- $R_{max}$ = asymptotic maximum response,
- $C_{50}$ = concentration causing 50 % of $R_{max}$,
- $k$ = sensitivity coefficient (species-specific).

The **No-Observed-Adverse-Effect Level (NOAEL)** corresponds to the concentration $C_{NOAEL}$ below which $R(C) \to 0$ — this is operationally the **Critical Level (CL)** used in regulation.

The **Lowest-Observed-Adverse-Effect Level (LOAEL)** marks the inflection of the sigmoidal curve where injury becomes statistically detectable.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Pathway of Phytotoxic Damage

This Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture flow** of how an air pollutant cascades from ambient atmosphere to plant yield loss. It is a controlled, deterministic schematic suitable for KTU 14-mark descriptive answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ambient Pollutant Source: SO2, O3, NO2, HF, PAN, SPM] --> B[Atmospheric Transport: Advection and Diffusion]
    B --> C[Leaf Boundary Layer Contact: Deposition Velocity vd]
    C --> D{Stomatal Aperture: Open or Closed}
    D -- Open --> E[Substomatal Cavity Entry: g_s Conductance]
    D -- Closed --> F[Cuticular Sorption: Slow Pathway]
    E --> G[Apoplastic Dissolution: Pollutant plus H2O]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Acidification: Formation of HSO3, NO3, F minus]
    H --> I[Cellular ROS Burst: O2 minus, H2O2, OH radical]
    I --> J[Membrane Lipid Peroxidation: Thylakoid and Tonoplast Damage]
    J --> K[Chloroplast and Enzyme Inactivation: Rubisco, Enolase]
    K --> L[Visible Injury: Necrosis and Chlorosis and Stippling]
    L --> M[Physiological Decline: Photosynthesis Reduced]
    M --> N[Growth Suppression: Reduced Biomass]
    N --> O[Yield Loss: Crop and Forest Productivity Decline]
    O --> P[Economic and Ecological Impact]
```

**Reading Guide for KTU Answer Writing:**
- Top-down flow = **logical sequence** of injury.
- The decision diamond (D) is the **rate-determining step** — emphasize stomatal conductance in your answer.
- The two output arms (Open vs Closed) explain **why fumigation timing matters**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Comparative Symptom Diagnosis Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SO2injury[SO2 Injury Profile]
        SO2A[Entry via Stomata] --> SO2B[Acidification of Mesophyll]
        SO2B --> SO2C[Bifacial Interveinal Necrosis]
        SO2C --> SO2D[Sensitive: Alfalfa, Soybean]
    end
    subgraph O3injury[O3 Injury Profile]
        O3A[Entry via Stomata] --> O3B[ROS Generation in Apoplast]
        O3B --> O3C[Upper Surface Stippling and Bronzing]
        O3C --> O3D[Sensitive: Tobacco Bel W3, Tomato]
    end
    subgraph HFinjury[HF Injury Profile]
        HFA[Entry via Stomata] --> HFB[Fluoride Ion Accumulation at Leaf Apex]
        HFB --> HFC[Tip and Marginal Necrosis with Abscission]
        HFC --> HFD[Sensitive: Gladiolus, Ponderosa Pine]
    end
    subgraph PANinjury[PAN Injury Profile]
        PANA[Entry via Stomata] --> PANB[Auxin Destruction and SH Oxidation]

        PANC --> PAND[Sensitive: Petunia, Lettuce]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagnostic Mnemonic for KTU:**
> - **S**O₂ → **S**ymmetrical, **S**hows both sides.
> - **O**₃ → **O**nly upper surface (O = One side).
> - **H**F → **H**igh in **H**eight (apex first, then margin).
> - **P**AN → **P**referentially **P**ronounced on **P**osterior/lower surface.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Dose-Response Sigmoidal Curve (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Concentration C: ppb or ppm] --> B{Threshold Zone}
    B -- Below NOAEL --> C[No Detectable Injury]
    B -- Between NOAEL and LOAEL --> D[Subclinical: Biochemical Changes]
    B -- Above LOAEL --> E[Visible Injury: Chlorosis and Necrosis]
    E --> F[Saturation: Maximum Response Reached]
    F --> G[Yield Loss Plateau: Economic Threshold Exceeded]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define phytotoxicity. List the major gaseous air pollutants that cause plant injury and identify the most damaging among them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Phytotoxicity** is the capacity of an air pollutant, at ambient concentrations, to cause harmful physiological, biochemical, or structural damage in plants — manifesting as reduced photosynthesis, foliar necrosis, chlorosis, premature senescence, or yield loss.

**Major gaseous pollutants causing plant injury (3 marks):**
1. **Sulphur dioxide (SO₂)** — classical phytotoxicant, causes bifacial interveinal necrosis.
2. **Ozone (O₃)** — currently the most damaging globally; causes upper-surface stippling.
3. **Nitrogen dioxide (NO₂)** — marginal chlorosis, growth suppression.
4. **Hydrogen fluoride (HF)** — most toxic per unit mass; tip and marginal necrosis.
5. **Peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN)** — silvering of lower leaf surface.
6. **Ethylene (C₂H₄)** — epinasty and abscission even at sub-ppm levels.

**Most damaging:** Ozone (O₃) — due to its high photochemical reactivity, ubiquity in tropospheric smog, and broad-spectrum cellular ROS damage.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]`

**Explain the role of stomata in plant injury from air pollutants. Why is fumigation timing critical?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of stomata:**
- Stomata are the **primary entry route** for gaseous air pollutants (SO₂, O₃, NO₂, HF, PAN).
- The pollutant flux into the leaf is governed by **stomatal conductance (gₛ)** and the **concentration gradient** between ambient air and the substomatal cavity.
- Once inside, the gas dissolves in the apoplastic water and exerts its phytotoxic action (acidification, enzyme inhibition, ROS generation).

**Criticality of fumigation timing:**
- Stomata are **open in daylight (especially mid-morning to mid-afternoon)** and **closed at night**.
- Therefore, a pollutant exposure of, say, 0.1 ppm at noon causes **far greater injury** than the same exposure at midnight.
- Diurnal fumigation experiments show that **midday exposure produces maximum visible injury**, while pre-dawn exposure often shows **no detectable injury** despite identical concentration.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Writing only the pollutant name without describing the **mechanism of cellular damage** → loses 3–4 marks. KTU examiners explicitly look for "how" the pollutant injures the cell.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Confusing **SO₂ necrosis (bifacial/interveinal)** with **O₃ stippling (upper surface only)** → loses 2 marks. Always state the **leaf side** of symptom expression.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Omitting **units and threshold values** in dose-response answers → loses 1–2 marks. Always cite NOAEL/LOAEL or AOT40 values.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Writing essays without diagrams. **A flowchart or pathway diagram carries 2 marks** in KTU 14-mark answers (see Section 4 of these notes).

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Describe in detail the mechanism of ozone (O₃) injury in plants. Differentiate it from SO₂ injury.**

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism of O₃ Injury (7 marks):**

1. **Entry (1 mark):** O₃ enters the leaf through open stomata and diffuses into the **apoplastic space** between the plasmalemma and the cell wall. Unlike SO₂, ozone **does not require dissolution in water** for entry — its high reactivity allows direct interaction with the **plasmalemma lipid bilayer**.

2. **ROS Generation (2 marks):** Upon contact with the plasma membrane, O₃ reacts with the ethylene and double-bond lipids to generate **reactive oxygen species (ROS)** — superoxide radical (O₂⁻•), hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂), and hydroxyl radical (•OH). These ROS initiate a **chain peroxidation of membrane lipids**, destroying membrane integrity.

3. **Cellular Damage (2 marks):** The lipid peroxidation cascade:
   - Inactivates membrane-bound **ATPases** and **transporters**.
   - Causes **K⁺ ion leakage** from the cytoplasm.
   - Disrupts **chloroplast thylakoid membranes**, degrading chlorophyll a and b.
   - Inactivates **Rubisco** and other Calvin-cycle enzymes through oxidative modification.

4. **Visible Injury (1 mark):** Upper-surface **stippling / bronzing / flecking** on older leaves. **Acutely**, **water-soaked lesions** appear, which then dry to tan necrotic spots. **Chronically**, **premature senescence, abscission, and yield suppression** are seen.

5. **Recovery and Repair (1 mark):** Antioxidant systems (ascorbate, glutathione, superoxide dismutase) attempt repair; however, chronic exposure overwhelms these defenses, leading to cumulative injury.

**Differentiation from SO₂ Injury (within the 7 marks):**

| Aspect | O₃ | SO₂ |
|---|---|---|
| Entry | Stomata (direct diffusion) | Stomata (dissolves in apoplastic water) |
| Primary action | ROS-mediated lipid peroxidation | Acidification (HSO₃⁻) and Rubisco sulfitolysis |
| Symptom location | **Upper** leaf surface (palisade cells) | **Bifacial** (both surfaces) |
| Symptom type | Stippling, bronzing, fleck | Interveinal necrosis, bifacial |
| Sensitive species | Tobacco Bel W3, tomato | Alfalfa, soybean |
| Threshold | ~ 80 ppb (8-hr) | ~ 0.03 ppm (1-hr) |
| Recovery | Slow (oxidative damage) | Moderate (acid-base buffering) |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Discuss the factors affecting plant susceptibility to air pollution. Explain synergism and antagonism with examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Factors affecting plant susceptibility (4 marks):**

1. **Genetic / Species Factors (1 mark):** Species exhibit intrinsic tolerance ranges. C3 plants (wheat, soybean) are more O₃-sensitive than C4 plants (maize, sugarcane). Lichens and mosses are ultra-sensitive SO₂ indicators (absent in polluted zones = "lichen desert").

2. **Leaf Morphology & Stomatal Density (1 mark):** Higher stomatal density, larger aperture, thinner cuticle → greater pollutant flux. Conifers with sunken stomata are more protected than broadleaves with exposed stomata.

3. **Developmental Stage (0.5 mark):** Young, expanding leaves are most vulnerable. Mature leaves are more resistant due to thickened cuticle.

4. **Environmental Conditions (1 mark):**
   - **Light, temperature, and soil moisture** all increase stomatal conductance → amplify damage.
   - Adequate N, P, K nutrition enhances tolerance.
   - High relative humidity increases wet-deposition of soluble gases.
   - Drought-stressed plants with closed stomata show **less visible injury** but also less photosynthesis.

5. **Co-pollutant Effects (0.5 mark):** Pre-exposure to one pollutant can induce antioxidant defenses (pre-conditioning) or worsen injury (synergism).

**Synergism and Antagonism (3 marks):**

- **Synergism** is the phenomenon where the combined effect of two or more pollutants **exceeds the sum of their individual effects** (1 + 1 > 2). Example: SO₂ + NO₂ exposure produces significantly more injury in alfalfa and soybean than either pollutant alone, because the combined acidification and oxidative stress overwhelm cellular pH buffering and antioxidant capacity (1.5 marks).

- **Antagonism** is the opposite — combined effect is **less than the sum** (1 + 1 < 2). Example: Pre-exposure to low-dose O₃ induces elevated ascorbate and glutathione pools, which then confer partial protection against a subsequent SO₂ exposure. Also, certain gaseous pollutants (e.g., NO) can **scavenge O₃**, reducing net O₃ flux to vegetation (1.5 marks).

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2022]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the concept of Critical Levels (CL) for vegetation. Compute AOT40 for the given hourly ozone data and interpret it against UN-ECE standards.**

**Data provided in question (synthetic, for illustration):**
| Hour (08:00–12:00) | O₃ (ppb) |
|---|---|
| 08:00 | 35 |
| 09:00 | 50 |
| 10:00 | 65 |
| 11:00 | 45 |
| 12:00 | 70 |

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Critical Levels (3 marks):**
- A **Critical Level (CL)** is the **concentration of an air pollutant** in the atmosphere **below which no significant adverse effect** is expected on a defined receptor (vegetation, in this case), based on present scientific knowledge (1 mark).
- It is derived from **dose-response relationships** and is intended to protect the **most sensitive species** (95 % protection) within an ecosystem (1 mark).
- The CL is expressed as either an **ambient concentration (ppm, ppb)** or as a **flux-based dose (AOT40, SUM06, accumulated exposure)** over a defined averaging time and growing season (1 mark).

**AOT40 Computation (3 marks):**

Applying the formula $\text{AOT40} = \sum \max(0, [O_3]_i - 40)$:

| Hour | $[O_3]_i$ (ppb) | $[O_3]_i - 40$ | Contribution (ppb·h) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 08:00 | 35 | −5 | 0 |
| 09:00 | 50 | +10 | 10 |
| 10:00 | 65 | +25 | 25 |
| 11:00 | 45 | +5 | 5 |
| 12:00 | 70 | +30 | 30 |

$$\text{AOT40 (5 hours)} = 0 + 10 + 25 + 5 + 30 = 70 \text{ ppb}\cdot\text{h}$$

Scaling to a 3-month (90-day) growing season at 12 daylight hours/day (≈ 1080 h):

$$\text{AOT40 (season)} = 70 \times \frac{1080}{5} = 70 \times 216 = 15{,}120 \text{ ppb}\cdot\text{h}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
- UN-ECE CL for **crops** = **3000 ppb·h** (over 3 months).
- UN-ECE CL for **forest trees** = **6000 ppb·h** (over 6 months).
- Computed AOT40 = **15,120 ppb·h**, which is **5× greater than the crop CL** and **2.5× greater than the forest CL**.
- **Conclusion:** This site exhibits severe photochemical ozone pollution with high potential for **yield loss in sensitive crops** (tomato, soybean, wheat) and **visible foliar injury in forest trees**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Discuss the role of HF and PAN as phytotoxic pollutants. How does fluoride accumulation differ from other pollutant accumulation patterns in plants?**

**Model Solution:**

**Hydrogen Fluoride (HF) Injury (3.5 marks):**

1. **Source:** Released from aluminium smelters, phosphate fertilizer plants, ceramic and brick kilns, and coal combustion.
2. **Toxicity:** HF is the **most phytotoxic gaseous air pollutant per unit mass** because fluoride ions (F⁻) form strong complexes with Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, and Al³⁺, disrupting calcium-dependent enzymes (e.g., enolase, calmodulin, ATPases) (1 mark).
3. **Visible Symptom:** **Tip and marginal necrosis** — distinct because fluoride ions are transported in the **xylem transpiration stream** to the **leaf apex and margins**, where they accumulate at the highest concentrations (1 mark).
4. **Sensitive Species:** Gladiolus, tulip, ponderosa pine, and apricot are classic sensitive indicators. Lichens are highly sensitive to HF (0.5 mark).
5. **Economic Impact:** Fluoride injury to cattle grazing on contaminated forage causes **fluorosis** in livestock — a classic example of air-to-plant-to-animal transfer (1 mark).

**Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN) Injury (2 marks):**

1. **Source:** Secondary pollutant formed in photochemical smog from VOC + NOₓ reactions.
2. **Mechanism:** PAN oxidises **sulfhydryl (-SH) groups** of proteins and destroys the **plant hormone auxin (IAA)**, leading to growth abnormalities and silvering of the lower leaf surface (1 mark).
3. **Visible Symptom:** **Silvery, bronzed, or glazed appearance on the lower leaf surface**; in acute cases, water-soaked lesions appear. Petunia and bean are classic bioindicators (1 mark).

**Fluoride Accumulation vs Other Pollutants (1.5 marks):**

Unlike SO₂ (which is metabolized to sulfate and stored) or NO₂ (which is incorporated as plant nitrogen), **fluoride ions accumulate progressively in leaf tissue** without significant metabolism. Sensitive species (e.g., gladiolus) can show injury at ambient HF levels as low as **0.1 ppb over 7 days** — making HF the lowest-threshold phytotoxicant. Also, **older leaves accumulate more F⁻** because the accumulation is **cumulative and irreversible** over the leaf lifespan.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 1 — Adverse Effects on Plants**

- **Phytotoxicity** = damage to plants from air pollutants; can be visible (necrosis/chlorosis) or invisible (physiological/biochemical).
- **Primary entry route** for gaseous pollutants = **stomata**; stomatal conductance (gₛ) is the rate-determining parameter.
- **Six major phytotoxic pollutants** (in syllabus priority order): **SO₂, O₃, NO₂, HF, PAN, SPM (dust)**. Plus ethylene as a hormonal disruptor.
- **SO₂ injury** = **bifacial interveinal necrosis**; classical phytotoxicant; sensitive species = alfalfa, soybean, lichens.
- **O₃ injury** = **upper-surface stippling/bronzing**; most damaging globally; sensitive species = tobacco Bel W3, tomato, grape.
- **HF injury** = **tip and marginal necrosis**; **most toxic per unit mass**; sensitive species = gladiolus, ponderosa pine.
- **PAN injury** = **lower-surface silvering/bronzing**; destroys auxin and -SH groups; sensitive species = petunia, lettuce.
- **NO₂ injury** = **marginal chlorosis** and growth suppression; sensitive species = spinach, tomato.
- **Critical Level (CL)** = pollutant concentration below which no significant vegetation effect is expected.
- **AOT40** = UN-ECE metric for O₃ exposure over 40 ppb; CL = 3000 ppb·h (crops), 6000 ppb·h (forest).
- **Conversion formula** (memorize): $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{C_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{\text{MW} \times 1000}$.
- **Molar volume at NTP (25 °C, 1 atm)** = **24.45 L/mol**.
- **Synergism** (e.g., SO₂ + NO₂) > additive effect; **Antagonism** (e.g., low O₃ pre-exposure induces antioxidant defense) < additive effect.
- **Plant susceptibility factors**: species, leaf morphology, developmental stage, stomatal conductance, light/temperature/humidity, nutrient status.
- **Stomata are open in daylight** → fumigation timing matters; midday exposure = maximum injury.
- **Lichens = most sensitive bioindicators**; their absence in industrial zones is called the "lichen desert."
- **C3 plants > C4 plants** in O₃ sensitivity.
- **Young expanding leaves** = most vulnerable; older leaves = more resistant (except for HF accumulation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Animals and Human Beings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Animals and Human Beings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Air Pollution Control Engineering**, "Animals and Human Beings" refers to the study of **bioreceptors** — the living organisms (humans, mammals, birds, aquatic fauna) that absorb, accumulate, and biologically respond to atmospheric contaminants. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Air Pollution Control*, this topic establishes the **receptor end of the source-pathway-receptor (S-P-R) model**, quantifying how criteria and hazardous air pollutants (HAPs) translate into adverse health endpoints such as mortality, morbidity, mutagenicity, and teratogenicity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Students must be able to (a) classify air pollutants, (b) identify their sources, and (c) **evaluate their effects on human health, vegetation, animals, and materials**. This topic directly addresses outcome **(c)** and maps to **CO1 (Understand the fundamentals of air pollution)** with Bloom's cognitive level **L2 – Understand** and **L3 – Apply**.

### 1.2 The Receptor Concept in Air Pollution

A **receptor** is any entity (human, animal, plant, material, or ecosystem) that experiences the impact of a pollutant after it is emitted from a source and transported through the atmosphere.

$$
\text{Source} \xrightarrow{\text{Transport (Pathway)}} \text{Receptor}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Source-Pathway-Receptor (S-P-R) Model:**
> - **Source** = Combustion, industrial processes, vehicles.
> - **Pathway** = Atmospheric dispersion, transformation, deposition.
> - **Receptor** = Human lungs, animal respiratory systems, leaf stomata, building facades.
> Control engineering aims to *interrupt* any one of these three links.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Smoky Kitchen**
> Imagine a kitchen where someone is deep-frying food (the **source**). The smoke drifts through the corridor (the **pathway**) and reaches the bedroom where a sleeping child is breathing it in (the **receptor**). The child coughs and develops watery eyes. Similarly, factories emit pollutants (sources), the wind carries them (pathway), and humans/animals downwind breathe them in (receptors) and fall sick. Air Pollution Control Engineering is the science of installing a chimney, an exhaust hood, or an air purifier — *interrupting the pathway*.

## 1.4 Why Human Beings Are the Most Critical Receptors

Human beings occupy the apex of the biological hierarchy in receptor analysis because:

1. **Respiratory System Vulnerability** — We inhale ~**11,000 L of air per day** (≈ 15 kg), far exceeding the mass of food (~2 kg) and water (~2 L) consumed.
2. **Bypassed Defences** — Gaseous pollutants and fine particulates (PM$_{2.5}$) bypass the nasal filtering mechanism and deposit deep in the **alveolar region** of the lungs.
3. **Systemic Distribution** — Once absorbed, pollutants enter the bloodstream and damage multiple organs.
4. **Socioeconomic Burden** — Health impacts translate into lost workdays, hospitalization costs, and reduced life expectancy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Did You Know?**
> The **WHO (2022)** estimates that **99% of the global population breathes air exceeding WHO guideline limits**, causing an estimated **6.7 million premature deaths annually** attributable to ambient and household air pollution combined.

---

## 1.5 Categories of Receptors

| Receptor Type | Examples | Key Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|
| **Human Beings** | Adults, children, elderly, asthmatics | Lungs, cardiovascular, nervous system |
| **Animals** | Cattle, poultry, birds, fish | Lungs, gills, eggs, fur contamination |
| **Vegetation** | Crops, forests, ornamental plants | Stomata, photosynthesis, leaf necrosis |
| **Materials** | Buildings, monuments, metals | Corrosion, soiling, aesthetic damage |
| **Ecosystems** | Lakes, soils, biodiversity | Acidification, eutrophication |

For this KTU module, focus remains on **animals and human beings**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Routes of Pollutant Entry into the Body

Air pollutants enter animals and human beings via **three primary exposure routes**:

### Route 1 — Inhalation (Most Critical)
- Pollutants enter via the **respiratory system**.
- Gases (CO, SO$_2$, NO$_2$, O$_3$) are absorbed across the alveolar membrane.
- Particulates (PM$_{10}$, PM$_{2.5}$) deposit in different regions based on aerodynamic diameter.

### Route 2 — Ingestion (Secondary)
- Deposition of particulates on food crops → entry via the **gastrointestinal (GI) tract**.
- Examples: Lead (Pb) on leafy vegetables; pesticides and heavy metals.

### Route 3 — Dermal Absorption (Tertiary)
- Direct contact of skin with deposited dust or dissolved gases.
- Examples: Pesticides, certain VOCs in occupational settings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Inhalation Dominates:**
> The lungs have a surface area of approximately **70–90 m²** (a tennis court) and a very thin **alveolar–capillary membrane (≈ 0.5 µm)**. This enormous, thin interface makes the lungs the most efficient and vulnerable route of pollutant absorption.

## 2.2 Particulate Matter (PM) Deposition in the Respiratory System

The deposition fraction of inhaled particles depends strongly on the **aerodynamic diameter ($d_p$)**:

| Size Range | Name | Deposition Region | Health Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| $d_p > 10\ \mu m$ | Coarse PM | Nasopharynx (trapped by nasal hair) | Minimal systemic impact |
| $2.5 < d_p \leq 10\ \mu m$ | Thoracic PM$_{10}$ | Trachea, bronchi, bronchioles | Cough, bronchitis, asthma aggravation |
| $d_p \leq 2.5\ \mu m$ | Fine PM$_{2.5}$ | Alveolar sacs (deep lung) | Cardiovascular, lung cancer, mortality |
