| $d_p \leq 0.1\ \mu m$ | Ultrafine | Alveoli, translocates to blood | Systemic inflammation, oxidative stress |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Favourite:**
> Always remember — **"The smaller the particle, the deeper it goes, the more dangerous it is."**

## 2.3 Effects of Specific Pollutants on Human Beings

### 2.3.1 Sulphur Dioxide (SO$_2$)
- **Mechanism:** Highly water-soluble → dissolves in the moist lining of the upper respiratory tract → forms **H$_2$SO$_3$ / H$_2$SO$_4$**.
- **Effects:** Bronchoconstriction, aggravated asthma, chronic bronchitis, decreased lung function.
- **Sensitive groups:** Asthmatics, children.
- **Odour threshold:** ~0.5 ppm.

### 2.3.2 Nitrogen Dioxide (NO$_2$)
- **Mechanism:** Lower water solubility → penetrates deeper into the lungs → generates **free radicals** upon reaction with lung fluid.
- **Effects:** Pulmonary oedema, bronchitis, reduced immunity to infection, increased susceptibility to influenza.
- **Critical for:** Children in urban environments with traffic exposure.

### 2.3.3 Carbon Monoxide (CO)
- **Mechanism:** Binds to haemoglobin (Hb) with an affinity **~240× that of oxygen** → forms **carboxyhaemoglobin (COHb)**.
- **Effects:** Tissue hypoxia, headache, dizziness, cardiovascular stress, death at high doses.
- **Formula (Key KTU):**

$$
\text{COHb}\ (\%) = \frac{[\text{COHb}]}{[\text{Hb}]_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

A **COHb level of 5%** is the WHO guideline to prevent adverse cardiovascular effects in sensitive individuals.

### 2.3.4 Ozone (O$_3$)
- **Mechanism:** Powerful oxidant → reacts with unsaturated fatty acids in cell membranes → produces **aldehydes, hydrogen peroxide, free radicals**.
- **Effects:** Chest pain, coughing, throat irritation, inflammation, reduced lung function, aggravation of asthma.

### 2.3.5 Particulate Matter (PM$_{2.5}$ / PM$_{10}$)
- **Mechanism:** Carries adsorbed toxicants (PAHs, heavy metals) into alveoli → triggers **oxidative stress and systemic inflammation**.
- **Effects:** Lung cancer, COPD, ischaemic heart disease, stroke, premature mortality.
- **Dose-response:** **10 µg/m³ increase in PM$_{2.5}$ → ~6–8% increase in all-cause mortality** (Pope et al., 2015).

### 2.3.6 Lead (Pb)
- **Mechanism:** Substitutes for Ca²⁺ in neural pathways → disrupts **neurotransmitter release**.
- **Effects:** Cognitive impairment in children, reduced IQ, anaemia, kidney damage, hypertension.
- **Critical:** **No safe threshold** for lead exposure (WHO).

### 2.3.7 Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) — Benzene, Formaldehyde
- **Effects:** Benzene → leukaemia (Group 1 carcinogen, IARC). Formaldehyde → nasopharyngeal cancer, eye irritation.

## 2.4 Effects on Animals

Animals are exposed to air pollutants through **inhalation, ingestion of contaminated fodder, and dermal contact**. Notable effects include:

| Animal | Pollutant | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Cattle (Bos taurus)** | SO$_2$, F (fluoride) | Fluorosis, reduced milk yield, bone damage |
| **Poultry** | NH$_3$ (in farms) | Respiratory diseases, reduced egg production |
| **Birds** | DDT, organochlorines | Eggshell thinning, population decline (e.g., Bald Eagle, Peregrine Falcon) |
| **Fish (aquatic)** | Acid rain (low pH) | Gill damage, reproductive failure, mortality |
| **Dogs in cities** | PM$_{2.5}$, NO$_2$ | Higher incidence of lung and nasal cancer |
| **Bees & pollinators** | O$_3$, NO$_2$ | Reduced foraging, navigation impairment |
| **Livestock near smelters** | Pb, As, Cd | Bioaccumulation, kidney/liver damage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Animals as Bioindicators:**
> The health of animals (especially urban birds, dogs, and livestock near industries) often serves as an **early warning system** for human health risks because they share the same atmosphere. **Canaries in coal mines** is the historical example — toxic gases would affect the canary before the miners.

## 2.5 Vulnerable Population Groups

| Group | Reason for Vulnerability |
|---|---|
| **Children** | Higher breathing rate per kg body weight; lungs still developing |
| **Elderly** | Weakened immune and cardiovascular systems |
| **Pregnant women** | Foetal vulnerability to CO, Pb, PM |
| **Asthmatics / COPD patients** | Already-compromised airways |
| **Outdoor workers** | Prolonged exposure to high concentrations |
| **Low-socioeconomic groups** | Often live near industrial corridors or use biomass fuels |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet** — This table is essential for KTU Board exam numericals and derivations. **Memorize all entries.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Standard Value | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **COHb formation** | $\text{COHb}\% = \dfrac{[\text{COHb}]}{[\text{Hb}]_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | CO exposure assessment |
| 2 | **Coburn-Forster-Kane (CFK) Equation** | $\dfrac{d[\text{COHb}]}{dt} = \dfrac{V_{\text{CO}} \cdot [\text{Hb}]}{V_{b} \cdot M_{b}} - \dfrac{[\text{COHb}]}{t_{1/2}}$ | Complex | Predict COHb from continuous CO exposure |
| 3 | **Dose (inhalation)** | $D = C \cdot IR \cdot t$ | mg | Total pollutant mass absorbed |
| 4 | **PM mortality coefficient** | $\Delta M = \beta \cdot \Delta C_{\text{PM}_{2.5}}$ | % | Per 10 µg/m³ → 6–8% mortality increase |
| 5 | **AQI (Indian CPCB)** | $I_p = \dfrac{I_{\text{Hi}} - I_{\text{Lo}}}{B_{\text{Hi}} - B_{\text{Lo}}} \cdot (C_p - B_{\text{Lo}}) + I_{\text{Lo}}$ | Dimensionless | Sub-index calculation |
| 6 | **Ventilation rate (adult)** | $IR \approx 15\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$ | m³/day | Inhalation dose estimation |
| 7 | **Lung surface area** | $A_L \approx 70\text{–}90\ \text{m}^2$ | m² | Alveolar deposition context |
| 8 | **Daily inhaled air volume** | $\approx 11\,000\ \text{L/day}$ | L/day | Exposure load calculation |
| 9 | **CO affinity vs O$_2$** | $\text{Affinity ratio} \approx 240:1$ | — | COHb vs oxyHb binding |
| 10 | **WHO PM$_{2.5}$ guideline (annual)** | $C_{\text{PM}_{2.5}} \leq 5\ \mu\text{g/m}^3$ | µg/m³ | Health-protective benchmark |
| 11 | **WHO PM$_{2.5}$ guideline (24-h)** | $C_{\text{PM}_{2.5}} \leq 15\ \mu\text{g/m}^3$ | µg/m³ | Acute exposure limit |
| 12 | **WHO NO$_2$ guideline (annual)** | $C_{\text{NO}_2} \leq 10\ \mu\text{g/m}^3$ | µg/m³ | Health-protective benchmark |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown Rendering Tip for Tables:** When writing `|x|` (absolute value) in a KTU answer, always write `\vert x \vert` instead. The pipe `|` character breaks Markdown table cells. This is a common student mistake in answer sheets when transferring from LaTeX to Word.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The knowledge of pollutant effects on animals and humans directly drives:

1. **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** — CPCB (India) and EPA (US) standards are derived from dose-response epidemiological data.
2. **Buffer Zone Design** — Industries and landfills are mandated to maintain green belts (typically 50–500 m) as receptor protection.
3. **Air Quality Index (AQI)** — Translates pollutant concentrations into health advisories for the public.
4. **Risk Assessment & EIA** — Environmental Impact Assessment uses receptor vulnerability to set emission limits.
5. **Ventilation & Indoor Air Quality Engineering** — ASHRAE standards for HVAC systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Derive These?**
> KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) emphasises **analytical understanding** over rote memorization. Board examiners (12–14 mark questions) frequently test derivations and numerical applications. Below are the **three most exam-relevant derivations** for this topic.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: The Coburn-Forster-Kane (CFK) Equation for COHb

The CFK equation is the **biologically validated model** used to predict the percentage of **carboxyhaemoglobin (COHb)** in blood as a function of inhaled CO concentration, exposure duration, and physiological parameters.

### Governing Differential Equation

$$
\frac{d[\text{COHb}]}{dt} = \frac{V_{\text{CO}} \cdot [\text{Hb}]}{V_{b} \cdot M_{b}} - \frac{[\text{COHb}]}{\tau_{b}}
$$

Where:

- $V_{\text{CO}}$ = Rate of CO uptake (mL/min)
- $[\text{Hb}]$ = Haemoglobin binding capacity for CO (mL CO per mL blood)
- $V_b$ = Blood volume (mL)
- $M_b$ = Body mass (kg)
- $\tau_b$ = Mean residence time of CO in blood (min)
- $[\text{COHb}]$ = Fraction of COHb in blood (decimal, not %)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the rate processes:**
The rate of change of COHb in blood is governed by **two competing processes**:
- (i) **Inflow of CO** into the blood via alveolar diffusion (source term).
- (ii) **Outflow / elimination** of CO via respiration and metabolic clearance (sink term).

**Step 2 — Define the source term (CO uptake):**
The CO uptake rate depends on alveolar ventilation, blood volume, and haemoglobin concentration:

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{CO}} = \frac{\text{Alveolar CO absorbed}}{\text{time}}
$$

This is proportional to the inhaled concentration, ventilation rate, and the CO-Hb binding constant.

**Step 3 — Define the sink term (elimination):**
CO is eliminated from the body predominantly through exhalation. Assuming first-order elimination kinetics:

$$
\text{Elimination rate} = \frac{[\text{COHb}]}{\tau_b}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine into the mass balance:**

$$
\frac{d[\text{COHb}]}{dt} = (\text{Uptake}) - (\text{Elimination})
$$

$$
\frac{d[\text{COHb}]}{dt} = \frac{V_{\text{CO}} \cdot [\text{Hb}]}{V_{b} \cdot M_{b}} - \frac{[\text{COHb}]}{\tau_{b}}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply steady-state condition (long exposure):**
At steady state, $\dfrac{d[\text{COHb}]}{dt} = 0$:

$$
\frac{V_{\text{CO}} \cdot [\text{Hb}]}{V_{b} \cdot M_{b}} = \frac{[\text{COHb}]_{ss}}{\tau_{b}}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for steady-state COHb:**

$$
[\text{COHb}]_{ss} = \frac{V_{\text{CO}} \cdot [\text{Hb}] \cdot \tau_{b}}{V_{b} \cdot M_{b}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The CFK equation is used by occupational hygienists to set **permissible exposure limits (PELs)** for CO. For example, to keep COHb below **5%** in workers, the maximum 8-h time-weighted-average CO concentration is set at **50 ppm** (OSHA, USA).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Air Quality Index (AQI) — Indian CPCB Method

The AQI is a piecewise linear interpolation function that converts a measured pollutant concentration into a single health-relevant number (0–500).

### Linear Interpolation Formula

$$
I_p = \frac{I_{\text{Hi}} - I_{\text{Lo}}}{B_{\text{Hi}} - B_{\text{Lo}}} \cdot (C_p - B_{\text{Lo}}) + I_{\text{Lo}}
$$

Where:

- $I_p$ = Sub-index of pollutant $p$
- $C_p$ = Measured concentration of pollutant $p$
- $B_{\text{Hi}}, B_{\text{Lo}}$ = Concentration breakpoints (higher and lower)
- $I_{\text{Hi}}, I_{\text{Lo}}$ = AQI values corresponding to those breakpoints

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define health-effect categories:**
The AQI scale (0–500) is divided into 8 health categories:

| AQI Range | Health Concern | Colour Code |
|---|---|---|
| 0–50 | Good | Green |
| 51–100 | Satisfactory | Yellow-green |
| 101–200 | Moderate | Yellow |
| 201–300 | Poor | Orange |
| 301–400 | Very Poor | Red |
| 401–500 | Severe | Maroon |

**Step 2 — Establish concentration breakpoints:**
Each pollutant has a set of $(B_{\text{Lo}}, B_{\text{Hi}}, I_{\text{Lo}}, I_{\text{Hi}})$ values defined by CPCB India. Example for **PM$_{2.5}$ (24-h average)**:

| $B_{\text{Lo}}$ | $B_{\text{Hi}}$ | $I_{\text{Lo}}$ | $I_{\text{Hi}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 30 | 0 | 50 |
| 31 | 60 | 51 | 100 |
| 61 | 90 | 101 | 200 |
| 91 | 120 | 201 | 300 |
| 121 | 250 | 301 | 400 |
| 251 | 380 | 401 | 500 |

**Step 3 — Apply piecewise linear interpolation:**
Within each bracket, the AQI sub-index is assumed to vary **linearly** with concentration.

**Step 4 — Determine the dominant pollutant:**
The **overall AQI = max$(I_p)$** for all measured pollutants $p$.

**Step 5 — Work a sample numerical:**

> **Sample Problem (KTU 2024 expected type):**
> A monitoring station reports the following 24-h concentrations: PM$_{2.5}$ = 78 µg/m³, PM$_{10}$ = 142 µg/m³, SO$_2$ = 35 µg/m³, NO$_2$ = 48 µg/m³. Compute the AQI and identify the dominant pollutant.

**Solution using the formula:**

For PM$_{2.5}$ = 78 µg/m³ (falls in bracket 61–90, AQI 101–200):

$$
I_{\text{PM}_{2.5}} = \frac{200 - 101}{90 - 61} \cdot (78 - 61) + 101
$$

$$
I_{\text{PM}_{2.5}} = \frac{99}{29} \cdot 17 + 101 = 3.414 \cdot 17 + 101
$$

$$
I_{\text{PM}_{2.5}} = 58.04 + 101 = \boxed{159.04 \approx 159}
$$

The same procedure is applied to each pollutant. The **maximum sub-index** is reported as the overall AQI, and the corresponding pollutant is declared the **dominant pollutant**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Inhalation Dose Calculation

### Governing Equation

$$
D_{\text{inh}} = C_a \cdot \text{IR} \cdot t \cdot f_r
$$

Where:

- $D_{\text{inh}}$ = Inhaled dose (mg)
- $C_a$ = Pollutant concentration in air (mg/m³)
- $\text{IR}$ = Inhalation rate (m³/h)
- $t$ = Exposure duration (h)
- $f_r$ = Retained fraction in the lung (dimensionless, 0–1)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Volume of air inhaled:**

$$
V_{\text{inhaled}} = \text{IR} \cdot t \quad [\text{m}^3]
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of pollutant in that volume:**

$$
m_{\text{inhaled}} = C_a \cdot V_{\text{inhaled}} = C_a \cdot \text{IR} \cdot t \quad [\text{mg}]
$$

**Step 3 — Retained fraction:**
Not all inhaled mass is retained. Some is exhaled. Multiply by $f_r$:

$$
D_{\text{inh}} = f_r \cdot C_a \cdot \text{IR} \cdot t
$$

**Step 4 — Example numerical:**

> A worker is exposed to benzene vapour at $C_a = 3.2\ \text{mg/m}^3$ for $t = 6\ \text{h}$ per day. The inhalation rate is $\text{IR} = 1.2\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$ and the lung retention fraction is $f_r = 0.6$. Calculate the daily inhaled dose.

**Solution:**

$$
D_{\text{inh}} = 0.6 \times 3.2 \times 1.2 \times 6
$$

$$
D_{\text{inh}} = 0.6 \times 3.2 \times 7.2 = 0.6 \times 23.04
$$

$$
D_{\text{inh}} = \boxed{13.82\ \text{mg/day}}
$$

This dose can then be compared with the **Reference Dose (RfD)** of benzene for cancer risk assessment.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Source-Pathway-Receptor (S-P-R) Model

This is the **fundamental conceptual model** of environmental engineering. The KTU examiner expects students to draw and label this diagram for a 7-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SOURCE]:::sourceNode --> B[PATHWAY]:::pathwayNode
    B --> C[RECEPTOR]:::receptorNode
    B --> D[ATMOSPHERIC DISPERSION]:::pathwayNode
    D --> C
    C --> E[HEALTH IMPACT]:::impactNode
    C --> F[ECOSYSTEM IMPACT]:::impactNode
    C --> G[MATERIAL DAMAGE]:::impactNode
    A1[Vehicle Exhaust]:::sourceNode --> A
    A2[Industrial Stack]:::sourceNode --> A
    A3[Domestic Combustion]:::sourceNode --> A
    A4[Power Plant]:::sourceNode --> A

    classDef sourceNode fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef pathwayNode fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    classDef receptorNode fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    classDef impactNode fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#00008B,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Key features of the diagram:**
- **Sources** (red) include vehicles, industries, domestic fuels, and power plants.
- **Pathway** (yellow) is the atmospheric transport — wind, dispersion, chemical transformation.
- **Receptors** (green) are human beings, animals, vegetation, and materials.
- **Impacts** (blue) cascade from receptor exposure.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark Scheme Tip:** Drawing the S-P-R triangle and labelling with at least **3 examples for each node** typically earns full marks (7/7). Be sure to show the **direction of flow** with arrows.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Routes of Pollutant Entry into the Human Body

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AIR[AIRBORNE POLLUTANT]:::pollutantNode

    AIR --> R1[INHALATION]:::routeNode
    AIR --> R2[INGESTION]:::routeNode
    AIR --> R3[DERMAL CONTACT]:::routeNode

    R1 --> O1[Nasopharynx]:::organNode
    R1 --> O2[Trachea and Bronchi]:::organNode
    R1 --> O3[Alveolar Sacs]:::organNode
    O3 --> O4[Bloodstream]:::organNode

    R2 --> O5[Gastrointestinal Tract]:::organNode
    O5 --> O4

    R3 --> O6[Skin]:::organNode
    O6 --> O4

    O4 --> O7[SYSTEMIC TOXICITY]:::impactNode
    O4 --> O8[ORGAN DAMAGE]:::impactNode
    O4 --> O9[CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE]:::impactNode
    O4 --> O10[NEUROLOGICAL EFFECTS]:::impactNode

    classDef pollutantNode fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef routeNode fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    classDef organNode fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    classDef impactNode fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#00008B,color:#FFFFFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Note:** The three-route entry diagram is a high-yield visual. Mark the **alveolar region** as the deepest and most critical site for PM deposition.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture: Health Risk Assessment Workflow

This block diagram shows how pollutant concentration data flows through the receptor assessment pipeline, which is essential for **Risk Assessment, EIA, and AQI calculation** — all high-yield KTU topics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[STEP 1 - EMISSION INVENTORY]:::stepBlock
    step2[STEP 2 - AMBIENT MONITORING]:::stepBlock
    step3[STEP 3 - EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT]:::stepBlock
    step4[STEP 4 - DOSE-RESPONSE EVALUATION]:::stepBlock
    step5[STEP 5 - RISK CHARACTERIZATION]:::stepBlock
    step6[STEP 6 - REGULATORY ACTION]:::stepBlock

    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6

    step6 --> outA[Air Quality Standards]:::outBlock
    step6 --> outB[Emission Limits]:::outBlock
    step6 --> outC[Buffer Zones]:::outBlock
    step6 --> outD[Public Advisories]:::outBlock

    classDef stepBlock fill:#A0E7E5,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000000
    classDef outBlock fill:#FFAEBC,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
```

**Description of each block:**

| Block | Function | Engineering Output |
|---|---|---|
| Emission Inventory | Quantify source strength | Pollutant emission rates (g/s) |
| Ambient Monitoring | Measure outdoor concentration | Hourly/daily average concentrations |
| Exposure Assessment | Estimate human/animal exposure | Inhaled dose (mg/day) |
| Dose-Response | Apply epidemiological data | Unit risk factor |
| Risk Characterization | Integrate exposure and dose-response | Lifetime cancer risk (ILCR) |
| Regulatory Action | Translate risk to policy | Standards, advisories, control mandates |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** A common 14-mark question is: *"Explain the receptor-oriented approach to air quality management with a block diagram."* The above block diagram is **exactly the type expected**.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — PM Deposition by Size in Respiratory System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PM[INHALED PARTICLE]:::particleNode

    PM --> P1[Greater than 10 micrometer]:::sizeNode
    PM --> P2[2.5 to 10 micrometer PM10]:::sizeNode
    PM --> P3[Less than 2.5 micrometer PM2.5]:::sizeNode
    PM --> P4[Less than 0.1 micrometer ULTRAFINE]:::sizeNode

    P1 --> D1[DEPOSITED IN NASOPHARYNX]:::depositionNode
    P2 --> D2[DEPOSITED IN TRACHEA AND BRONCHI]:::depositionNode
    P3 --> D3[DEPOSITED IN ALVEOLI]:::depositionNode
    P4 --> D4[TRANSLOCATED TO BLOODSTREAM]:::depositionNode

    D1 --> H1[Cough Sneezing]:::healthNode
    D2 --> H2[Asthma Bronchitis]:::healthNode
    D3 --> H3[Lung Cancer COPD Mortality]:::healthNode
    D4 --> H4[Systemic Inflammation Cardiovascular Disease]:::healthNode

    classDef particleNode fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef sizeNode fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    classDef depositionNode fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    classDef healthNode fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#00008B,color:#FFFFFF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic (KTU Boards):**
> **"N = N (Nasal), T = T (Tracheal), A = A (Alveolar), B = B (Blood) — Big to Big damages, Small to Big kills."**
> Use this in your 7-mark answers to show the examiner that you understand the **size–depth–severity** relationship.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (short answer, definition-type)
> - Part B: 1 question × 14 marks (with internal choice, two sub-parts of 7 marks each)
> - Total Continuous Assessment (ESE Module-wise) for this topic: **20 marks**
> - Cognitive levels tested: L1 (Remember), L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply)

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Define a receptor in air pollution. Give two examples.

**[Model Answer — 3 marks]**

A **receptor** is the entity (human, animal, plant, material, or ecosystem) that receives and responds to airborne pollutants after they are transported from the source. It is the third component of the **Source–Pathway–Receptor (S-P-R) model**.

**Examples:**
1. **Human beings** — the most critical receptor; inhale ~11,000 L/day of air and absorb pollutants through the alveolar surface (~70–90 m²).
2. **Animals** (cattle, poultry, birds) — exposed via inhalation, ingestion of contaminated fodder, and dermal contact with deposited dust.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners expect the **S-P-R context** for full marks. Simply writing "human being" gets 1 mark; the definition + 2 examples + S-P-R context gets full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 — What is carboxyhaemoglobin (COHb)? Why is CO more toxic than O$_2$?

**[Model Answer — 3 marks]**

**Carboxyhaemoglobin (COHb)** is the stable complex formed when carbon monoxide binds with the haemoglobin (Hb) molecule in red blood cells, replacing oxygen.

$$
\text{Hb} + \text{CO} \rightleftharpoons \text{Hb}\cdot\text{CO}\ (\text{COHb})
$$

**CO is more toxic than O$_2$** because:
1. The **affinity of Hb for CO is ~240 times greater** than for O$_2$, so even low CO concentrations displace large amounts of oxygen.
2. The COHb complex is **highly stable** (slow dissociation), causing prolonged tissue hypoxia.
3. CO also binds to **myoglobin** and **cytochrome oxidase**, impairing cellular respiration.

A COHb level of **>40%** causes unconsciousness; **>60%** is often fatal.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **INSTRUCTION (KTU 2024):** *Answer any ONE full question. Each question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks.*

---

### Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Source–Pathway–Receptor (S-P-R) model** with a neat sketch. Discuss in detail the **three routes** by which air pollutants enter the human body. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **deposition of particulate matter (PM) of different sizes** in the human respiratory system. Discuss the **health effects of PM$_{2.5}$** on human beings. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define the S-P-R Model (2 marks):**
The S-P-R model is a conceptual framework used in environmental engineering to describe the movement of pollutants from emission to impact. It has three components:
- **Source** — the origin of the pollutant (combustion, industries, vehicles).
- **Pathway** — the atmospheric transport, dispersion, transformation, and deposition.
- **Receptor** — the entity (human, animal, plant, material) that absorbs and responds to the pollutant.

**Step 2 — Draw the S-P-R diagram (2 marks):**
A triangular flow diagram with arrows from Source → Pathway → Receptor, plus an example for each node. *(See Section 4.1.)*

**Step 3 — Describe the three routes (3 marks):**
1. **Inhalation** — primary route; gases and particulates enter the respiratory system. The alveolar surface (~70–90 m²) absorbs gases like CO, SO$_2$, NO$_2$, and O$_3$ into the bloodstream.
2. **Ingestion** — particulates deposit on food crops and water; pollutants (Pb, pesticides) enter the GI tract. ~5–15% of total dose for most non-occupational exposures.
3. **Dermal Contact** — direct skin absorption of certain gases (e.g., VOCs, pesticides) and dust. More significant in occupational settings.

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Draw the respiratory deposition diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to the PM deposition diagram in Section 4.4. Label all four size classes and the corresponding deposition regions.

**Step 2 — Tabulate deposition vs size (2 marks):**
- $d_p > 10\ \mu m$ → Nasopharynx (filtered by nasal hair)
- $2.5 < d_p \leq 10\ \mu m$ → Trachea, bronchi (PM$_{10}$)
- $d_p \leq 2.5\ \mu m$ → Alveoli (PM$_{2.5}$)
- $d_p \leq 0.1\ \mu m$ → Translocates to bloodstream (ultrafine)

**Step 3 — Discuss PM$_{2.5}$ health effects (2 marks):**
PM$_{2.5}$ is the most dangerous size class. Effects:
- **Respiratory:** Asthma exacerbation, chronic bronchitis, COPD, lung cancer.
- **Cardiovascular:** Ischaemic heart disease, stroke, arrhythmias.
- **Systemic:** Oxidative stress, inflammation, insulin resistance.
- **Mortality:** Every 10 µg/m³ increase in long-term PM$_{2.5}$ → ~6–8% increase in all-cause mortality (epidemiological evidence).

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **adverse effects of SO$_2$, NO$_2$, CO, and Ozone (O$_3$)** on human health. For each pollutant, mention the pollutant's mechanism of action and at least two health effects. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is an **Air Quality Index (AQI)**? Explain the **CPCB AQI calculation** with a sample numerical. The following 24-h average concentrations are reported: PM$_{2.5}$ = 78 µg/m³, PM$_{10}$ = 142 µg/m³, NO$_2$ = 48 µg/m³, SO$_2$ = 35 µg/m³. Compute the AQI and identify the **dominant pollutant**. Use the standard CPCB India breakpoints. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

| Pollutant | Mechanism of Action | Health Effects (any two) |
|---|---|---|
| **SO$_2$** | Water-soluble; dissolves in lung fluid to form H$_2$SO$_3$ / H$_2$SO$_4$ | (i) Bronchoconstriction (ii) Aggravated asthma, bronchitis |
| **NO$_2$** | Lower solubility; penetrates to alveoli; generates free radicals | (i) Pulmonary oedema (ii) Reduced immunity to infection |
| **CO** | Binds to Hb; affinity 240× O$_2$ → forms COHb → tissue hypoxia | (i) Headache, dizziness (ii) Cardiovascular stress; death at high dose |
| **O$_3$** | Powerful oxidant; attacks unsaturated fatty acids in cell membranes | (i) Chest pain, cough (ii) Reduced lung function; asthma aggravation |

**Allocation:** 1.75 marks per pollutant (mechanism + 2 effects).

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition of AQI (1.5 marks):**
The AQI is a single numerical scale (0–500) that summarises the cumulative health impact of multiple air pollutants in ambient air. It enables easy communication of air quality status to the public.

**Step 2 — AQI Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$
I_p = \frac{I_{\text{Hi}} - I_{\text{Lo}}}{B_{\text{Hi}} - B_{\text{Lo}}} \cdot (C_p - B_{\text{Lo}}) + I_{\text{Lo}}
$$

The **overall AQI = maximum** of all sub-indices $I_p$, and the corresponding pollutant is the **dominant pollutant**.

**Step 3 — Sample calculation for PM$_{2.5}$ = 78 µg/m³ (2 marks):**
This value lies in the breakpoint range **(61–90)** with AQI range **(101–200)**.

$$
I_{\text{PM}_{2.5}} = \frac{200 - 101}{90 - 61} \cdot (78 - 61) + 101
$$

$$
I_{\text{PM}_{2.5}} = \frac{99}{29} \cdot 17 + 101 = 3.414 \cdot 17 + 101
$$

$$
I_{\text{PM}_{2.5}} = 58.04 + 101 \approx 159
$$

**Step 4 — Show sub-indices for all pollutants and identify dominant (2 marks):**
Performing the same calculation for each pollutant (using standard CPCB breakpoints), the sub-indices are approximately:
- $I_{\text{PM}_{2.5}} \approx 159$
- $I_{\text{PM}_{10}} \approx 92$
- $I_{\text{NO}_2} \approx 67$
- $I_{\text{SO}_2} \approx 58$

$$
\boxed{\text{AQI} = \max(159, 92, 67, 58) = 159}
$$

**Dominant pollutant = PM$_{2.5}$** with health concern "**Moderate**" (yellow zone, AQI 101–200).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks (Common Pitfalls)**
>
> 1. **Confusing source with receptor** (–1 mark): A common mistake is naming a chimney as a "receptor". The receptor is the entity *exposed to* the pollutant, not the one emitting it.
>
> 2. **Missing the S-P-R context in definitions** (–1 mark): Always link the definition of receptor to the S-P-R framework. A bare definition is incomplete.
>
> 3. **Writing "CO binds to Hb 240 times more" without units** (–0.5 mark): Specify it is the **affinity ratio** (240:1), or mention the rate constant.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to convert COHb to percentage** (–0.5 mark): The CFK equation gives COHb as a fraction (0–1). Always multiply by 100 to express as %.
>
> 5. **AQI numerical — wrong breakpoint selection** (–2 marks): Before plugging into the formula, **always identify the correct (B_Lo, B_Hi, I_Lo, I_Hi) bracket**. This is the most common error.
>
> 6. **Not labelling the diagram in long-answer questions** (–1 to –2 marks): A diagram without labels earns partial credit. Label **all nodes, arrows, and units**.
>
> 7. **Ignoring animal effects** (–1 mark): The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mentions "Animals and Human Beings". A 7-mark question on receptors that discusses only humans loses marks. Always include at least one paragraph on **animal impacts** (e.g., fluorosis in cattle, eggshell thinning in birds).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Effects on Animals and Human Beings**
>
> ✅ **Receptor** = Entity exposed to pollutant (human, animal, plant, material). Third node of **S-P-R model**.
>
> ✅ **Daily inhaled air** ≈ 11,000 L/day; **lung surface area** ≈ 70–90 m²; **alveolar membrane thickness** ≈ 0.5 µm.
>
> ✅ **PM size → Depth of penetration:**
> > 10 µm → Nose | 2.5–10 µm → Bronchi | < 2.5 µm → Alveoli | < 0.1 µm → Bloodstream.
>
> ✅ **CO toxicity** is due to **240× greater affinity** for Hb vs O$_2$, forming **carboxyhaemoglobin (COHb)**.
>
> ✅ **SO$_2$** is water-soluble → upper airways; **NO$_2$** is less soluble → deep lung.
>
> ✅ **O$_3$** is a powerful oxidant attacking cell membrane fatty acids.
>
> ✅ **Pb** has no safe threshold; affects neurodevelopment in children.
>
> ✅ **CFK Equation** = $\dfrac{d[\text{COHb}]}{dt} = \dfrac{V_{\text{CO}} \cdot [\text{Hb}]}{V_b \cdot M_b} - \dfrac{[\text{COHb}]}{\tau_b}$ — gives steady-state COHb %.
>
> ✅ **AQI Formula** = $I_p = \dfrac{I_{\text{Hi}} - I_{\text{Lo}}}{B_{\text{Hi}} - B_{\text{Lo}}} \cdot (C_p - B_{\text{Lo}}) + I_{\text{Lo}}$ — overall AQI = max of all sub-indices.
>
> ✅ **Inhalation Dose** = $D_{\text{inh}} = C_a \cdot \text{IR} \cdot t \cdot f_r$ — used in risk assessment.
>
> ✅ **Vulnerable groups** = Children, elderly, pregnant women, asthmatics, outdoor workers.
>
> ✅ **Animals as bioindicators:** Cattle (fluorosis), birds (eggshell thinning), fish (acid rain), bees (O$_3$ foraging loss).
>
> ✅ **WHO 2022 fact:** 99% of global population breathes air above WHO guideline; 6.7 million premature deaths/year attributed to air pollution.
>
> ✅ **Mnemonics:**
> - PM depth: **"N-T-A-B"** = Nose-Trachea-Alveoli-Blood.
> - CO toxicity: **"240 is the lethal ratio."**
> - Pollutant solubility: **"SO$_2$ stays shallow, NO$_2$ dives deep."**
>
> ✅ **Engineering applications:** NAAQS setting, EIA, AQI dissemination, green buffer design, occupational exposure limits, indoor air quality (HVAC/ASHRAE).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties - Tolerance Levels - Carbon Monoxide
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Properties - Tolerance Levels - Carbon Monoxide ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** is a colorless, odorless, and tasteless toxic gaseous air pollutant, with the chemical formula **CO** and a molecular weight of **28.01 g/mol**. It is produced primarily as a result of the **incomplete combustion of carbonaceous fuels** (coal, gasoline, natural gas, biomass) under oxygen-deficient conditions. According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, Carbon Monoxide is classified as a **"Criteria Pollutant"** under the National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS), and is uniquely characterized as a **chemical asphyxiant** because it does not irritate the respiratory system but instead disrupts oxygen transport at the cellular level. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Silent Cabin Heater"** > > Imagine you are sitting in a closed garage with a car engine idling. You cannot *see*, *smell*, or *taste* the deadly gas being produced. Hemoglobin, the red protein in your blood that carries oxygen, is like a fleet of taxi-cabs waiting at an airport (your lungs) to pick up passengers (oxygen molecules, O₂). Carbon Monoxide is a much more aggressive, pushy traveler that **hijacks these taxis** and refuses to get out. The taxis (hemoglobin) are now occupied and cannot transport oxygen to your organs — even though the lungs are full of air, your body effectively suffocates from within. This silent, deceptive nature is precisely why Carbon Monoxide is often called the **"Silent Killer"** in air pollution literature. ### 1.3 Core Physical & Chemical Properties (Key Highlights) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU board exams, the following physical and chemical properties of CO are *high-yield* and frequently asked. | Property | Value / Description | | :--- | :--- | | Chemical Formula | **CO** | | Molecular Weight | **28.01 g/mol** | | Appearance | **Colorless gas** | | Odor | **Odorless** | | Taste | **Tasteless** | | Density (at 25°C, 1 atm) | **1.145 kg/m³** | | Specific Gravity (relative to air) | **0.967** (slightly lighter than air) | | Boiling Point | **-191.5 °C (81.65 K)** | | Melting Point | **-205 °C (68.15 K)** | | Solubility in Water (at 25°C) | **28 mg/L** (slightly soluble) | | Flammability Range | **12.5% – 74% in air** (highly flammable) | | Bond Type | **Triple bond (C≡O)** — strongest known chemical bond | | Bond Dissociation Energy | **1077 kJ/mol** (highest of any diatomic molecule) | ### 1.4 Key Industrial / Environmental Significance > [!NOTE] > - **Major Source:** Vehicular exhaust (≈ 75% of urban CO emissions), industrial furnaces, power plants, wildfires, cigarette smoke, and gas heaters. > - **Reactivity:** CO is thermodynamically unstable but kinetically stable — it reacts sluggishly at room temperature. > - **Environmental Role:** CO is a precursor to **ground-level ozone (O₃)** through photochemical reactions with NOₓ and hydrocarbons, and contributes to the **greenhouse effect** with a Global Warming Potential (GWP) of **1.9** (100-year horizon). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mechanism of Toxicity — The Biochemistry of CO Poisoning Carbon Monoxide exerts its toxic effect through the following structured mechanism: - **Step 1 — Inhalation:** CO enters the alveoli of the lungs and diffuses across the alveolar-capillary membrane into the bloodstream. - **Step 2 — Competitive Binding:** CO competes with O₂ for binding sites on the **heme group** of hemoglobin (Hb). However, CO binds with hemoglobin to form **carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)** with an affinity approximately **200–250 times greater** than that of oxygen. - **Step 3 — Reduced O₂ Carrying Capacity:** The formation of COHb reduces the number of available hemoglobin sites for O₂ transport, leading to **functional anemia** at the tissue level. - **Step 4 — Left-Shift of Oxyhemoglobin Dissociation Curve:** CO also causes the remaining oxyhemoglobin to hold onto oxygen more tightly, preventing O₂ release at the tissue level (a phenomenon called the **Bohr Effect suppression**). - **Step 5 — Cellular Asphyxiation:** Tissues and organs (especially the brain and heart) become oxygen-starved, leading to symptoms ranging from headaches to death. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following formulas are **exam-essential** for tolerance/limit calculations in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). | Formula / Expression | Description | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $$ \text{Conversion: } 1\ \text{ppm} = \frac{MW}{24.45}\ \text{mg/m}^3 $$ | Universal ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversion (at 25 °C, 1 atm) | mg/m³ | | $$ \text{CO Mass: } m = C_{ppm} \times \frac{28.01}{24.45} \times V $$ | Mass of CO in volume V at concentration C | mg | | $$ \text{COHb \%} = \frac{[\text{COHb}]}{[\text{Hb}]_{total}} \times 100 $$ | Percentage of carboxyhemoglobin in blood | % | | $$ \text{Coburn Equation: } \frac{d[\text{COHb}]}{dt} = \frac{V_{CO} - V_{CO,\text{out}}}{V_b \cdot [\text{Hb}]} $$ | Predicts COHb uptake in blood over time | %/min | | $$ \text{Volume of gas: } V = nRT/P $$ | Ideal gas law (used in dilution & chamber calculations) | m³ | | $$ \text{Emission Rate: } E = C \times Q $$ | Mass emission = concentration × volumetric flow rate | mg/s | | $$ \text{Ratio } \frac{\text{CO}}{\text{NO}_x} = \frac{[\text{CO}]}{[\text{NO}_x]} $$ | Used in combustion diagnostics (air-fuel ratio) | — | | $$ \text{Air-Fuel Equivalence: } \phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}} $$ | Determines combustion completeness (φ > 1 = rich → CO) | — | > **CRITICAL FORMULA NOTE:** In the table above, the ppm-to-mass conversion is given as $C_{ppm} \times \frac{MW}{24.45}$ where **24.45** is the molar volume of an ideal gas at **25 °C and 1 atm** in liters (24.45 L/mol). For CO (MW = 28.01): $$ 1\ \text{ppm CO} = \frac{28.01}{24.45} \approx 1.145\ \text{mg/m}^3 $$ ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Traffic Engineers:** Use CO concentration models (like the **Caline-3, CAL3QHC, AERMOD**) to design roadside barriers and tunnels. > - **Industrial Hygienists:** Calculate permissible exposure durations to ensure worker safety (OSHA compliance). > - **Environmental Engineers:** Design catalytic converters where CO + ½O₂ → CO₂ (oxidation reaction removes > 95% of CO from exhaust). > - **Combustion Engineers:** Use CO/CO₂ ratio as a primary indicator of **combustion efficiency**. ### 2.4 Sources and Sinks of Atmospheric CO | Category | Major Sources | Major Sinks | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Anthropogenic** | Motor vehicles, industrial processes, residential heating, biomass burning, cigarette smoke | Catalytic oxidation, soil microbial uptake | | **Natural** | Volcanic eruptions, forest fires, methane oxidation by hydroxyl radicals (·OH) | Tropospheric OH· radical oxidation → CO₂ | | **Atmospheric Lifetime** | — | **~ 1–4 months** (residence time) | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: ppm to mg/m³ Conversion for CO The conversion between volumetric mixing ratio (ppm) and mass concentration (mg/m³) is fundamental for KTU problems. > **Starting Principle:** **1 ppm** means **1 part of pollutant per million parts of air by volume**. **Step 1:** Express 1 ppm as a molar fraction. $$ 1\ \text{ppm} = \frac{1\ \text{mole of CO}}{1{,}000{,}000\ \text{moles of air}} $$ **Step 2:** Assume ideal gas behavior. At 25 °C and 1 atm, the molar volume is: $$ V_m = 24.45\ \text{L/mol} = 0.02445\ \text{m}^3/\text{mol} $$ This is derived from the ideal gas equation $V_m = \frac{RT}{P}$, where: - $R = 0.08206\ \text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$ - $T = 298.15\ \text{K}$ - $P = 1\ \text{atm}$ **Step 3:** The volume of CO in 1 m³ of air at 1 ppm is: $$ V_{CO} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^3\ \text{per m}^3\ \text{of air} $$ **Step 4:** Convert this volume to moles using the molar volume: $$ n_{CO} = \frac{V_{CO}}{V_m} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^3}{0.02445\ \text{m}^3/\text{mol}} = 4.09 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{mol} $$ **Step 5:** Convert moles of CO to mass using molecular weight (MW = 28.01 g/mol): $$ m_{CO} = n_{CO} \times MW = 4.09 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{mol} \times 28.01\ \text{g/mol} = 1.145 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{g} = 1.145\ \text{mg} $$ **Final Generalized Formula:** $$ C\ (\text{mg/m}^3) = C\ (\text{ppm}) \times \frac{MW_{pollutant}}{24.45} $$ > **For CO specifically:** $$ 1\ \text{ppm CO} = \frac{28.01}{24.45} = 1.145\ \text{mg/m}^3 $$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style > **[Problem — KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > The CO concentration in a busy traffic intersection in Kochi is measured as **35 ppm** at 25 °C and 1 atm. Convert this concentration to (a) mg/m³ and (b) µg/m³. **Solution:** **(a) ppm to mg/m³ conversion:** $$ C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \frac{MW_{CO}}{24.45} $$ $$ C_{mg/m^3} = 35 \times \frac{28.01}{24.45} $$ $$ C_{mg/m^3} = 35 \times 1.1455 $$ $$ \boxed{C_{mg/m^3} = 40.09\ \text{mg/m}^3} $$ **(b) mg/m³ to µg/m³ conversion:** $$ C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{mg/m^3} \times 1000 $$ $$ \boxed{C_{\mu g/m^3} = 40{,}092\ \mu g/m^3} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — CO Tolerance Levels (Worldwide Standards) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Data — Memorize the bold values for Part A questions.** | Standard / Authority | Limit | Duration / Type | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **OSHA PEL (USA)** | **50 ppm** (55 mg/m³) | 8-hour TWA (Time-Weighted Average) | | **NIOSH REL (USA)** | **35 ppm** (40 mg/m³) | 10-hour TWA | | **ACGIH TLV (USA)** | **25 ppm** (29 mg/m³) | 8-hour TWA | | **WHO Air Quality Guideline** | **10 mg/m³ (≈ 8.7 ppm)** | 8-hour mean | | **WHO Indoor Air Quality** | **7 mg/m³ (≈ 6.1 ppm)** | 24-hour mean | | **NAAQS – CPCB (India)** | **4 mg/m³ (≈ 3.5 ppm)** | 8-hour average | | **NAAQS – CPCB (India)** | **10 mg/m³ (≈ 8.7 ppm)** | 1-hour average | | **EPA NAAQS (USA)** | **9 ppm (10 mg/m³)** | 8-hour average | | **EPA NAAQS (USA)** | **35 ppm (40 mg/m³)** | 1-hour average | | **IDLH (Immediate Danger)** | **1200 ppm** (1375 mg/m³) | 30-minute limit | | **LC₅₀ (lethal, 50%)** | **5000 ppm** (rats, 4-hour exposure) | — | ### 3.4 Health Effects Matrix — Symptom Severity vs. COHb% > **KTU Examiner Tip:** The COHb percentage (carboxyhemoglobin saturation) is the gold-standard clinical indicator of CO poisoning severity. | COHb % in Blood | Symptoms / Clinical Effects | | :--- | :--- | | **0 – 5 %** | Normal background level (non-smokers); no symptoms. | | **5 – 10 %** | Slight headache, mild dyspnea on exertion. | | **10 – 20 %** | Headache, throbbing temples, irritability, fatigue. | | **20 – 30 %** | Severe headache, weakness, nausea, vision disturbance. | | **30 – 40 %** | Severe headache, confusion, syncope (fainting), vomiting. | | **40 – 50 %** | Convulsions, coma, possible cardiovascular collapse. | | **> 50 %** | Coma, **death** (lethal threshold). | | **> 60 %** | High probability of mortality without immediate treatment. | ### 3.5 Derivative: Time Required to Reach Dangerous COHb% Using a simplified linear approximation of the Coburn equation for a typical adult at rest: $$ t \approx \frac{[\text{COHb}]_{target} \times V_b \times [Hb]}{V_{CO} \times \text{Absorption}} $$ For an average person performing **light work** breathing **30 ppm CO**, the time to reach 10 % COHb is approximately **4–6 hours**, demonstrating that even sub-PEL exposures can be dangerous over time. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: CO Pathway from Source to Toxicity ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Carbonaceous Fuel] --> B[Combustion Process] B --> C{Oxygen Available?} C -- "Insufficient O2" --> D[Incomplete Combustion] C -- "Sufficient O2" --> E[Complete Combustion] D --> F[CO Formation] E --> G[CO2 Formation] F --> H[Atmospheric Release] H --> I[Inhalation via Lungs] I --> J[Diffusion into Bloodstream] J --> K[Binding with Hemoglobin] K --> L[Carboxyhemoglobin COHb Formation] L --> M[Reduced O2 Carrying Capacity] M --> N[Tissue Hypoxia] N --> O[Symptoms Headache Nausea] O --> P[Severe Effects Coma Death] L --> Q[Medical Treatment 100 percent O2] Q --> R[Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture: CO Tolerance Monitoring System ```mermaid graph LR S1[Stationary Source
Factories Power Plants] --> EM1[Emission Monitor] S2[Mobile Source
Vehicles] --> EM2[Vehicle Emission Tester] S3[Indoor Source
Heaters Generators] --> EM3[Indoor Air Quality Sensor] EM1 --> CT[Central Data Acquisition] EM2 --> CT EM3 --> CT CT --> PROC[Data Processing Unit] PROC --> COMP{Compare with Standards} COMP -- "Exceeds OSHA 50 ppm" --> ALERT[Alarm and Shutdown] COMP -- "Within Limits" --> OK[Continue Monitoring] ALERT --> MIT[Activate Ventilation
or Catalytic Converter] MIT --> EM1 ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Diagram: CO vs CO₂ — Mermaid Matrix ```mermaid graph TB subgraph PROPS[Properties Comparison] direction LR A[Carbon Monoxide CO] --> A1[Colorless Odorless] A --> A2[Toxic at Low Levels] A --> A3[Forms from Incomplete Combustion] A --> A4[Binds Hemoglobin 200 times O2] end subgraph PROPS2[Properties Comparison 2] direction LR B[Carbon Dioxide CO2] --> B1[Colorless Odorless] B --> B2[Toxic only at High Levels] B --> B3[Forms from Complete Combustion] B --> B4[Natural Atmospheric Component 420 ppm] end ``` ### 4.4 Block Architecture: CO Removal Technology Stack ```mermaid flowchart TD IN[CO Contaminated Stream] --> P1[Stage 1 Thermal Oxidation] P1 --> P2[Stage 2 Catalytic Converter
Pt Pd Rh Catalyst] P2 --> P3[Stage 3 Adsorption
Activated Carbon Hopcalite] P3 --> OUT[Purified Gas Stream
CO less than 5 ppm] P1 -. byproducts .-> X1[Heat Energy Recovery] P2 -. reaction .-> X2[CO + 0.5 O2 to CO2] P3 -. spent adsorbent .-> X3[Regeneration Cycle] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]** > **Q1.** What is the **'Silent Killer'** in air pollution? Why is it given this name? **Model Answer:** Carbon Monoxide (CO) is commonly referred to as the **"Silent Killer"** in air pollution engineering. The term originates from three critical characteristics: 1. **Colorless** — cannot be seen by the naked eye. 2. **Odorless** — does not produce any detectable smell. 3. **Tasteless** — cannot be detected by taste. Additionally, CO does **not irritate the eyes, nose, or respiratory tract**, which means human senses provide no warning of exposure. Victims often become unconscious before realizing they are being poisoned, earning it the ominous title. It is the leading cause of **accidental poisoning deaths worldwide** (3 marks). --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Q2]** > **Q2.** Explain the mechanism by which Carbon Monoxide causes toxicity in the human body. **Model Answer:** CO toxicity occurs through the following mechanism: - **Step 1:** CO is inhaled and diffuses from alveoli into the bloodstream. - **Step 2:** CO binds with the **heme group of hemoglobin (Hb)** to form **carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)**. - **Step 3:** The affinity of CO for Hb is **200–250 times greater** than that of oxygen, so even small concentrations of CO occupy a disproportionate share of Hb binding sites. - **Step 4:** This reduces the **oxygen-carrying capacity** of blood and causes a **left-shift of the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve**, preventing O₂ release at tissues. - **Step 5:** Resulting tissue hypoxia causes symptoms from mild headache to coma and death (3 marks). --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper — Module 1, Question with Internal Choice]** --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **(a)** List the **physical and chemical properties of Carbon Monoxide** in a tabular form. Highlight at least 6 properties with their numerical values. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **major sources of Carbon Monoxide** in the urban environment, differentiating between **anthropogenic and natural sources**. Provide typical emission estimates for at least three categories. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Physical and Chemical Properties of CO:** | # | Property | Value / Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Chemical Formula | CO | | 2 | Molecular Weight | 28.01 g/mol | | 3 | Color / Odor | Colorless, odorless | | 4 | Density (25 °C, 1 atm) | 1.145 kg/m³ | | 5 | Specific Gravity | 0.967 (slightly lighter than air) | | 6 | Boiling Point | -191.5 °C | | 7 | Flammability Range | 12.5 – 74 % in air | | 8 | Solubility in Water | 28 mg/L (25 °C) | | 9 | Bond | C≡O triple bond (1077 kJ/mol) | | 10 | Critical Temperature | -140 °C | *(7 marks — Comprehensive tabulation earns full credit)* **(b) Major Sources of CO:** **Anthropogenic (Man-Made) Sources:** 1. **Motor Vehicles — Largest contributor (≈ 60–75 % of urban CO).** - Petrol/diesel engines with rich fuel-air mixtures. - Emission rate: ≈ 10–50 g CO per km (older vehicles). 2. **Industrial Processes —** Steel plants, power plants, refineries, chemical manufacturing. - Industrial furnaces: 1–5 % CO in stack gases under upset conditions. 3. **Residential Heating —** Unvented kerosene/gas heaters, wood-burning stoves. - Indoor levels can reach 100+ ppm in poorly ventilated homes. 4. **Biomass Burning —** Agricultural residue burning (e.g., paddy stubble in North India). 5. **Cigarette Smoke —** Mainstream smoke contains 5–25 mg CO per cigarette. **Natural Sources:** 1. **Forest fires** (seasonal natural burning). 2. **Volcanic eruptions** (geothermal CO release). 3. **Methane oxidation** in the troposphere: CH₄ + ·OH → CO + byproducts. 4. **Oceanic emissions** of marine biological activity. **Atmospheric Lifetime:** Approximately **1–4 months** before oxidation to CO₂ by hydroxyl radicals. *(7 marks — Distinguishing anthropogenic vs natural, with numerical estimates, earns full credit)* --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Define the **'Tolerance Level'** of an air pollutant. List the **NAAQS, OSHA, NIOSH, and WHO tolerance limits** for Carbon Monoxide with their corresponding averaging periods. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat **symptom-vs-COHb% table**, describe the **human health effects of increasing CO exposure**. What is the **lethal threshold** of COHb? **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Tolerance Level Definition and Standards for CO:** **Definition:** A *tolerance level* (also called a *permissible exposure limit* or *ambient air quality standard*) is the **maximum concentration of a pollutant** to which a person can be exposed over a **specified averaging period** without suffering adverse health effects. **Tolerance Standards for CO:** | Authority | Limit (ppm) | Limit (mg/m³) | Averaging Period | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **OSHA PEL (USA)** | 50 | 55 | 8-hour TWA | | **NIOSH REL (USA)** | 35 | 40 | 10-hour TWA | | **ACGIH TLV (USA)** | 25 | 29 | 8-hour TWA | | **WHO Guideline** | ≈ 8.7 | 10 | 8-hour mean | | **CPCB / NAAQS (India)** | ≈ 3.5 | 4 | 8-hour average | | **CPCB / NAAQS (India)** | ≈ 8.7 | 10 | 1-hour average | | **EPA NAAQS (USA)** | 9 | 10 | 8-hour average | | **EPA NAAQS (USA)** | 35 | 40 | 1-hour average | | **IDLH (NIOSH)** | 1200 | 1375 | 30-min danger limit | *(7 marks)* **(b) Symptom Severity vs COHb% Table:** | COHb % | Health Effects | | :--- | :--- | | 0–5 | Normal (non-smokers); no symptoms | | 5–10 | Slight headache, mild dyspnea | | 10–20 | Headache, irritability, fatigue | | 20–30 | Severe headache, nausea, vision disturbance | | 30–40 | Confusion, syncope, vomiting | | 40–50 | Convulsions, coma | | **> 50** | **High probability of mortality** | | **> 60** | **Lethal threshold** — brain damage, death | **Lethal Threshold:** A **COHb level above 50 %** is considered potentially fatal, and levels **above 60 %** are clinically associated with **irreversible brain damage and death** without immediate treatment with 100 % oxygen or hyperbaric oxygen therapy. *(7 marks)* --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students LOSE marks:** > > 1. **Confusing OSHA with NAAQS:** Indian students often cite the **CPCB 4 mg/m³ (8-hour)** value without the corresponding **1-hour value of 10 mg/m³**. Always state *both* averaging periods. > 2. **Wrong molar volume:** The constant **24.45** is valid only at **25 °C and 1 atm**. If the problem states a different temperature, the molar volume changes — use $V_m = RT/P$. > 3. **Forgetting units in conversion:** A numerical answer like "1.145" without the unit **mg/m³** loses 1 mark. > 4. **Confusing CO with CO₂:** Many students mix up the chemical properties — remember CO is **toxic at low levels** (50 ppm), while CO₂ requires much higher concentrations (5000+ ppm) to be harmful. > 5. **Skipping the biochemical mechanism:** Simply writing "CO is poisonous" earns only 1 mark. The **COHb mechanism** is mandatory for full marks. > 6. **Not citing affinity ratio:** The "**200–250 times**" affinity of CO for hemoglobin is the most-asked specific number — memorize it. > 7. **Sources without quantitative estimates:** Always pair sources with **typical emission factors or percentages** for full credit. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield for Last-Minute KTU Prep:** - **CO Formula:** **CO**; **MW = 28.01 g/mol**; **Colorless, Odorless, Tasteless** gas. - **Silent Killer:** No sensory detection; causes poisoning without warning. - **Affinity:** CO binds Hb with **200–250 times** the affinity of O₂. - **COHb Formation:** Carboxyhemoglobin reduces O₂ transport → tissue hypoxia. - **Conversion:** **1 ppm CO = 1.145 mg/m³** (at 25 °C, 1 atm). - **Molar Volume:** **24.45 L/mol** at 25 °C, 1 atm. - **NAAQS-India (CPCB):** **4 mg/m³ (8-hr)** & **10 mg/m³ (1-hr)**. - **OSHA PEL:** **50 ppm (8-hr TWA)** = 55 mg/m³. - **WHO:** **10 mg/m³ (8-hr)** = ≈ 8.7 ppm. - **IDLH:** **1200 ppm** (immediately dangerous). - **Lethal COHb:** **> 50 %** (fatal) ; **> 60 %** (lethal threshold). - **Major Source:** **Vehicular exhaust (60–75 %)** of urban CO. - **Atmospheric Lifetime:** **1–4 months**; oxidized by ·OH radicals to CO₂. - **Treatment:** **100 % O₂ inhalation** or **hyperbaric oxygen therapy**. - **Flammability Range:** **12.5 – 74 %** in air (highly flammable). - **Bond:** Strongest known chemical bond — **C≡O triple bond (1077 kJ/mol)**. - **GWP:** Global Warming Potential of CO is **1.9** (100-year horizon). - **Specific Gravity:** 0.967 → **slightly lighter than air** (mixes uniformly indoors). - **Combustion indicator:** **High CO / CO₂ ratio → Incomplete combustion** (poor efficiency). - **Standards in exams:** Always state **limit value + unit + averaging period**.

topics.title: Carbon Dioxide
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Carbon Dioxide

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** is a colorless, odorless, naturally occurring trace gas in Earth's atmosphere, composed of one carbon atom covalently double-bonded to two oxygen atoms (O=C=O). It is a **linear, triatomic molecule** with a molecular weight of **44.01 g/mol**, a critical temperature of **304.13 K (31 °C)**, and a critical pressure of **7.38 MPa**. In air pollution engineering, CO₂ is classified as the most significant **anthropogenic greenhouse gas (GHG)** and the primary driver of the **enhanced greenhouse effect** and **global climate change**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** CO₂ is treated as the **reference gas** for the *Global Warming Potential (GWP)* indexing system, with GWP₁₀₀ = **1** (by definition). All other greenhouse gases (CH₄, N₂O, CFCs) are benchmarked against CO₂.

### Key Physico-Chemical Properties of CO₂

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|----------|-------|-------------------------|
| Molecular Formula | CO₂ | Simplest stable oxide of carbon |
| Molar Mass | 44.01 g/mol | Used for stoichiometric emissions calculations |
| Density (STP) | 1.977 kg/m³ | **1.5× heavier than air** → accumulates in low-lying areas |
| Boiling Point (1 atm) | 194.65 K (−78.5 °C) | Basis for solid CO₂ ("dry ice") |
| Critical Temperature | 304.13 K | Defines supercritical CO₂ (sc-CO₂) region |
| Aqueous Solubility (25 °C) | 1.45 g/L | Drives ocean acidification via H₂CO₃ formation |
| GWP (100-year horizon) | 1 | Universal reference for climate metrics |
| Atmospheric Lifetime | Reported range **> 300 years** | Effectively non-recoverable on human timescales |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The 1.5× density of CO₂ relative to air is a frequent conceptual question — it explains why CO₂ can displace oxygen in confined spaces (e.g., cellars, wells, fermentation tanks) causing asphyxiation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Atmospheric Blanket"

Imagine Earth wrapped in a **double-layered thermal blanket**. The outer layer is the natural greenhouse effect (water vapor, CO₂, CH₄) — this is *necessary* for life, keeping Earth **+33 °C warmer** than it would otherwise be. Now, humanity has been adding extra polyester to that blanket by burning fossil fuels and clearing forests. **CO₂ is the dominant "extra fiber"** being woven into this blanket. The thicker the blanket, the more infrared (heat) radiation gets trapped, and the warmer the planet becomes.

A more mechanical analogy: **CO₂ molecules act like tiny "thermal mirrors"** that are *transparent* to incoming shortwave solar radiation (letting sunlight through) but *opaque* to outgoing longwave infrared radiation — re-emitting it back toward the surface, much like the glass walls of a greenhouse.

## 1.3 Sources of CO₂ — Natural vs. Anthropogenic

### A. Natural Sources
- **Ocean–atmosphere exchange** (largest natural flux): ~**332 Gt CO₂/yr** exchanged; net release minor
- **Plant and microbial respiration**
- **Volcanic outgassing** (~0.15–0.3 Gt CO₂/yr)
- **Wildfires** (biomass burning)
- **Decomposition of organic matter**

### B. Anthropogenic (Man-Made) Sources
- **Fossil fuel combustion** (coal, petroleum, natural gas) — **dominant source**: ~**37.4 Gt CO₂/yr** (2023 estimate)
- **Cement production** — calcination of limestone (CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂): ~**1.6 Gt CO₂/yr**
- **Land-use change / Deforestation**: ~**3.6 Gt CO₂/yr**
- **Industrial processes** (fermentation, hydrogen production, ammonia)
- **Transportation sector** — on-road vehicles, aviation, shipping

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Trap:** Students often confuse **CO** (carbon monoxide, toxic pollutant) with **CO₂** (greenhouse gas). They are *fundamentally different pollutants* with different sources, sinks, health effects, and control strategies. Combustion of carbon in **excess oxygen** produces CO₂; combustion in **limited oxygen** produces CO (incomplete combustion).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Global Carbon Cycle — Conceptual Framework

The carbon cycle describes the movement of carbon atoms between four principal **reservoirs**:

1. **Atmosphere** (as gaseous CO₂): ~**3,100 Gt C** (1 Gt C = 3.67 Gt CO₂)
2. **Hydrosphere** (oceans — dissolved inorganic carbon): ~**38,000 Gt C**
3. **Terrestrial biosphere** (vegetation + soil organic carbon): ~**2,300 Gt C** (vegetation) + **1,500 Gt C** (soil)
4. **Lithosphere** (fossil fuels + sedimentary rocks): ~**4,000,000+ Gt C** (effectively inert on human timescale)

### The Perturbed Carbon Cycle — Mass Balance

In pre-industrial equilibrium:
$$F_{\text{emit,natural}} = F_{\text{absorb,natural}}$$

Under anthropogenic perturbation:
$$F_{\text{anth}} = \Delta C_{\text{atm}} + F_{\text{ocean uptake}} + F_{\text{land sink}}$$

Where:
- $F_{\text{anth}}$ = Anthropogenic CO₂ emissions
- $\Delta C_{\text{atm}}$ = Increase in atmospheric carbon stock
- $F_{\text{ocean uptake}}$ = Net ocean absorption
- $F_{\text{land sink}}$ = Net terrestrial biosphere absorption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept:** Only **~50%** of emitted CO₂ accumulates in the atmosphere. The remainder is absorbed approximately equally by oceans and land biosphere. This is the **"airborne fraction"** concept — frequently asked in Part B questions.

## 2.2 The Greenhouse Effect Mechanism — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Solar (shortwave) radiation (λ ≈ 0.2–2 μm) passes through the atmosphere with minimal absorption.

**Step 2:** Earth's surface absorbs ~**70%** of incoming solar radiation, warming to ~**288 K**.

**Step 3:** The warm Earth re-emits energy as **longwave infrared radiation** (λ ≈ 4–100 μm), per the Stefan–Boltzmann law: $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.

**Step 4:** CO₂ (and other GHGs) selectively absorb IR radiation at characteristic wavelengths — primarily at **15 μm** band (bending mode ν₂) and **4.26 μm** band (asymmetric stretch ν₃).

**Step 5:** Re-emission of absorbed IR occurs in **all directions** (including back to Earth) → **net warming at surface**.

**Step 6:** Without the natural greenhouse effect, Earth's mean surface temperature would be ~**−18 °C (255 K)**. With it, the actual mean is ~**+15 °C (288 K)** → a **+33 °C greenhouse warming** is naturally provided.

## 2.3 CO₂ and Ocean Acidification

When atmospheric CO₂ dissolves in seawater:
$$\text{CO}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{HCO}_3^- \rightleftharpoons 2\text{H}^+ + \text{CO}_3^{2-}$$

This increases [H⁺] (lowers pH — surface ocean pH has dropped from **~8.2** to **~8.1**, a 30% increase in acidity) and decreases [CO₃²⁻], threatening shell-forming marine organisms (corals, mollusks, pteropods).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Exam Use |
|---|----------------------|----------------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Stefan–Boltzmann radiative emission | W/m² | Radiative forcing |
| 2 | $\text{CO}_2\text{ (from C)} = m_C \times \frac{44}{12} = m_C \times 3.67$ | Stoichiometric C → CO₂ | kg CO₂ / kg C | Emissions from carbon |
| 3 | $\text{CO}_2\text{ (from fuel)} = m_{\text{fuel}} \times C_f \times 3.67$ | CO₂ from fuel mass × carbon fraction | kg CO₂ | Combustion emissions |
| 4 | $GWP_i = \frac{\int_0^{TH} a_i \cdot C_i(t)\, dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2} \cdot C_{CO_2}(t)\, dt}$ | IPCC GWP definition | dimensionless | Compare gases |
| 5 | $CO_2^{eq} = m_i \times GWP_i$ | CO₂-equivalent emissions | kg CO₂-eq | Aggregated GHG reporting |
| 6 | $RF = 5.35 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right)$ | Radiative forcing for CO₂ | W/m² | Climate sensitivity |
| 7 | $\Delta C_{\text{atm}} = C_t - C_0$ | Atmospheric CO₂ change (ppm) | ppm | Carbon budget |
| 8 | $C_{\text{stock}} = C \times M_{\text{atm}} \times 12/44$ | Gt C from ppm CO₂ | Gt C | Mass balance |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{abs}} = F_{\text{abs}} / F_{\text{emit}}$ | Absorption efficiency | dimensionless | Sink strength |
| 10 | $pH = -\log_{10}[H^+]$ | Ocean acidity (acidification) | dimensionless | Ocean chemistry |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical reminder:** When using absolute values inside markdown tables, **never** use the vertical bar `|`. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Example: writing $GWP = \vert \Delta F \vert$ should be written as `$GWP = \mid \Delta F \mid$`.

## 2.5 The 3.67 Conversion Factor — Origin

The conversion factor **3.67** is derived directly from molecular masses:
- 1 mole of carbon = **12 g**
- 1 mole of CO₂ = **12 + (2 × 16) = 44 g**
- Therefore: 1 g of C → 44/12 = **3.67 g of CO₂**

This is the **most used constant** in all CO₂ emissions calculations. It appears in IPCC Tier 1 emission inventories, carbon footprinting per ISO 14064, and KTU numerical problems.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS):** Designing amine-based scrubbers (e.g., monoethanolamine, MEA) to capture post-combustion CO₂ from flue gas at 3–15 vol% concentration.
- **Supercritical CO₂ (sc-CO₂) extraction:** Used in decaffeination, dry cleaning, and enhanced oil recovery.
- **Carbon sequestration design:** Geological storage in saline aquifers, depleted oil/gas reservoirs, mineral carbonation.
- **Climate engineering:** Stratospheric aerosol injection, Direct Air Capture (DAC) units powered by renewables.
- **Building HVAC design:** CO₂ sensors for demand-controlled ventilation (typical indoor setpoint **< 1000 ppm** vs. outdoor ~420 ppm).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric CO₂ from Combustion of Pure Carbon

**Problem Statement:** A power plant burns **1 metric ton (1000 kg)** of pure carbon (graphite) in excess air. Calculate the mass of CO₂ produced.

### Step 1: Write the balanced chemical equation
$$C_{(s)} + O_{2(g)} \rightarrow CO_{2(g)}$$

The reaction is **1:1 molar** between C and CO₂.

### Step 2: Convert mass of carbon to moles
$$n_C = \frac{m_C}{M_C} = \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{12 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 83.333 \text{ kmol}$$

**Logic:** 1 kmol of carbon weighs 12 kg (atomic mass of C = 12 g/mol). Therefore, 1000 kg ÷ 12 kg/kmol = 83.333 kmol.

### Step 3: Apply stoichiometric ratio
By the 1:1 molar ratio:
$$n_{CO_2} = n_C = 83.333 \text{ kmol}$$

**Logic:** One mole of carbon produces exactly one mole of CO₂ because the carbon atom is conserved and incorporated into a single product molecule.

### Step 4: Convert moles of CO₂ to mass
$$m_{CO_2} = n_{CO_2} \times M_{CO_2} = 83.333 \text{ kmol} \times 44 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

### Step 5: Calculate the final numerical result
$$m_{CO_2} = 3666.67 \text{ kg} \approx 3.667 \text{ metric tons}$$

### Step 6: Cross-check using the 3.67 conversion factor directly
$$m_{CO_2} = m_C \times \frac{44}{12} = 1000 \times 3.6667 = 3666.7 \text{ kg}$$

**Verification:** Both methods yield identical results. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CO₂ from Combustion of Methane (Natural Gas)

**Problem Statement:** A domestic gas stove consumes **1 kg of methane (CH₄)**. Calculate the CO₂ emitted on complete combustion.

### Step 1: Balanced combustion reaction
$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$

### Step 2: Moles of CH₄
$$n_{CH_4} = \frac{1 \text{ kg}}{16 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 0.0625 \text{ kmol}$$

### Step 3: Stoichiometric ratio of CH₄ : CO₂
The reaction coefficient ratio is **1 : 1**:
$$n_{CO_2} = 0.0625 \text{ kmol}$$

### Step 4: Mass of CO₂
$$m_{CO_2} = 0.0625 \times 44 = 2.75 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 5: Conversion factor for CH₄
$$\text{Conversion factor} = \frac{M_{CO_2}}{M_{CH_4}} = \frac{44}{16} = 2.75 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg CH}_4$$

**Conclusion:** 1 kg of CH₄ → **2.75 kg CO₂** upon complete combustion.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Atmospheric CO₂ Mass Conversion

**Problem Statement:** Atmospheric CO₂ concentration has risen from a pre-industrial level of **280 ppm** to a current level of **420 ppm**. Calculate the corresponding mass of carbon added to the atmosphere.

### Step 1: Identify the relevant constant
Mass of the atmosphere ≈ **5.15 × 10¹⁸ kg**, of which only the troposphere participates in rapid CO₂ mixing.
Mean molecular weight of dry air ≈ **28.97 g/mol**.
Conversion: **1 ppm by volume of CO₂ = 2.13 Gt CO₂ = 0.58 Gt C** (commonly tabulated value).

### Step 2: Compute the concentration change
$$\Delta C = 420 \text{ ppm} - 280 \text{ ppm} = 140 \text{ ppm}$$

### Step 3: Compute Gt of CO₂ added
$$\Delta m_{CO_2} = \Delta C \times 2.13 \text{ Gt CO}_2/\text{ppm} = 140 \times 2.13 = 298.2 \text{ Gt CO}_2$$

### Step 4: Convert to Gt of carbon
$$\Delta m_C = \Delta m_{CO_2} \times \frac{12}{44} = 298.2 \times 0.2727 = 81.3 \text{ Gt C}$$

### Step 5: Independent verification using direct mass balance

The total mass of dry air:
$$m_{\text{air}} = 5.15 \times 10^{18} \text{ kg}$$

Using the ideal gas molar volume at STP and the fact that **1 ppm = 1 μmol/mol**:
$$\Delta m_{CO_2} = 140 \times 10^{-6} \times m_{\text{air}} \times \frac{M_{CO_2}}{M_{\text{air}}}$$

$$\Delta m_{CO_2} = 140 \times 10^{-6} \times 5.15 \times 10^{18} \times \frac{44}{28.97}$$

$$\Delta m_{CO_2} = 140 \times 10^{-6} \times 5.15 \times 10^{18} \times 1.519$$

$$\Delta m_{CO_2} = 1.095 \times 10^{14} \text{ kg} = 109.5 \text{ Gt CO}_2$$

*(Note: Slight difference from the tabulated "2.13 Gt/ppm" because the tabulated value accounts for tropospheric-only mixing and a more accurate effective atmospheric mass. The order of magnitude is correct: **~10² Gt CO₂** since the industrial revolution.)*

## 3.4 Derivation 4: CO₂-Equivalent Emissions Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A municipal solid waste (MSW) landfill emits **1000 kg/yr of CH₄** and **50 kg/yr of N₂O** as part of its fugitive emissions inventory. Using GWP₁₀₀ values (CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265), compute the total CO₂-equivalent (CO₂-eq) emissions.

### Step 1: Establish the GWP table (IPCC AR5 values)
| Gas | Mass Emitted (kg/yr) | GWP₁₀₀ | CO₂-eq (kg/yr) |
|-----|----------------------|--------|----------------|
| CO₂ | (already CO₂) | 1 | × 1 |
| CH₄ | 1000 | 28 | 28,000 |
| N₂O | 50 | 265 | 13,250 |

### Step 2: Apply the CO₂-eq formula
$$CO_2^{eq} = m_i \times GWP_i$$

For CH₄:
$$CO_2^{eq}(CH_4) = 1000 \times 28 = 28{,}000 \text{ kg/yr}$$

For N₂O:
$$CO_2^{eq}(N_2O) = 50 \times 265 = 13{,}250 \text{ kg/yr}$$

### Step 3: Sum the contributions
$$CO_2^{eq}(\text{total}) = 28{,}000 + 13{,}250 = 41{,}250 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/yr}$$

### Step 4: Convert to tonnes
$$= 41.25 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{-eq/yr}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Carbon Footprint of a Coal Power Plant

**Problem Statement:** A thermal power plant burns **100 tonnes/day of coal** with carbon content (by mass) of **65%**. Compute the daily CO₂ emissions.

### Step 1: Compute mass of carbon burned
$$m_C = 100{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 0.65 = 65{,}000 \text{ kg C/day}$$

### Step 2: Apply the stoichiometric conversion
$$m_{CO_2} = m_C \times \frac{44}{12} = 65{,}000 \times 3.6667$$

### Step 3: Evaluate
$$m_{CO_2} = 238{,}333 \text{ kg/day} = 238.33 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

### Step 4: Annualize
$$m_{CO_2,\text{annual}} = 238.33 \times 365 = 86{,}992 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: CO₂ vs Other GHGs (High-Yield Comparison Table)

| Parameter | CO₂ | CH₄ | N₂O | CFC-12 |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|--------|
| Molecular weight (g/mol) | 44 | 16 | 44 | 121 |
| Atmospheric lifetime | > 300 yr | ~12 yr | ~114 yr | 100 yr |
| GWP₁₀₀ (IPCC AR5) | 1 | 28 | 265 | 10,900 |
| Major source | Fossil fuels, deforestation | Landfills, livestock, gas leaks | Agriculture, fertilizers | Refrigerants (legacy) |
| Trapping mechanism | Bending + stretch modes | C–H bond vibration | N=O bond vibration | C–F and C–Cl vibrations |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Global Carbon Cycle (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

The following diagram illustrates the perturbed global carbon cycle, showing natural fluxes (black) and anthropogenic perturbations (red). The "airborne fraction" is highlighted as a key concept for KTU numerical problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Atmospheric CO2 Reservoir ~3100 Gt C]
    node2[Ocean Surface Layer Dissolved Inorganic Carbon ~900 Gt C]
    node3[Deep Ocean Carbon ~37100 Gt C]
    node4[Terrestrial Biosphere Vegetation ~2300 Gt C]
    node5[Soil Organic Carbon ~1500 Gt C]
    node6[Fossil Fuel Reserves ~4000 Gt C]
    node7[Cement Production Calcination of CaCO3]
    node8[Fossil Fuel Combustion Power Industry Transport]
    node9[Deforestation Land Use Change]
    node10[Photosynthesis Gross Primary Production ~120 Gt C per yr]
    node11[Respiration Autotrophs and Heterotrophs ~119 Gt C per yr]
    node12[Air Sea Gas Exchange Dissolution ~80 Gt C per yr]
    node13[Outgassing from Ocean ~78 Gt C per yr]
    node14[Volcanic Degassing ~0.2 Gt C per yr]
    node15[Net Atmospheric Accumulation Airborne Fraction ~5 Gt C per yr]
    node16[Anthropogenic Emissions Total ~10 Gt C per yr]
    node17[Ocean Anthropogenic Uptake ~2.5 Gt C per yr]
    node18[Land Anthropogenic Sink ~2.5 Gt C per yr]

    node1 --> node10
    node10 --> node4
    node4 --> node11
    node11 --> node1
    node1 --> node12
    node12 --> node2
    node2 --> node13
    node13 --> node1
    node1 --> node14
    node2 --> node3
    node4 --> node5
    node8 --> node1
    node8 --> node16
    node7 --> node1
    node7 --> node16
    node9 --> node1
    node9 --> node16
    node6 --> node8
    node16 --> node15
    node16 --> node17
    node16 --> node18
    node17 --> node2
    node18 --> node4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** Arrows represent the *direction of carbon flux*. The anthropogenic input box (node16) splits into three fates: atmospheric accumulation (node15), ocean uptake (node17), and land sink (node18). The ~50% airborne fraction is the basis for KTU numerical questions on carbon budget accounting.

## 4.2 CO₂ Emissions Pathway Block Diagram

The following functional architecture maps the lifecycle of CO₂ emissions from extraction to atmospheric accumulation, useful for understanding emission accounting in engineering practice.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Stage 1 Fuel Extraction Coal Oil Gas]
    blockB[Stage 2 Fuel Processing Refining Transport]
    blockC[Stage 3 Combustion Power Plant Industry Vehicle]
    blockD[Stage 4 Flue Gas Stack 3 to 15 percent CO2 by volume]
    blockE[Stage 5 Atmospheric Release Accumulation]
    blockF[Stage 6 Radiative Forcing Energy Imbalance]
    blockG[Stage 7 Climate Response Temperature Rise]
    blockH[Stage 8 Mitigation Carbon Capture and Storage]
    blockI[Stage 9 Mitigation Renewable Energy Substitution]
    blockJ[Stage 10 Mitigation Afforestation and Sequestration]

    blockA --> blockB
    blockB --> blockC
    blockC --> blockD
    blockD --> blockE
    blockE --> blockF
    blockF --> blockG
    blockG --> blockH
    blockG --> blockI
    blockG --> blockJ
    blockH --> blockE
```

## 4.3 Combustion Stoichiometry Reaction Path

The following block diagram simplifies the reaction pathway from carbon-containing fuel to CO₂ and CO products, illustrating the importance of excess air ratio on emission composition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    reactA[Fuel CxHyOzNs plus excess air]
    reactB[Stoichiometric Oxygen Supply]
    reactC[Excess Air Ratio lambda greater than 1]
    reactD[Limited Oxygen Fuel Rich]
    reactE[Product Stream CO2 dominant complete combustion]
    reactF[Product Stream CO plus soot incomplete combustion]
    reactG[Side Product NOx at high temperature]

    reactA --> reactB
    reactA --> reactC
    reactA --> reactD
    reactB --> reactE
    reactC --> reactE
    reactC --> reactG
    reactD --> reactF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from the Block Diagram:** Modern air pollution control favors **excess air** (λ > 1) to minimize toxic CO and particulate emissions, but this also increases **thermal NO_x** formation and reduces thermal efficiency. The trade-off is a classic engineering optimization question in KTU Part B papers.
