## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree: Aldehyde Source Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Detected Aldehyde in Air Sample] --> Q1{Which Aldehyde Dominates}
    Q1 -->|HCHO| Q2{Formaldehyde to Acetaldehyde Ratio}
    Q1 -->|CH3CHO| PATH2[Likely Vehicle Exhaust or Biomass Burning]
    Q1 -->|Acrolein| PATH3[Likely Diesel Exhaust or Plastic Combustion]
    Q2 -->|Ratio greater than 3| PATH4[Photochemical Smog Signature]
    Q2 -->|Ratio less than 3| PATH5[Direct Combustion or Industrial Source]
    PATH2 --> OUT[Source Apportionment Complete]
    PATH3 --> OUT
    PATH4 --> OUT
    PATH5 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagnostic Tip for KTU Exam:** The **formaldehyde-to-acetaldehyde ratio (C₁/C₂ ratio)** is a powerful forensic tool:
> - **C₁/C₂ > 3:** Indicates **secondary photochemical formation** (aldehydes produced by atmospheric reactions, not direct emission).
> - **C₁/C₂ < 2:** Indicates **primary emission from combustion sources** (vehicles, biomass burning).
>
> This concept is frequently tested as a short-answer question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define aldehydes. Give two examples of aldehydes that are significant air pollutants.**

**Model Answer:**

Aldehydes are a class of organic compounds characterized by the general formula **R–CHO**, where the carbonyl carbon is bonded to at least one hydrogen atom. They contain the **formyl group (–CHO)** as their functional group and are produced during the incomplete combustion of hydrocarbons and the atmospheric photo-oxidation of VOCs.

**Two examples of significant air pollutants:**

1. **Formaldehyde (HCHO)** — the most abundant atmospheric carbonyl compound; emitted by vehicle exhaust, biomass burning, and formed as a secondary product from hydrocarbon oxidation. Classified as a **Group 1 carcinogen** (IARC) with a TLV-TWA of **0.75 ppm** (OSHA).

2. **Acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO)** — a common product of combustion and atmospheric oxidation of ethane, propane, and other alkanes. It is a **probable human carcinogen** (IARC Group 2B) and contributes to photochemical smog and PAN formation.

**Examiner's Note:** Award full 3 marks only if the student gives a clear definition AND provides specific examples with environmental relevance.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Criegee mechanism? Explain briefly with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Criegee mechanism** describes the atmospheric reaction of ozone (O₃) with alkenes (C=C double bond containing hydrocarbons), which proceeds through a **Criegee intermediate** (a carbonyl oxide biradical) to ultimately produce **two carbonyl compounds** (aldehydes and/or ketones) and **hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂)**.

**Example — Ozonolysis of Ethene:**

$$
\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{[Criegee Intermediate]} \rightarrow \text{HCHO} + \text{HCHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2
$$

The Criegee mechanism is one of the most important **secondary sources of aldehydes** in the troposphere, particularly for biogenic VOCs such as **isoprene** and **terpenes** emitted by forests.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various **primary and secondary sources** of aldehydes in the atmosphere. Explain why formaldehyde is considered the most abundant atmospheric carbonyl compound. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat chemical equations, explain the **atmospheric oxidation mechanism of methane (CH₄)** to produce formaldehyde. Also discuss the subsequent photolysis reactions of formaldehyde. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

Aldehydes enter the atmosphere from two distinct categories of sources:

**Primary Sources (Direct Emissions):**

1. **Incomplete Combustion of Fossil Fuels:** Automobile exhaust is the largest urban source of aldehydes. Gasoline and diesel engines emit formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, and acrolein directly because the fuel is not fully oxidized to CO₂ and H₂O in the combustion chamber. Lean-burn engines and cold-start conditions produce especially high aldehyde levels.

2. **Biomass Burning:** Forest fires, agricultural residue burning, and residential wood combustion release large quantities of aldehydes. Formaldehyde and acetaldehyde are the dominant species, with emission factors often exceeding several grams per kilogram of fuel burned.

3. **Industrial Processes:** Formaldehyde is manufactured industrially by the **catalytic oxidation of methanol** (Formox process) and is used extensively in the production of **urea-formaldehyde resins, phenol-formaldehyde resins, and polyacetal plastics**. Acetaldehyde is produced by the **Wacker process** (oxidation of ethylene) and is used in acetic acid manufacture.

4. **Indoor Sources:** Cigarette smoke, pressed wood products (particleboard, medium-density fiberboard), insulation materials, carpets, and household cleaning products release formaldehyde through off-gassing. Indoor concentrations often exceed outdoor levels by a factor of **2 to 10**.

5. **Natural Sources:** Direct emission from vegetation, decaying organic matter, and forest fires.

**Secondary Sources (Atmospheric Formation):**

1. **Photochemical Oxidation of VOCs:** Hydrocarbons (alkanes, alkenes, aromatics) react with hydroxyl radicals (**•OH**) during the day, with nitrate radicals (**•NO₃**) at night, and with ozone (O₃) to produce aldehydes as intermediate oxidation products.

2. **Ozonolysis of Biogenic VOCs:** Isoprene (C₅H₈) and monoterpenes (C₁₀H₁₆) emitted by trees react with O₃ via the Criegee mechanism to yield **methyl vinyl ketone, methacrolein, formaldehyde, and other aldehydes**.

3. **Photochemical Smog:** Urban atmospheres with high NOₓ and VOC levels produce aldehydes as integral components of smog.

**Why Formaldehyde is the Most Abundant Atmospheric Carbonyl:**

- It is the **simplest aldehyde** and the most thermodynamically stable C₁ carbonyl.
- It is produced from the oxidation of **every hydrocarbon** with at least one C–H bond.
- Methane, the most abundant atmospheric VOC, oxidizes to formaldehyde as its first carbonyl product.
- It has both primary and secondary sources.
- Its atmospheric lifetime (~1.2 days) is long enough to allow accumulation.
- Biogenic VOC emissions (especially isoprene) are massive global sources of HCHO.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Hydrogen Abstraction**

The hydroxyl radical abstracts a hydrogen atom from methane:

$$
\text{CH}_4 + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH} \rightarrow {}^{\bullet}\text{CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 2: Methyl Radical + Oxygen**

The methyl radical combines with molecular oxygen (with a third-body M to remove excess energy):

$$
{}^{\bullet}\text{CH}_3 + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OO}^{\bullet} + \text{M}
$$

**Step 3: Methyl Peroxy + NO**

The methyl peroxy radical reacts with nitric oxide to form nitrogen dioxide and a methoxy radical:

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{OO}^{\bullet} + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{O}^{\bullet} + \text{NO}_2
$$

**Step 4: Methoxy Radical + Oxygen → Formaldehyde**

The methoxy radical reacts with O₂ to produce **formaldehyde (HCHO)** and a hydroperoxyl radical (HO₂•):

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{O}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{HCHO} + \text{HO}_2^{\bullet}
$$

**Net Stoichiometric Result:**

$$
\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{HCHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{O} \quad (\text{via radical chain, ultimately producing HO}_2^{\bullet} \text{ and NO}_2)
$$

**Subsequent Photolysis of Formaldehyde:**

Formaldehyde absorbs UV light (240–360 nm) and undergoes two pathways:

**Pathway 1 — Molecular Channel (dominant at low pressure):**

$$
\text{HCHO} + h\nu \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \text{CO}
$$

**Pathway 2 — Radical Channel (dominant at tropospheric pressures):**

$$
\text{HCHO} + h\nu \rightarrow \text{H}^{\bullet} + \text{HCO}^{\bullet}
$$

The H• radical reacts with O₂:

$$
\text{H}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{HO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{M}
$$

The formyl radical (HCO•) reacts with O₂:

$$
\text{HCO}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{HO}_2^{\bullet}
$$

**Net Photolysis Product:** CO + 2 HO₂•

The HO₂• radicals then convert NO to NO₂, driving ozone formation in photochemical smog. This is why formaldehyde is considered a **key photochemical smog precursor**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **health and environmental effects of aldehydes**. Discuss the analytical method for measuring atmospheric aldehydes using the **DNPH-HPLC technique** with relevant chemical reactions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory emits **50,000 m³/h** of air containing **8 ppm** of formaldehyde at **30°C and 1 atm**. A wet scrubber removes **85%** of the formaldehyde. Calculate:
   (i) The mass of HCHO removed per hour.
   (ii) The exit concentration in mg/m³.
   (iii) The exit concentration in ppm.
   **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Health Effects:**

1. **Formaldehyde (HCHO):** Classified as a **Group 1 carcinogen** (IARC) — confirmed human carcinogen. Causes **nasopharyngeal cancer, sinonasal cancer, and leukemia** with chronic exposure. Acute exposure at 0.1–5 ppm causes **sensory irritation** (eye, nose, throat), headaches, and respiratory distress. TLV-TWA = **0.75 ppm** (OSHA), **0.016 ppm** (ACGIH), **0.1 ppm** (NIOSH REL).

2. **Acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO):** IARC Group 2B (probable carcinogen). Causes **respiratory irritation, bronchial irritation,** and is a precursor to PAN formation. TLV-TWA = **25 ppm** (OSHA).

3. **Acrolein (CH₂=CHCHO):** The most toxic of the common aldehydes. TLV-TWA = **0.1 ppm**. Causes **severe pulmonary edema, eye irritation,** and is a **lacrimator** (tear gas component). Even at 1 ppm, exposure is intolerable.

**Environmental Effects:**

1. **Photochemical Smog:** Aldehydes are both **products and drivers** of photochemical smog. They photolyze to produce HO₂• radicals, which convert NO to NO₂, fueling O₃ production.
2. **PAN Formation:** Acetaldehyde → Acetyl peroxy radical → **Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN)**, a severe eye irritant and phytotoxin.
3. **Secondary Organic Aerosol (SOA):** Aldehydes contribute to fine particulate matter (PM₂.₅) formation through oxidation and oligomerization.
4. **Acid Rain Contribution:** Some aldehydes (especially HCHO) oxidize to **formic acid (HCOOH)**, contributing to atmospheric acidity.

**DNPH-HPLC Analytical Method (EPA Method TO-11A / NIOSH 2016):**

**Principle:** Aldehydes and ketones react with **2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH)** in the presence of an acid catalyst to form stable, colored **2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone derivatives**.

**Chemical Reaction:**

$$
\text{RCHO} + \text{H}_2\text{N-NH-C}_6\text{H}_3(\text{NO}_2)_2 \rightarrow \text{RCH=N-NH-C}_6\text{H}_3(\text{NO}_2)_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Procedure:**

1. **Sampling:** Air is drawn through a cartridge containing DNPH-coated silica gel (or an impinger containing DNPH in acidified acetonitrile) at a known flow rate (typically 0.1–1 L/min) for a defined period.
2. **Derivatization:** Aldehydes react with DNPH to form hydrazones on the cartridge itself.
3. **Extraction:** The hydrazones are eluted with **acetonitrile**.
4. **Analysis:** The eluate is analyzed by **High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC)** with a UV detector at **360 nm**.
5. **Quantification:** Peak areas are compared to calibration standards of known DNPH-aldehyde derivatives.

**Detection Limits:** Sub-ppb (parts per billion) levels achievable.

**Advantages:** High sensitivity, specificity for carbonyls, stable derivatives (samples can be stored for weeks).

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Volumetric flow rate: $Q = 50{,}000 \text{ m}^3\text{/h}$
- Inlet concentration: $C_{in} = 8 \text{ ppm}$
- Temperature: $T = 30°C = 303.15 \text{ K}$
- Pressure: $P = 1 \text{ atm}$
- Removal efficiency: $\eta = 85\% = 0.85$
- Molar mass of HCHO: $M = 30.03 \text{ g/mol}$

**Part (i): Mass of HCHO Removed per Hour**

**Step 1:** Calculate the molar volume at 30°C:

$$
V_m = \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{(0.08206)(303.15)}{1} = 24.88 \text{ L/mol} = 0.02488 \text{ m}^3\text{/mol}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert inlet concentration from ppm to mg/m³:

$$
C_{in,mg/m^3} = 8 \text{ ppm} \times \frac{30.03 \text{ g/mol}}{0.02488 \text{ m}^3\text{/mol}} = 8 \times 1207.0 = 9656 \text{ \mu g/m}^3 = 9.656 \text{ mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate inlet mass flow rate of HCHO:

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = Q \times C_{in,mg/m^3} = 50{,}000 \text{ m}^3\text{/h} \times 9.656 \text{ mg/m}^3 = 482{,}800 \text{ mg/h}
$$

Convert to grams:

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = 482.8 \text{ g/h}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate mass removed (85% efficiency):

$$
\dot{m}_{removed} = 0.85 \times 482.8 = 410.4 \text{ g/h}
$$

### Result for (i):
$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{removed} = 410.4 \text{ g/h}}
$$

**Part (ii): Exit Concentration in mg/m³**

**Step 5:** Calculate exit concentration (15% of inlet):

$$
C_{out,mg/m^3} = C_{in,mg/m^3} \times (1 - 0.85) = 9.656 \times 0.15
$$

### Result for (ii):
$$
\boxed{C_{out} = 1.448 \text{ mg/m}^3}
$$

**Part (iii): Exit Concentration in ppm**

**Step 6:** Convert mg/m³ back to ppm:

$$
C_{out,ppm} = C_{out,mg/m^3} \times \frac{V_m(\text{m}^3\text{/mol})}{M(\text{g/mol})} = 1.448 \times \frac{0.02488}{30.03}
$$

$$
C_{out,ppm} = 1.448 \times 8.284 \times 10^{-4} = 1.20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ppm}
$$

### Result for (iii):
$$
\boxed{C_{out} = 1.20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ppm} = 1.20 \text{ ppb}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Aldehyde Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert molar volume for the specific temperature given** (25°C vs 30°C). Always recalculate $V_m = RT/P$ if $T \neq 25°C$. Using $24.45$ L/mol at the wrong temperature costs **2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing the conversion direction** between ppm and mg/m³. The conversion factor is $M/24.45$ (or $M/V_m$ at non-standard conditions). Memorizing the wrong direction will invert the answer.
>
> 3. **Writing the Criegee mechanism without showing the intermediate.** The Criegee biradical is the mechanistic heart of ozonolysis — skipping it loses marks.
>
> 4. **Omitting the chain propagation steps** in the methane oxidation mechanism. Showing only "CH₄ + O₂ → HCHO" is too vague for full marks.
>
> 5. **Mixing up PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate) and PBN (Peroxybenzoyl Nitrate).** PAN is the more commonly asked and arises specifically from acetaldehyde oxidation.
>
> 6. **Not stating IARC classifications** for formaldehyde (Group 1) vs acetaldehyde (Group 2B). Examiners expect this specific terminology.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Aldehydes (Air Pollution Context)**

- **General Formula:** R–CHO (carbonyl group + at least one H on carbonyl carbon)
- **Three Most Important Atmospheric Aldehydes:** Formaldehyde (HCHO), Acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO), Acrolein (CH₂=CHCHO)
- **Primary Sources:** Vehicle exhaust, biomass burning, industrial emissions, cigarette smoke, off-gassing from building materials
- **Secondary Sources:** Photochemical oxidation of VOCs, ozonolysis of alkenes (Criegee mechanism)
- **Methane Oxidation Chain:** CH₄ → •CH₃ → CH₃OO• → CH₃O• → **HCHO** (formaldehyde)
- **Formaldehyde Photolysis:** Two pathways — molecular (H₂ + CO) and radical (H• + HCO•); both ultimately feed the HO₂• radical pool
- **Criegee Mechanism:** Alkene + O₃ → Criegee intermediate → two carbonyls + H₂O₂
- **PAN Formation:** RCHO → RCO• → RCO-OO• + NO₂ → **RC(O)OONO₂** (Peroxyacyl Nitrate)
- **Health Effects:** HCHO is a Group 1 carcinogen; acrolein TLV = 0.1 ppm (extremely toxic)
- **Analytical Method:** **DNPH-HPLC** (EPA TO-11A) — derivatization with 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine, analysis at 360 nm
- **Henry's Law:** Formaldehyde is highly water-soluble (H = 3400 M/atm) → wet scrubbing is effective
- **Atmospheric Lifetime of HCHO:** ~1.2 days (driven by •OH reaction, $k \approx 9.4 \times 10^{-12}$ cm³·molecule⁻¹·s⁻¹)
- **Conversion Formulas (CRITICAL):** 
  - $C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times M/24.45$ (at 25°C)
  - $C_{ppm} = C_{mg/m^3} \times 24.45/M$
- **Formaldehyde/Acetaldehyde Ratio (C₁/C₂):** >3 indicates photochemical (secondary); <2 indicates primary combustion
- **Control Technologies:** Wet scrubbing (80–95%), catalytic oxidation (95–99.9%), thermal oxidation (>99%), activated carbon (90–99%), biofiltration (70–90%)
- **Diagnostic IR Bands:** C=O stretch at ~1720–1740 cm⁻¹; aldehyde C–H stretch at ~2720 and 2820 cm⁻¹ (Fermi doublet)
- **Catalytic Converter Role:** Three-way catalysts oxidize aldehydes to CO₂ + H₂O
- **Building Material Link:** Urea-formaldehyde resins in particleboard = major **indoor** HCHO source

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrocarbons - Compounds of Sulphur
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hydrocarbons - Compounds of Sulphur # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Hydrocarbons as Air Pollutants ### Formal Academic Definition **Hydrocarbons (HC)** are organic compounds composed exclusively of hydrogen and carbon atoms. In the context of air pollution engineering, the term refers to the **non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs)** and **reactive hydrocarbons** emitted into the atmosphere, primarily through incomplete combustion of fossil fuels, evaporation of solvents, and industrial processes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Methane (CH₄) is often treated separately as it is relatively inert in tropospheric photochemistry. All other hydrocarbons participate in **photochemical smog formation** and are therefore the focus of this module. ### Classification of Atmospheric Hydrocarbons | Class | Generic Formula | Example | Reactivity | |---|---|---|---| | **Alkanes (Paraffins)** | CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ | Methane (CH₄), Butane (C₄H₁₀) | Low | | **Alkenes (Olefins)** | CₙH₂ₙ | Ethylene (C₂H₄), Propylene (C₃H₆) | **Very High** | | **Aromatics** | CₙH₂ₙ₋₆ | Benzene (C₆H₆), Toluene (C₇H₈) | High (carcinogenic) | | **Alkynes** | CₙH₂ₙ₋₂ | Acetylene (C₂H₂) | Moderate | ### Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Think of hydrocarbons as "Unburnt Food Scraps" of an engine.** When petrol/diesel combusts in a car engine, ideally it should be fully converted to **CO₂ and H₂O**. But due to imperfect mixing, low temperatures, and insufficient oxygen, some fuel escapes combustion. This unburnt fuel — the hydrocarbons — escapes through the exhaust and into the atmosphere, where it "spoils" the air by reacting with sunlight to form toxic secondary pollutants like **ozone** and **peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN)**. ### Major Sources of Atmospheric Hydrocarbons - **Mobile Sources** (~60% in urban areas): Automobiles, aircraft, ships - **Stationary Sources:** Power plants, refineries, chemical plants - **Area Sources:** Solvent evaporation (paints, dry cleaning), gasoline filling stations - **Biogenic Sources:** Terpenes from trees, isoprene from vegetation --- ## 1.2 Compounds of Sulphur ### Formal Academic Definition **Sulphur compounds** are inorganic and organic gases containing sulphur in various oxidation states (−2, +4, +6). In air pollution engineering, the most significant members are **sulphur dioxide (SO₂)**, **sulphur trioxide (SO₃)**, **hydrogen sulphide (H₂S)**, and **particulate sulphates (SO₄²⁻)**. They are responsible for **acid rain**, **respiratory ailments**, and **material corrosion**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sulphur compounds are emitted as primary pollutants but undergo atmospheric oxidation to form secondary pollutants like sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) and sulphate aerosols — the principal culprits in acid deposition.** ### Key Sulphur Pollutants at a Glance | Compound | Formula | Oxidation State of S | Nature | |---|---|---|---| | **Hydrogen Sulphide** | H₂S | −2 | Reducing, malodorous | | **Carbon Disulphide** | CS₂ | −2 | Industrial solvent | | **Sulphur Dioxide** | SO₂ | +4 | **Most critical** | | **Sulphur Trioxide** | SO₃ | +6 | Acid precursor | | **Dimethyl Sulphide** | (CH₃)₂S | −2 | Biogenic (ocean) | | **Mercaptans** | R–SH | −2 | Natural gas odorants | ### Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Sulphur dioxide is like a "Sour Gas" emitted by burning dirty fuel.** Just as burning a match releases a sharp, choking smell, the sulphur in coal and diesel reacts with oxygen to form SO₂ — a colourless, suffocating gas. Once in the atmosphere, it acts like a "lazy acid" that slowly converts into sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) — the same acid present in battery fluid — and falls as **acid rain**, damaging forests, lakes, buildings, and human lungs. ### Major Sources of Sulphur Compounds - **Combustion of fossil fuels** containing sulphur impurities (coal: 0.5–5% S; diesel: 0.1–1.2% S) - **Metal smelting** of sulphide ores (CuS, ZnS, PbS) - **Petroleum refineries** and **natural gas processing** (H₂S) - **Volcanic eruptions** and **biogenic emissions** from oceans (DMS) # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hydrocarbons: Atmospheric Chemistry & Behaviour ### 2.1.1 Why Hydrocarbons Are Problematic Hydrocarbons are not directly toxic in low concentrations, but they become **photochemically reactive** in the presence of **NOₓ and sunlight (λ < 400 nm)**. They initiate a chain of free-radical reactions producing **secondary pollutants**: 1. **Ozone (O₃)** at ground level — a major component of smog 2. **Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN)** — a powerful lachrymator and phytotoxin 3. **Aldehydes** (formaldehyde, acetaldehyde) — carcinogens 4. **Organic acids** — contributors to acid rain ### 2.1.2 Mechanism of Photochemical Smog Formation **Step 1: Photolysis of NO₂ (initiation step)** $$NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O \quad (\lambda < 430 \text{ nm})$$ **Step 2: Formation of tropospheric ozone** $$O + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$$ where **M** is a third body (N₂ or O₂) that absorbs excess energy. **Step 3: Hydrocarbon oxidation by OH· radicals (chain propagation)** $$OH \cdot + RH \rightarrow R \cdot + H_2O$$ **Step 4: Formation of PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate)** $$CH_3CHO + O_2 \rightarrow CH_3COO \cdot$$ $$CH_3COO \cdot + NO_2 \rightarrow CH_3COOONO_2 \text{ (PAN)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **PAN is unique to photochemical smog.** It is thermally unstable and decomposes back to its precursors. It serves as a marker compound for the presence of photochemical smog and is extremely toxic to vegetation (e.g., causes silvering of leafy vegetables like spinach). ### 2.1.3 BTX (Benzene, Toluene, Xylene) — Special Concern Aromatics - **Benzene** is a confirmed **human carcinogen (Group 1, IARC)** — causes leukaemia. - **Toluene and Xylene** cause central nervous system depression and liver damage. - They are regulated under **WHO guidelines** and **Indian National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)**. --- ## 2.2 Sulphur Compounds: Atmospheric Chemistry ### 2.2.1 Oxidation States and Stability Sulphur exhibits multiple oxidation states (−2, +4, +6), which is why it participates in diverse atmospheric reactions. The conversion from **+4 (in SO₂)** to **+6 (in SO₃/H₂SO₄)** is the central transformation in acid rain chemistry. ### 2.2.2 Mechanism of SO₂ Oxidation **Homogeneous gas-phase oxidation (slow):** $$2SO_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2SO_3 \quad (\Delta H = -198 \text{ kJ/mol})$$ **Catalysed gas-phase oxidation (faster):** $$SO_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{UV, dust, NO}} SO_3$$ **Aqueous-phase oxidation (dominant in clouds, ~80%):** $$SO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_3 \text{ (Sulphurous acid)}$$ $$H_2SO_3 \rightarrow H^+ + HSO_3^-$$ $$HSO_3^- \rightarrow H^+ + SO_3^{2-}$$ $$SO_3^{2-} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow SO_4^{2-}$$ $$SO_4^{2-} + 2H^+ \rightarrow H_2SO_4$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The aqueous-phase oxidation in atmospheric water droplets is **10–100 times faster** than the gas-phase oxidation. This is why industrial plumes appear "blue" on humid days — water droplets absorb SO₂ and create a corrosive acid mist. ### 2.2.3 Formation of Acid Rain $$SO_2 \xrightarrow{\text{oxidation}} SO_3 \xrightarrow{H_2O} H_2SO_4 \text{ (sulphuric acid)}$$ $$2H_2SO_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow \text{Acid Deposition (pH < 5.6)}$$ **Natural rainwater pH ≈ 5.6** (due to dissolved CO₂ forming carbonic acid). Any precipitation with **pH < 5.6** is classified as **acid rain**. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this table. These are the most frequently tested equations in KTU 2024 ESE papers on this module.** | # | Equation | Context | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C_nH_{2n+2}$ | Alkanes (Paraffins) | — | | 2 | $C_nH_{2n}$ | Alkenes (Olefins) | — | | 3 | $NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O$ | Initiation of photochemical smog | λ < 430 nm | | 4 | $O + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$ | Ozone formation | M = N₂ or O₂ | | 5 | $CH_3COO \cdot + NO_2 \rightarrow PAN$ | PAN formation | g/mol = 121 | | 6 | $S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$ | Source emission (combustion) | — | | 7 | $2SO_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2SO_3$ | Homogeneous SO₂ oxidation | ΔH = −198 kJ/mol | | 8 | $SO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4$ | Sulphuric acid formation | — | | 9 | $SO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_3$ | Sulphurous acid (intermediate) | — | | 10 | $pH = -\log[H^+]$ | Acid rain criterion (pH < 5.6) | — | | 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{C_i - C_f}{C_i} \times 100\%$ | Removal efficiency of pollutant | % | | 12 | $ER = \dfrac{64}{32} = 2.0$ | SO₂ to SO₃ stoichiometric ratio | kg SO₂ : kg S | | 13 | $E_{SO_2} = 2 \times \%S \times m_{fuel}$ | SO₂ emission from fuel | kg SO₂/tonne fuel | ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Design of Flue Gas Desulphurisation (FGD) units** uses stoichiometric ratios to size limestone slurry feed. - **Atmospheric dispersion modelling (AERMOD, ISCST3)** requires emission rates (g/s) of SO₂ and HC for predicting ground-level concentrations. - **BTEX monitoring** in refineries uses **photoionisation detectors (PIDs)** with calibration constants based on molecular weights. - **Air Quality Index (AQI) computation** for Indian cities uses **NAAQS breakpoints** for SO₂ (80 μg/m³, 24-hr) and benzene (5 μg/m³, annual). # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Mass of SO₂ Emitted per kg of Coal Combusted This is a **standard KTU numerical problem**. We derive the relationship between the sulphur content of fuel and the SO₂ mass produced. **Given:** - Mass of coal burned = $m$ (kg) - Sulphur content in coal = $S\%$ (by mass) **Step 1:** Mass of sulphur in the coal $$m_S = \dfrac{S}{100} \times m \quad \text{(kg)}$$ **Step 2:** Sulphur reacts with oxygen to form SO₂ $$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$$ One mole of S (32 g) produces one mole of SO₂ (64 g). **Step 3:** Mass ratio of SO₂ to S $$\dfrac{M_{SO_2}}{M_S} = \dfrac{64}{32} = 2$$ **Step 4:** Therefore, mass of SO₂ produced $$m_{SO_2} = 2 \times m_S = \dfrac{2 \times S \times m}{100} \quad \text{(kg)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **This 2.0 multiplier is the key constant in emission inventory calculations.** It applies for 100% conversion of S to SO₂. In practice, 90–95% of sulphur is converted to SO₂ in coal-fired boilers. **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style):** A thermal power plant burns 1000 tonnes/day of coal containing 1.5% sulphur. Calculate the daily SO₂ emission, assuming 95% conversion. **Solution:** $$m_{SO_2} = 2 \times 0.015 \times 1000 \times 0.95$$ $$m_{SO_2} = 28.5 \text{ tonnes/day}$$ Convert to kg/s: $$28.5 \times 1000 \div (24 \times 3600) = 0.33 \text{ kg/s}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: PAN Formation Stoichiometry and Mass Balance The complete pathway from acetaldehyde to PAN involves three steps. **Step 1: Photolysis of acetaldehyde by UV** $$CH_3CHO + h\nu \rightarrow CH_3 \cdot + CHO \cdot$$ **Step 2: Oxidation of methyl radical by O₂** $$CH_3 \cdot + O_2 + M \rightarrow CH_3OO \cdot + M$$ **Step 3: Abstraction of hydrogen from methylperoxy radical** $$CH_3OO \cdot + CH_3CHO \rightarrow CH_3COOH + CH_3CO \cdot$$ **Step 4: Formation of acetylperoxy radical** $$CH_3CO \cdot + O_2 + M \rightarrow CH_3COO \cdot + M \quad \text{(acetylperoxy radical)}$$ **Step 5: Final PAN formation with NO₂** $$CH_3COO \cdot + NO_2 + M \rightarrow CH_3COOONO_2 + M \quad \text{(PAN, M = 121.05 g/mol)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Net Reaction (simplified):** > $$CH_3CHO + 2O_2 + NO_2 \rightarrow CH_3COOONO_2 + \text{by-products}$$ > One mole of acetaldehyde produces one mole of PAN. The molar mass of PAN is **121.05 g/mol** — a frequently tested constant in KTU exams. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hydrocarbons vs. Sulphur Compounds — A Comparative Study > [!IMPORTANT] > **This matrix is KTU-high-yield. Examiners love comparison questions.** | Parameter | **Hydrocarbons (HC)** | **Sulphur Compounds (SOₓ, H₂S)** | |---|---|---| | **Primary State** | Gas (VOCs), some particulate (PAHs) | Gas (SO₂, H₂S), aerosol (H₂SO₄ mist) | | **Major Source** | Incomplete combustion, solvent evaporation | Combustion of S-bearing fossil fuels | | **Key Pollutant** | Ethylene (C₂H₄), Benzene, PAN | SO₂ (most critical), H₂S | | **Secondary Pollutants** | O₃, PAN, aldehydes, organic acids | H₂SO₄, sulphates, acid rain | | **Toxicity Type** | Carcinogenic (benzene), phytotoxic (PAN) | Respiratory irritant, corrosive | | **NAAQS Limit (24-hr)** | Benzene: 5 μg/m³ (annual) | SO₂: 80 μg/m³ (24-hr) | | **Control Method** | Catalytic converters, activated carbon, thermal oxidation | Wet/Dry FGD, lime scrubbers | | **Characteristic Smell** | Sweet (aromatics), odourless (alkanes) | Sharp/choking (SO₂), rotten egg (H₂S) | | **Reactivity in Atmosphere** | High (with NOₓ + sunlight) | Moderate (oxidative conversion) | | **Acid Rain Contribution** | Minor (organic acids) | **Major (60–70% of acid deposition)** | --- ## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Acid Rain pH Calculation Given that ambient SO₂ concentration is $C_{SO_2} = 100 \ \mu g/m^3$ and complete conversion to H₂SO₄ occurs in rainwater. **Step 1:** Convert SO₂ concentration to moles $$n_{SO_2} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{64} = 1.5625 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol/m}^3$$ **Step 2:** Moles of H₂SO₄ produced (1:1 stoichiometry, since each SO₂ gives one H₂SO₄) $$n_{H_2SO_4} = 1.5625 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol/m}^3$$ **Step 3:** Each mole of H₂SO₄ produces 2 moles of H⁺ $$[H^+] = 2 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-6} = 3.125 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol/L}$$ **Step 4:** pH calculation $$pH = -\log[H^+] = -\log(3.125 \times 10^{-6})$$ $$pH = 6 - \log(3.125) = 6 - 0.495 = 5.505$$ **Interpretation:** $pH = 5.505 < 5.6$ → This rainwater qualifies as **acid rain**. The acidity is only marginally above the threshold, indicating that typical ambient SO₂ levels produce mildly acidic precipitation. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Source-Receptor Pathway for Hydrocarbons and Sulphur Compounds This block-level architecture flow shows how primary pollutants transform into secondary pollutants in the atmosphere. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Mobile Sources
Vehicle Exhaust] --> B1[Unburnt HCs
CO, NOx] A2[Stationary Sources
Power Plants] --> B2[SO2 from S in coal] A3[Industrial Solvents
Refineries] --> B3[Benzene Toluene Xylene] A4[Biogenic Sources
Oceans and Forests] --> B4[H2S DMS Isoprene] B1 --> C1[Photochemical Smog
Reactions with NOx and Sunlight] B2 --> C2[Atmospheric Oxidation
SO2 to SO3 in water droplets] B3 --> C1 B4 --> C2 C1 --> D1[Secondary Pollutants
Ozone PAN Aldehydes] C2 --> D2[Secondary Pollutants
H2SO4 Sulphate Aerosols] D1 --> E1[Environmental Impacts
Respiratory issues vegetation damage visibility] D2 --> E2[Environmental Impacts
Acid rain corrosion of metals soil acidification] E1 --> F1[Control Measures
Catalytic converters cleaner fuels] E2 --> F2[Control Measures
FGD scrubbers low-S fuel desulphurisation] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Left column:** Source categories - **Middle-left column:** Primary pollutants released - **Middle column:** Atmospheric transformation reactions - **Middle-right column:** Secondary pollutants formed - **Right column:** Final environmental damage - **Far right column:** Remedial engineering measures --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Photochemical Smog Formation Mechanism This schematic details the step-by-step radical chain mechanism that produces photochemical smog from hydrocarbons. ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Sunlight UV
lambda less than 430 nm] --> P1[Photolysis of NO2] P1 --> P2[NO and atomic O] P2 --> P3[O3 formation via M] H1[Hydrocarbons
from vehicle exhaust] --> R1[OH radical attack on RH] R1 --> R2[Alkyl radical R] R2 --> R3[Peroxy radical RO2] R3 --> R4[NO to NO2 conversion] R1 --> R5[Aldehyde formation] R5 --> R6[Acetylperoxy radical] R6 --> R7[PAN formation with NO2] P3 --> F1[Accumulated O3 smog] R7 --> F1 P3 --> E1[Eye irritation and lung damage] R7 --> E2[Plant leaf damage PAN] F1 --> E1 ``` **Engineering takeaway:** Control of photochemical smog requires simultaneous reduction of **NOₓ** AND **hydrocarbons**, because the radical chain mechanism cannot be broken by removing just one precursor. --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Sulphur Dioxide Atmospheric Transformation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Coal or Oil Combustion
containing sulphur impurity] --> B[SO2 Emission
oxidation state plus 4] B --> C{Gas phase or Aqueous phase} C -->|Gas phase slow| D[SO3 via O2 and UV] C -->|Aqueous phase fast 80 percent| E[H2SO3 formation in droplets] E --> F[Sulphite ions SO3 2 minus] F --> G[Sulphate ions SO4 2 minus] G --> H[H2SO4 aerosol] D --> I[SO3 reacts with H2O] I --> J[H2SO4 acid rain] H --> J J --> K[Environmental Damage
Forests Lakes Buildings Statues] ``` > [!NOTE] > **The two parallel pathways (gas-phase and aqueous-phase) is the most important KTU concept here.** The aqueous route is dominant in real atmosphere because water droplets provide the solvent medium, and dissolved oxidants like H₂O₂ and O₃ accelerate the SO₃²⁻ → SO₄²⁻ conversion. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Q1. Define Photochemical Smog. List its major components. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]` **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Photochemical smog is a secondary air pollution phenomenon formed by the photochemical reaction of **nitrogen oxides (NOₓ)** and **volatile organic compounds (VOCs/hydrocarbons)** in the presence of **sunlight** (particularly UV radiation, λ < 430 nm), resulting in a brownish haze that irritates the eyes and damages vegetation. **Major Components (3 marks, list 4–5):** 1. **Ozone (O₃)** — the principal component and indicator 2. **Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN)** — a unique smog marker 3. **Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)** — gives the brown colour 4. **Aldehydes (formaldehyde, acetaldehyde)** — lachrymators 5. **Particulate Matter (PM₂.₅)** — visibility reduction --- ### Q2. Differentiate between Primary and Secondary Sulphur Pollutants with two examples each. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]` **Model Answer:** | Aspect | **Primary Sulphur Pollutants** | **Secondary Sulphur Pollutants** | |---|---|---| | Definition | Emitted directly into the atmosphere | Formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions | | Examples | SO₂ (most common), H₂S, CS₂ | H₂SO₄, SO₃, sulphate aerosols (SO₄²⁻) | | Source | Combustion of fossil fuels, refineries | Atmospheric oxidation of SO₂ | | Significance | Origin/source of acid rain | Actual cause of acid deposition | > [!NOTE] > **Examiners' tip:** Always give at least 2 examples in each category to score full marks. --- ## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks) ### ⭐ Question A: Photochemical Smog — Role of Hydrocarbons [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) Explain with neat sketches the mechanism of photochemical smog formation. Discuss the role of hydrocarbons as precursors. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** Photochemical smog is a mixture of oxidised hydrocarbons, ozone, nitrogen oxides, and other secondary pollutants formed by photolysis in the troposphere. **Step 2 — Mechanism (4 marks):** The mechanism proceeds in three stages: **(i) Initiation:** $NO_2$ absorbs UV light and dissociates $$NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O \cdot$$ **(ii) Ozone formation:** Atomic oxygen combines with molecular oxygen $$O \cdot + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$$ This ozone reacts with NO to re-form NO₂: $$O_3 + NO \rightarrow NO_2 + O_2$$ A photostationary equilibrium is established with no net O₃ accumulation. **(iii) Hydrocarbon intervention (role of HC):** Hydrocarbons react with OH· radicals, producing alkyl and peroxy radicals. These peroxy radicals convert NO to NO₂ without consuming O₃: $$RO_2 \cdot + NO \rightarrow RO \cdot + NO_2$$ This **breaks the photostationary equilibrium**, causing O₃ to accumulate. The acetylperoxy radical also reacts with NO₂ to form PAN. **Step 3 — Role of hydrocarbons (2 marks):** - Hydrocarbons supply the **free radicals** that shift the NO-NO₂-O₃ balance - They are the **carbon source** for organic secondary pollutants (PAN, aldehydes) - The higher the HC/NOₓ ratio, the more severe the smog - Aromatic HCs (benzene, toluene) are particularly reactive and form more PAN **Sketch:** A neat block diagram showing the radical chain (initiation → propagation → termination) with proper labels. --- **(b) Discuss the harmful effects of photochemical smog on human health, vegetation, and materials. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. Effects on Human Health (3 marks):** - **Eye irritation and lacrimation** — caused by formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, and PAN - **Respiratory problems** — ozone causes pulmonary edema, bronchitis, reduced lung function - **Aggravation of asthma** — even at 0.1 ppm O₃, asthmatics show measurable distress - **Headache, chest pain, cough** — typical of smog exposure - **Long-term effects** — chronic bronchitis, emphysema, possible carcinogenic risk from aldehydes **2. Effects on Vegetation (2 marks):** - **PAN** causes silvering/bronzing of leaf surfaces (especially leafy vegetables like spinach, tomato) - **Ozone** damages stomata, reduces photosynthesis - Crop yield losses of 5–15% are common in smog-affected regions - Sensitive species: tobacco, bean, soybean, lettuce **3. Effects on Materials (2 marks):** - **Rubber cracking** — ozone attacks the C=C bonds in natural rubber, causing brittleness - **Paints and dyes fade** — aldehydes react with pigments - **Textile degradation** — strength reduction in cotton and synthetic fibres - **Corrosion of metals** — accelerates rusting (acts synergistically with SO₂) --- ### ⭐ Question B: Sulphur Compounds — Sources, Effects & Acid Rain [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) Describe the major sources of sulphur compounds in the atmosphere. Estimate SO₂ emission from a coal-fired power plant burning 500 tonnes/day of coal containing 2% sulphur. Assume 90% conversion efficiency. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Sources of sulphur compounds (3 marks):** - **Combustion of fossil fuels** — coal (0.5–5% S), petroleum (0.1–1.2% S), natural gas (H₂S) - **Metal smelting** — roasting of sulphide ores (FeS₂, CuS, ZnS, PbS) - **Petroleum refineries** — H₂S from desulphurisation units, Claus plant emissions - **Volcanic eruptions** — natural source, releases SO₂ and H₂S - **Biogenic sources** — dimethyl sulphide (DMS) from oceans, H₂S from anaerobic decomposition - **Industrial processes** — paper/pulp mills (Kraft process), sulphuric acid plants **Step 2 — Calculation (4 marks):** Given: - Coal consumption: $m = 500$ tonnes/day - Sulphur content: $S = 2\%$ - Conversion efficiency: $\eta = 90\%$ Mass of sulphur in coal: $$m_S = \dfrac{2}{100} \times 500 = 10 \text{ tonnes/day}$$ Stoichiometric ratio of SO₂ to S: $$\dfrac{M_{SO_2}}{M_S} = \dfrac{64}{32} = 2$$ Mass of SO₂ emitted (with conversion efficiency): $$m_{SO_2} = 2 \times m_S \times \eta$$ $$m_{SO_2} = 2 \times 10 \times 0.90 = 18 \text{ tonnes/day}$$ Convert to kg/s: $$m_{SO_2} = \dfrac{18 \times 1000}{24 \times 3600} = 0.208 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Answer:** SO₂ emission = **18 tonnes/day ≈ 0.208 kg/s** --- **(b) Explain the chemistry of acid rain formation. Discuss its environmental impacts. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Chemistry of acid rain (4 marks):** **Emission of SO₂** from combustion: $$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$$ **Atmospheric oxidation** (homogeneous and aqueous phase): $$2SO_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2SO_3$$ $$SO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4 \text{ (Sulphuric acid)}$$ $$SO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_3 \xrightarrow{\text{oxidation}} H_2SO_4$$ **For nitrogen oxides (NOₓ) contribution:** $$2NO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow HNO_3 + HNO_2 \text{ (Nitric acid)}$$ **Precipitation:** Both H₂SO₄ and HNO₃ dissolve in rainwater, lowering pH below 5.6. A typical acid rain in industrial regions has pH 4.0–4.5. **Step 2 — Environmental impacts (3 marks):** - **Aquatic ecosystems:** Lowering of lake pH kills fish (especially at pH < 4.5); disrupts food chains - **Soil:** Leaches essential nutrients (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, K⁺); mobilises toxic Al³⁺ ions - **Vegetation:** Damages leaf cuticle, reduces growth; forest decline (e.g., Black Forest in Germany) - **Materials:** Corrosion of marble monuments (Taj Mahal, Parthenon), metals, concrete - **Human health:** Acid aerosols cause respiratory ailments; drinking water contamination > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse** H₂SO₃ (sulphurous acid, +4) with H₂SO₄ (sulphuric acid, +6). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this error. > 2. **In SO₂ calculation problems**, forgetting to multiply by the conversion efficiency is the #1 mistake. Always check whether the question specifies complete or partial conversion. > 3. **For pH calculations**, the threshold for acid rain is **pH < 5.6**, NOT pH = 7. Many students write 7 and lose marks. > 4. **In photochemical smog questions**, do not forget to mention the **role of UV light** and the **radical chain mechanism**. A vague "smog forms due to cars" answer will score poorly. > 5. **PAN vs. Ozone:** PAN and O₃ are both secondary pollutants but are formed by different pathways. Examiners test this distinction. > 6. **Drawing diagrams** is mandatory in 7-mark questions. A missing sketch can cost 1–2 marks. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (10-second KTU last-minute review)** - ✅ **Hydrocarbons** = organic compounds of C and H; emitted from **incomplete combustion**; participate in **photochemical smog**. - ✅ **Methane (CH₄)** is **excluded** from typical HC air pollution studies due to its low reactivity. - ✅ **Alkenes (ethylene, propylene)** are the **most reactive** hydrocarbons in smog formation. - ✅ **BTX (Benzene, Toluene, Xylene)** are **carcinogenic** aromatic hydrocarbons. - ✅ **PAN (CH₃COOONO₂, M = 121 g/mol)** is the **signature compound of photochemical smog**. - ✅ **Photochemical smog requires:** NOₓ + Hydrocarbons + Sunlight (λ < 430 nm). - ✅ **Ozone (O₃)** at ground level is a **secondary pollutant** — it is NOT emitted directly in significant amounts. - ✅ **SO₂ is the most critical sulphur pollutant** — emitted from coal/diesel combustion. - ✅ **SO₂/S mass ratio = 64/32 = 2** (use this for emission calculations). - ✅ **H₂SO₄ is the end product** of sulphur oxidation in atmosphere — the main acid in acid rain. - ✅ **Acid rain criterion: pH < 5.6** (natural rain pH = 5.6 due to dissolved CO₂). - ✅ **Aqueous-phase oxidation** of SO₂ is **dominant** over gas-phase in real atmosphere. - ✅ **Taj Mahal marble corrosion** is a classic Indian case study of SO₂-induced damage (sulphation of CaCO₃ → CaSO₄·2H₂O, i.e., gypsum). - ✅ **NAAQS limits (India):** SO₂ = 80 μg/m³ (24-hr); Benzene = 5 μg/m³ (annual). - ✅ **Control technologies:** Catalytic converter (HC), Wet FGD with limestone slurry (SO₂), activated carbon (VOCs).

topics.title: Compounds of Nitrogen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compounds of Nitrogen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Nitrogen Compounds in the Atmosphere — Formal Definition

In the context of **Air Pollution Control Engineering**, the term **"Compounds of Nitrogen"** refers collectively to the family of nitrogen-based gases and aerosols that are emitted into the troposphere and stratosphere through natural and anthropogenic (man-made) processes, and which adversely affect human health, ecosystem balance, climate, and material integrity. The major compounds of air pollution interest are:

| Compound | Chemical Formula | Oxidation State of N | Primary Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nitric Oxide | NO | +2 | Primary emitted NOx, smog precursor |
| Nitrogen Dioxide | $\text{NO}_2$ | +4 | Toxic gas, smog & acid rain precursor |
| Nitrous Oxide | $\text{N}_2\text{O}$ | +1 | Greenhouse gas, stratospheric O₃ depleter |
| Ammonia | $\text{NH}_3$ | −3 | Secondary aerosol (PM2.5) precursor |
| Nitric Acid | $\text{HNO}_3$ | +5 | Acid rain component |
| Peroxyacetyl Nitrate | $\text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OONO}_2$ (PAN) | — | Powerful photochemical oxidant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT524 – Module 1):**
> The collective term **NOx** = NO + NO₂ is used universally by regulatory agencies like the **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board, India)** and **US-EPA**. Every student must remember that **NO is the primary emitted form**, while **NO₂ is the secondary, more toxic form** formed in the atmosphere.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Think of the atmosphere as a giant **"open kitchen"** with a stove (the sun) constantly cooking the gases that humans and nature release.

- **The Stove Burner (Combustion Engines & Power Plants):** When you burn fuel at very high temperatures (above **1300 °C / 1573 K**), the normally inert nitrogen in the air (N₂, which makes up ~78% of air) "cracks open" and reacts with oxygen, just like how a sealed egg breaks when you put it in boiling water. This creates **NO**, the "raw, unstable" nitrogen oxide.
- **The Sun's UV Rays (Photochemistry):** Once NO escapes into the air, ultraviolet sunlight "stirs the pot" and converts NO into the more toxic **NO₂**, which is the brownish gas you see hovering over heavily polluted cities.
- **The Rain Cloud Effect:** NO₂ further reacts with water vapor in clouds to form **nitric acid (HNO₃)** — this is what becomes **acid rain**, slowly dissolving marble monuments and killing fish in lakes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Nitrogen compounds in the atmosphere start as harmless N₂, become toxic NO/NO₂ due to high-temperature combustion, and end up as acid rain (HNO₃) or greenhouse gases (N₂O), creating a chain reaction of pollution.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Regulatory Limits

| Pollutant | NAAQS (India, 2009) — 24-hr | NAAQS — Annual | WHO 2021 Guideline |
|---|---|---|---|
| NO₂ | **80 µg/m³** | **40 µg/m³** | 25 µg/m³ (24-hr) |
| NH₃ | **400 µg/m³** (24-hr) | **100 µg/m³** | — |
| N₂O | Not specified in NAAQS | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - Molar mass of NO₂ = **46.0055 g/mol**
> - Molar mass of NO = **30.006 g/mol**
> - Conversion factor (NO₂): 1 ppb ≈ **1.88 µg/m³** at **25 °C, 1 atm**
> - 1 ppm NO₂ ≈ **1880 µg/m³**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Nitrogen Compounds in Air Pollution

Nitrogen compounds relevant to air pollution are classified based on their origin:

### A. Nitrogen Oxides (NOx)
The collective term NOx encompasses all oxides of nitrogen, but the two most important from an air pollution standpoint are:
- **NO (Nitric Oxide)** — colourless, odourless gas; relatively less toxic; primary pollutant.
- **NO₂ (Nitrogen Dioxide)** — reddish-brown gas with pungent odour; highly toxic; secondary pollutant.

### B. Other Nitrogen Compounds
- **N₂O (Nitrous Oxide / Laughing Gas)** — long-lived greenhouse gas (GWP ≈ **273** over 100 years).
- **NH₃ (Ammonia)** — alkaline gas; major contributor to secondary inorganic PM2.5.
- **HNO₃ (Nitric Acid)** — strong acid; primary driver of acid deposition.
- **PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate)** — secondary organic nitrate; eye irritant, phytotoxic.

## 2.2 Sources of Nitrogen Compounds

### Natural Sources
- **Biological fixation** by soil bacteria and legumes (≈ 90–130 Tg N/yr globally).
- **Lightning** (high-temperature discharge) — produces NOx naturally.
- **Forest fires** and **volcanic emissions**.
- **Stratospheric photolysis** of N₂O.

### Anthropogenic Sources
- **Stationary combustion** (thermal power plants, industrial boilers).
- **Mobile sources** (motor vehicles — the dominant urban source).
- **Industrial processes** (nitric acid manufacture, fertilizer production).
- **Biomass burning** (agricultural residue burning, especially relevant in northern India).
- **Agricultural NH₃ emissions** from livestock waste and urea fertilizer.

## 2.3 Formation Mechanisms of NOx

### 1. Thermal NOx (Zeldovich Mechanism)
At very high combustion temperatures (> 1300 °C), atmospheric N₂ and O₂ dissociate and recombine:

$$
\text{N}_2 + \text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{N}
$$
$$
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{O}
$$

This is the **dominant mechanism** in boilers, gas turbines, and internal combustion engines.

### 2. Fuel NOx
Nitrogen chemically bound in fuel (e.g., in coal, heavy oil) is oxidized during combustion. Coal typically contains **0.5–2% N by mass**, and 20–80% of this fuel-N converts to NOx.

### 3. Prompt NOx (Fenimore Mechanism)
Hydrocarbon radicals (CH, CH₂) react with molecular N₂ in the flame front, producing HCN and NH species that later oxidize to NO. Significant only in fuel-rich flames.

### 4. Photochemical NOx Formation
Once NO is released, atmospheric photochemistry converts it to NO₂:

$$
\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \quad (\lambda < 420 \text{ nm})
$$
$$
\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}
$$
$$
\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The NO–NO₂–O₃ triad establishes a **photostationary state** during daytime, where the ratio of NO₂ to NO is governed by ozone concentration and UV intensity.

## 2.4 Atmospheric Sinks and Fate

| Sinks | Reaction/Process | Product |
|---|---|---|
| Dry deposition of NO₂ | Surface absorption | Nitrate (NO₃⁻) |
| Wet deposition | $\text{2NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HNO}_3 + \text{HNO}_2$ | Acid rain |
| Reaction with OH• | $\text{NO}_2 + \text{OH} \cdot \rightarrow \text{HNO}_3$ | Nitric acid vapour |
| Stratospheric destruction | $\text{N}_2\text{O} + \text{O}(^1D) \rightarrow \text{2NO}$ | Catalytic O₃ destruction |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppb} \times \dfrac{M}{24.45}$ | ppb → µg/m³ conversion at 25 °C, 1 atm | µg/m³ |
| 2 | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{C_{ppb}}{1000}$ | ppb → ppm conversion | ppm |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{NOx}} = \dfrac{[\text{NOx}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{NOx}]_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | Removal efficiency of control device | % |
| 4 | $E_{\text{NOx}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{NOx}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} \times 10^6$ | Emission factor | g/kg fuel or g/GJ |
| 5 | $[\text{NO}]_{eq} = K_P \cdot [\text{N}_2][\text{O}_2]^{1/2}$ | Extended Zeldovich equilibrium | mol/m³ |
| 6 | $\text{GWP}_{100} = \dfrac{\int_0^{100} RF_x \, dt}{\int_0^{100} RF_{\text{CO}_2} \, dt}$ | Global Warming Potential (N₂O = 273) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Acidity (as CaCO}_3) = \dfrac{V_{\text{HNO}_3} \times N \times 50 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | Acid rain analysis | mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| 8 | $\text{PV} = nRT$ | Ideal gas law for concentration calculations | atm, L, mol, K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are essential in **(a)** stack monitoring reports, **(b)** Environmental Impact Assessments (EIA), **(c)** design of De-NOx systems (SCR, SNCR), and **(d)** estimation of acid deposition fluxes to terrestrial ecosystems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between ppm/ ppb and µg/m³

This is the **most frequently asked numerical question** in KTU examinations. Let us derive it rigorously.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Start with the Ideal Gas Equation.**

$$
PV = nRT
$$

where:
- $P$ = pressure (atm)
- $V$ = volume (m³)
- $n$ = number of moles
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **0.082057 L·atm/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Step 2 — Express the number of moles.**

$$
n = \dfrac{m}{M}
$$

where $m$ = mass of gas (g) and $M$ = molar mass (g/mol).

**Step 3 — Substituting and solving for mass per unit volume (concentration in µg/m³).**

$$
\dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{PM}{RT}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the mixing ratio definition.**

1 ppm (by volume) = 1 part of pollutant gas per 10⁶ parts of air by volume.
At standard conditions (1 atm, 25 °C = 298.15 K), the molar volume of an ideal gas is:

$$
V_m = \dfrac{RT}{P} = 24.45 \text{ L/mol}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the conversion.**

1 ppm of gas = (1 mol of gas) / (10⁶ mol of air) → equivalent to **1/24.45 mol/L of gas per 10⁶ mol of air per L**.

Multiplying by molar mass $M$ to get mass concentration:

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{M \times 10^6}{V_m} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{M}{24.45} \times 1000
$$

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppb} \times \dfrac{M}{24.45}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Compact Formula:**
> $$C \left( \frac{\mu g}{m^3} \right) = C(ppb) \times \dfrac{M}{24.45}$$
> This is the standard form the KTU examiner expects you to write in numerical answers.

## 3.2 Worked Example: ppb to µg/m³ Conversion for NO₂

**Problem:** The ambient air NO₂ concentration is measured as **150 ppb** at 25 °C and 1 atm. Convert this to µg/m³.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $C_{ppb}$ = 150
- $M_{NO_2}$ = **46.0 g/mol**
- $V_m$ = 24.45 L/mol

Applying the formula:

$$
C \left( \frac{\mu g}{m^3} \right) = 150 \times \dfrac{46.0}{24.45}
$$

$$
= 150 \times 1.8814
$$

$$
= 282.21 \; \mu g/m^3
$$

**Conclusion:** 150 ppb of NO₂ corresponds to **282.2 µg/m³**, which **exceeds the Indian NAAQS 24-hr standard of 80 µg/m³** by a factor of 3.5.

## 3.3 Derivation: Extended Zeldovich Equilibrium NO Concentration

For thermal NOx in high-temperature combustion, the equilibrium [NO] is:

$$
[\text{NO}]_{eq} = K_P(T) \cdot \sqrt{[\text{N}_2]_{eq} \cdot [\text{O}_2]_{eq}}
$$

**Step 1 — Write the overall Zeldovich reaction:**

$$
\tfrac{1}{2}\text{N}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{NO}, \quad \Delta H = +90.4 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 2 — The equilibrium constant (function of temperature):**

$$
K_P(T) = 4.0 \times \exp\left(-\dfrac{21650}{T}\right)
$$

This is the empirical Zeldovich expression valid in the temperature range 1500–3000 K.

**Step 3 — Sample calculation at 2000 K (≈ 1727 °C):**

$$
K_P(2000) = 4.0 \times \exp\left(-\dfrac{21650}{2000}\right)
$$

$$
= 4.0 \times \exp(-10.825)
$$

$$
= 4.0 \times 1.974 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
= 7.896 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Assuming air composition with $X_{N_2} = 0.79$ and $X_{O_2} = 0.21$:

$$
[\text{NO}]_{eq} = 7.896 \times 10^{-5} \times \sqrt{0.79 \times 0.21}
$$

$$
= 7.896 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.4074
$$

$$
\approx 3.22 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{(mole fraction)}
$$

**Conversion to ppm:**

$$
[\text{NO}]_{eq} \approx 32.2 \; \text{ppm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The equilibrium NO concentration doubles for every ~200 K rise in flame temperature above 2000 K. This is why **low-NOx burner design** focuses on reducing peak flame temperature (e.g., air-staging, flue gas recirculation).

## 3.4 Worked Example: Removal Efficiency Calculation for SCR

**Problem:** A Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) unit receives flue gas with **NOx = 450 mg/Nm³** at the inlet and **NOx = 90 mg/Nm³** at the outlet. Calculate the removal efficiency.

**Solution:**

Given:
- Inlet NOx = 450 mg/Nm³
- Outlet NOx = 90 mg/Nm³

Applying the formula:

$$
\eta_{\text{NOx}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{NOx}} = \dfrac{450 - 90}{450} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= \dfrac{360}{450} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= 80\%
$$

**Result:** The SCR unit achieves **80% De-NOx efficiency**, which satisfies the typical regulatory benchmark of ≥ 80% for coal-fired power plants.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Atmospheric Nitrogen Cycle & Pollution Pathways

The following block diagram shows the complete atmospheric fate of nitrogen compounds — from emission sources through transformations to eventual sinks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Anthropogenic Combustion] --> B1[Thermal NOx Formation]
    A2[Vehicle Exhaust] --> B1
    A3[Industrial Processes] --> B1
    A4[Lightning and Forest Fires] --> B1
    A5[Agricultural NH3 Emission] --> B2[Direct NH3 Release]
    A6[Soil Microbial Activity] --> B3[N2O Emission]

    B1 --> C1[NO Primary Pollutant]
    C1 --> C2[Ozone Oxidation in Air]
    C2 --> C3[NO2 Secondary Pollutant]
    C3 --> C4[UV Photolysis]
    C4 --> C5[O Radical and O3 Formation]
    C5 --> C6[Photochemical Smog]
    C3 --> C7[Reaction with Water Vapour]
    C7 --> C8[Nitric Acid HNO3]
    C8 --> C9[Acid Rain Deposition]
    B2 --> C10[Secondary Inorganic Aerosol]
    C10 --> C11[PM2.5 Formation]
    B3 --> C12[Stratospheric Transport]
    C12 --> C13[Ozone Layer Depletion]
    C12 --> C14[Greenhouse Warming N2O]

    C6 --> D1[Human Health Impact]
    C6 --> D2[Vegetable Crop Damage]
    C9 --> D3[Aquatic Ecosystem Acidification]
    C9 --> D4[Building Material Corrosion]
    C11 --> D5[Visibility Impairment]
    C11 --> D6[Respiratory Diseases]
    C13 --> D7[UV Radiation Increase]
    C14 --> D8[Climate Change]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Source nodes (A1–A6) → Transformation nodes (B1–B3, C1–C14) → Impact nodes (D1–D8). This is the standard layout KTU examiners expect in 7-mark descriptive answers.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: SCR Control System for NOx

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Boiler Flue Gas Inlet] --> PR1[Particulate Removal ESP]
    PR1 --> PR2[Heat Exchanger and Gas Cooling]
    PR2 --> SC[SCR Reactor with Catalyst]
    AM[Anhydrous Ammonia Storage] --> INJ[Ammonia Injection Grid]
    INJ --> SC
    SC --> OX[Oxidation Catalyst Section]
    OX --> STK[Stack Emission to Atmosphere]
    SC -.-> BYP[Bypass Damper]
    BYP -.-> STK
    SC --> MON[Continuous Emission Monitoring System CEMS]
    MON --> CTRL[Feedback Controller]
    CTRL --> INJ
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** In a typical SCR unit, the operating temperature window is **300–400 °C**, the space velocity is **2,500–10,000 hr⁻¹**, and ammonia slip is maintained below **5 ppm** to avoid downstream fouling.

## 4.3 Photochemical Smog Formation (NO–NO₂–O₃ Triad)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUN[Sunlight UV less than 420 nm] --> PHOTO
    NO2[NO2 Molecule] --> PHOTO[Photolysis]
    PHOTO --> NO[NO Regenerated]
    PHOTO --> ORAD[Atomic Oxygen O]
    ORAD --> O3[Ozone O3 Formation]
    O3 --> RXN
    NO --> RXN[NO plus O3 Reaction]
    RXN --> NO2B[NO2 Regenerated]
    NO2B --> VOCs[Reaction with VOCs]
    VOCs --> OZONEOX[Ozone Accumulation]
    OZONEOX --> PAN[PAN Formation]
    OZONEOX --> SMOG[Photochemical Smog]
    PAN --> SMOG
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define NOx. List the major natural and anthropogenic sources of NOx in the atmosphere. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

NOx refers to the family of nitrogen oxides relevant to air pollution, primarily **nitric oxide (NO)** and **nitrogen dioxide (NO₂)**, although the term technically includes N₂O, NO, NO₂, N₃O₄, N₂O₅, etc.

**Natural Sources:**
- Lightning discharge (high-temperature atmospheric fixation of N₂).
- Biological nitrogen fixation by soil bacteria and ocean plankton.
- Forest fires and volcanic eruptions.

**Anthropogenic Sources:**
- Stationary combustion (coal-fired thermal power plants, industrial boilers).
- Mobile sources (petrol and diesel vehicle exhaust — the dominant urban source).
- Industrial processes (nitric acid manufacture, welding, explosives production).
- Agricultural residue burning.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to distinguish between **NO (primary)** and **NO₂ (secondary)**. Always mention that NO is the dominant species in fresh flue gas (≈ 90–95%), while NO₂ forms later in the atmosphere through oxidation. Losing this distinction costs you ½ to 1 mark.

### Question 2: Differentiate between N₂O and NO₂ in terms of their environmental impact. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | N₂O (Nitrous Oxide) | NO₂ (Nitrogen Dioxide) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Mainly natural (soil, ocean) + agricultural | Mainly anthropogenic (combustion) |
| Atmospheric lifetime | **114 years** | Hours to a few days |
| Primary environmental impact | **Greenhouse gas** (GWP = 273); stratospheric O₃ depletion | **Toxic air pollutant**; smog and acid rain precursor |
| Colour | Colourless | Reddish-brown |
| Health effect | Anaesthetic (laughing gas) | Respiratory irritant, bronchitis |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Discuss in detail the sources, formation mechanisms, harmful effects, and control measures of nitrogen oxides (NOx) in the atmosphere. [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### (a) Sources and Formation Mechanisms of NOx [7 Marks]

**Sources:**

**1. Natural Sources:**
- **Biological fixation:** Soil and marine microorganisms convert atmospheric N₂ to organic nitrogen. Lightning strikes produce high-temperature arcs that dissociate N₂ and O₂ to form NO.
- **Forest fires** release NOx from biomass combustion.

**2. Anthropogenic Sources:**
- **Mobile sources:** Petrol and diesel vehicles emit ~80% of urban NOx. High in-cylinder temperatures (1500–2000 °C) drive the Zeldovich mechanism.
- **Stationary sources:** Coal-fired power plants (coal contains 0.5–2% fuel-N), industrial boilers, and gas turbines.
- **Industrial processes:** Nitric acid manufacturing (Ostwald process), fertilizer production, explosives.

**Formation Mechanisms:**

**(i) Thermal NOx (Zeldovich Mechanism):**
At flame temperatures above 1300 °C:

$$
\text{N}_2 + \text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{N}
$$
$$
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{O}
$$

**(ii) Fuel NOx:**
Nitrogen bound in fuel is released as HCN and NH₃ intermediates, which are oxidized to NO. This mechanism dominates in coal combustion.

**(iii) Prompt NOx (Fenimore Mechanism):**
Hydrocarbon radicals (CH, CH₂) react with N₂ in the flame front to form HCN → NO. Important only in fuel-rich, low-NOx flames.

**(iv) Photochemical Formation:**
In ambient air, NO is oxidized to NO₂ by ozone:

$$
\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2
$$

#### (b) Harmful Effects and Control Measures of NOx [7 Marks]

**Harmful Effects:**

**1. Human Health:**
- NO₂ causes respiratory irritation, bronchitis, reduced lung function, and increased susceptibility to viral infections.
- Forms **methemoglobin** in blood, reducing oxygen-carrying capacity.
- WHO classifies diesel exhaust (rich in NO₂) as a **Group 1 carcinogen**.

**2. Environmental Effects:**
- **Acid rain:** $\text{2NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HNO}_3 + \text{HNO}_2$ damages aquatic ecosystems, corrodes marble (Taj Mahal), and leaches soil nutrients.
- **Photochemical smog:** NOx + VOC + sunlight → ozone and PAN, causing crop damage and visibility reduction.
- **Eutrophication:** Nitrogen deposition causes algal blooms in water bodies.
- **Stratospheric ozone depletion:** N₂O transported to stratosphere → NO catalytically destroys O₃.
- **Global warming:** N₂O has GWP of 273 over 100 years.

**Control Measures:**

**1. Combustion Modifications:**
- **Low-NOx burners** (air staging, fuel staging) — reduces peak flame temperature.
- **Flue Gas Recirculation (FGR)** — dilutes O₂ and lowers temperature.
- **Water/steam injection** — reduces adiabatic flame temperature.

**2. Flue Gas Treatment:**
- **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR):** NH₃ + NO + ½O₂ → N₂ + 2H₂O at 300–400 °C over V₂O₅/TiO₂ catalyst. Efficiency: 80–95%.
- **Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction (SNCR):** Urea or NH₃ injected at 800–1100 °C without catalyst. Efficiency: 30–70%.
- **Wet scrubbing** (limited use; produces wastewater).

**3. Vehicular Control:**
- Three-way catalytic converters (TWC) in petrol cars.
- Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) in diesel engines.
- Euro VI / BS-VI emission norms in India (NOx limit: 80 mg/km for diesel cars).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting the difference between SCR and SNCR** (catalyst requirement, temperature window, efficiency range) — KTU examiners specifically look for this in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Writing "NOx causes global warming" without specifying N₂O** — that is, mixing up NO₂ (not a major GHG) with N₂O (a major GHG with GWP 273).
> 3. **Skipping numerical examples** (e.g., efficiency calculation, ppb–µg/m³ conversion) — at least one numerical is expected in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Not mentioning Indian NAAQS standards** (80 µg/m³ for 24-hr NO₂) — citing only WHO/EPA standards is incomplete for KTU.
> 5. **Failing to draw a labelled diagram** of SCR/SNCR — diagrams carry 2–3 marks and skipping them is a costly mistake.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): Explain the chemistry of photochemical smog formation. Discuss the role of nitrogen oxides, ozone, and PAN in smog episodes. [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – May 2022]**

#### (a) Initiation and NO–NO₂–O₃ Chemistry [7 Marks]

**Definition:**
Photochemical smog is a complex mixture of **oxidants** (O₃, PAN, H₂O₂, aldehydes), **NOx**, **VOCs**, and **fine particulates** that forms in urban and industrial atmospheres under the influence of **sunlight**, particularly UV radiation of wavelength **< 430 nm**. It was first characterized in **Los Angeles in the 1940s** and is therefore sometimes called "Los Angeles-type smog" (as opposed to the London-type reducing smog).

**Step 1 — Photolysis of NO₂ (Initiation Step):**

The reaction chain begins when NO₂ absorbs a UV photon and dissociates:

$$
\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \quad (\lambda < 420 \text{ nm})
$$

**Step 2 — Ozone Formation:**

The atomic oxygen produced combines with molecular oxygen in a three-body reaction (M is N₂ or O₂ acting as a third body to absorb excess energy):

$$
\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}
$$

**Step 3 — NO Regeneration (Ozone Titration):**

Ozone reacts with NO to regenerate NO₂, which would seem to terminate the chain. This is called **ozone titration**:

$$
\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2
$$

**Step 4 — Photostationary State:**

In clean air with only NO, NO₂, and O₃, the above three reactions establish a **photostationary state** where the ratio of [NO₂]/[NO] is determined by:

$$
\dfrac{[\text{NO}_2]}{[\text{NO}]} = \dfrac{k_3 \cdot [\text{O}_3]}{j_{\text{NO}_2}}
$$

where $j_{\text{NO}_2}$ is the NO₂ photolysis rate constant (depends on solar intensity) and $k_3$ is the rate constant of the NO + O₃ reaction.

#### (b) Role of VOCs, PAN, and Smog Episode Dynamics [7 Marks]

**Step 5 — Involvement of VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds):**

When VOCs (RH) are present, they provide **alternative pathways to oxidize NO to NO₂ without consuming O₃**. This breaks the photostationary state and allows **O₃ to accumulate**.

A typical OH•-initiated oxidation of an alkane (e.g., isobutane) is:

$$
\text{OH} \cdot + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{R} \cdot + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$
$$
\text{R} \cdot + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{RO}_2 \cdot
$$
$$
\text{RO}_2 \cdot + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{RO} \cdot + \text{NO}_2
$$
$$
\text{RO} \cdot + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{R'CHO} + \text{HO}_2 \cdot
$$
$$
\text{HO}_2 \cdot + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{OH} \cdot + \text{NO}_2
$$

**Net effect:** Each OH• radical converts **two NO molecules to NO₂** while being regenerated, propagating the chain. The aldehydes (R'CHO) produced are themselves photolyzed to produce more radicals and PAN precursors.

**Step 6 — PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate) Formation:**

PAN is a secondary pollutant formed from the reaction of the peroxyacetyl radical (CH₃C(O)OO•) with NO₂:

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OO} \cdot + \text{NO}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OONO}_2 \; (\text{PAN})
$$

PAN is a powerful **lachrymator** (eye irritant), **phytotoxin** (damages plants even at ppb levels), and a marker of photochemical smog. It is thermally unstable above ~5 °C but can persist in cold upper troposphere and be transported over long distances.

**Step 7 — Smog Episode Dynamics:**

A typical urban smog episode unfolds as follows:
- **Morning rush hour (6–9 AM):** Vehicle emissions release NO and VOCs. NO dominates and O₃ remains low due to titration.
- **Mid-morning (9–11 AM):** Sunlight intensity rises; NO₂ photolysis begins; OH• radicals form; chain oxidation of VOCs accelerates.
- **Midday to afternoon (12–4 PM):** O₃, PAN, and aldehyde concentrations peak. This is when the **O₃ concentration can exceed 200–400 µg/m³**, far above the NAAQS 8-hr limit of 100 µg/m³.
- **Evening (after sunset):** Photolysis stops; NO₃• radical chemistry takes over; O₃ levels drop due to dry deposition and titration by NO from ongoing traffic.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Photochemical Smog Questions:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse photochemical smog with reducing (London) smog.** Photochemical = oxidizing, warm climate, NOx + VOCs, O₃; reducing = SO₂ + particulates, cold climate, winter.
> 2. **You must write the full reaction sequence (Steps 1–3 minimum)** — skipping the photolysis equation of NO₂ is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 3. **PAN formation reaction is mandatory** — many students forget the CH₃C(O)OO• intermediate.
> 4. **Mentioning the time evolution of O₃ during a day** shows the examiner that you understand the system dynamically — this is a high-impact addition that can earn full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 Rapid Revision — Nitrogen Compounds Module 1 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

### Core Concepts
- **NOx family = NO + NO₂** (the two most important species); N₂O, NH₃, HNO₃, PAN are also covered.
- **NO is primary (combustion-emitted)**; **NO₂ is secondary (atmospherically formed)**.
- **NO₂ is reddish-brown**; **NO is colourless**.
- NAAQS (India): NO₂ — 80 µg/m³ (24-hr), 40 µg/m³ (annual).

### Key Mechanisms
- **Thermal NOx** (Zeldovich) → dominant in high-temperature combustion (> 1300 °C).
- **Fuel NOx** → from nitrogen bound in fuel (e.g., coal).
- **Prompt NOx** (Fenimore) → hydrocarbon radical attack on N₂.
- **Photochemical formation** → NO + O₃ → NO₂ (in atmosphere).

### Atmospheric Chemistry Essentials
- **NO₂ + hv → NO + O** (λ < 420 nm) — this is the **initiation** step of smog.
- **O + O₂ + M → O₃ + M** — ozone formation.
- **NO + O₃ → NO₂ + O₂** — ozone titration.
- **2NO₂ + H₂O → HNO₃ + HNO₂** — acid rain formation.

### Harmful Effects (MUST KNOW)
- Respiratory illness (NO₂).
- Acid rain (HNO₃).
- Photochemical smog (O₃ + PAN).
- Eutrophication (NH₃ and NO₃⁻).
- Stratospheric O₃ depletion (N₂O → NO).
- Global warming (N₂O, GWP = 273).

### Control Technologies
- **SCR:** Catalyst (V₂O₅/TiO₂), 300–400 °C, NH₃ reductant, 80–95% efficiency.
- **SNCR:** No catalyst, 800–1100 °C, urea/ammonia, 30–70% efficiency.
- **Low-NOx burners, FGR, EGR** (combustion modification).
- **Three-way catalytic converter** (vehicular).
- **BS-VI / Euro VI** emission norms in India.

### Numerical Conversions to Memorize
- **1 ppb NO₂ ≈ 1.88 µg/m³** at 25 °C, 1 atm.
- **1 ppm NO₂ ≈ 1880 µg/m³**.
- Conversion formula: $C(\mu g/m^3) = C(ppb) \times M / 24.45$.
- Removal efficiency: $\eta = (C_{in} - C_{out}) / C_{in} \times 100\%$.

### Common Examiner Traps
1. Mixing up NO₂ (not a significant GHG) with N₂O (GWP 273).
2. Forgetting the photolysis of NO₂ in smog chemistry.
3. Confusing SCR with SNCR (catalyst + temperature).
4. Skipping the photostationary state explanation.
5. Not writing the Zeldovich equations in thermal NOx questions.
6. Forgetting to mention NAAQS standards (Indian context).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU PEPOT524 Aspirants:** Almost every KTU question on this topic either asks about **(i) NOx formation mechanisms**, **(ii) photochemical smog chemistry**, or **(iii) De-NOx control technologies**. Master the three reaction equations of the NO–NO₂–O₃ triad and you will secure 60–70% of the marks on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Oxidants in Air Pollution

In the context of **Atmospheric Chemistry and Air Pollution Control (KTU PEPOT524)**, **oxidants** are defined as a class of air pollutants that possess a high affinity for electrons and are capable of accepting electrons from other chemical species, thereby oxidizing them. They are predominantly **secondary air pollutants**, meaning they are not emitted directly from a source but are formed in the troposphere through a complex series of photochemical reactions involving precursor gases — primarily **Nitrogen Oxides (NOx)** and **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** — in the presence of **sunlight (solar radiation)**.

The most significant atmospheric oxidants, in order of atmospheric abundance and reactivity, are:

| Rank | Oxidant | Chemical Formula | Nature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Ozone | $\text{O}_3$ | Secondary, dominant |
| 2 | Nitrogen Dioxide | $\text{NO}_2$ | Primary + Secondary |
| 3 | Peroxyacetyl Nitrate | $\text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OONO}_2$ (PAN) | Secondary |
| 4 | Hydrogen Peroxide | $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ | Secondary |
| 5 | Atomic Oxygen | $\text{O}(^3\text{P})$ and $\text{O}(^1\text{D})$ | Transient radical |
| 6 | Hydroxyl Radical | $\text{OH}^\bullet$ | Daytime dominant |
| 7 | Nitrate Radical | $\text{NO}_3^\bullet$ | Nighttime dominant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The course code **PEPOT524** places primary emphasis on **Ozone ($\text{O}_3$)**, **Nitrogen Dioxide ($\text{NO}_2$)**, and **PAN**, as these constitute the "photochemical oxidant triad" responsible for photochemical smog.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Atmospheric Oven"

Imagine your city as a giant, slow-cooking oven. The **fire** at the bottom is the sun's ultraviolet (UV) radiation. The **raw ingredients** thrown into this oven are the exhaust fumes from cars (NOx) and vapors from gasoline stations and paints (VOCs). As the sun cooks these ingredients, they don't just remain as they were — they transform through a slow "baking" process into highly reactive, corrosive gases (oxidants). 

These oxidants, much like the heat from an oven, attack everything they touch: the **paint on your car** fades, the **rubber in your tires** cracks, the **leaves of roadside plants** develop bronze spots, and your **lungs** feel a burning sensation. The "oven" is essentially what we call **photochemical smog**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Metrics to Memorize for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Standard Photochemical Dissociation Threshold:** $\lambda < 430 \text{ nm}$ (for $\text{NO}_2$ photolysis)
> - **Tropospheric Ozone Background Concentration:** $20 - 40 \text{ ppb}$ (clean air)
> - **WHO/EPA 8-hour Ozone Standard:** $\mathbf{70 \text{ ppb}}$ ($\approx 137 \text{ }\mu\text{g/m}^3$ at $25^\circ\text{C, 1 atm}$)
> - **PAN Smog Threshold:** $>\mathbf{5 \text{ ppb}}$ causes vegetation damage
> - **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$

### 1.4 The Photochemical Smog — A Historical Perspective

The term **smog** is a portmanteau of "**smoke**" and "**fog**." There are two fundamentally distinct types:

1. **London-type (Reducing/Classic) Smog:** Caused by $\text{SO}_2$ and particulate matter from coal combustion, predominant in cold, humid conditions (winter).
2. **Los Angeles-type (Photochemical/Oxidizing) Smog:** Caused by oxidants (ozone, PAN) formed by photolysis reactions of NOx + VOCs, predominant in warm, sunny, dry conditions (summer). 

This module focuses on the **Los Angeles-type smog**, which is the primary domain of oxidant chemistry.
