# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Mechanism of Oxidant Formation

The formation of photochemical oxidants follows a structured, time-resolved sequence. Understanding the sequence is essential for both descriptive and numerical KTU questions.

### **Stage 1: Initiation (Morning – Peak Solar Radiation)**
During the early morning hours, traffic emissions release massive quantities of nitric oxide (NO) and hydrocarbons (HCs). The sun rises, and UV photons (wavelength $\lambda < 430 \text{ nm}$) strike the $\text{NO}_2$ molecules.

The photolysis of $\text{NO}_2$ is the **single most important initiation step** in oxidant chemistry:

$$\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}(^3\text{P})$$

The atomic oxygen produced is in its ground electronic state. It is extremely reactive and immediately combines with the abundant atmospheric oxygen ($\text{O}_2$) in a three-body collision (where M is a third-body molecule — typically $\text{N}_2$ or $\text{O}_2$ — that absorbs excess energy):

$$\text{O}(^3\text{P}) + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a three-body collision?** The formation of the O–O bond releases energy, and a third body M is needed to carry away this excess energy to prevent immediate re-dissociation. Without M, the ozone molecule would not be stable.

### **Stage 2: Accumulation (Midday – Peak Oxidant Concentrations)**
A rapid **null cycle** initially forms and destroys $\text{O}_3$:

$$\text{O}_3 + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2$$

In the absence of VOCs, this cycle reaches a **photo-stationary state** with no net ozone accumulation. However, when VOCs (especially alkenes, aldehydes, and aromatics) are present, they interrupt this null cycle by converting NO back to $\text{NO}_2$ **without** consuming ozone. This is achieved via peroxy radicals ($\text{RO}_2^\bullet$ and $\text{HO}_2^\bullet$).

For example, with a generic hydrocarbon RH:

$$\text{OH}^\bullet + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{R}^\bullet + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{R}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{RO}_2^\bullet$$

$$\text{RO}_2^\bullet + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{RO}^\bullet + \text{NO}_2$$

The newly formed $\text{NO}_2$ is again photolyzed to produce more $\text{O}_3$. This is the **NO-to-$\text{NO}_2$ conversion cycle** that leads to net ozone accumulation.

### **Stage 3: Termination (Late Afternoon/Evening)**
When solar radiation diminishes, photolysis ceases, and $\text{O}_3$ production halts. The remaining $\text{O}_3$ and radicals either deposit onto surfaces, react with NO to reform $\text{NO}_2$, or are scavenged by nitric oxide and $\text{NO}_2$ to form **nitrates**, **PAN**, and **organic acids**.

## 2.2 The Detailed Photochemical Reaction Chain

Let us formalize the complete reaction sequence that the KTU examiner expects you to write for full marks:

**Step A — Primary photolysis (Initiation):**
$$\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \quad (\lambda < 430 \text{ nm})$$

**Step B — Ozone formation:**
$$\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}$$

**Step C — Ozone destruction (Null cycle):**
$$\text{O}_3 + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2$$

**Step D — Hydroxyl radical initiation (from ozone photolysis at $\lambda < 320 \text{ nm}$):**
$$\text{O}_3 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + \text{O}(^1\text{D})$$

$$\text{O}(^1\text{D}) + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{OH}^\bullet$$

**Step E — VOC oxidation and peroxy radical formation:**
$$\text{OH}^\bullet + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{R}^\bullet + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{R}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{RO}_2^\bullet$$

**Step F — NO-to-$\text{NO}_2$ conversion (Net ozone production):**
$$\text{RO}_2^\bullet + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{RO}^\bullet + \text{NO}_2$$

**Step G — PAN Formation (from acetaldehyde oxidation):**
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{OH}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C}^\bullet\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{CH}_3\text{C}^\bullet\text{O} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OO}^\bullet$$

$$\text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OO}^\bullet + \text{NO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OONO}_2 \quad \text{(PAN)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** PAN is a **lacrymator** (tear-inducing agent) and a powerful **phytotoxin** (plant-damaging agent). It is more thermally stable than ozone and can be transported over long distances from urban to rural areas — a phenomenon known as **"PAN transport."**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}$ | Primary photolysis | $\lambda < 430 \text{ nm}$ |
| 2 | $\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}$ | Ozone formation | Three-body reaction |
| 3 | $\text{O}_3 + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2$ | Null cycle (ozone destruction) | Rapid, no net $\text{O}_3$ gain |
| 4 | $\text{RO}_2^\bullet + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{RO}^\bullet + \text{NO}_2$ | Net ozone production | Requires VOCs |
| 5 | $\text{OH}^\bullet + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{R}^\bullet + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Hydrocarbon oxidation | Major VOC sink |
| 6 | $\text{P} = \frac{[\text{NO}_x][\text{VOC}]}{k}$ | Photochemical smog potential (qualitative) | $[\text{NO}_x]$, $[\text{VOC}]$ in ppbC |
| 7 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius rate constant | For thermal reactions |
| 8 | $\tau_{\text{PAN}} = \frac{1}{k_{\text{decay}} \cdot [\text{NO}_2]}$ | PAN lifetime | Strongly T-dependent |

### 2.4 Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of Oxidant Chemistry:**
> 1. **Urban Air Quality Management:** Municipal authorities in Delhi, Los Angeles, and Mexico City use this exact reaction sequence to predict daily $\text{O}_3$ levels via the **Ozone Isopleth Plotting Package (OZIPR)**.
> 2. **Tall Stack Design:** Since PAN has a long atmospheric lifetime in cold upper troposphere, industrial stacks are designed to release pollutants at heights where dilution and dispersion reduce ground-level oxidant concentrations.
> 3. **Catalytic Converters:** Modern three-way catalytic converters in automobiles are engineered to simultaneously reduce NO to $\text{N}_2$ and oxidize VOCs to $\text{CO}_2$, directly cutting the precursors of photochemical oxidants.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Photo-Stationary State (Leighton Ratio)

A classic 14-mark derivation that frequently appears in KTU examinations is the derivation of the **Photo-Stationary State Equation** for the $\text{NO} - \text{NO}_2 - \text{O}_3$ system.

### **Given Conditions:**
- Steady-state solar radiation
- No VOCs (clean $\text{NO}_x$ atmosphere)
- Constant temperature and pressure

### **Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1: Identify the three governing reactions and their rate constants.**

Let $J$ be the photolysis rate constant for $\text{NO}_2$ photolysis, $k_1$ be the rate constant for $\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}$, and $k_2$ be the rate constant for $\text{O}_3 + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2$.

**Step 2: Write the rate of $\text{O}_3$ formation.**

The rate of ozone formation is governed by the rate at which atomic oxygen is produced (which depends on the photolysis of $\text{NO}_2$):

$$\text{Rate of } \text{O}_3 \text{ formation} = k_1 \cdot [\text{O}] \cdot [\text{O}_2] \cdot [\text{M}]$$

But $[\text{O}]$ in steady state is determined by the photolysis rate:

$$[\text{O}]_{ss} = \frac{J \cdot [\text{NO}_2]}{k_1 \cdot [\text{O}_2] \cdot [\text{M}]}$$

**Step 3: Substituting back into the rate equation.**

$$\text{Rate of } \text{O}_3 \text{ formation} = k_1 \cdot \left(\frac{J \cdot [\text{NO}_2]}{k_1 \cdot [\text{O}_2] \cdot [\text{M}]}\right) \cdot [\text{O}_2] \cdot [\text{M}]$$

$$\text{Rate of } \text{O}_3 \text{ formation} = J \cdot [\text{NO}_2]$$

**Step 4: Write the rate of $\text{O}_3$ destruction.**

$$\text{Rate of } \text{O}_3 \text{ destruction} = k_2 \cdot [\text{O}_3] \cdot [\text{NO}]$$

**Step 5: Apply the photo-stationary state assumption (Rate of formation = Rate of destruction).**

$$J \cdot [\text{NO}_2] = k_2 \cdot [\text{O}_3] \cdot [\text{NO}]$$

**Step 6: Solve for the Leighton Ratio (L).**

$$\boxed{L = \frac{[\text{O}_3]}{[\text{NO}_2]} = \frac{J}{k_2 \cdot [\text{NO}]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The Leighton Ratio $L$ is a **measurable quantity** in the field. Deviations from the theoretical Leighton Ratio indicate the presence of **VOCs or other radical species** that perturb the steady state. This is a frequent 7-mark question in KTU ESE.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Properties of Major Oxidants

| Property | Ozone ($\text{O}_3$) | Nitrogen Dioxide ($\text{NO}_2$) | PAN |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Molecular weight** | $48 \text{ g/mol}$ | $46 \text{ g/mol}$ | $121 \text{ g/mol}$ |
| **Color** | Pale blue (high conc.) | Reddish-brown | Colorless |
| **Odor** | Pungent, "electric" | Sharp, acrid | Sharp, irritating |
| **Boiling point** | $-112 ^\circ\text{C}$ | $21 ^\circ\text{C}$ | $\sim 105 ^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Solubility in water** | Moderate | Highly soluble | Low |
| **Primary source** | Photochemistry | Combustion + photochem. | Photochemistry |
| **Lifetime (summer)** | Days to weeks | $\sim 1 \text{ day}$ | $\sim 1 \text{ hour}$ (warm) to months (cold) |
| **Plant damage threshold** | $40 - 80 \text{ ppb}$ | $>\mathbf{200 \text{ ppb}}$ | $>\mathbf{5 \text{ ppb}}$ |
| **Health effect (respiratory)** | Severe irritant | Lung inflammation | Lacrymation, asthma |
| **KTU priority** | Very High | High | Medium-High |

## 3.3 Quantitative Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Numerical:** At noon in an urban atmosphere, the photolysis rate constant is $J = 0.015 \text{ s}^{-1}$ and the rate constant for $\text{O}_3 + \text{NO}$ reaction is $k_2 = 26.6 \text{ ppm}^{-1}\text{min}^{-1}$. If the observed NO concentration is $30 \text{ ppb}$, calculate the photo-stationary ozone concentration.

**Solution:**

From the Leighton Ratio:

$$[\text{O}_3] = \frac{J \cdot [\text{NO}_2]}{k_2 \cdot [\text{NO}]}$$

But in photo-stationary state, we use the inverse form. First, convert $k_2$ to consistent units:

$$k_2 = \frac{26.6}{60} = 0.443 \text{ ppm}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$J = 0.015 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Assuming the simpler steady-state relation where the net production is balanced:

$$[\text{O}_3]_{ss} = \frac{J \cdot [\text{NO}_2]}{k_2 \cdot [\text{NO}]}$$

For a typical urban NO/$\text{NO}_2$ ratio of about 1:1 (early morning), with $\text{NO}_2 = 30 \text{ ppb}$:

$$[\text{O}_3]_{ss} = \frac{0.015 \cdot 30}{0.443 \cdot 30} = \frac{0.45}{13.29} = 0.0338 \text{ ppm}$$

$$[\text{O}_3]_{ss} \approx 33.8 \text{ ppb}$$

This is below the EPA threshold of $70 \text{ ppb}$ but is a typical mid-afternoon urban value.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define photochemical oxidants. List the major oxidants present in the troposphere.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Photochemical oxidants are secondary air pollutants formed in the troposphere by the action of sunlight on mixtures of $\text{NO}_x$ and VOCs. They have a high electron affinity and can oxidize other substances. The major oxidants are: (1) **Ozone ($\text{O}_3$)** — the most abundant, (2) **Nitrogen Dioxide ($\text{NO}_2$)** — both primary and secondary, (3) **PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate)** — a powerful phytotoxin, (4) **Hydrogen Peroxide ($\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$)**, and (5) the **hydroxyl radical ($\text{OH}^\bullet$)** — the atmospheric "detergent."

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Differentiate between reducing smog and oxidizing smog.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Feature | Reducing Smog (London-type) | Oxidizing Smog (LA-type) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cause** | $\text{SO}_2$ + particulates from coal burning | $\text{NO}_x$ + VOCs under sunlight |
| **Season** | Winter, cold, humid | Summer, warm, dry, sunny |
| **Chemistry** | Reducing (electron-donating) | Oxidizing (electron-accepting) |
| **Major pollutant** | $\text{SO}_2$ and soot | $\text{O}_3$ and PAN |
| **Time of day** | Early morning | Midday to afternoon |
| **Health effect** | Bronchitis, throat irritation | Eye irritation, asthma, lung damage |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper]**
> 
> **(a)** With balanced chemical equations, explain the complete mechanism of photochemical smog formation highlighting the role of solar radiation. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Derive the photo-stationary state relation for the $\text{NO} - \text{NO}_2 - \text{O}_3$ system and explain its significance. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Photochemical Smog Formation Mechanism (7 Marks):**

The formation of photochemical smog proceeds through the following sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Primary Photolysis of $\text{NO}_2$:** Solar UV radiation of wavelength less than 430 nm dissociates $\text{NO}_2$ into nitric oxide and atomic oxygen. This is the **rate-limiting initiation step**.

**Step 2 — Ozone Formation:** The highly reactive atomic oxygen combines with molecular oxygen in a three-body collision (M is $\text{N}_2$ or $\text{O}_2$ acting as an energy sink) to form ozone.

**Step 3 — Null Cycle:** In clean air, the freshly formed ozone reacts with NO to regenerate $\text{NO}_2$, leading to no net ozone accumulation. This is a photo-stationary state.

**Step 4 — Hydroxyl Radical Formation:** At shorter wavelengths ($\lambda < 320 \text{ nm}$), ozone itself undergoes photolysis, producing excited singlet atomic oxygen, which reacts with water vapor to form the hydroxyl radical — the most important atmospheric oxidant.

**Step 5 — Hydrocarbon Oxidation:** $\text{OH}^\bullet$ abstracts hydrogen from VOCs, generating alkyl radicals that rapidly add $\text{O}_2$ to form peroxy radicals ($\text{RO}_2^\bullet$).

**Step 6 — Net Ozone Production:** Peroxy radicals convert NO to $\text{NO}_2$ without consuming ozone. The new $\text{NO}_2$ is photolyzed, yielding more ozone. This breaks the null cycle and leads to **net ozone accumulation**.

**Step 7 — PAN Formation:** The acetyl radical, formed from aldehyde oxidation, combines with $\text{O}_2$ and $\text{NO}_2$ to form PAN.

**(b) Derivation of Photo-Stationary State (7 Marks):**

Refer to the complete derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final result is:

$$[\text{O}_3] = \frac{J \cdot [\text{NO}_2]}{k_2 \cdot [\text{NO}]}$$

**Significance:** This equation allows environmental engineers to (i) predict ozone concentrations using measured $J$ and $\text{NO}_x$ data, (ii) detect the presence of unmeasured VOCs through deviations from the theoretical ratio, and (iii) design control strategies targeting specific precursor emissions.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
> 
> **(a)** What is PAN? Describe its formation, properties, and environmental significance. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Discuss the harmful effects of photochemical oxidants on human health, vegetation, and materials. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) PAN — Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (7 Marks):**

**Definition:** PAN is a secondary organic oxidant of formula $\text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OONO}_2$ with molecular weight $121 \text{ g/mol}$.

**Formation Mechanism (to be written):**
1. $\text{OH}^\bullet$ abstracts H from acetaldehyde: $\text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{OH}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CO}^\bullet + \text{H}_2\text{O}$
2. Acetyl radical adds $\text{O}_2$: $\text{CH}_3\text{CO}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OO}^\bullet$
3. Peroxyacetyl radical combines with $\text{NO}_2$: $\text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OO}^\bullet + \text{NO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OONO}_2$

**Properties:** It is a colorless liquid at room temperature, has a sharp irritating odor, and is a strong lacrymator.

**Environmental Significance:** PAN is a more potent phytotoxin than ozone. It is thermally unstable at high temperatures, so it has a long lifetime in cold upper troposphere and can be transported over hundreds of kilometers, damaging vegetation far from urban sources.

**(b) Harmful Effects (7 Marks):**

**On Human Health:**
- $\text{O}_3$ causes respiratory inflammation, reduced lung function, asthma exacerbation, and chest pain.
- $\text{NO}_2$ increases susceptibility to respiratory infections.
- PAN causes eye irritation (lacrimation) and reduces visibility.

**On Vegetation:**
- Chlorosis (yellowing), necrosis (bronze flecking), and premature leaf senescence.
- Reduced photosynthetic activity and crop yield losses (estimated 5-10% globally).
- Visible damage to conifers and broadleaf trees.

**On Materials:**
- Cracking of rubber products (tires, seals).
- Fading of dyes and textile deterioration.
- Corrosion of metals, especially copper and ferrous alloys.
- Damage to limestone monuments and statues (synergistic with $\text{SO}_2$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students lose 2 to 3 marks in 14-mark questions by:
> 1. **Writing reactions without stating the wavelength condition** for photolysis — always mention $\lambda < 430 \text{ nm}$ for $\text{NO}_2$ and $\lambda < 320 \text{ nm}$ for $\text{O}_3$.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of M (third body)** in the ozone formation reaction — this is a 1-mark loss if omitted.
> 3. **Confusing PAN with PBN** (peroxybenzoyl nitrate) — PAN is $\text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OONO}_2$, not benzene-based.
> 4. **Not writing the net reaction** for PAN formation — examiners expect three balanced equations.
> 5. **Skipping the photo-stationary state derivation** when asked — this is the most common cause of losing 7 marks in part (b).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PEPOT524 - Module 1: Oxidants**

- **Definition:** Oxidants are secondary pollutants (mainly $\text{O}_3$, $\text{NO}_2$, PAN) formed by photochemical reactions of $\text{NO}_x$ + VOCs under sunlight.
- **Initiation:** $\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}$ (requires $\lambda < 430 \text{ nm}$).
- **Ozone formation:** $\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}$ (three-body, M is energy carrier).
- **Null cycle:** $\text{O}_3 + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2$ — this alone does not produce net ozone.
- **Net ozone production** requires **peroxy radicals** ($\text{RO}_2^\bullet$ or $\text{HO}_2^\bullet$) to convert NO to $\text{NO}_2$ without consuming ozone.
- **$\text{OH}^\bullet$ radical** is the most important daytime oxidant; formed from $\text{O}_3 + h\nu + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ chain.
- **PAN** is a powerful phytotoxin with formula $\text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OONO}_2$; it can be transported over long distances.
- **Photo-stationary state:** $L = [\text{O}_3]/[\text{NO}_2] = J / (k_2 \cdot [\text{NO}])$.
- **Diurnal pattern:** $\text{O}_3$ and PAN peak 3-4 hours after solar noon; NO peaks at morning rush hour.
- **Health standards:** $\text{O}_3$ 8-hour standard is $70 \text{ ppb}$ (EPA/WHO); vegetation damage starts at $40 \text{ ppb}$.
- **Two smog types:** London (reducing, $\text{SO}_2$-based, winter) vs Los Angeles (oxidizing, $\text{O}_3$-based, summer).
- **Control strategy:** Reduce precursors — $\text{NO}_x$ and VOCs — through catalytic converters, vapor recovery, and reformulated fuels.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Fluoride - Control of Gaseous Pollutants - Automobile Pollution Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Fluoride - Control of Gaseous Pollutants - Automobile Pollution Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hydrogen Fluoride (HF) as an Air Pollutant

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Hydrogen Fluoride (chemical formula **HF**, molecular weight ≈ **20.01 g/mol**) is a colourless, highly corrosive, and extremely water-soluble gaseous air pollutant that originates primarily from industrial processes involving fluorine-containing raw materials, fuels, and chemical reactions. It is classified under the family of halogen acid gases and is considered one of the most reactive inorganic acidic pollutants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** HF is grouped with gaseous inorganic pollutants alongside SO₂, HCl, NOₓ, and H₂S because all are acid gases amenable to similar **absorption-based control technologies**. KTU frequently tests the linkage between pollutant chemistry and the choice of control device.

**Key Physical Properties (Examinable):**

| Property | Value/Description |
|---|---|
| Molecular formula | HF |
| Molecular weight | 20.01 g/mol |
| Boiling point | 19.5 °C |
| Density (gas) | 0.818 kg/m³ at 20 °C |
| Odour threshold | 0.04 ppm (very low) |
| Solubility in water | Infinitely miscible |
| TLV-TWA | 0.5 ppm (8-hour exposure) |

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Invisible Acid Rain Factory":**
Imagine a tiny invisible drop of "super-vinegar" floating in the air. HF is like vinegar's much more dangerous, volatile cousin — except you cannot smell it strongly, you cannot see it, and even a single breath in concentrated form burns the lung tissue the way strong acid would burn skin. In industry, it behaves like a "ghost" pollutant that escapes from aluminium smelters, phosphate fertilizer kilns, and ceramic kilns, and then drifts downwind, corroding metals, etching glass, and damaging vegetation — silently.

**Primary Sources of HF Emissions:**

1. **Aluminium smelting** (Halls–Héroult process) — electrolysis of Al₂O₃ in cryolite bath releases HF.
2. **Phosphate fertilizer manufacturing** — acidulation of phosphate rock with H₂SO₄ releases SiF₄ which hydrolyses to HF.
3. **Ceramic, brick, and glass industries** — fluoride impurities in clays and silica volatilise at high kiln temperatures.
4. **Coal combustion** — trace fluorides in coal oxidise to HF.
5. **Semiconductor and electronics manufacturing** — wafer etching processes.
6. **Hydrogen fluoride production plants** — direct leaks and process vents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Health & Environmental Impact:** HF causes severe respiratory irritation, pulmonary oedema, skeletal fluorosis (chronic exposure), and is phytotoxic to sensitive plants (e.g., gladiolus, apricot) at concentrations as low as 0.1 ppb. It is one of the few pollutants that damages both human health AND lithic structures (stone monuments, glass) measurably.

---

### 1.2 Control of Gaseous Pollutants — General Framework

**Formal Definition:**
Gaseous pollutant control refers to the set of engineering strategies and unit operations used to remove or convert airborne gaseous contaminants (acid gases, VOCs, odours, inorganic vapours) from industrial gas streams to levels compliant with emission standards.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Cleaning Conveyor Belt":**
Think of the exhaust gas leaving an industrial stack as dirty, smoke-filled air moving on a conveyor belt. As this conveyor passes through different "cleaning stations" (scrubbers, filters, burners), the dirty gases are progressively stripped of pollutants — much like washing dirty clothes stage by stage: rinse, scrub, rinse, dry.

**Six Principal Engineering Methods for Gaseous Pollutant Control:**

1. **Absorption** (Wet scrubbing) — gas dissolved in a liquid.
2. **Adsorption** — gas adheres to solid surface (e.g., activated carbon).
3. **Incineration / Thermal Oxidation** — combustible gases burned.
4. **Catalytic Oxidation** — oxidation assisted by a catalyst.
5. **Condensation** — vapours cooled to liquid.
6. **Biofiltration** — microbial degradation of biodegradable gases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Rule (KTU Favourite):** The control method is selected based on (i) pollutant solubility, (ii) reactivity, (iii) concentration in stream, (iv) desired removal efficiency, and (v) economics. **Soluble/reactive gases → Absorption; High concentration VOCs → Incineration; Dilute VOCs → Adsorption or Biofiltration.**

---

### 1.3 Automobile Pollution Control

**Formal Definition:**
Automobile pollution control encompasses the engineering systems and devices installed in motor vehicles (predominantly internal combustion engines — petrol, diesel, CNG) to reduce the emission of regulated pollutants such as Carbon Monoxide (CO), unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC), Oxides of Nitrogen (NOₓ), Particulate Matter (PM), and Lead (Pb) into the atmosphere.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Car's Three Filters & One Recirculator":**
Picture your car's tailpipe as a small factory chimney. A modern car adds a **"chemical reactor" (catalytic converter)**, a **"recycle loop" (EGR)**, and several **"sealed vents" (PCV, EVAP)** to make sure that the gases leaving the chimney are 95–99 % cleaner than those from an uncontrolled car of the 1960s.

**Regulated Automobile Pollutants:**

| Pollutant | Chemical Symbol | Primary Source | Hazard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Monoxide | CO | Incomplete combustion | Toxic (asphyxiant) |
| Unburnt Hydrocarbons | HC / VOC | Quench layer, fuel evaporation | Smog, carcinogenic |
| Oxides of Nitrogen | NO, NO₂ (NOₓ) | High-temperature combustion | Acid rain, smog |
| Particulate Matter | PM (esp. PM2.5) | Diesel soot | Respiratory disease |
| Lead compounds | Pb (historical) | Leaded petrol | Neurotoxic |
| Sulphur Dioxide | SO₂ | Sulphur in diesel fuel | Acid rain |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hydrogen Fluoride — Generation Chemistry and Control Logic

**Step-by-Step Conceptual Logic of HF Generation:**

- **Step 1 — Origin of Fluorine:** Fluorine is present as fluoride (F⁻) in minerals such as **fluorite (CaF₂)**, **cryolite (Na₃AlF₆)**, **fluorapatite (Ca₅(PO₄)₃F)**, and as trace impurity in coal, clays, and phosphate rock.
- **Step 2 — High-Temperature Release:** During smelting or combustion, fluoride minerals decompose. F⁻ is converted to HF or SiF₄ in the presence of water vapour or silica.
- **Step 3 — Gas-Phase Transport:** HF remains gaseous until the flue gas cools below 19.5 °C (when it would condense to liquid). It is therefore emitted in gaseous form through stacks unless intercepted.
- **Step 4 — Environmental Fate:** HF reacts with vegetation, water, and minerals in the environment, producing fluorosis and acid deposition.

**Key Generation Reactions (KTU Examinable):**

$$2\ \text{CaF}_2 + \text{SiO}_2 + 2\ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{SiF}_4 + 2\ \text{CaSO}_4 + 2\ \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step explanation:** Fluorite + silica + sulphuric acid produce silicon tetrafluoride (gas).

$$\text{SiF}_4 + 2\ \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + 4\ \text{HF}$$

**Step explanation:** Hydrolysis of SiF₄ in the moist flue gas yields HF.

$$2\ \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\ \text{C} + 6\ \text{NaF} \longrightarrow 4\ \text{Al} + 3\ \text{CO}_2 + 3\ \text{Na}_2\text{O}\text{ (in cryolite bath with HF evolution)}$$

**Step explanation:** Aluminium smelting — fluoride evolves from the cryolite bath when water vapour reacts with electrolyte.

---

### 2.2 Control Methods for HF (Detailed Analytical Treatment)

**(A) Wet Scrubbing (Absorption) — Most Common Method:**

**Operating Principle:** HF is highly soluble in water and reacts readily with alkaline solutions. The flue gas is passed through a scrubbing liquor (typically **NaOH solution, lime slurry (Ca(OH)₂), or limestone slurry (CaCO₃)**). HF is absorbed and neutralised to form soluble or insoluble fluoride salts.

**Key Absorption Reaction:**

$$2\ \text{HF} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CaF}_2\downarrow + 2\ \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step explanation:** Lime slurry neutralises HF to form insoluble calcium fluoride (precipitate) and water. This precipitate (CaF₂) is a marketable by-product.

$$2\ \text{HF} + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \longrightarrow 2\ \text{NaF} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$$

**Step explanation:** Soda ash scrubbing produces soluble NaF (recoverable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When asked for HF control in an aluminium smelter, the standard answer is **"wet scrubbing with NaOH or lime slurry in a packed tower or Venturi scrubber, achieving 95–99 % removal efficiency."**

**(B) Dry Sorbent Injection (Dry Scrubbing):**

- A dry alkaline sorbent (**sodium carbonate, lime, or limestone**) is injected directly into the flue gas duct.
- HF reacts in the dry phase to form particulate fluoride salts, which are then removed by a fabric filter (baghose).

**Reaction:**

$$2\ \text{HF}_{(g)} + \text{Ca(OH)}_{2(s)} \longrightarrow \text{CaF}_{2(s)} + 2\ \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)}$$

- Advantages: No wastewater generation; suitable for hot/dry gas streams.
- Disadvantages: Sorbent utilisation is lower than wet systems.

**(C) Adsorption:**

- Activated alumina (Al₂O₃) is an effective adsorbent for HF.
- Reaction: $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 6\ \text{HF} \longrightarrow 2\ \text{AlF}_3 + 3\ \text{H}_2\text{O}$
- AlF₃ is recovered and recycled to the aluminium smelting cell.

---

### 2.3 Control of Gaseous Pollutants — Engineering Methods Compared

| Method | Pollutants Targeted | Operating Principle | Removal Efficiency | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wet Scrubbing (Absorption) | SO₂, HCl, HF, NOₓ, NH₃ | Gas-liquid mass transfer | 90–99 % | Wastewater disposal |
| Adsorption (Carbon) | VOCs, odours, Hg | Surface adhesion | 90–95 % | Carbon regeneration |
| Thermal Incineration | VOCs, CO, odours | High-T oxidation (>750 °C) | 95–99 % | Fuel cost |
| Catalytic Oxidation | VOCs, CO | Lower-T oxidation with catalyst | 90–98 % | Catalyst poisoning |
| Condensation | High-BP Vapours | Cooling to dew point | 50–90 % | Limited to high conc. |
| Biofiltration | Odours, biodegradable VOCs | Microbial oxidation | 70–95 % | Slow kinetics |

**Key Mass Transfer Equation for Absorption (HTU-NTU Concept):**

$$Z = (\text{HTU}) \times (\text{NTU})$$

Where:
- $Z$ = required packed height of scrubber (m).
- $\text{HTU}$ = Height of a Transfer Unit (m) — function of gas/liquid flow rates.
- $\text{NTU}$ = Number of Transfer Units = $\int \dfrac{dY}{Y - Y^*}$, where $Y$ is the gas-phase mole ratio and $Y^*$ is the equilibrium gas-phase mole ratio.

**Simplified Removal Efficiency Formula (KTU Favourite):**

$$\eta = \frac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\ \%$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = fractional removal efficiency.
- $C_{\text{in}}$ = inlet pollutant concentration (mg/m³ or ppm).
- $C_{\text{out}}$ = outlet pollutant concentration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Wet scrubbing dominates HF and SO₂ control because it offers high efficiency, continuous operation, and recovery of by-products (CaF₂, CaSO₄, gypsum). Industries worldwide prefer Venturi scrubbers for HF due to high gas–liquid contact and ability to handle particulate + gaseous pollutants simultaneously.

---

### 2.4 Automobile Pollution Control — Technology Stack

**Layer 1 — Engine Design Modifications:**

- Optimised combustion chamber geometry.
- Electronic fuel injection (EFI) for stoichiometric air–fuel ratio control.
- Variable valve timing.
- Stratified-charge engines (lean burn).

**Layer 2 — Crankcase Emission Control (PCV System):**

- Blow-by gases (HC) from the crankcase are routed back to the intake manifold through the **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** valve.
- Inlet: HC-laden blow-by → PCV valve → Intake manifold → Combustion.

**Layer 3 — Evaporative Emission Control (EVAP System):**

- Fuel vapours from the tank and carburettor are absorbed in an **activated carbon canister** and later purged into the engine for combustion.

**Layer 4 — Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):**

- A portion of exhaust gas (rich in inert CO₂ and H₂O) is recirculated into the intake manifold.
- Effect: Lowers peak combustion temperature, reducing **thermal NOₓ formation**.

**Layer 5 — Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) — Heart of Modern Petrol Cars:**

- Reduces CO and HC (oxidation) and NOₓ (reduction) simultaneously.
- Operating window: Stoichiometric air–fuel ratio only (λ = 1 ± 0.01).
- Active components: Platinum (Pt), Palladium (Pd), Rhodium (Rh) on a ceramic honeycomb.

**Core TWC Reactions (KTU Examinable):**

$$2\ \text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\ \text{CO}_2\ \ (\text{Oxidation})$$

$$2\ \text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 25\ \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 16\ \text{CO}_2 + 18\ \text{H}_2\text{O}\ \ (\text{HC Oxidation})$$

$$2\ \text{NO} \longrightarrow \text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2\ \ (\text{Reduction})$$

$$\text{CO} + \text{NO} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{N}_2\ \ (\text{Reduction})$$

**Layer 6 — Diesel-Specific Controls:**

- **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC):** oxidises CO, HC, soluble organic fraction of PM.
- **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF):** traps soot; periodically regenerated by burning.
- **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR):** injects urea (AdBlue) → ammonia → reduces NOₓ to N₂.

**SCR Reaction:**

$$4\ \text{NH}_3 + 4\ \text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 4\ \text{N}_2 + 6\ \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\ \%$ | $\eta$ (%) ; $C$ in mg/m³ or ppm | Removal efficiency of any control device |
| 2 | $m_{\text{pol}} = Q \times C$ | $m$ = mass flow (g/s) ; $Q$ = gas flow (m³/s) ; $C$ = concentration (g/m³) | Emission rate calculation |
| 3 | $Z = \text{HTU} \times \text{NTU}$ | $Z$ = packed height (m) | Scrubber design |
| 4 | $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = 14.7\ \text{(petrol)}$ | Mass basis; stoichiometric ratio | Combustion air requirement |
| 5 | $\text{NO}_{x} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E}{RT}\right)$ | Zeldovich kinetics — T dependence | Why EGR reduces NOₓ |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{14.7}$ | Stoichiometric mass balance | Engine fuel consumption |
| 7 | $\text{L}_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2} = \dfrac{m_{\text{HF}} \times 74}{80}$ | Stoichiometric lime dose | HF scrubbing reagent dose |
| 8 | $\text{PM}_{2.5} = \text{filter mass gain}/\text{air volume}$ | μg/m³ | Particulate measurement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:** *"For HF, give Lime, give NaOH, give Soda — CaF₂ or NaF will be the product."* This single sentence covers 70 % of HF control viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Stoichiometric Lime Requirement for HF Scrubbing

**Problem Statement:**
A phosphate fertilizer plant emits HF at a rate of **2.0 kg/h**. Design the lime (Ca(OH)₂) slurry dosing system. Calculate the stoichiometric Ca(OH)₂ requirement and the CaF₂ by-product mass flow.

**Given:**
- HF emission rate, $\dot{m}_{\text{HF}} = 2.0\ \text{kg/h}$.
- Molar mass of HF = 20.01 kg/kmol.
- Molar mass of Ca(OH)₂ = 74.09 kg/kmol.
- Molar mass of CaF₂ = 78.07 kg/kmol.

**Step 1 — Convert HF mass flow to molar flow:**

$$\dot{n}_{\text{HF}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{HF}}}{M_{\text{HF}}} = \frac{2.0\ \text{kg/h}}{20.01\ \text{kg/kmol}}$$

$$\dot{n}_{\text{HF}} = 0.09995\ \text{kmol/h} \approx 0.10\ \text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Apply stoichiometry of neutralisation reaction:**

$$2\ \text{HF} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CaF}_2 + 2\ \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

From the balanced equation, **2 moles of HF require 1 mole of Ca(OH)₂**. So:

$$\dot{n}_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2} = \frac{\dot{n}_{\text{HF}}}{2} = \frac{0.10}{2} = 0.05\ \text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Convert molar flow of Ca(OH)₂ to mass flow:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2} = \dot{n}_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2} \times M_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2} = 0.05\ \text{kmol/h} \times 74.09\ \text{kg/kmol}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2} = 3.7045\ \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the CaF₂ by-product mass flow:**

From the same equation, 2 mol HF produce 1 mol CaF₂. Therefore:

$$\dot{n}_{\text{CaF}_2} = \frac{\dot{n}_{\text{HF}}}{2} = 0.05\ \text{kmol/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{CaF}_2} = 0.05\ \text{kmol/h} \times 78.07\ \text{kg/kmol} = 3.9035\ \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Account for 20 % excess lime (industrial practice):**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2, \text{actual}} = 1.20 \times 3.7045 = 4.4454\ \text{kg/h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Industrial scrubbers operate with 10–25 % stoichiometric excess of reagent to ensure that outlet HF meets the regulatory limit (typically 5 mg/m³ in India under EPA rules). Excess lime also maintains alkaline pH in the slurry to prevent re-emission of HF.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Scrubber Outlet Concentration & Removal Efficiency

**Problem Statement:**
A packed-bed wet scrubber is designed to treat a flue gas stream of **10,000 m³/h** at 200 °C carrying HF at **inlet concentration 200 mg/m³**. If the scrubber achieves **95 % removal efficiency**, calculate the outlet concentration, mass of HF removed per hour, and mass of Ca(OH)₂ required.

**Given:**
- $Q = 10{,}000\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$.
- $C_{\text{in}} = 200\ \text{mg/m}^3$.
- $\eta = 95\ \% = 0.95$.

**Step 1 — Outlet concentration:**

$$C_{\text{out}} = C_{\text{in}} (1 - \eta) = 200 \times (1 - 0.95)$$

$$C_{\text{out}} = 200 \times 0.05 = 10\ \text{mg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Mass of HF removed per hour:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{HF, removed}} = Q \times C_{\text{in}} \times \eta$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{HF, removed}} = 10{,}000\ \text{m}^3/\text{h} \times 200\ \text{mg/m}^3 \times 0.95$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{HF, removed}} = 1{,}900{,}000\ \text{mg/h} = 1.9\ \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Ca(OH)₂ requirement (using stoichiometric ratio from §3.1):**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2, stoich} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{HF, removed}} \times 74}{2 \times 20}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2, stoich} = \frac{1.9 \times 74}{40} = 3.515\ \text{kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Ca(OH)}_2, \text{actual with 15\% excess}} = 1.15 \times 3.515 = 4.042\ \text{kg/h}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Automobile Emission Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A petrol car consumes **5.0 kg of fuel per 100 km**. The fuel contains 85 % carbon and 15 % hydrogen by mass, and the engine operates with **5 % excess air**. Calculate the mass of CO₂, CO (assuming 2 % of carbon goes to CO), and NOₓ (assume 0.05 % of N₂ in air forms NO₂ equivalent) emitted per km.

**Given:**
- Fuel consumption = 5.0 kg / 100 km = 0.05 kg/km.
- Carbon content = 85 %; Hydrogen content = 15 %.
- Excess air = 5 %; CO fraction of carbon = 2 %.

**Step 1 — Carbon and hydrogen in fuel per km:**

$$m_{\text{C}} = 0.05 \times 0.85 = 0.0425\ \text{kg/km}$$

$$m_{\text{H}} = 0.05 \times 0.15 = 0.0075\ \text{kg/km}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of CO₂ produced:**

Carbon to CO₂: $C + O_2 \to CO_2$ (44 kg CO₂ per 12 kg C).

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.0425 \times \frac{44}{12} \times (1 - 0.02)$$

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.0425 \times 3.667 \times 0.98$$

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.1527\ \text{kg/km} = 152.7\ \text{g/km}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of CO produced:**

Carbon to CO: $2C + O_2 \to 2CO$ (28 kg CO per 12 kg C).

$$m_{\text{CO}} = 0.0425 \times 0.02 \times \frac{28}{12}$$

$$m_{\text{CO}} = 0.0425 \times 0.02 \times 2.333 = 0.001983\ \text{kg/km} \approx 2.0\ \text{g/km}$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric O₂ required:**

For 1 kg fuel (0.85 kg C + 0.15 kg H):
- $O_2$ for C: $0.85 \times \tfrac{32}{12} = 2.267\ \text{kg}$.
- $O_2$ for H: $0.15 \times \tfrac{32}{4} = 1.200\ \text{kg}$.
- Total stoich O₂: $3.467\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$.

**Step 5 — Actual O₂ supplied (5 % excess):**

$$m_{O_2, \text{actual}} = 3.467 \times 1.05 = 3.640\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of air (air is 23 % O₂ by mass):**

$$m_{\text{air}} = \frac{3.640}{0.23} = 15.83\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 7 — Mass of N₂ in inlet air:**

$$m_{\text{N}_2} = 0.77 \times 15.83 = 12.19\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 8 — NOₓ (as NO₂ equivalent, 0.05 % of N₂ reacts):**

N₂ to NO₂: $N_2 + 2O_2 \to 2NO_2$ (92 kg NO₂ per 28 kg N₂).

$$m_{\text{NO}_x} = 12.19 \times 0.0005 \times \frac{92}{28}$$

$$m_{\text{NO}_x} = 12.19 \times 0.0005 \times 3.286 = 0.02003\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$$

**Per km emission:**

$$m_{\text{NO}_x, \text{per km}} = 0.02003 \times 0.05 = 0.00100\ \text{kg/km} = 1.0\ \text{g/km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A typical uncontrolled petrol car emits ≈ 153 g CO₂/km, ≈ 2 g CO/km, and ≈ 1 g NOₓ/km. With a TWC, CO and NOₓ drop by >95 % — which is why a modern car is dramatically cleaner than a 1970s car.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Control Method

| Pollutant | Best Control Method | Reagent / Catalyst | Removal Efficiency | Major By-product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| HF | Wet scrubbing | Ca(OH)₂ slurry | 95–99 % | CaF₂ (precipitate) |
| HF | Dry sorbent injection | Na₂CO₃ / Ca(OH)₂ | 90–95 % | Particulate fluoride |
| SO₂ | Wet scrubber (Lime/Limestone) | CaCO₃ slurry | 90–98 % | Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) |
| NOₓ (stationary) | SCR | NH₃ + V₂O₅/TiO₂ | 80–95 % | N₂ + H₂O |
| NOₓ (mobile) | EGR + TWC | Recirculated exhaust | 70–90 % | N₂ + CO₂ |
| VOCs (high conc.) | Thermal incineration | Natural gas burner | 95–99 % | CO₂ + H₂O |
| VOCs (low conc.) | Activated carbon adsorption | Carbon bed | 90–95 % | Recovered solvent |
| Odours | Biofiltration | Compost / biofilm | 80–95 % | CO₂ + biomass |
| CO / HC (auto) | Three-Way Catalyst | Pt-Pd-Rh on ceramic | 95–99 % | CO₂ + H₂O + N₂ |
| PM (diesel) | DPF + DOC | Wall-flow ceramic | 85–99 % | Ash + CO₂ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — HF Emission Control in a Phosphate Fertilizer Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phosphate Rock<br>and Sulphuric Acid<br>Reactor] --> B[Hot Reactor Off-Gas<br>Temp 150 to 200 C<br>Contains HF and SiF4]
    B --> C[Quench Tower<br>Water spray<br>SiF4 hydrolyses to HF]
    C --> D[Venturi Scrubber<br>with Lime Slurry<br>CaOH2 reagent]
    D --> E[Packed Tower Scrubber<br>Secondary alkaline stage<br>polishing HF removal]
    E --> F[Induced Draft Fan<br>provides suction]
    F --> G[Stack Emission<br>HF less than 5 mg per m3<br>Compliant with EPA]
    D --> H[Sludge Treatment<br>Thickener and Filter Press]
    H --> I[Calcium Fluoride<br>CaF2 by-product<br>Sold to aluminium industry]
    E --> J[Recycled CaOH2 Slurry<br>make-up water added]

    style A fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style D fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style E fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style G fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style I fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
```

**Reading Guide:** The hot gas first goes through a quench (which converts any SiF₄ to HF via hydrolysis), then through a Venturi scrubber (high-velocity contact) and finally a packed polishing tower. Lime slurry is recirculated, and the CaF₂ sludge is dewatered and sold.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — General Strategy for Gaseous Pollutant Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Industrial Process<br>Source of Gas] --> B[Characterisation<br>Concentration<br>Flow rate<br>Temperature]
    B --> C{Soluble<br>and Reactive?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Adsorption Tower<br>Wet Scrubbing]
    C -- No --> E{Combustible?}
    E -- Yes --> F{Concentration<br>High or Low?}
    F -- High --> G[Thermal Incinerator]
    F -- Low --> H[Catalytic Oxidiser]
    E -- No --> I[Biofilter or<br>Adsorption]
    D --> J[Treated Gas to Stack]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Continuous Emission<br>Monitoring System]
    K --> L[Regulatory<br>Compliance Check]

    style A fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style D fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460
    style G fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style H fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style L fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
```

**Reading Guide:** This decision tree mirrors the standard KTU 2024 syllabus flowchart. Pollutants are first characterised, then routed to the most appropriate control method based on solubility, reactivity, and combustibility.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Automobile Pollution Control System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Petrol Tank] -->|Fuel vapours| B[EVAP Canister<br>Activated Carbon]
    B -->|Purged HC| C[Engine Intake Manifold]
    D[Crankcase Blow-by<br>HC rich gas] -->|PCV valve| C
    C -->|Air Fuel Mixture| E[Engine Cylinder<br>Combustion]
    E -->|Exhaust gases<br>CO, HC, NOx| F[Exhaust Manifold]
    F --> G[Three Way Catalytic Converter<br>Pt Pd Rh on ceramic]
    G -->|Cleaned exhaust<br>CO2 H2O N2| H[Muffler]
    H --> I[Tailpipe Emission]
    F -->|Partial exhaust| J[EGR Valve<br>Recirculation to Intake]
    J --> C
    G --> K[Lambda Oxygen Sensor<br>Feedback to ECU]
    K -->|Air Fuel Trim| C

    style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style G fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style K fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
```

**Reading Guide:** A modern petrol car uses a closed-loop control strategy. The **lambda (O₂) sensor** downstream of the TWC continuously measures the air–fuel ratio and signals the ECU to adjust fuel injection, keeping the engine within the narrow TWC operating window (λ = 1 ± 0.01).

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Diesel Engine After-Treatment System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Diesel Engine<br>High Compression] -->|Exhaust with PM NOx| B[Diesel Oxidation Catalyst<br>DOC]
    B -->|CO HC oxidised<br>PM reduced| C[Diesel Particulate Filter<br>DPF]
    C -->|Trapped soot<br>Periodic regeneration| D[SCR Unit<br>Urea AdBlue Injection]
    D -->|NH3 reduces NOx| E[Clean Exhaust<br>to tailpipe]
    F[Urea Tank AdBlue] -->|Dosed by injector| D
    C -->|Soot burnt at 600 C| G[Ash Residue<br>collected in DPF]
    E --> H[NOx Sensor<br>Feedback to ECU]

    style A fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style D fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460
    style E fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

**Reading Guide:** Diesel after-treatment is more complex than petrol because diesel engines run lean (excess air), so a TWC alone cannot reduce NOₓ. SCR with urea is mandatory for Euro VI / BS-VI compliance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List any two major sources of Hydrogen Fluoride emissions in the atmosphere and write the chemical reactions involved.**

**Model Answer:**
The two major sources of HF emissions are:
1. **Aluminium smelting** — During the Halls–Héroult process, cryolite (Na₃AlF₆) reacts with water vapour in the electrolyte to release HF.
2. **Phosphate fertilizer manufacture** — Fluorapatite in phosphate rock reacts with sulphuric acid, releasing SiF₄ which hydrolyses to HF.

Reactions:

$$2\ \text{CaF}_2 + \text{SiO}_2 + 2\ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{SiF}_4 + 2\ \text{CaSO}_4 + 2\ \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{SiF}_4 + 2\ \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + 4\ \text{HF}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is a Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)? List the pollutants it controls.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** is an exhaust after-treatment device installed in the exhaust system of petrol vehicles. It uses a ceramic honeycomb substrate coated with platinum (Pt), palladium (Pd), and rhodium (Rh) catalysts to simultaneously perform three reactions: oxidation of CO to CO₂, oxidation of unburnt hydrocarbons to CO₂ and H₂O, and reduction of NOₓ to N₂.

**Pollutants controlled:** Carbon monoxide (CO), unburnt hydrocarbons (HC), and oxides of nitrogen (NOₓ). It operates efficiently only at the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio (λ = 1).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the various **methods used for the control of gaseous pollutants** with a comparative table highlighting pollutants targeted, principle, and removal efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **wet scrubber** is to be designed to treat an HF-containing gas stream. Discuss in detail the **chemistry, equipment, operating parameters, and by-product recovery**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Methods for Control of Gaseous Pollutants:**

The major methods are:

1. **Absorption (Wet Scrubbing):** Gaseous pollutant is dissolved in a suitable liquid. Used for SO₂, HCl, HF, NH₃, NO₂. Removal efficiency: 90–99 %.

2. **Adsorption:** Gas adheres to a solid surface such as activated carbon. Used for VOCs, odours, mercury vapour. Efficiency: 90–95 %.

3. **Thermal Incineration:** Combustible gases oxidised at high temperature (>750 °C). Used for high-concentration VOCs. Efficiency: 95–99 %.

4. **Catalytic Oxidation:** Oxidation at lower temperature with a catalyst (Pt, Pd). Efficiency: 90–98 %.

5. **Condensation:** Vapour cooled below dew point to liquid. Used for high-boiling VOCs. Efficiency: 50–90 %.

6. **Biofiltration:** Microbial degradation of biodegradable gases. Used for odour control. Efficiency: 70–95 %.

(Comparative table — refer to §2.3 Analytical Matrix.)

**(b) Wet Scrubber for HF — Detailed Treatment:**

**Chemistry:** HF is a water-soluble acid gas. In a wet scrubber, HF is absorbed in an alkaline liquor (typically **NaOH solution, lime slurry, or soda ash**). The reactions are:

$$2\ \text{HF} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CaF}_2\downarrow + 2\ \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$2\ \text{HF} + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \longrightarrow 2\ \text{NaF} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$$

**Equipment Selection:**
- **Venturi Scrubber** — for high inlet concentrations and high removal efficiency.
- **Packed Tower Scrubber** — for polishing and low outlet concentrations.
- **Plate Tower** — for moderate flow rates and cleanable operation.

**Operating Parameters:**
- Liquid-to-gas ratio (L/G): typically 1–3 L/m³.
- pH of scrubbing liquor: 8–10 (alkaline, to maintain driving force).
- Inlet gas temperature: usually pre-cooled to 50–80 °C to improve solubility.
- Superficial gas velocity in packed bed: 0.5–2 m/s.

**By-product Recovery:** CaF₂ sludge is thickened, filtered, dried, and sold to aluminium smelters (where it is fed back to the cryolite bath). NaF solution is evaporated and crystallised for use in water fluoridation or pesticide manufacture.

**Design Equation (HTU-NTU):**

$$Z = \text{HTU} \times \text{NTU} = \text{HTU} \times \int \frac{dY}{Y - Y^*}$$

For HF in lime slurry, the equilibrium partial pressure $Y^* \to 0$, simplifying NTU to $\ln(C_{\text{in}}/C_{\text{out}})$. A typical packed height of 3–6 m is sufficient for >95 % removal.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the **various air pollution control devices used in automobiles**. Explain the working of a **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** with chemical reactions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **mass of CO₂, CO, and NOₓ emitted per km** by a petrol car that consumes 6 kg of fuel per 100 km. The fuel contains 84 % C and 16 % H by mass. The engine operates with 10 % excess air, 2 % of the carbon forms CO, and 0.04 % of the inlet N₂ forms NO₂ equivalent. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Automobile Pollution Control Devices:**

1. **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV):** Routes blow-by gases (HC) back to intake.
2. **Evaporative Emission Control (EVAP):** Captures fuel vapours in activated carbon canister.
3. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):** Recirculates exhaust to lower combustion temperature and reduce NOₓ.
4. **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC):** Reduces CO, HC, and NOₓ simultaneously.
5. **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC):** For diesel engines, oxidises CO and HC.
6. **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF):** Traps and burns soot periodically.
7. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR):** Uses urea (AdBlue) to reduce NOₓ in diesel exhaust.

**Working of TWC:**
The TWC contains a ceramic honeycomb monolith (cordierite) wash-coated with γ-Al₂O₃ and impregnated with Pt, Pd, and Rh. It performs three simultaneous reactions:

- **CO Oxidation:** $2\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO}_2$
- **HC Oxidation:** $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + (x + y/4)\text{O}_2 \to x\text{CO}_2 + (y/2)\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- **NOₓ Reduction:** $2\text{NO} \to \text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2$ and $\text{CO} + \text{NO} \to \text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{N}_2$

The TWC operates at 400–800 °C. A **lambda (O₂) sensor** in the exhaust provides feedback to the ECU to maintain air–fuel ratio within λ = 1 ± 0.01 (the narrow window where all three reactions proceed simultaneously).

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Fuel consumption = 6 kg/100 km = 0.06 kg/km.
- Carbon = 84 %, Hydrogen = 16 %.
- Excess air = 10 %, CO fraction of carbon = 2 %, NO₂ fraction of N₂ = 0.04 %.

**Step 1 — C and H in fuel per km:**

$$m_{\text{C}} = 0.06 \times 0.84 = 0.0504\ \text{kg/km}$$

$$m_{\text{H}} = 0.06 \times 0.16 = 0.0096\ \text{kg/km}$$

**Step 2 — CO₂ from carbon (98 % to CO₂):**

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.0504 \times 0.98 \times \frac{44}{12} = 0.0504 \times 0.98 \times 3.667 = 0.1811\ \text{kg/km}$$

**Step 3 — CO from carbon (2 % to CO):**

$$m_{\text{CO}} = 0.0504 \times 0.02 \times \frac{28}{12} = 0.0504 \times 0.02 \times 2.333 = 0.00235\ \text{kg/km}$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric O₂ per kg fuel:**

For 1 kg fuel: $m_{\text{O}_2, \text{stoich}} = 0.84 \times \tfrac{32}{12} + 0.16 \times \tfrac{32}{4} = 2.240 + 1.280 = 3.520\ \text{kg}$.

**Step 5 — Actual O₂ (10 % excess):**

$$m_{\text{O}_2, \text{actual}} = 3.520 \times 1.10 = 3.872\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of air and N₂ per kg fuel:**

$$m_{\text{air}} = \frac{3.872}{0.23} = 16.835\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$$

$$m_{\text{N}_2} = 0.77 \times 16.835 = 12.963\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 7 — NOₓ (as NO₂) per kg fuel:**

N₂ → 2 NO₂ (28 kg N₂ gives 92 kg NO₂):

$$m_{\text{NO}_x} = 12.963 \times 0.0004 \times \frac{92}{28} = 12.963 \times 0.0004 \times 3.286 = 0.01704\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 8 — Per km NOₓ:**

$$m_{\text{NO}_x, \text{per km}} = 0.01704 \times 0.06 = 0.00102\ \text{kg/km} = 1.02\ \text{g/km}$$

**Final Answer:**

| Pollutant | Emission per km |
|---|---|
| CO₂ | 181.1 g/km |
| CO | 2.35 g/km |
| NOₓ | 1.02 g/km |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently forget to apply the **stoichiometric factor of 44/12** for CO₂ and **28/12 for CO** — losing 1 mark.
> - Many students use the **molar ratio of 14.7 (air–fuel)** incorrectly as a *mass* ratio for petrol; it is mass-based, but the **23 % O₂ by mass** must be used explicitly for air calculations.
> - In HF scrubber design, students often **omit the equilibrium assumption** ($Y^* = 0$) when the reagent is a strong base, which simplifies NTU and packed height calculations. This shortcut is acceptable and **earns full marks** if stated clearly.
> - In TWC, students confuse **oxidation and reduction zones**; remember — **CO and HC are oxidised, NOₓ is reduced**, and Rh is the key reduction catalyst.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **HF sources:** Aluminium smelting, phosphate fertiliser, ceramics, coal combustion.
- **HF properties:** Colourless, corrosive, MW = 20.01, BP = 19.5 °C, infinitely water-soluble, TLV = 0.5 ppm.
- **HF primary control:** Wet scrubbing with **Ca(OH)₂ or NaOH**; by-product **CaF₂**.
- **HF key reaction:** $2\text{HF} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \to \text{CaF}_2\downarrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- **Six gaseous control methods:** Absorption, Adsorption, Incineration, Catalytic Oxidation, Condensation, Biofiltration.
- **Selection rule:** Soluble → Scrubber; Combustible high-conc → Incinerator; Combustible low-conc → Adsorption/Catalytic; Odour → Biofilter.
- **Removal efficiency formula:** $\eta = (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}})/C_{\text{in}} \times 100\ \%$.
- **HTU-NTU design:** $Z = \text{HTU} \times \text{NTU}$; for strong base, $Y^* = 0$ simplifies NTU.
- **Automobile pollutants:** CO, HC, NOₓ, PM (Lead historically).
- **Automobile controls:** PCV, EVAP, EGR, TWC, DOC, DPF, SCR.
- **TWC key facts:** λ = 1 ± 0.01; Pt-Pd-Rh; operates 400–800 °C.
- **EGR mechanism:** Recirculated exhaust lowers flame temperature → reduces thermal NOₓ.
- **SCR:** Urea (AdBlue) → NH₃ → reduces NOₓ to N₂.
- **Stoichiometric air–fuel ratio (petrol):** 14.7 (mass basis).
- **CO₂/CO conversion factors:** $44/12$ for CO₂, $28/12$ for CO from carbon.
- **Air composition (mass):** 23 % O₂, 77 % N₂.
- **Scrubber design rule of thumb:** L/G = 1–3 L/m³, pH = 8–10, gas velocity 0.5–2 m/s.
- **Industrial practice:** 10–25 % stoichiometric excess of alkaline reagent in scrubber.
- **Euro VI / BS-VI standards:** Mandate TWC + EGR (petrol) and DOC + DPF + SCR (diesel).
- **HTU/HDU definitions:** Height of Transfer Unit (HTU) = packed height per transfer unit; for HF–water–lime, HTU ≈ 0.5–1.5 m.
- **Environmental threshold:** HF detectable phytotoxic effect at ≈ 0.1 ppb; regulatory limit ≈ 5 mg/m³ in stack gas (India EPA).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Particulates in the Air - Source
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Particulates in the Air - Source

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Particulates in Air

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Particulate Matter (PM)** is defined as a complex mixture of extremely small solid particles and liquid droplets suspended in the air. These particles vary widely in size, shape, chemical composition, and origin. The term *"particulates"* generally refers to the dispersed solid or liquid phase (discontinuous phase) within a gaseous continuous phase (air).

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEPOT524, Module 2), particulates in ambient air are operationally classified by their **aerodynamic diameter**, which is the diameter of a unit-density sphere ($\rho_p = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$) that settles at the same terminal velocity as the particle in question.

Key standard fractions defined by regulatory bodies:

- **PM$_{10}$** — Coarse fraction, aerodynamic diameter $\leq 10 \;\mu m$ (inhalable).
- **PM$_{2.5}$** — Fine fraction, aerodynamic diameter $\leq 2.5 \;\mu m$ (respirable).
- **Ultrafine** — Particles with diameter $\leq 0.1 \;\mu m$ (100 nm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why aerodynamic diameter and not geometric diameter?**
> Particles in the atmosphere are never perfect spheres. They can be flakes, fibers, or agglomerates. Since health and engineering impacts (deposition in lungs, settling velocity, light scattering) depend on how a particle *behaves* in a fluid, the aerodynamic diameter is the most physically meaningful metric.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of air as a **glass of clean water**. If you sprinkle a handful of fine sand into it, the sand does not dissolve — it remains suspended as discrete, visible specks for a while before slowly settling. Now imagine you cannot see the specks because they are too tiny, but they are still there, each behaving like a tiny balloon (or tiny cannonball) that can be carried by breezes, lodge deep in your lungs, or settle on your window sill. **That is essentially what Particulate Matter is — invisible or barely visible "grains" floating in the air we breathe, originating from a wide range of natural and human activities.**

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world connection:** After a Diwali celebration in Delhi, satellite images often show a thick brown haze. That haze is *not gas* — it is a concentrated mass of suspended particulates (sulphate particles, metal oxides, soot) that originated from fireworks, vehicular traffic, and stubble burning.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are routinely used in particulate source analysis:

- **Density of standard air:** $\rho_a = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at $15^\circ C$, sea level)
- **Dynamic viscosity of air:** $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$
- **Kinematic viscosity of air:** $\nu = 1.51 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Reference unit particle density:** $\rho_p = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Concentration units:** $\mu g/m^3$ (ambient), $mg/Nm^3$ (stack emissions)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Particulate Sources

Sources of airborne particulates are broadly divided into two master categories and then further sub-classified by mechanism, origin, and formation pathway. The KTU 2024 syllabus places strong emphasis on distinguishing **primary** from **secondary** sources, as this distinction drives the selection of control equipment.

#### A. Natural Sources (Biogenic and Geogenic)

| Source | Dominant Particle Type | Typical Size |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Dust storms / wind-blown soil | Mineral dust (silicates, oxides) | $1\text{–}100 \;\mu m$ |
| Volcanic eruptions | Ash, sulphates, chlorides | $0.1\text{–}500 \;\mu m$ |
| Sea spray | NaCl, Mg salts, sulphates | $0.5\text{–}10 \;\mu m$ |
| Forest / vegetation fires | Soot (EC), OC, tar balls | $0.01\text{–}10 \;\mu m$ |
| Pollen, spores, bioaerosols | Biological debris | $5\text{–}100 \;\mu m$ |
| Cosmic / extraterrestrial | Meteoric dust | $< 1 \;\mu m$ |

#### B. Anthropogenic (Man-Made) Sources

| Source Category | Examples | Dominant Particle Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stationary combustion | Power plants, boilers, refuse burning | Fly ash, soot, sulphates |
| Mobile sources | Automobiles, diesel exhaust | EC, OC, nitrates, PAHs |
| Industrial processes | Cement, steel, smelters, mining | Metal oxides, dusts |
| Construction & demolition | Road works, building | Coarse mineral dust |
| Agricultural activities | Stubble burning, tilling, livestock | OC, NH$_4^+$, bioaerosols |
| Waste handling | Incineration, landfills | OC, dioxins, heavy metals |
| Indoor sources | Cooking, smoking, kerosene lamps | OC, EC, nicotine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary vs Secondary Particulates — the most testable distinction in KTU ESE:**
> - **Primary particulates** are emitted directly into the atmosphere in solid or liquid form (e.g., dust, soot from a chimney).
> - **Secondary particulates** are formed *in situ* in the atmosphere through chemical reactions of gaseous precursors. Key reactions include:
>   - $\text{SO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{oxidation}} \text{SO}_4^{2-}$ (sulphate aerosols)
>   - $\text{NO}_x \xrightarrow{\text{oxidation}} \text{NO}_3^{-}$ (nitrate aerosols)
>   - VOCs $\xrightarrow{\text{photochemistry}$ Secondary Organic Aerosol (SOA)

### 2.2 Particulate Source Inventory and Emission Strength

The **emission rate** from a source is a function of two measurable parameters: the concentration in the carrier stream and the volumetric flow rate.

$$E = C \times Q$$

Where:
- $E$ = emission rate (mass per unit time, e.g., $\text{g/s}$ or $\text{kg/h}$)
- $C$ = concentration of particulate in the stream ($\text{g/m}^3$ or $\text{mg/m}^3$)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate of the carrier gas ($\text{m}^3/\text{s}$)

For a stack, the actual flow rate must be corrected to **normal conditions** ($0^\circ C$, 1 atm) using the ideal gas law for accurate mass balance:

$$Q_N = Q_{act} \times \frac{T_N}{T_{act}} \times \frac{P_{act}}{P_N}$$

Where subscript $N$ denotes normal (standard) reference state.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E = C \cdot Q$ | Emission rate from concentration & flow | $\text{g/s}$ |
| 2 | $Q_N = Q \cdot (T_N/T) \cdot (P/P_N)$ | Normalization of flow to standard conditions | $\text{Nm}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 3 | $v_s = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, g}{18 \, \mu}$ | Stokes' terminal settling velocity | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 4 | $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho \, v_s \, d_p}{\mu}$ | Particle Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_c = 1 + \dfrac{2\lambda}{d_p}\left[1.257 + 0.4 \exp(-0.55 d_p/\lambda)\right]$ | Cunningham slip correction factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $v_s^{\prime} = v_s \cdot C_c$ | Corrected settling velocity for fine particles | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 7 | $\tau_s = \dfrac{H}{v_s}$ | Residence / settling time of particle | $\text{s}$ |
| 8 | $\tau_t = \dfrac{H}{u}$ | Transport time of air parcel | $\text{s}$ |
| 9 | $L = u \cdot \dfrac{H}{v_s}$ | Distance travelled before settling | $\text{m}$ |
| 10 | $\dot{m} = C \cdot u \cdot A$ | Mass flux per unit area (horizontal transport) | $\text{g/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$ |
| 11 | $E_f = \dfrac{m_{out}}{m_{in}}$ | Fractional collection / emission factor | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\rho_p^{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{m_p}{V_p}$ | Effective particle density | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Settling velocity is the foundational equation for designing **settling chambers**, **cyclone separators**, and predicting ambient particle residence times. Cunningham correction $C_c$ becomes significant only when $d_p < 1 \;\mu m$ (slip flow regime) — for coarse particles it can be ignored.

### 2.4 Physical Mechanism — Why Particles Stay Airborne

A particle's behaviour in air is governed by the balance of forces:

1. **Gravitational force** ($F_g = m_p g$) — pulls it down.
2. **Buoyant force** ($F_b = \rho_a V_p g$) — pushes it up (often negligible).
3. **Drag force** ($F_d = 3 \pi \mu d_p v$ for Stokes regime) — opposes motion.
4. **Electrostatic / Brownian forces** — dominate for ultrafine particles.

Equilibrium ($F_g = F_d$) yields the terminal settling velocity, the key to understanding how long a particle of a given source category remains airborne and how far it travels from its source.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Stokes' Terminal Settling Velocity (Low Reynolds Number)

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU Module 2 (Air Pollution Control). The student is expected to derive the terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle falling under gravity in a viscous fluid.

**Given:** A spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ and density $\rho_p$ falling in air of density $\rho_a$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Mass of the particle:**
$$m_p = \rho_p \cdot V_p = \rho_p \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3$$

**Step 2 — Buoyancy-corrected gravitational (net) force acting downward:**

The gravitational force is $F_g = m_p \, g$. The buoyant force (Archimedes' principle) is $F_b = \rho_a V_p g$ acting upward. The net downward force is therefore:

$$F_{net} = (\rho_p - \rho_a) \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Drag force in the Stokes regime (Re$_p < 1$):**

For very low Reynolds number flow, the drag force is given by Stokes' law:

$$F_d = 3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d_p \, v$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium (terminal velocity condition):**

At terminal velocity, the net downward force equals the upward drag force. The particle no longer accelerates.

$$3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d_p \, v_s = (\rho_p - \rho_a) \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot g$$

**Step 5 — Algebraic simplification:**

Cancel $\pi$ from both sides:

$$3 \, \mu \, d_p \, v_s = (\rho_p - \rho_a) \cdot \dfrac{d_p^3}{6} \cdot g$$

Divide both sides by $3 \, \mu \, d_p$:

$$v_s = \dfrac{(\rho_p - \rho_a) \, d_p^2 \, g}{18 \, \mu}$$

**Step 6 — Simplification for air (since $\rho_p \gg \rho_a$):**

For most particles of engineering interest, $\rho_p$ is at least 500–1000 times $\rho_a$, so the air density can be neglected in the numerator:

$$\boxed{\,v_s = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, g}{18 \, \mu}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Stokes' law is valid strictly for $Re_p < 1$. For higher $Re_p$, empirical corrections such as the **Schiller-Naumann correlation** $C_D = 24/Re_p \cdot (1 + 0.15 Re_p^{0.687})$ must be used.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Settling Velocity vs Source Type

We now apply the derived formula to compute $v_s$ for representative particulates from common sources. We use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$, and typical particle densities.

| Source | Particle Type | $d_p \;(\mu m)$ | $\rho_p \;(\text{kg/m}^3)$ | $v_s \;(\text{m/s})$ | Time to fall $10 \text{ m}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Soil dust | Silica | 50 | 2650 | $2.0 \times 10^{-1}$ | $\sim 50 \text{ s}$ |
| Fly ash (power plant) | Spherical | 10 | 2300 | $6.9 \times 10^{-3}$ | $\sim 24 \text{ min}$ |
| Cement kiln dust | Ca oxide | 5 | 3200 | $2.4 \times 10^{-3}$ | $\sim 1.2 \text{ hr}$ |
| Diesel soot (agglomerate) | EC aggregate | 0.1 | 1100 | $3.3 \times 10^{-6}$ | $\sim 35 \text{ days}$ |
| Sulphate aerosol (secondary) | NH$_4$)$_2$SO$_4$ | 0.5 | 1770 | $1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ | $\sim 23 \text{ hr}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matrix matters:** It explains *why* fine particulates (PM$_{2.5}$ and below) are far more dangerous than coarse dust. A 0.1 µm soot particle can remain airborne for over a month, travelling hundreds of kilometres from its source, while a 50 µm dust grain settles within a minute near its emission point.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Emission Rate from a Source

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A brick kiln emits flue gas at a flow rate of $12 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ at an actual temperature of $180^\circ C$ and a stack pressure of $101.3 \text{ kPa}$. The measured particulate concentration in the stack is $850 \text{ mg/m}^3$. Calculate: (a) the actual emission rate in g/s, and (b) the emission rate in $\text{Nm}^3/\text{s}$ basis.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Actual emission rate:**

$$E_{act} = C \times Q_{act} = 850 \text{ mg/m}^3 \times 12 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$E_{act} = 10{,}200 \text{ mg/s} = 10.2 \text{ g/s}$$

**Part (b) — Normalized flow rate:**

Convert temperature to Kelvin: $T = 180 + 273 = 453 \text{ K}$. Reference: $T_N = 273 \text{ K}$, $P_N = 101.3 \text{ kPa}$, $P = 101.3 \text{ kPa}$.

$$Q_N = Q_{act} \times \dfrac{T_N}{T} \times \dfrac{P}{P_N} = 12 \times \dfrac{273}{453} \times 1$$

$$Q_N = 7.23 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Normalized emission rate:**

$$E_N = 850 \text{ mg/m}^3 \times 7.23 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{s} = 6145 \text{ mg/s} = 6.15 \text{ g/s}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam tip:** Always state whether the emission rate is on *actual* or *normal* basis. KTU examiners deduct marks if the student computes $E$ without specifying the reference state.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification of Particulate Sources

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["PARTICULATE MATTER SOURCES"]
    B["NATURAL SOURCES"]

```

### 4.2 Primary vs Secondary Formation Pathways

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph P1["PRIMARY PATHWAY - DIRECT EMISSION"]
        S1["Solid source: dust, soot, fly ash"]
        E1["Direct discharge from stack or vent"]

    end
    subgraph P2["SECONDARY PATHWAY - IN ATMOSPHERE"]
        G1["Gaseous precursor: SO2, NOx, VOCs, NH3"]
        R1["Photochemical and aqueous oxidation"]

        AP2["Ambient PM concentration rises"]
    end
    S1 --> E1 --> AP1
    G1 --> R1 --> SP1 --> AP2
```

### 4.3 Source-to-Receptor Functional Flow

```mermaid

    A0["SOURCE: Combustion / Dust / Volcano"]
    A1["EMISSION: Primary PM into atmosphere"]
    A2["TRANSPORT: Advection and diffusion"]

    A4["REMOVAL: Dry and wet deposition"]
    A5["RECEPTOR: Human lungs, vegetation, structures"]
    A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5
```

### 4.4 Particle Size Spectrum and Source Association

