
> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block diagram maps the *functional flow* from a particulate source to its eventual deposition at a receptor. This is the conceptual framework that all control strategies (Module 3 onwards) are built upon — either prevent emission, intercept transport, or remove the particle before it reaches the receptor.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set 2]**
*Distinguish between primary and secondary particulate matter with two examples each.*

**Model Answer:**

**Primary Particulates:** These are emitted directly into the atmosphere in solid or liquid form from a source. They do not undergo chemical transformation in the air before measurement.

Examples:
1. Fly ash from a coal-fired thermal power plant stack.
2. Wind-blown soil dust from agricultural fields.

**Secondary Particulates:** These are formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions of gaseous precursors (e.g., SO$_2$, NO$_x$, VOCs, NH$_3$) involving oxidation, photolysis, and gas-to-particle conversion.

Examples:
1. Sulphate aerosols (H$_2$SO$_4$, (NH$_4$)$_2$SO$_4$) formed by atmospheric oxidation of SO$_2$.
2. Secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formed by photo-oxidation of biogenic or anthropogenic VOCs.

The key distinction is that secondary particulates are **not emitted** but **created** in the atmosphere, often hundreds of kilometres downwind of the precursor source.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Paper]**
*Define aerodynamic diameter. Why is it preferred over geometric (physical) diameter for characterising airborne particulates?*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The aerodynamic diameter $d_a$ of an arbitrary particle is defined as the diameter of a unit-density sphere ($\rho_0 = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$) that has the same terminal settling velocity in still air as the particle in question.

**Why preferred over geometric diameter:**

1. **Shape irregularity:** Atmospheric particles are rarely spherical (e.g., chain-like soot agglomerates, flake-like mineral dust). Geometric diameter is ambiguous for non-spherical bodies.
2. **Behavioural relevance:** Health impacts (lung deposition), control equipment performance (cyclones, scrubbers), and atmospheric residence time all depend on how a particle *moves* in air — and this motion depends on the *aerodynamic* size, not the physical size.
3. **Standardisation:** Regulatory standards (NAAQ, WHO, USEPA) are framed in terms of PM$_{10}$ and PM$_{2.5}$ — i.e., by aerodynamic cut-off — enabling uniform monitoring across instruments worldwide.

---

### PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Paper Module 2, Q2]**

> **(a)** With neat classification diagrams, describe the major natural and anthropogenic sources of airborne particulate matter. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A cement plant emits particulates at a concentration of $1200 \text{ mg/m}^3$ through a stack of cross-sectional area $1.5 \text{ m}^2$ with an exit gas velocity of $8 \text{ m/s}$. Calculate the actual mass emission rate in $\text{kg/h}$. If the exit gas temperature is $200^\circ C$ at $101.3 \text{ kPa}$, also calculate the emission rate in $\text{g/s}$ on a normal basis. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of particulate sources (7 marks):**

Particulate sources are broadly classified into **Natural** and **Anthropogenic** categories.

**Natural sources (3.5 marks):**
- **Geogenic sources:** Wind-blown soil and dust (silica, clay, carbonates), volcanic eruptions (ash, sulphates, halides), sea spray (NaCl, Mg salts), meteoric dust.
- **Biogenic sources:** Pollen, fungal spores, bacteria, plant debris, forest and grassland fires (biomass burning emits both primary OC and secondary aerosol precursors).

**Anthropogenic sources (3.5 marks):**
- **Stationary combustion:** Coal-fired power plants, biomass burning, incinerators, domestic cooking.
- **Mobile sources:** Petrol and especially diesel vehicles, aircraft, ships.
- **Industrial processes:** Cement manufacturing, metallurgy, mining, oil refining, paper mills.
- **Miscellaneous:** Construction and demolition, agricultural stubble burning, road dust re-suspension, waste disposal.

A further sub-classification by *formation pathway* is **Primary** (direct emission) versus **Secondary** (formed in atmosphere from SO$_2$, NO$_x$, VOCs).

A schematic classification block diagram should accompany this answer (as in Section 4.1).

**(b) Mass emission rate calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow rate of the stack gas:**

$$Q_{act} = A \times v = 1.5 \text{ m}^2 \times 8 \text{ m/s} = 12 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Actual emission rate:**

$$E_{act} = C \times Q_{act} = 1200 \text{ mg/m}^3 \times 12 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$E_{act} = 14{,}400 \text{ mg/s} = 14.4 \text{ g/s}$$

Converting to $\text{kg/h}$:

$$E_{act} = 14.4 \text{ g/s} \times \dfrac{3600 \text{ s}}{1 \text{ h}} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ kg}}{1000 \text{ g}} = 51.84 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to normal (NTP) flow:**

$T = 200 + 273 = 473 \text{ K}$, $T_N = 273 \text{ K}$, $P = P_N = 101.3 \text{ kPa}$

$$Q_N = Q_{act} \times \dfrac{T_N}{T} \times \dfrac{P}{P_N} = 12 \times \dfrac{273}{473} \times 1 = 6.93 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Normalized emission rate:**

$$E_N = 1200 \text{ mg/m}^3 \times 6.93 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{s} = 8316 \text{ mg/s} = 8.32 \text{ g/s}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\mathbf{E_{act} = 51.84 \; kg/h}$ or $14.4 \text{ g/s}$ on actual basis.
- $\mathbf{E_N = 8.32 \; g/s}$ on normal basis.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper, Module 2, Q3]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle falling under gravity through a viscous fluid (Stokes' regime). State clearly the assumptions used. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A fly-ash particle of density $2200 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and diameter $20 \;\mu m$ settles through air of viscosity $1.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$ and density $1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Calculate the terminal settling velocity, the distance it travels in 1 hour, and verify that Stokes' law is applicable. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of terminal settling velocity (7 marks):**

Refer to the **complete derivation given in Section 3.1** of these notes. The student should reproduce the following logical steps:

1. Express mass of the spherical particle: $m_p = \rho_p \cdot (\pi/6) d_p^3$.
2. Write the net downward force (gravity minus buoyancy): $F_{net} = (\rho_p - \rho_a) \cdot (\pi/6) d_p^3 \cdot g$.
3. State Stokes' drag: $F_d = 3 \pi \mu d_p v$.
4. Apply equilibrium at terminal velocity: $F_d = F_{net}$.
5. Solve for $v_s = (\rho_p - \rho_a) g d_p^2 / (18 \mu) \approx \rho_p g d_p^2 / (18 \mu)$ (since $\rho_a \ll \rho_p$).

**Assumptions:**
- Particle is a perfect sphere.
- Flow regime is laminar (Re$_p < 1$, no wake formation).
- Particle falls in an infinite, quiescent medium.
- No slip between particle and fluid far from the surface (continuum assumption).
- Particle density is uniform and much greater than fluid density.
- Particle does not deform during fall and no electrostatic interactions.

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $d_p = 20 \;\mu m = 20 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- $\rho_p = 2200 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Terminal settling velocity:**

$$v_s = \dfrac{(\rho_p - \rho_a) \, g \, d_p^2}{18 \, \mu}$$

$$v_s = \dfrac{(2200 - 1.2) \times 9.81 \times (20 \times 10^{-6})^2}{18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$v_s = \dfrac{2198.8 \times 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-10}}{3.258 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$v_s = \dfrac{8.628 \times 10^{-6}}{3.258 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0265 \text{ m/s}$$

So $v_s \approx 2.65 \text{ cm/s}$.

**Step 2 — Distance travelled in 1 hour:**

$$d = v_s \times t = 0.0265 \text{ m/s} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 95.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Verify Stokes' law applicability (compute $Re_p$):**

$$Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_a \, v_s \, d_p}{\mu} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 0.0265 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re_p = \dfrac{6.36 \times 10^{-7}}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.0351$$

Since $Re_p = 0.035 \ll 1$, **Stokes' law is valid** for this particle.

**Final Answers:**
- $\mathbf{v_s = 0.0265 \; m/s} \;(2.65 \text{ cm/s})$
- $\mathbf{d = 95.4 \; m}$ in 1 hour
- $\mathbf{Re_p = 0.035 < 1}$ → Stokes' law applicable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** during Stokes' derivation — costs 2 marks even if the algebra is perfect.
> 2. **Confusing primary and secondary particulates** by writing vague definitions; the KTU key requires explicit examples of *both* gaseous precursors and end products.
> 3. **Mixing up aerodynamic vs geometric diameter** in definitions — examiners explicitly look for the unit-density sphere clarification.
> 4. **Not normalising the flow rate to NTP** in emission calculations — a 2-mark penalty is typical when the units on $Q$ are inconsistent.
> 5. **Failing to verify $Re_p < 1$** after computing $v_s$ — examiners award a "validity check" mark only if the student explicitly computes and interprets the Reynolds number.
> 6. **Not specifying the basis (actual or normal)** of the final emission rate answer — a frequent, costly omission.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Particulate Matter (PM)** = a heterogeneous mixture of solid particles and liquid droplets suspended in air; characterised by **aerodynamic diameter** ($d_a$).
- **Operational cut-offs:** PM$_{10}$ ($\leq 10 \;\mu m$), PM$_{2.5}$ ($\leq 2.5 \;\mu m$), Ultrafine ($\leq 0.1 \;\mu m$).
- **Source categories:** Natural (geogenic + biogenic) vs Anthropogenic (combustion + industrial + miscellaneous).
- **Formation pathway:** **Primary** (direct emission) vs **Secondary** (formed in atmosphere from SO$_2$, NO$_x$, VOCs, NH$_3$).
- **Stokes' settling velocity:** $v_s = \rho_p g d_p^2 / (18 \mu)$ — valid only for $Re_p < 1$, spherical particles, continuum regime.
- **Cunningham correction** $C_c$ required for $d_p < 1 \;\mu m$ (slip flow).
- **Emission rate formula:** $E = C \times Q$; always normalise to NTP for regulatory reporting.
- **Fine particles are far more persistent** — a $0.1 \;\mu m$ soot particle can remain airborne for ~35 days, travelling inter-regional distances.
- **Sample sources of secondary PM:** Sulphate from SO$_2$ oxidation; nitrate from NO$_x$ oxidation; SOA from VOC photo-oxidation.
- **Always state assumptions, units, and basis (actual or normal)** in numerical answers to earn full marks in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Nature and Adverse Effects - Control of Particulates - Settling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Particulate Matter (PM)

**Particulate Matter (PM)** is a complex, heterogeneous mixture of extremely small solid particles and liquid droplets suspended in the atmosphere. The physical and chemical characteristics of these particulates vary widely depending on their source, formation mechanism, and atmospheric residence time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> Particulate matter is classified primarily by **aerodynamic diameter (Dₐ)**, not physical size, because it is the aerodynamic behavior that determines how the particle moves in air and where it deposits in the human respiratory system.

### Standard Classification Thresholds

| Abbreviation | Aerodynamic Diameter Cutoff | Regulatory Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **TSP** (Total Suspended Particulates) | $\le 50\ \mu m$ | Legacy metric; rarely used in new standards |
| **PM₁₀** (Thoracic Particles) | $\le 10\ \mu m$ | Can penetrate beyond the larynx |
| **PM₂.₅** (Fine Particles) | $\le 2.5\ \mu m$ | Reach alveoli; cause systemic effects |
| **Ultrafine PM** | $\le 0.1\ \mu m$ | Cross pulmonary barrier into bloodstream |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units of Measurement:** Concentration is reported in **µg/m³** (micrograms per cubic meter) for ambient air, and **mg/m³** or **g/m³** for source emissions and stack monitoring.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sunbeam Phenomenon"

Imagine walking into a dark, dusty room and switching on a flashlight. The visible streaks in the beam are tiny airborne particulates, suspended by the slightest air currents. Now, if you wait quietly for 5 minutes, the larger, heavier specks slowly drift down to the floor. The smallest specks, however, still float indefinitely. This is precisely the principle exploited by an **industrial gravity settling chamber**.

- **Large/heavy particles (Dₐ > 50 µm)** behave like the big specks — gravity pulls them down quickly, so they are easy to capture.
- **Fine particles (Dₐ < 10 µm)** behave like the small specks — they have such a large surface-area-to-mass ratio and low inertia that they essentially "ignore" gravity and stay airborne.

This intuition is critical to understanding **why settling chambers are effective only for coarse dust**, and why finer particles require cyclones, scrubbers, fabric filters, or ESPs.

---

## 1.3 Nature (Physical & Chemical Characteristics) of Particulates

### 1.3.1 Physical Characteristics
1. **Size** — ranges from ~0.001 µm (molecular clusters) to ~500 µm (visible dust).
2. **Shape** — irregular, fibrous, or spherical; affects drag coefficient.
3. **Density** — varies from ~0.5 g/cm³ (oily mists) to >5 g/cm³ (metallic oxides).
4. **Surface area** — extremely high for fine particles; drives heterogeneous chemistry.
5. **Hygroscopicity** — tendency to absorb water; affects size and visibility effects.
6. **Optical properties** — light scattering coefficient drives visibility reduction.

### 1.3.2 Chemical Nature
- **Inorganic fraction:** SO₄²⁻, NO₃⁻, Cl⁻, silicates, metal oxides (Fe, Pb, Cd, Hg).
- **Organic fraction:** PAHs, VOCs condensed on soot, dioxins, furans.
- **Carbonaceous core:** Elemental carbon (EC) and Organic carbon (OC) — the "soot" fraction.

---

## 1.4 Adverse Effects of Particulates

> [!WARNING]
> Particulate pollution is the **single largest environmental health risk globally** according to WHO data, exceeding even the combined impact of other criteria pollutants in mortality terms.

### 1.4.1 Human Health Effects
| Effect Category | Affected Particle Size | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Eye & throat irritation** | Coarse PM (2.5–10 µm) | Mechanical deposition |
| **Asthma / bronchitis** | PM₁₀ | Deposition in bronchi |
| **Cardiopulmonary mortality** | PM₂.₅ | Alveolar penetration, systemic inflammation |
| **Lung cancer** | PM₂.₅ (especially diesel soot) | Genotoxic PAH adsorption |
| **Translocation to blood** | Ultrafine (<0.1 µm) | Cross alveolar-capillary barrier |

### 1.4.2 Environmental & Material Effects
- **Visibility reduction (haze):** Mie scattering by fine particles.
- **Acid deposition:** SO₄²⁻ and NO₃⁻ aerosols cause acid rain.
- **Climate forcing:** Black carbon warms; sulfates cool — net effect is significant.
- **Soiling of buildings:** Sulfation reaction on marble/limestone ($\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$).
- **Crop yield loss:** Deposition on leaves blocks stomata.
- **Ecosystem eutrophication:** Nutrient-laden dust deposition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Particle Categories by Formation Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the distinction between **primary** and **secondary** particulates. Memorize this binary.

1. **Primary particulates** — emitted directly as solid/liquid in the flue gas.
   Examples: Fly ash from coal combustion, dust from cement kilns, metal fumes.
2. **Secondary particulates** — formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions of precursor gases.
   Examples: Sulfate aerosols from SO₂ oxidation, nitrate aerosols from NOₓ, secondary organic aerosols (SOA) from VOCs.

---

## 2.2 Gravity Settling — Theoretical Foundation

A **gravity settling chamber** (also called a **dust-settling chamber** or **inertial separator**) is the simplest, oldest, and cheapest particulate control device. It relies on a single physical principle: **the horizontal gas stream slows down inside a large expansion chamber, giving entrained particles enough residence time to fall out under gravity**.

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle (Bulleted Logic)
- **Step 1:** Particle-laden gas enters the chamber at horizontal velocity $u$.
- **Step 2:** Cross-sectional area expands, so gas velocity drops sharply (continuity: $u \cdot A = \text{constant}$).
- **Step 3:** Gas residence time $t_r = L/u$ becomes long (typically 1–3 seconds).
- **Step 4:** During $t_r$, the particle must fall a vertical distance equal to the chamber height $H$.
- **Step 5:** If terminal settling velocity $v_t$ satisfies $v_t \cdot t_r \ge H$, the particle is captured on the floor.
- **Step 6:** Clean gas exits; collected dust is removed by a hopper and screw conveyor.

### 2.2.2 The "Why" Behind Low Efficiency
A particle is captured only if $v_t \ge H/t_r$. Solving for the **cut diameter** $d_c$ (the smallest particle that is 100% collected under ideal Stokes flow):

$$d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\ \mu\ u_s\ H}{g\ L\ (\rho_p - \rho)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For typical industrial conditions, $d_c$ is in the range **40–100 µm**. This is why settling chambers are **not** used for fine dust control — they are only suitable for **coarse, non-hazardous dust** like sawdust, grain dust, or large fly-ash particles.

---

## 2.3 Terminal Settling Velocity — The Three Regimes

The terminal settling velocity $v_t$ is reached when the gravitational force on the particle is exactly balanced by the aerodynamic drag. The drag force is $F_D = C_D \cdot A_p \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_t^2$, and the formulation changes with **Reynolds number** $\text{Re}_p = \rho v_t d / \mu$.

### 2.3.1 Regime Boundaries
| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number $\text{Re}_p$ | Drag Coefficient $C_D$ | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stokes (laminar)** | $\text{Re}_p < 1$ | $C_D = 24/\text{Re}_p$ | $v_t = \dfrac{g\,d^2(\rho_p-\rho)}{18\mu}$ |
| **Transition** | $1 < \text{Re}_p < 10^3$ | Empirical (e.g., Schiller–Naumann) | Iterative solution required |
| **Newton (turbulent)** | $\text{Re}_p > 10^3$ | $C_D \approx 0.44$ | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\,g\,d\,(\rho_p-\rho)}{\rho}}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In Part B problems, you will almost always be told (or expected to verify) that the flow is in the **Stokes regime**. Always confirm $\text{Re}_p < 1$ after computing $v_t$, or your answer is wrong.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols use SI units. Always show units in your final answer. **In exam answers, marks are deducted for missing units.**

| # | Quantity | Symbol / Equation | Unit | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds number | $\text{Re}_p = \dfrac{\rho\, v_t\, d}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Check flow regime |
| 2 | Stokes terminal velocity | $v_t = \dfrac{g\,d^2(\rho_p-\rho)}{18\mu}$ | m/s | $\text{Re}_p < 1$ |
| 3 | Newton terminal velocity | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\,g\,d(\rho_p-\rho)}{\rho}}$ | m/s | $\text{Re}_p > 10^3$ |
| 4 | Residence time | $t_r = L/u_s$ | s | Settling chamber |
| 5 | Chamber efficiency (laminar) | $\eta = \dfrac{v_t \cdot L}{H \cdot u_s}$ | fraction | Single-pass settling |
| 6 | Cut diameter | $d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\mu u_s H}{g L(\rho_p-\rho)}}$ | m | Design parameter |
| 7 | Cunningham slip correction | $C_c = 1 + \dfrac{\lambda}{d}\left(2.34 + 1.05\ e^{-0.39\,d/\lambda}\right)$ | dimensionless | $d < 5\ \mu m$ |
| 8 | Corrected $v_t$ (sub-micron) | $v_t^{'} = v_t \cdot C_c$ | m/s | Slip flow regime |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cement industry:** Pre-cleaner before electrostatic precipitators (ESPs); reduces loading.
- **Boiler plants:** Captures coarse fly-ash (≥50 µm) before stack release.
- **Woodworking & grain handling:** Removes combustible sawdust/dust — fire/explosion prevention.
- **Mining & quarrying:** Pre-separation of coarse ore dust.
- **Lead smelters:** Reduces total mass; ESP or baghouse follows for fine Pb fume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering trade-off:** Settling chambers have very low capital and operating cost, **zero pressure drop penalty** (negligible), and require no water/chemicals. Their disadvantage is the **massive floor space** required ($L$ is large), making them impractical above ~5 m³/s gas flow.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Terminal Settling Velocity in the Stokes Regime

### 3.1.1 Free-Body Diagram (Conceptual Setup)
A spherical particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_p$ falls through fluid of density $\rho$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$. Three forces act on it:
1. **Weight (down):** $W = m_p \, g = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \rho_p \, g$
2. **Buoyancy (up):** $F_B = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \rho \, g$
3. **Drag (up, opposing motion):** $F_D = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_t^2$

### 3.1.2 Force Balance at Terminal Velocity
At terminal velocity, acceleration is zero, so net force is zero:

$$W - F_B - F_D = 0$$

Substituting all three:

$$\dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}\rho_p\,g - \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}\rho\,g - C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_t^2 = 0$$

Cancel the common factor $\pi d^2/12$ throughout:

$$2 d\,g(\rho_p - \rho) - \tfrac{3}{2} C_D\, \rho\, v_t^2 = 0$$

Solve for $v_t^2$:

$$v_t^2 = \dfrac{4 d\,g(\rho_p - \rho)}{3 C_D \rho}$$

Take square root:

$$v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 d\,g(\rho_p - \rho)}{3 C_D \rho}} \quad \text{(General, valid for any regime)}$$

### 3.1.3 Specialization to Stokes Regime
In the Stokes regime, $C_D = 24/\text{Re}_p = 24\mu/(\rho v_t d)$. Substitute into the general expression:

$$v_t^2 = \dfrac{4 d\,g(\rho_p - \rho)}{3 \cdot \dfrac{24\mu}{\rho v_t d} \cdot \rho} = \dfrac{4 d\,g(\rho_p-\rho) \cdot v_t d}{72\mu}$$

Multiply both sides by $72\mu$:

$$72\mu v_t^2 = 4 d^2 g (\rho_p - \rho) v_t$$

Divide both sides by $4 v_t$ (assuming $v_t \ne 0$):

$$18\mu v_t = d^2 g (\rho_p - \rho)$$

Rearrange:

$$\boxed{v_t = \dfrac{g\,d^2(\rho_p - \rho)}{18\mu}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Stokes' Law** expression. Note the **quadratic dependence on diameter $d$** — halving the particle diameter reduces the settling velocity by a factor of 4, which is why fine particles are so hard to remove by gravity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Collection Efficiency of a Settling Chamber

Consider a rectangular chamber of **length $L$** and **height $H$** (depth $W$ into the page is irrelevant for this 2-D analysis). Gas flows horizontally with mean velocity $u_s$.

### 3.2.1 Trajectory of a Particle Entering at the Top
A particle entering at the inlet ceiling (height $H$) must fall a distance $H$ to be captured. The time available is the residence time:

$$t_r = \dfrac{L}{u_s}$$

A particle is captured **only if** it falls distance $\ge H$ during $t_r$:

$$v_t \cdot t_r \ge H \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_t \ge \dfrac{H \cdot u_s}{L}$$

### 3.2.2 Efficiency for a Given Particle Size
A particle entering at any vertical position $y$ (measured from the floor, $0 \le y \le H$) will reach the floor only if $v_t \cdot t_r \ge (H - y)$. So the fraction of the inlet cross-section whose particles are captured is:

$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{captured inlet area}}{\text{total inlet area}} = \dfrac{v_t \cdot t_r}{H} = \dfrac{v_t \cdot L}{H \cdot u_s}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\eta = \dfrac{v_t \cdot L}{H \cdot u_s} = \dfrac{g\,d^2(\rho_p-\rho)\,L}{18\mu\,H\,u_s}}$$

### 3.2.3 Efficiency Constraint
The expression is valid only when $\eta \le 1$. If the computed $\eta > 1$, the chamber is **over-designed** for that size — all particles of that size (and larger) are 100% collected.

> [!NOTE]
> The formula assumes **laminar plug flow** and **uniform inlet distribution**. Real chambers achieve only 50–70% of the theoretical value due to turbulence, re-entrainment, and dead zones. A **multi-tray / Howard-type** design is often used to improve efficiency by creating many parallel horizontal settling zones (effectively reducing $H$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cut Diameter (Diameter of 100% Captured Particle)

Setting $\eta = 1$ in the efficiency formula and solving for $d$:

$$1 = \dfrac{g\,d_c^2(\rho_p-\rho)\,L}{18\mu\,H\,u_s}$$

Rearrange:

$$d_c^2 = \dfrac{18\mu\,H\,u_s}{g\,L(\rho_p-\rho)}$$

Take square root:

$$\boxed{d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\mu\,u_s\,H}{g\,L\,(\rho_p-\rho)}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Students often use the gas density $\rho$ in the numerator (i.e., subtract it as $(\rho_p - \rho)$), but for air at standard conditions $\rho = 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$ is small compared to typical particle densities (1000–5000 kg/m³), so it's often neglected for **rough work**. In KTU 14-mark problems, you should show the full expression and then justify the approximation.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A settling chamber handles a gas stream of density $\rho = 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and viscosity $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ flowing at $u_s = 1.5\ \text{m/s}$. The chamber is $L = 12\ \text{m}$ long, $H = 2.5\ \text{m}$ high. Particles have density $\rho_p = 2400\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and diameter $d = 60\ \mu m$. Find:
(a) The terminal settling velocity.
(b) The collection efficiency.
(c) The cut diameter.

### 3.3.1 Step (a): Terminal Settling Velocity
Using Stokes' law:

$$v_t = \dfrac{g\,d^2(\rho_p - \rho)}{18\mu}$$

Insert values: $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$, $d = 60 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$, $\rho_p - \rho \approx 2398.8\ \text{kg/m}^3$:

$$v_t = \dfrac{9.81 \cdot (60 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot 2398.8}{18 \cdot 1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$$

Compute numerator: $9.81 \times 3600 \times 10^{-12} \times 2398.8 = 8.471 \times 10^{-5}$

Compute denominator: $18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} = 3.24 \times 10^{-4}$

$$v_t = \dfrac{8.471 \times 10^{-5}}{3.24 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.2615\ \text{m/s}$$

Verify Stokes regime: $\text{Re}_p = \dfrac{1.2 \times 0.2615 \times 60 \times 10^{-6}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.046$

> [!WARNING]
> $\text{Re}_p \approx 1.05$ is **borderline** — strictly speaking, this is just outside Stokes regime. In exam answers, you should state this and note that the Stokes result is a **slight overestimate** of the true $v_t$. For a 14-mark answer, you may also be asked to apply the **Schiller–Naumann** correction:
> $C_D = 24/\text{Re}_p (1 + 0.15\,\text{Re}_p^{0.687})$
> and re-solve iteratively.

### 3.3.2 Step (b): Collection Efficiency

$$\eta = \dfrac{v_t \cdot L}{H \cdot u_s} = \dfrac{0.2615 \times 12}{2.5 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{3.138}{3.75} = 0.8368 \approx 83.7\%$$

### 3.3.3 Step (c): Cut Diameter

$$d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\mu\,u_s\,H}{g\,L(\rho_p - \rho)}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \times 1.8\times 10^{-5} \times 1.5 \times 2.5}{9.81 \times 12 \times 2398.8}}$$

Numerator: $18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.5 \times 2.5 = 1.215 \times 10^{-3}$

Denominator: $9.81 \times 12 \times 2398.8 = 2.8234 \times 10^{5}$

$$d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.215 \times 10^{-3}}{2.8234 \times 10^{5}}} = \sqrt{4.303 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.56 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 65.6\ \mu m$$

**Interpretation:** Particles of diameter $\ge 65.6\ \mu m$ are 100% collected; 60 µm particles (our test case) are collected at 83.7% — consistent with the formula.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between primary and secondary particulate pollutants. Give two examples of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
**Primary particulates** are emitted directly into the atmosphere in solid or liquid form from a source.
Examples: Fly ash from coal combustion, dust from cement manufacturing.

**Secondary particulates** are formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions of gaseous precursors (SO₂, NOₓ, VOCs, NH₃).
Examples: Sulfate aerosols ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ mist) from SO₂ oxidation; nitrate aerosols from NOₓ reaction with ammonia.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marker logic:** 1 mark for the correct definition of each; 0.5 mark per example. Total 3 marks.

---

### Q2. Why is the efficiency of a gravity settling chamber very low for fine particles? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The terminal settling velocity is given by Stokes' law: $v_t = \dfrac{g\,d^2(\rho_p - \rho)}{18\mu}$.
Since $v_t$ is proportional to $d^2$, halving the particle diameter reduces the settling velocity by a factor of 4. For very fine particles (say, $d < 10\ \mu m$), $v_t$ becomes so small (typically $< 0.01\ \text{m/s}$) that the particle does not have time to fall the chamber height $H$ before the gas exits. Consequently, the collection efficiency $\eta = v_t L / (H u_s)$ becomes very small, and the chamber fails to capture fine dust.

> [!NOTE]
> **2 marks for explaining the $d^2$ dependence; 1 mark for linking it to the efficiency formula and giving a numerical argument.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End Semester Examinations follow a **module-internal choice** pattern. **Answer either Question A or Question B in full.** Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **gravity settling chamber** for particulate control. Discuss its merits, demerits, and typical applications. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A settling chamber is 8 m long, 2 m high, and handles 25 000 m³/h of flue gas at 150 °C. The particle density is 1800 kg/m³ and the gas viscosity at this temperature is $2.4 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. Determine:
1. The cut diameter $d_c$ of the chamber.
2. The collection efficiency for 40 µm particles.
3. Whether the Stokes regime assumption is valid for the 40 µm particles. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Settling Chamber Description (7 Marks)**

A gravity settling chamber is a large, empty, rectangular expansion chamber installed in a gas duct. The particulate-laden gas enters through a sudden expansion that drops the velocity. Because the chamber is wide, the gas residence time (1–3 s) is long enough for coarse particles to fall under gravity onto a collection floor. The collected dust slides into a hopper and is removed by a screw conveyor or rotary valve. Clean gas exits through an outlet duct on the far end. **(3 marks for sketch + description)**

*Merits:*
- Very low capital cost; no moving parts; negligible pressure drop; no water or chemicals required. **(1.5 marks)**

*Demerits:*
- Very large floor space required; effective only for particles > 50 µm; efficiency is 40–60% in practice due to turbulence. **(1.5 marks)**

*Applications:* Pre-cleaner in cement, fertilizer, and boiler plants; wood-dust collection in sawmills. **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

*Step 1: Gas velocity* $u_s$
Cross-sectional area $A = 2\ \text{m (height)} \times W$ (assume width, but for $u_s$ use total flow / area — in this simplified problem, we treat chamber as 2 m × 2 m effective, so $A = 4\ \text{m}^2$):
$$u_s = \dfrac{25000/3600}{4} = 1.736\ \text{m/s}$$

*Step 2: Cut diameter*
Using $d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\mu u_s H}{g L (\rho_p - \rho)}}$ with $\rho$ neglected (or $\rho \approx 0.7$ at 150 °C, $\rho_p - \rho \approx 1799.3$):
$$d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.736 \times 2}{9.81 \times 8 \times 1799.3}}$$

Numerator: $18 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.736 \times 2 = 1.500 \times 10^{-3}$
Denominator: $9.81 \times 8 \times 1799.3 = 1.412 \times 10^{5}$

$$d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.500 \times 10^{-3}}{1.412 \times 10^{5}}} = \sqrt{1.062 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.031 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} \approx 103\ \mu m$$

$$\boxed{d_c \approx 103\ \mu m}$$

*Step 3: Settling velocity of 40 µm particles*
$$v_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times (40 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 1799.3}{18 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{9.81 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-9} \times 1799.3}{4.32 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0654\ \text{m/s}$$

*Step 4: Efficiency for 40 µm particles*
$$\eta = \dfrac{v_t \cdot L}{H \cdot u_s} = \dfrac{0.0654 \times 8}{2 \times 1.736} = \dfrac{0.5232}{3.472} = 0.1507$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 15.1\%}$$

*Step 5: Verify Stokes regime*
$$\text{Re}_p = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v_t \cdot d}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.7 \times 0.0654 \times 40 \times 10^{-6}}{2.4 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.0763$$

Since $\text{Re}_p = 0.0763 < 1$, **Stokes regime is valid**. ✓ **(1 mark for the verification step)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Total marks distribution for Part (b):** 2 marks for $d_c$, 2 marks for $\eta$, 2 marks for Stokes verification, 1 mark for unit consistency and final answer presentation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What are the major **adverse effects of particulate pollution** on human health, visibility, materials, and climate? Discuss the special significance of **PM₂.₅** in the context of cardiopulmonary mortality. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A gas stream at 200 °C ($\mu = 2.6 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $\rho = 0.75\ \text{kg/m}^3$) carries spherical particles of density $\rho_p = 2000\ \text{kg/m}^3$ at a horizontal velocity of $2.0\ \text{m/s}$ through a settling chamber that is 10 m long and 1.5 m high. Calculate:
1. The diameter of the largest particle that is **not** collected (assuming the chamber collects particles whose settling velocity $v_t \ge H \cdot u_s / L$).
2. The efficiency of collection for 50 µm particles.
3. Comment on the practicality of the design. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Adverse Effects (7 Marks)**

*Human Health (2 marks):*
Particulates cause eye and throat irritation (coarse PM), bronchitis and asthma (PM₁₀), and most critically, **cardiopulmonary mortality** due to PM₂.₅ penetration into alveoli, triggering systemic inflammation, oxidative stress, and cardiac arrhythmias. PM₂.₅ is associated with **lung cancer** due to adsorbed PAHs and genotoxic species.

*Visibility (1 mark):*
Fine particles (0.1–1 µm) scatter visible light most efficiently (Mie scattering regime), reducing visual range and causing regional haze.

*Materials (1 mark):*
Sulfuric acid mist and SO₂ in the presence of PM catalyze **sulfation of marble and limestone**: $\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$, damaging heritage buildings and statues. Metals corrode faster.

*Climate (1 mark):*
**Black carbon** (a primary PM component) absorbs solar radiation and **warms the atmosphere**. **Sulfate aerosols** reflect sunlight and have a **cooling effect**. The net aerosol forcing is one of the largest uncertainties in climate models.

*Special significance of PM₂.₅ (2 marks):*
PM₂.₅ can reach the **alveolar region** of the lungs where gas exchange occurs. Its high surface-area-to-mass ratio means it carries concentrated toxic species (PAHs, heavy metals) deep into the body. Epidemiological studies (e.g., the Harvard Six Cities Study) established that every $10\ \mu g/m^3$ rise in PM₂.₅ is associated with a **6–8% increase in all-cause mortality**. WHO has set the 2021 guideline at $5\ \mu g/m^3$ annual mean.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

*Step 1: Critical settling velocity*
$$v_{t,\text{crit}} = \dfrac{H \cdot u_s}{L} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 2.0}{10} = 0.30\ \text{m/s}$$

*Step 2: Diameter of largest non-collected particle (cut diameter)*
Using Stokes' law (assume valid; verify later):
$$d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \mu \cdot v_{t,\text{crit}}}{g (\rho_p - \rho)}}$$

$$d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \times 2.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.30}{9.81 \times (2000 - 0.75)}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{1.404 \times 10^{-4}}{1.962 \times 10^{4}}} = \sqrt{7.155 \times 10^{-9}} = 8.46 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} \approx 84.6\ \mu m$$

So **particles of diameter ≥ 84.6 µm are 100% collected**; the **largest non-collected** particle has diameter just under 84.6 µm. **(2 marks)**

*Step 3: Efficiency for 50 µm particles*
$$v_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times (50 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 1999.25}{18 \times 2.6 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{9.81 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 1999.25}{4.68 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.1048\ \text{m/s}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{v_t \cdot L}{H \cdot u_s} = \dfrac{0.1048 \times 10}{1.5 \times 2.0} = \dfrac{1.048}{3.0} = 0.349$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 35\%}$$ **(2 marks)**

*Step 4: Stokes regime verification* **(1 mark)**
$$\text{Re}_p = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v_t \cdot d}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.75 \times 0.1048 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{2.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.151$$

Stokes regime is valid. ✓

*Step 5: Practicality comment* **(2 marks)**
The design is **impractical for fine dust control**:
- 35% efficiency for 50 µm particles is unacceptable for modern emission standards.
- The chamber is already 10 m long, illustrating the major drawback of large floor-space requirements.
- **Recommended improvement:** Use **multiple horizontal trays (Howard-type chamber)** to reduce effective $H$. Each tray increases the number of parallel settling zones, dramatically raising efficiency without increasing footprint. Alternatively, use a **cyclone** or **ESP** downstream.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Most Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the Stokes regime** ($\text{Re}_p < 1$). If $\text{Re}_p > 1$, the use of Stokes' law is invalid, and the answer is **wrong by definition**. (Penalty: **1–2 marks**)
> 2. **Mixing up the cut-diameter formula and the efficiency formula.** Cut diameter is set by $\eta = 1$; it is the **smallest fully collected size**, not the largest. (Penalty: **2 marks**)
> 3. **Not converting units** (e.g., using diameter in mm instead of m). Always write units. (Penalty: **0.5–1 mark**)
> 4. **Neglecting gas density $\rho$** in $(\rho_p - \rho)$. For air, $\rho \approx 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$ is small, but for **hot flue gas** it can be 0.6–0.8 kg/m³. Always show the term; you can justify neglecting it only if $\rho_p \gg \rho$.
> 5. **Confusing aerodynamic diameter with physical diameter.** Aerodynamic diameter is defined as the diameter of a unit-density sphere that has the same settling velocity. The Stokes formula uses the **physical diameter** $d$ but with the **aerodynamic-density correction** built in. For non-spherical particles, an additional **shape factor $\chi$** is needed: $v_t = \dfrac{g d^2 (\rho_p - \rho)}{18\mu\chi}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the day before the exam.**

### Nature of Particulates
- Particulates are **solid or liquid** particles suspended in air, classified by **aerodynamic diameter**.
- **Primary PM** = emitted directly; **Secondary PM** = formed by atmospheric reactions of SO₂, NOₓ, VOCs, NH₃.
- Major size bins: **PM₁₀ (≤ 10 µm)**, **PM₂.₅ (≤ 2.5 µm)**, **Ultrafine (≤ 0.1 µm)**.
- Concentration units: **µg/m³** (ambient) or **mg/m³** (source).

### Adverse Effects
- **Health:** respiratory irritation, asthma, lung cancer, cardiovascular mortality (PM₂.₅ is the deadliest).
- **Visibility:** Mie scattering by 0.1–1 µm particles.
- **Materials:** sulfation of marble/limestone, metal corrosion.
- **Climate:** Black carbon (warm); sulfate aerosols (cool) — net effect uncertain but significant.

### Control by Settling
- Settling chamber is the **simplest and cheapest** control device; **zero pressure drop**.
- **Operating principle:** slow the gas in a wide chamber; gravity removes coarse particles.
- **Design parameters:** $L$ (length), $H$ (height), $u_s$ (gas velocity), $\rho_p$, $\mu$, $T$.
- **Stokes' Law:** $v_t = \dfrac{g d^2 (\rho_p - \rho)}{18\mu}$ — **valid only for $\text{Re}_p < 1$**.
- **Efficiency (ideal):** $\eta = \dfrac{v_t L}{H u_s}$.
- **Cut diameter:** $d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\mu u_s H}{g L (\rho_p - \rho)}}$ — typically **40–100 µm**.
- **Improvement:** Howard-type **multi-tray** chamber reduces effective $H$.

### Engineering Take-Home Points
- Settling chambers are **not** used for fine PM control. They are used as **pre-cleaners** or for **coarse, non-toxic dust** (wood, grain, sand).
- For sub-10 µm particles, use **cyclones, ESPs, fabric filters (baghouses), or wet scrubbers** — these are covered in subsequent modules.
- Always check $\text{Re}_p$ before applying Stokes' law; use the **Schiller–Naumann** correlation for the transition regime.
- For very fine particles ($d < 5\ \mu m$), apply the **Cunningham slip correction** $C_c > 1$ to increase the effective settling velocity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filtration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Filtration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Filtration** is a particulate control technique in which an aerosol (dust-laden gas stream) is passed through a porous medium (filter media), and the particulate matter is collected on the medium by a combination of physical and electrostatic mechanisms. The cleaned gas (filtrate) passes through while the solids (filter cake/dust) are retained on the upstream side.

In air pollution control engineering, **fabric filtration** (using woven or felted cloth) is the dominant industrial application. The key performance indices are:

- **Collection Efficiency (η)** – fraction of incoming particulates removed.
- **Pressure Drop (ΔP)** – energy cost of forcing gas through the filter.
- **Air-to-Cloth Ratio (A/C or V_f)** – volumetric gas flow per unit cloth area, typically **0.5 – 2.5 m³/min per m²** of cloth.
- **Cake Resistance (α)** – resistance offered by accumulated dust layer.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The N95 Mask Analogy**
> Think of filtration like a person wearing a thick cotton mask (an N95 or a traditional cloth mask) in a dusty environment.
> - The mask fabric acts like the **filter media** — fibers act as obstacles.
> - Dust particles in the air can be tiny (PM₂.₅ ≈ 2.5 μm) or large (PM₁₀ ≈ 10 μm). Different sized particles are captured by **different mechanisms**, just as a coarse mask stops large dust while only fine weaves catch small smoke particles.
> - As you breathe longer in dust, particles accumulate on the mask → the filter **cake builds up**.
> - The cake itself starts acting as a filter (better efficiency) but it also **clogs** the mask → making it **harder to breathe** (more pressure drop).
> - Eventually, you must **clean or replace** the mask (this is the **regeneration cycle** in industrial baghouses).

## 1.3 Why Filtration is Critical in APC

- Capable of **>99.9 % collection efficiency** for sub-micron particles, which is higher than cyclones and many scrubbers.
- Effective on **fine particulates (PM₂.₅ and PM₁₀)** where mechanical collectors fail.
- Operates dry, hence no wet-sludge disposal problem (advantage over scrubbers).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Favourite** – Examiners frequently test whether students can **rank** control devices by efficiency. Memorize the order: **Fabric Filter > ESP > Wet Scrubber > Cyclone**.

---

# 2. Categories of Air Filtration

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Filtration** | Particles form a cake on the upstream surface of a woven fabric. | Industrial baghouses (cement, steel, lime) |
| **Depth Filtration** | Particles penetrate *into* a thick felt/non-woven pad and are captured inside the media. | HVAC, HEPA, cleanrooms, respirators |
| **Membrane (Absolute) Filtration** | Particles are captured on a membrane with defined pore size. | Laboratory, pharmaceutical, nuclear |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus emphasizes **surface (cake) filtration** because it is the basis of industrial **baghouses**.

---

# 3. Filtration Mechanisms (The Heart of the Topic)

A single fiber in the filter can capture a particle by **one or more** of the following mechanisms. In an exam, you must draw and explain each.

## 3.1 Interception (Direct Interception)

- A particle following a gas streamline comes within **one particle radius (r_p)** of the fiber surface and physically touches it.
- Effective when the particle trajectory passes close to the fiber.

$$N_R = \frac{d_p}{d_f}$$

where $N_R$ is the **interception parameter**, $d_p$ = particle diameter, $d_f$ = fiber diameter.

- Dominant for particles in the range **0.1 – 1 μm**.

## 3.2 Inertial Impaction

- A particle has inertia (mass × velocity) and cannot follow a curving streamline around a fiber → it crashes into the fiber.
- Governed by the **Stokes number**:

$$Stk = \frac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, v_0}{18 \, \mu \, d_f}$$

where $\rho_p$ = particle density, $v_0$ = face velocity, $\mu$ = gas viscosity.

- Dominant for **larger particles (> 1 μm)** and **higher velocities**.

## 3.3 Brownian Diffusion

- Sub-micron particles (< 0.1 μm) undergo **random Brownian motion** and "wander" into fibers.
- Governed by the **Peclet number**:

$$Pe = \frac{d_f \, v_0}{D_B}$$

where $D_B$ = Brownian diffusion coefficient.

- **Efficiency INCREASES as particle size DECREASES** in this regime.

## 3.4 Electrostatic Attraction

- Charged particles (or charged fibers) attract each other.
- Important when the aerosol is charged (e.g., upstream of an ESP) or when fibers are statically charged (e.g., electret filters).

## 3.5 Gravitational Settling

- Large/heavy particles settle onto fibers by gravity.
- Only significant for particles **> 5 μm** at low face velocities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trick** – The *most penetrating particle size (MPPS)* is typically around **0.1 – 0.5 μm**, where neither impaction (too small) nor diffusion (too large) is dominant. **Filter efficiency is MINIMUM at MPPS** and increases on both sides.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 4. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 4.1 The Single-Fiber Efficiency Equation

The **total single-fiber collection efficiency** is the sum of individual mechanism efficiencies (linear superposition is acceptable for KTU exam purposes):

$$\eta_{total} = \eta_R + \eta_D + \eta_I + \eta_E + \eta_G$$

where subscripts R, D, I, E, G stand for interception, diffusion, impaction, electrostatic, and gravity respectively.

### 4.1.1 Approximate Closed-Form Expressions (For Exam Use)

**Diffusion efficiency (Lee & Liu correlation, 1982):**

$$\eta_D = 2.6 \, \left(\frac{1-\alpha_s}{Ku}\right)^{1/3} Pe^{-2/3}$$

where $\alpha_s$ = solidity (packing density) of the filter, $Ku$ = Kuwabara hydrodynamic factor.

**Impaction efficiency (Landahl & Hermann approximation):**

$$\eta_I = \frac{Stk^3}{Stk^3 + 0.77 \, Stk^2 + 0.22}$$

**Interception efficiency:**

$$\eta_R = \frac{1-\alpha_s}{Ku} \, N_R^2$$

**Kuwabara hydrodynamic factor:**

$$Ku = -\frac{\ln \alpha_s}{2} - \frac{3}{4} + \alpha_s - \frac{\alpha_s^2}{4}$$

## 4.2 Overall Filter Collection Efficiency (Penetration Model)

If a filter has $n$ fibers in series (or thickness $L$ of filter media with $n$ fibers per unit depth), the **fraction penetrating** the filter is:

$$P = (1 - \eta_s)^n$$

and the **overall collection efficiency** is:

$$\eta_{filter} = 1 - P = 1 - (1 - \eta_s)^n$$

> [!NOTE]
> For high-efficiency filters, $\eta_s$ is small, so $(1-\eta_s)^n \approx e^{-n\eta_s}$, and thus:
>
> $$\eta_{filter} \approx 1 - e^{-n\eta_s}$$

## 4.3 Pressure Drop Across a Clean Filter (Davies Equation, 1973)

The clean-filter pressure drop depends on the **solidity ($\alpha_s$)** of the filter and the **face velocity ($v_0$)**:

$$\Delta P_{clean} = f_0 \, \mu \, v_0 \, L$$

where $f_0$ is the **Davies drag coefficient**:

$$f_0 = 16 \, \alpha_s \, \left[ 1 + 0.25 \, \alpha_s \left(1 - \alpha_s \right) \right] \, Ku$$

Alternatively, a **simpler form** widely used in APC textbooks:

$$\Delta P_{clean} = \frac{64 \, \mu \, v_0 \, L \, \alpha_s^{3/2} \, (1 + 56 \, \alpha_s^3)}{d_f^2}$$

## 4.4 Pressure Drop with Dust Cake (Operative Filter)

As the cake builds, pressure drop rises. The **cake filtration equation**:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{clean} + \Delta P_{cake}$$

The cake contribution follows the **specific cake resistance model**:

$$\Delta P_{cake} = \alpha \, \mu \, v_0 \, W$$

where $W$ = mass of dust per unit filter area (kg/m²), $\alpha$ = **specific cake resistance** (m/kg).

Or, more practically, with the **filter cake modulus $K_2$**:

$$\Delta P = (K_1 + K_2 \, W) \, v_0$$

where $K_1$ is the resistance of the clean filter, $K_2$ is the resistance per unit dust loading.

## 4.5 Air-to-Cloth Ratio (A/C)

The **superficial face velocity** of the filter is:

$$v_f = \frac{Q}{A_{cloth}}$$

Units: m³/min per m² of cloth (often called "ft/min" in older texts, with values of 1–8 ft/min).

> [!TIP]
> A high A/C = smaller baghouse, more frequent cleaning. A low A/C = larger baghouse, better collection.

## 4.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum** set of equations you must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE on this topic.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Stk = \dfrac{\rho_p d_p^2 v_0}{18 \mu d_f}$ | Stokes number — controls impaction |
| 2 | $N_R = \dfrac{d_p}{d_f}$ | Interception parameter |
| 3 | $Pe = \dfrac{d_f v_0}{D_B}$ | Peclet number — controls diffusion |
| 4 | $\eta_{filter} = 1 - (1-\eta_s)^n$ | Overall filter efficiency from single-fiber efficiency |
| 5 | $\Delta P_{clean} = f_0 \mu v_0 L$ | Clean-filter pressure drop |
| 6 | $f_0 = 16 \alpha_s \left[ 1 + 0.25 \alpha_s(1-\alpha_s) \right] Ku$ | Davies drag coefficient |
| 7 | $Ku = -\dfrac{\ln \alpha_s}{2} - \dfrac{3}{4} + \alpha_s - \dfrac{\alpha_s^2}{4}$ | Kuwabara hydrodynamic factor |
| 8 | $\Delta P_{cake} = \alpha \mu v_0 W$ | Cake pressure drop |
| 9 | $\Delta P_{total} = (K_1 + K_2 W) v_0$ | Operative filter pressure drop |
| 10 | $v_f = \dfrac{Q}{A_{cloth}}$ | Air-to-cloth (face) velocity |

> **Boundary conditions / typical values**
> - $\alpha_s$ (solidity): **0.02 – 0.30** for typical felts/wovens.
> - $v_f$ (face velocity): **0.5 – 2.5 m/min** for industrial baghouses.
> - $\mu$ for air at 25 °C ≈ **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**.

---

# 5. Fabric Filters (Baghouses) — Engineering Details

## 5.1 Components of a Baghouse

1. **Filter media (bags)** — woven or felted fabric, cylindrical or envelope-shaped.
2. **Cage (wire frame)** — supports the bag against negative pressure.
3. **Tube sheet** — separates dirty and clean sides.
4. **Hopper** — collects dislodged dust.
5. **Cleaning mechanism** — shaking, reverse air, or pulse-jet.
6. **Fan & ductwork** — moves gas through the system.

## 5.2 Filter Media — Selection Guide

| Media | Max Temp (°C) | Acid Resistance | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cotton | 80 | Poor | Wood, grain |
| Polyester | 135 | Good (alkali poor) | Cement, coal |
| Nomex (Aramid) | 200 | Good | Asphalt, steel |
| Fibreglass (with PTFE) | 260 | Excellent | Incinerators, kilns |
| PTFE (Teflon) | 260 | Excellent | Aggressive chemistry |
| Polypropylene | 90 | Excellent (acid) | Fertilizer, chemicals |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why temperature matters** — high temperature gas can degrade the fiber. Always pick fabric whose max continuous service temperature > gas temperature by **at least 15 °C margin**.

## 5.3 Cleaning Mechanisms

| Method | Pressure Used | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Shaking | — | Small, intermittent units |
| Reverse Air | Low (sub-ΔP) | Non-woven felts, large units |
| **Pulse-Jet** | High (≈ 5–7 bar compressed air) | Most common industrial type |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse-Jet Baghouse** is the **most widely used** modern design. A short burst (≈ 0.1 s) of compressed air through a venturi induces secondary air, creating a shock wave that flexes the bag and dislodges the cake. Cleaning is **on-line** (filtration is not interrupted).

## 5.4 Design Steps (KTU Board Exam Style)

1. **Determine volumetric flow rate** $Q$ at actual conditions (Nm³/s or m³/s).
2. **Select air-to-cloth ratio** $v_f$ (from experience tables).
3. **Calculate required cloth area**:
   $$A_{cloth} = \frac{Q}{v_f}$$
4. **Choose bag size** (typical: 0.13 m dia × 2.5–4 m long → 1–1.5 m² per bag).
5. **Compute number of bags**:
   $$N_{bags} = \frac{A_{cloth}}{A_{bag}}$$
6. **Select cleaning cycle** (typically ΔP operating range 1.0 – 1.5 kPa).
7. **Check pressure drop** using cake filtration model.
8. **Estimate fan power**:
   $$P_{fan} = \dfrac{Q \, \Delta P_{total}}{\eta_{fan}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 6. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are **fully worked-out derivations** of the two most important KTU exam derivations on this topic. Every algebraic step is shown.

## 6.1 Derivation: Overall Filter Efficiency from Single-Fiber Efficiency

**Given:**
- Single-fiber efficiency $\eta_s$.
- Filter has $n$ fibers of identical type in series.
- $P$ = penetration (fraction of particles passing through).

**Step 1 — Define penetration through ONE fiber.**

When a fiber intercepts a fraction $\eta_s$ of particles, the **fraction that penetrates one fiber** is:

$$P_1 = 1 - \eta_s$$

**Step 2 — Penetration through $n$ identical, independent fibers in series.**

Because each fiber acts independently on the *remaining* particles, the penetrations **multiply**:

$$P_n = P_1 \cdot P_1 \cdot P_1 \cdots (n \text{ times}) = (1 - \eta_s)^n$$

**Step 3 — Express overall efficiency.**

Efficiency = 1 − penetration:

$$\eta_{filter} = 1 - P_n = 1 - (1 - \eta_s)^n$$

**Step 4 — Simplification for $\eta_s \ll 1$ (typical for high-efficiency fibrous filters).**

Using the Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$:

$$(1 - \eta_s)^n \approx e^{-n \eta_s}$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{filter} \approx 1 - e^{-n \eta_s}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation.**

- As $n \to \infty$, $\eta_{filter} \to 1$ — *very thick filters achieve complete collection*.
- For finite $n$, doubling the number of fibers (e.g., thicker mat) raises $\eta$ **exponentially** in the high-efficiency regime. $\blacksquare$

---

## 6.2 Derivation: Pressure Drop Across a Filter Cake (Specific Cake Resistance)

**Given:**
- Gas face velocity $v_0$ (m/s).
- Cake mass per unit area $W$ (kg/m²).
- Dynamic gas viscosity $\mu$ (Pa·s).
- Specific cake resistance $\alpha$ (m/kg).

**Step 1 — Start with Darcy's law for flow through porous media.**

Darcy's law states that superficial velocity $v_0$ through a porous cake is proportional to the pressure gradient divided by viscosity and cake resistance:

$$v_0 = \frac{\Delta P_{cake}}{\mu \, R_{cake}}$$

where $R_{cake}$ is the cake resistance (1/m).

**Step 2 — Express cake resistance in terms of mass loading.**

Empirically, the cake resistance scales linearly with the mass of dust deposited per unit area:

$$R_{cake} = \alpha \, W$$

where $\alpha$ is the **specific cake resistance** (units: m/kg), a property of the dust.

**Step 3 — Substitute into Darcy's law.**

$$v_0 = \frac{\Delta P_{cake}}{\mu \, \alpha \, W}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for pressure drop across the cake.**

Rearranging:

$$\Delta P_{cake} = \mu \, \alpha \, W \, v_0$$

**Step 5 — Add clean-filter pressure drop (total operating pressure drop).**

For the operative (dirty) filter:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{clean} + \Delta P_{cake}$$

$$\Delta P_{total} = f_0 \, \mu \, v_0 \, L + \alpha \, \mu \, W \, v_0$$

**Step 6 — Factor out common terms.**

$$\Delta P_{total} = \mu \, v_0 \, \left( f_0 L + \alpha W \right)$$

Define $K_1 = f_0 L$ (clean-filter resistance per unit area) and $K_2 = \alpha$ (cake resistance coefficient):

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{total} = (K_1 + K_2 W) \, v_0}$$

**Step 7 — Operating window of a baghouse.**

In a pulse-jet baghouse, filtration continues until $\Delta P_{total}$ reaches a setpoint (typically **1.0 – 1.5 kPa**), at which point the cleaning cycle is triggered. After cleaning, $W$ drops to nearly zero and $\Delta P$ returns to $\Delta P_{clean}$ ≈ **0.3 – 0.5 kPa**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 6.3 Worked Numerical Example (For Practice)

**Problem:** A baghouse handles $Q = 60 \, \text{m}^3/\text{min}$ of gas with face velocity $v_f = 1.2 \, \text{m/min}$. The specific cake resistance is $\alpha = 1.0 \times 10^{10} \, \text{m/kg}$, gas viscosity $\mu = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa·s}$, and the cake mass loading is $W = 0.4 \, \text{kg/m}^2$. Clean-filter resistance coefficient $K_1 = 50 \, \text{1/m}$.

**Step 1 — Cloth area.**

$$A_{cloth} = \frac{Q}{v_f} = \frac{60}{1.2} = 50 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert face velocity to m/s.**

$$v_0 = \frac{1.2}{60} = 0.02 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Cake pressure drop.**

$$\Delta P_{cake} = \alpha \, \mu \, v_0 \, W = (1.0 \times 10^{10})(2.0 \times 10^{-5})(0.02)(0.4)$$

$$\Delta P_{cake} = 1.0 \times 10^{10} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \times 10^{4} \, \text{Pa}$$

Wait — this value is unusually high; let us re-check using the **alternate operative form**:

$$\Delta P_{total} = (K_1 + K_2 W) \, v_0$$

with $K_2 = \alpha \mu$:

$$K_2 = (1.0 \times 10^{10})(2.0 \times 10^{-5}) = 2.0 \times 10^{5} \, \text{1/m}$$

**Step 4 — Operative ΔP.**

$$\Delta P_{total} = (50 + 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 0.4)(0.02)$$

$$\Delta P_{total} = (50 + 80000)(0.02) = 80050 \times 0.02 = 1601 \, \text{Pa} \approx 1.6 \, \text{kPa}$$

This is within the typical operating range of a pulse-jet baghouse. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **Note on $K_2$ units** — Some textbooks fold $\mu$ into $K_2$, others keep them separate. KTU typically uses the form $\Delta P = (K_1 + K_2 W) v_0$ where $K_1$ has units m⁻¹ and $K_2$ has units m/kg. **Memorize whichever form your module staff uses.**

---

## 6.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Variables

| Variable | Effect on $\eta$ | Effect on $\Delta P$ | KTU Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase face velocity $v_0$ | Slight increase (impaction ↑) | **Strong increase (linear)** | Penalty: more fan power |
| Increase particle size $d_p$ | Increases (impaction ↑) | Slight increase | Larger particles easier to filter |
| Increase fiber diameter $d_f$ | Decreases (single-fiber efficiency ↓) | Decreases (∝ $1/d_f^2$) | Finer fibers = better filter |
| Increase solidity $\alpha_s$ | Increases (more obstacles) | Increases (∝ $\alpha_s^{3/2}$) | Trade-off with ΔP |
| Increase cake mass $W$ | Marginal effect (cake is its own filter) | **Strong increase (linear)** | Triggers cleaning cycle |
| Increase gas viscosity $\mu$ | Slight increase (Stk ↑) | Strong increase | Hotter gas → thinner → less ΔP |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 7. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 7.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (`node1`, `step1`, `mediaA`).
- Labels use **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** — no bold, italics, or HTML.
- No reserved keywords used as standalone node names.

## 7.2 Diagram 1 — Particle Collection Mechanisms on a Single Fiber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DIRTY GAS STREAM] --> B[FIBER IN FILTER MEDIA]
    B --> C1[IMPACTION LARGE PARTICLES]
    B --> C2[INTERCEPTION MEDIUM PARTICLES]
    B --> C3[DIFFUSION SUBMICRON PARTICLES]
    B --> C4[ELECTROSTATIC CHARGED PARTICLES]
    B --> C5[GRAVITY HEAVY PARTICLES]
    C1 --> D[PARTICLES DEPOSITED ON FIBER]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[CAKE BUILDS UP ON MEDIA]
    E --> F[CLEAN GAS EXITS TO OUTLET]
```

## 7.3 Diagram 2 — Filter Efficiency vs Particle Size (U-Shaped Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[VERY SMALL 0.01 UM] --> P1A[DIFFUSION DOMINANT]
    P1A --> P1B[EFFICIENCY HIGH]
    P2[MPPS 0.1 TO 0.5 UM] --> P2A[MINIMUM EFFICIENCY]
    P2A --> P2B[WORST PENETRATION]
    P3[LARGE 1 TO 10 UM] --> P3A[IMPACTION DOMINANT]
    P3A --> P3B[EFFICIENCY HIGH]
    P3B --> P4[EFFICIENCY RISES]
    P1B --> P4
    P2B --> P4
```

## 7.4 Diagram 3 — Pulse-Jet Baghouse Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[DIRTY GAS INLET] --> S2[INLET PLENUM HOPPER SECTION]
    S2 --> S3[FABRIC BAGS WITH CAGING]
    S3 --> S4[FILTER CAKE ACCUMULATES ON BAG SURFACE]
    S4 --> S5[CLEAN GAS THROUGH BAG TO OUTLET]
    S5 --> S6[CLEAN GAS PLENUM]
    S6 --> S7[FAN EXHAUST STACK]
    P1[COMPRESSED AIR TANK 5 TO 7 BAR] --> P2[DIAPHRAGM VALVE TRIGGER]
    P2 --> P3[PULSE JET INTO VENTURI]
    P3 --> S4
    S4 --> P4[CAKE DISLODGED INTO HOPPER]
    P4 --> S2
    S2 --> P5[ROTARY AIRLOCK OR SCREW CONVEYOR]
    P5 --> P6[COLLECTED DUST DISPOSAL]
```

## 7.5 Diagram 4 — Filter Pressure Drop Operating Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CLEAN FILTER] --> A2[FILTRATION BEGINS]
    A2 --> A3[CAKE BUILDS W INCREASES]
    A3 --> A4[DELTA P RISES LINEARLY]
    A4 --> A5[REACHES SETPOINT 1.0 TO 1.5 KPA]
    A5 --> A6[PULSE JET CLEANING]
    A6 --> A7[CAKE REMOVED W NEAR ZERO]
    A7 --> A8[DELTA P RETURNS TO CLEAN VALUE]
    A8 --> A2
```

## 7.6 Diagram 5 — Solidity αs vs Kuwabara Factor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[ALPHA S 0.02] --> A2[KU LARGE 30 PLUS]
    A3[ALPHA S 0.10] --> A4[KU MODERATE 5]
    A5[ALPHA S 0.30] --> A6[KU SMALL 1 TO 2]
    A2 --> B[LOW PRESSURE DROP BUT LOW EFFICIENCY]
    A4 --> C[BALANCED OPERATING POINT]
    A6 --> D[HIGH EFFICIENCY BUT HIGH DELTA P]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 8. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are **modeled on the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern**: 3-mark short questions + 14-mark long questions with internal choice. Mark distribution and choice format follow official 2024 KTU norms.

---

## 8.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three mechanisms by which particles are collected on a fiber in a fabric filter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per mechanism):**

1. **Inertial Impaction** — A particle with sufficient inertia deviates from the gas streamline around a fiber and impacts it. Governed by the Stokes number $Stk = \rho_p d_p^2 v_0 / (18 \mu d_f)$. Dominant for large particles (> 1 μm) and high face velocity.

2. **Interception** — A particle following a streamline that passes within one particle radius of the fiber touches the fiber. The interception parameter is $N_R = d_p / d_f$. Dominant in the 0.1 – 1 μm range.

3. **Brownian Diffusion** — Sub-micron particles (< 0.1 μm) undergo random thermal motion and collide with fibers. Efficiency rises as particle size decreases, governed by the Peclet number $Pe = d_f v_0 / D_B$.

*(Any 3 of: Impaction, Interception, Diffusion, Electrostatic, Gravity)*

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define "Most Penetrating Particle Size" (MPPS) and state its typical range for fabric filters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Most Penetrating Particle Size (MPPS)** is the particle diameter at which the filter collection efficiency is **minimum**, i.e., penetration is maximum. It occurs because diffusion dominates for very small particles, and impaction dominates for larger particles, while neither is strong in the intermediate range. The typical MPPS for fabric filters is in the range **0.1 – 0.5 μm** (sometimes quoted as 0.3 μm for HEPA-grade filters). **(3 marks)**

---

## 8.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Choice Pattern** — Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a **pulse-jet baghouse** for air pollution control. Discuss the role of **(i)** filter media, **(ii)** cleaning mechanism, and **(iii)** air-to-cloth ratio in its design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **overall filter collection efficiency** in terms of **single-fiber efficiency** and the **number of fibers in the filter**. State the assumption made. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Working of a Pulse-Jet Baghouse (Sketch 3 marks + Explanation 4 marks):**

Refer to the functional architecture in Section 7.4. The baghouse has:

- A **dirty gas inlet** at the bottom (hopper section).
- An array of **cylindrical fabric bags** suspended from a tube sheet, supported internally by wire cages.
- A **clean gas outlet plenum** above the tube sheet.
- A **pulse-jet cleaning system** comprising a compressed air reservoir, solenoid-actuated diaphragm valve, and venturi nozzle on each bag.

**Operation cycle:**

1. Dust-laden gas enters the **inlet plenum** and flows **outward through the fabric bags**. Particles are captured on the outer surface, forming a cake.
2. Clean gas passes through the bags into the **clean plenum** and exits via the induced-draft fan.
3. As the cake builds, the pressure drop rises. When it reaches a setpoint (typically **1.0 – 1.5 kPa**), a **diaphragm valve** opens briefly (≈ 60 – 100 ms), releasing a burst of high-pressure (5 – 7 bar) compressed air into the venturi.
4. The pulse creates a **shock wave** that travels down the bag, **flexing the fabric** outward (against the cage) and then inward. The flexing cracks the cake, which falls into the hopper.
5. The dislodged dust is removed by a **rotary airlock** or **screw conveyor**.

**Role of design parameters:**

- **(i) Filter Media** — Determines max temperature, chemical resistance, efficiency, and cleanability. Common choices: polyester (135 °C), Nomex (200 °C), PTFE/fibreglass (260 °C). Must be selected for the specific dust and gas temperature.
- **(ii) Cleaning Mechanism** — Pulse-jet allows **on-line cleaning** (no flow interruption), high air-to-cloth ratios, and reduced baghouse size. The cleaning frequency, pressure, and duration must be tuned to prevent **bag fatigue**.
- **(iii) Air-to-Cloth Ratio (A/C)** — Higher A/C (1.5 – 2.5 m/min) reduces capital cost but increases pressure drop and cleaning frequency. Lower A/C (0.5 – 1.0 m/min) gives lower ΔP and longer bag life. Typical industrial design uses **A/C ≈ 1.0 – 1.5 m/min** for pulse-jet systems.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Derivation: Overall Filter Efficiency from Single-Fiber Efficiency**

Refer to the **full derivation in Section 6.1**. Key steps (recap for the answer script):

- **Assumption (1 mark)**: Each fiber acts independently on the **remaining** particles penetrating previous fibers. Fibers are identical and in series.

- **Step 1 (2 marks)**: For one fiber, fraction penetrating is $P_1 = 1 - \eta_s$.

- **Step 2 (2 marks)**: For $n$ fibers in series, penetration multiplies: $P_n = (1 - \eta_s)^n$.

- **Step 3 (2 marks)**: Overall efficiency is $\eta_{filter} = 1 - (1 - \eta_s)^n \approx 1 - e^{-n\eta_s}$ for small $\eta_s$.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Davies equation** for pressure drop across a **clean fabric filter**, defining all terms. How is the equation modified when a **dust cake** forms on the filter? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A baghouse handles **75 m³/min** of gas at an **air-to-cloth ratio of 1.5 m/min**. The cake specific resistance is $\alpha = 8 \times 10^{9} \, \text{m/kg}$, gas viscosity $\mu = 1.9 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa·s}$, and clean-filter resistance coefficient $K_1 = 60 \, \text{m}^{-1}$. If the cake mass loading is $W = 0.5 \, \text{kg/m}^2$, calculate:
   (i) Required cloth area
   (ii) Cake resistance coefficient $K_2$
   (iii) Total operating pressure drop **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Davies Equation — Clean Filter Pressure Drop (5 marks):**

$$\Delta P_{clean} = f_0 \, \mu \, v_0 \, L$$

where:
- $f_0$ = Davies drag coefficient (dimensionless), $f_0 = 16 \alpha_s \left[ 1 + 0.25 \alpha_s (1-\alpha_s) \right] Ku$
- $\mu$ = dynamic gas viscosity (Pa·s)
- $v_0$ = superficial face velocity (m/s)
- $L$ = filter thickness (m)
- $\alpha_s$ = filter solidity (volume fraction of fibers)
- $Ku$ = Kuwabara hydrodynamic factor, $Ku = -\frac{\ln \alpha_s}{2} - \frac{3}{4} + \alpha_s - \frac{\alpha_s^2}{4}$

**Physical interpretation:** $\Delta P$ is proportional to gas viscosity, face velocity, and filter thickness. Higher solidity (denser felt) means more flow resistance.

**Modification for Dust Cake (2 marks):**

Once dust deposits and forms a cake, the cake itself acts as a filter. The total pressure drop becomes:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{clean} + \Delta P_{cake} = (K_1 + K_2 W) \, v_0$$

where $K_2 W$ represents the cake's contribution, $K_2 = \alpha \mu$ is the cake resistance coefficient, and $W$ is the dust mass per unit area (kg/m²). The cake-dominated $\Delta P$ is what triggers baghouse cleaning cycles.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Required cloth area (2 marks):**

$$A_{cloth} = \frac{Q}{v_f} = \frac{75}{1.5} = 50 \, \text{m}^2$$

**(ii) Cake resistance coefficient (2 marks):**

$$K_2 = \alpha \cdot \mu = (8 \times 10^{9}) \times (1.9 \times 10^{-5}) = 1.52 \times 10^{5} \, \text{m/kg}$$

**(iii) Total operating pressure drop (3 marks):**

Convert face velocity to m/s:

$$v_0 = \frac{1.5}{60} = 0.025 \, \text{m/s}$$

Apply:

$$\Delta P_{total} = (K_1 + K_2 W) \, v_0$$

$$\Delta P_{total} = (60 + 1.52 \times 10^{5} \times 0.5)(0.025)$$

$$\Delta P_{total} = (60 + 76000)(0.025) = 76060 \times 0.025$$

$$\Delta P_{total} \approx 1901.5 \, \text{Pa} \approx 1.9 \, \text{kPa}$$

This is **slightly above** the typical 1.5 kPa setpoint, so the cleaning cycle should trigger more frequently, or the air-to-cloth ratio should be lowered to reduce $\Delta P$.

---

## 8.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Filtration Questions — READ CAREFULLY**
>
> 1. **Confusing face velocity with gas velocity in pores.** The formula uses **superficial (face) velocity $v_0$**, not interstitial velocity. Students writing $v = Q / A_{pore}$ lose 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the cake pressure drop.** In a long numerical, students often compute $\Delta P_{clean}$ only and forget $\Delta P_{cake}$. The cake term **dominates** in mature baghouse operation.
>
> 3. **Stokes number formula errors.** Mixing up $\rho_p$ with $\rho_{gas}$, or using $d_p$ instead of $d_p^2$. The Stokes number has $d_p^2$ in the numerator.
>
> 4. **Mixing up "depth" and "surface" filtration.** Surface filtration = cake on top (industrial). Depth filtration = particles inside the mat (HVAC). Examiners deduct for wrong classification.
>
> 5. **Unit errors in air-to-cloth ratio.** It is normally given in **m/min** (or ft/min) for industrial baghouse design, **not** m/s. Convert before computing $A_{cloth}$.
>
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations. The KTU marking scheme allocates **at least 1 mark** for clearly stating assumptions (e.g., "fibers act independently," "isothermal flow," "incompressible gas").
>
> 7. **Ignoring temperature effects.** A higher gas temperature reduces $\mu$, which lowers $\Delta P$ but may exceed the fabric's service temperature — both effects must be considered.

---

## 8.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before the Exam**

- **Filtration** removes particulates by passing gas through a porous medium. Industrial version is the **baghouses (fabric filters)**.
- **Five collection mechanisms:** Impaction, Interception, Diffusion, Electrostatic, Gravity. Each dominates in a specific particle-size range.
- **MPPS (Most Penetrating Particle Size)** is **0.1 – 0.5 μm** for fabric filters — minimum efficiency.
- **Stokes number** $Stk = \rho_p d_p^2 v_0 / (18 \mu d_f)$ controls impaction.
- **Interception parameter** $N_R = d_p / d_f$ controls interception.
- **Peclet number** $Pe = d_f v_0 / D_B$ controls diffusion.
- **Overall filter efficiency**: $\eta_{filter} = 1 - (1 - \eta_s)^n \approx 1 - e^{-n\eta_s}$.
- **Davies equation** for clean filter: $\Delta P_{clean} = f_0 \mu v_0 L$, where $f_0$ depends on $\alpha_s$ and $Ku$.
- **Operative pressure drop** (with cake): $\Delta P_{total} = (K_1 + K_2 W) v_0$.
- **Kuwabara factor**: $Ku = -\frac{\ln \alpha_s}{2} - \frac{3}{4} + \alpha_s - \frac{\alpha_s^2}{4}$.
- **Air-to-cloth ratio (A/C)**: $v_f = Q / A_{cloth}$, typical values **0.5 – 2.5 m/min** for pulse-jet.
- **Pulse-jet cleaning** is the **most common** industrial type; allows **on-line** cleaning.
- **Filter media selection** depends on **gas temperature** (cotton < 80 °C, polyester < 135 °C, Nomex < 200 °C, PTFE/fibreglass < 260 °C) and **chemical resistance**.
- **Filter cake** acts as a *better* filter than the fabric itself but causes **rising ΔP**; this drives the **cleaning cycle**.
- **Efficiency ranking in APC**: Fabric Filter > ESP > Wet Scrubber > Cyclone.
- **Solidity $\alpha_s$** is the key design parameter — higher $\alpha_s$ means more efficiency but more pressure drop.
- **For KTU numerics**, always convert $v_f$ from m/min to m/s before applying the pressure-drop formula.
- **Cake specific resistance $\alpha$** is typically in the range **10⁹ – 10¹¹ m/kg** for common industrial dusts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collection in Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Collection in Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Collection in Fluids"?

In Air Pollution Control Engineering, **Collection in Fluids** refers to the broad family of gas–liquid contact devices (commonly called **Wet Scrubbers** or **Wet Collectors**) used to remove gaseous and particulate pollutants from an industrial exhaust stream by bringing the contaminated gas into intimate contact with a scrubbing liquid (usually water or a reactive chemical solution).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Collection in fluids is the process of separating airborne contaminants (particulates and/or soluble/ reactive gases) from a carrier gas stream by transferring them into a liquid phase through mechanisms of impaction, diffusion, absorption, and chemical reaction. The liquid acts as the collection medium.*

The system operates on the principle of **momentum transfer** and **mass transfer** between the dispersed phase (dust particles) and the continuous scrubbing liquid.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Wash Tunnel"

Imagine a dusty car entering an automatic car wash:

- The **dusty car** = the **polluted gas stream** laden with particulate matter.
- The **high-pressure water jets** = the **scrubbing liquid** injected into the scrubber.
- The **soapy foam that traps dirt** = the **droplets acting as collection targets**.
- The **clean, shiny car exiting the tunnel** = the **purified gas stream** released to the atmosphere.

Just as the car cannot exit without being hit by multiple jets of water from different angles, the dust particle cannot exit a well-designed scrubber without colliding with (and being captured by) a liquid droplet. The more droplets, the larger the droplets, and the longer the contact time — the higher the cleaning efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 Module 2):**
> - Particle collection mechanisms in fluid media
> - Classification of wet collectors (Spray Tower, Cyclonic, Venturi, Packed Bed)
> - **Deutsch–Anderson Equation** for collection efficiency
> - Contact Power Theory (Cut-Height Correlation)
> - Scrubber design parameters: L/G ratio, Pressure drop, Gas residence time

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water (scrubbing liquid) | $\rho_L$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Density of standard air | $\rho_g$ | **1.2 kg/m³** |
| Dynamic viscosity of air | $\mu_g$ | **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** |
| Gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Penetration | $P_t$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Collection Efficiency | $\eta$ | % (0 to 100) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Collection Mechanisms

When a dust particle approaches a liquid droplet, capture occurs by one or more of the following physical mechanisms:

### (a) Inertial Impaction
A particle, due to its inertia, **fails to follow the gas streamlines** around a droplet and **strikes the droplet surface**, becoming embedded.
- Dominant for: **Large particles (d_p > 1 µm)**
- Quantified by: **Stokes Number (Stk)**
- Higher relative velocity → Higher impaction efficiency.

### (b) Brownian Diffusion
Very small particles undergo random molecular bombardment (Brownian motion) and **diffuse across streamlines** to reach the droplet surface.
- Dominant for: **Sub-micron particles (d_p < 0.1 µm)**
- Quantified by: **Peclet Number (Pe)**
- Efficiency increases as particle size decreases.

### (c) Direct Interception
A particle follows the gas streamline but is captured because its **center passes within one particle radius** of the droplet.
- Depends on: Ratio of particle diameter to droplet diameter (d_p / D_d).

### (d) Electrostatic Attraction
If the particle and/or droplet carry a charge, Coulombic forces enhance capture.
- Used in: **Electrostatically-Augmented Scrubbers**.

### (e) Gravitational Settling
Particles fall under gravity into liquid sprays from below or are trapped on wetted surfaces.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Each mechanism dominates a different particle size range. Wet scrubbers are unique because **liquid droplets can be engineered (in size and number)** to cover the *entire* size spectrum — from sub-micron to coarse dust — making them the most versatile control device.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Wet Collectors (Scrubbers)

| Type | Description | Typical η | Pressure Drop |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spray Tower** | Gas rises through falling liquid spray | 70–85% (coarse) | 1–2 kPa |
| **Cyclonic Scrubber** | Centrifugal force + water film | 85–95% | 1–3 kPa |
| **Venturi Scrubber** | High-velocity gas + liquid injection at throat | 95–99%+ | 5–15 kPa |
| **Packed Bed Scrubber** | Gas through wetted packing | 90–97% (gases) | 1–4 kPa |
| **Plate Tower** | Gas through perforated plates with liquid | 85–95% | 2–5 kPa |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE FORMULA BANK — Memorize These for KTU Board Exams**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{N_t - N_o}{N_t} = 1 - \dfrac{N_o}{N_t}$ | Single-stage fractional collection efficiency |
| 2 | $P_t = 1 - \eta = \dfrac{N_o}{N_t}$ | Penetration (fraction escaping) |
| 3 | $\eta = 1 - e^{-n \cdot A \cdot Q}$ | Deutsch–Anderson Equation |
| 4 | $P_t = e^{-n \cdot A \cdot Q}$ | Deutsch–Anderson (penetration form) |
| 5 | $Stk = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, v \, C_c}{18 \, \mu_g \, D_d}$ | Stokes Number |
| 6 | $\eta_{overall} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{N}(1 - \eta_i)$ | Overall efficiency of N stages in series |
| 7 | $P_{t,overall} = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P_{t,i}$ | Overall penetration of N stages |
| 8 | $N_t = N_o \cdot \left(\dfrac{d_{p,o}}{d_{p,t}}\right)^{2}$ | Particle count in Venturi correlation |
| 9 | $S = \dfrac{L}{G}$ | Liquid-to-Gas ratio (L/G) |
