| 10 | $\Delta P_T = \Delta P_{gas} + \Delta P_{liquid}$ | Total pressure drop |
| 11 | $P_T = \Delta P \cdot Q_g$ | Contact Power (Semrau's correlation) |
| 12 | $\ln P_t = -\alpha \cdot P_T^{\beta}$ | Cut-Height / Contact Power correlation |

**Where:**
- $N_t, N_o$ = Number of particles entering and leaving the collector (number/m³)
- $n$ = Number density of droplets (droplets/m³)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of collector (m²)
- $Q$ = Volumetric gas flow rate (m³/s)
- $\rho_p$ = Particle density (kg/m³)
- $d_p$ = Particle diameter (m)
- $D_d$ = Droplet diameter (m)
- $C_c$ = Cunningham slip correction factor
- $L, G$ = Liquid and Gas mass flow rates (kg/s)
- $\alpha, \beta$ = Empirical constants (function of scrubber type)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wet scrubbers are the **workhorse devices** in industries such as:
- **Cement kilns** (particulate + HCl removal)
- **Incinerators** (acid gas + dioxin scrubbing)
- **Fertilizer plants** (NH₃, HF, dust)
- **Sulphuric acid plants** (SO₂ absorption)
- **Steel & foundry** (metallurgical fumes)
- **Semiconductor industry** (HCl, NH₃ etching gases)

Their **dual function** — removing particulates AND absorbing soluble gases — makes them indispensable when **multi-pollutant control** is required.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Deutsch–Anderson Equation

This is the **single most important equation** in wet scrubber theory. It models a scrubber as a *uniform cloud* of identical droplets through which a gas–particle mixture flows.

### Assumptions
1. Droplets are **spherical** and **uniformly distributed**.
2. Gas flows in **plug flow** (no axial mixing).
3. Each droplet is a **perfect sink** — any particle touching it is captured.
4. Droplet density is **constant** throughout the collector.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Single Droplet Efficiency**

Define the fractional collection efficiency of a single droplet as:

$$E = \dfrac{\text{Volume of gas swept clean per unit time}}{\text{Volume of gas approaching the droplet per unit time}}$$

For a droplet of diameter $D_d$, the projected area presented to the gas is $\tfrac{\pi D_d^2}{4}$. The volumetric gas flow rate approaching the droplet is:

$$q = \dfrac{\pi D_d^2}{4} \cdot v_{rel}$$

where $v_{rel}$ is the relative velocity between gas and droplet.

**Step 2 — Volume of gas cleaned per unit time**

The volume cleaned per unit time by ONE droplet is:

$$q_{clean} = E \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_d^2}{4} \cdot v_{rel}$$

**Step 3 — Total cleaning rate of all droplets in the scrubber**

If $n$ is the number density of droplets (droplets per m³) and $V_c = A \cdot L$ is the scrubber volume:

$$Q_{clean,total} = n \cdot A \cdot L \cdot E \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_d^2}{4} \cdot v_{rel}$$

**Step 4 — Differential Mass Balance on Particles**

Consider a thin slice $dx$ of the scrubber. Let $N(x)$ = number of particles per m³ at position $x$. The change in particle concentration over $dx$:

$$\dfrac{dN}{dx} = -\dfrac{n \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_d^2}{4} \cdot v_{rel} \cdot E \cdot N}{v_g}$$

where $v_g$ is the superficial gas velocity.

**Step 5 — Separation of Variables**

Rearranging and integrating from inlet ($x=0$, $N=N_t$) to outlet ($x=L$, $N=N_o$):

$$\int_{N_t}^{N_o} \dfrac{dN}{N} = -\dfrac{n \cdot \pi D_d^2 \cdot v_{rel} \cdot E}{4 \, v_g} \int_{0}^{L} dx$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{N_o}{N_t}\right) = -\dfrac{n \cdot \pi D_d^2 \cdot v_{rel} \cdot E \cdot L}{4 \, v_g}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Droplet Projected Area Term**

The term $\dfrac{\pi D_d^2}{4} \cdot n \cdot L$ represents the total droplet projected area per unit gas flow area, which we denote as $A^*$. Combining with volumetric flow $Q = v_g \cdot A$:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{N_o}{N_t}\right) = -\dfrac{n \cdot A \cdot L \cdot E}{Q}$$

**Step 7 — Final Formulation**

The penetration is $P_t = N_o / N_t$, so:

$$\boxed{P_t = \exp\left(-\dfrac{n \cdot A \cdot L \cdot E}{Q}\right) = \exp(-n \cdot A \cdot Q_{rel})}$$

The **fractional collection efficiency** becomes:

$$\boxed{\eta = 1 - P_t = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{n \cdot A \cdot L \cdot E}{Q}\right)}$$

This is the celebrated **Deutsch–Anderson Equation** in its most general form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If the question states "each droplet captures with efficiency E", the formula contains the factor E. If the question states "each droplet is a perfect sink (E=1)", then the formula simplifies to $\eta = 1 - e^{-nAL/Q}$. Read the question carefully!

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Multi-Stage Scrubber Efficiency

For $N$ identical scrubber stages connected in series, each with efficiency $\eta_s$:

**Single-stage penetration:**

$$P_{t,1} = 1 - \eta_s$$

**For N stages in series** (the particles escaping stage 1 enter stage 2, etc.):

$$P_{t,total} = (1 - \eta_s)^N$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1 - \eta_s)^N$$

**Step-by-step expansion for N = 2:**

$$\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1 - \eta_s)(1 - \eta_s) = 1 - (1 - 2\eta_s + \eta_s^2) = 2\eta_s - \eta_s^2$$

For small $\eta_s$ (e.g., $\eta_s = 0.1$): $\eta_{overall} \approx 0.19$ — only marginal gain. But for large $\eta_s$ (e.g., $\eta_s = 0.9$): $\eta_{overall} = 0.99$ — enormous improvement. This is why **high-efficiency primary scrubbers are economically preferred** over multiple low-efficiency units.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A venturi scrubber handles 5 m³/s of flue gas laden with 10 g/m³ of fly ash. The scrubber achieves 98% collection efficiency on a mass basis. The slurry flow rate is 0.02 m³/s with density 1100 kg/m³. Calculate:
1. Mass of ash collected per hour
2. Overall penetration
3. L/G ratio

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass flow of incoming ash:**

$$\dot{m}_{ash,in} = 10 \, \text{g/m}^3 \times 5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 50 \, \text{g/s} = 180 \, \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Mass collected (98% efficiency):**

$$\dot{m}_{ash,collected} = 0.98 \times 180 = 176.4 \, \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Penetration:**

$$P_t = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.98 = 0.02 \; (2\%)$$

**Step 4 — L/G ratio:**

$$L = 0.02 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} \times 1100 \, \text{kg/m}^3 = 22 \, \text{kg/s}$$

$$G = 5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} \times 1.2 \, \text{kg/m}^3 = 6 \, \text{kg/s}$$

$$S = \dfrac{L}{G} = \dfrac{22}{6} = 3.67 \; \text{L/m}^3$$

> [!TIP]
> **Typical L/G range for Venturi scrubbers: 0.5 to 5 L/m³.** Our value of 3.67 L/m³ lies in the upper range, consistent with high-efficiency PM removal.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Architecture — Venturi Scrubber

The Venturi scrubber is the **flagship** of wet collectors, and KTU frequently tests this. Below is a Block-Level Functional Architecture showing the gas–liquid interaction sequence.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["RAW POLLUTED GAS INLET\nFlue gas with PM load"] --> B["CONVERGING SECTION\nGas velocity accelerates\nfrom 10 to 60 m/s"]
    B --> C["VENTURI THROAT\nHigh turbulence zone\nPressure drop maximum"]
    C --> D["LIQUID INJECTION NOZZLE\nScrubbing liquid atomized\ninto fine droplets 50 to 500 micron"]
    D --> E["PARTICLE DROPLET CONTACT\nImpaction + Diffusion\nRelative velocity maximum"]
    E --> F["DROPLET FORMATION ZONE\nDroplets entrap PM\nSlurry formation begins"]
    F --> G["DIVERGING SECTION\nVelocity decelerates\nPressure recovery"]
    G --> H["MIST ELIMINATOR\nDemister pads remove\nentrained droplets"]
    H --> I["CLEAN GAS OUTLET\nto stack or further treatment"]
    F --> J["SLURRY RECYCLE TANK\nSolids settling and water reuse"]
    J --> K["PUMP RECIRCULATION\nRecirculated to nozzle D"]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Spray Tower (Counter-Current)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["POLLUTED GAS INLET\nBottom of tower"] --> B["RISING GAS PLUME\nCounter current to liquid"]
    B --> C["PACKING OR BAFFLE LAYER\nIncreases contact time"]
    C --> D["SPRAY NOZZLE BANK\nTop mounted liquid injectors"]
    D --> E["FALLING DROPLET CURTAIN\nDroplets capture PM"]
    E --> C
    C --> F["WASHED GAS OUTLET\nTop of tower"]
    C --> G["DIRTY LIQUID SUMP\nBottom collection"]
    G --> H["RECYCLE PUMP\nOptional chemical dosing"]
    H --> D
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Cyclonic Scrubber

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TANGENTIAL GAS INLET\nSwirl motion induced"] --> B["INNER VORTEX\nCentrifugal force throws\nparticles outward"]
    B --> C["WETTED WALL FILM\nWater film on cylinder wall"]
    C --> D["PARTICLE TRAP\nPM captured in film"]
    D --> E["DRAIN SUMP\nSlurry collection"]
    B --> F["CENTRAL GAS CORE\nRising clean gas"]
    F --> G["VANE MIST ELIMINATOR\nRemoves entrained droplets"]
    G --> H["CLEAN GAS OUTLET\nTop exit"]
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix of Scrubbers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["WET SCRUBBER FAMILY"] --> B["LOW ENERGY\nSpray Tower\nPacked Bed"]
    A --> C["MEDIUM ENERGY\nPlate Tower\nCyclonic"]
    A --> D["HIGH ENERGY\nVenturi\nOrifice"]
    B --> B1["1 to 2 kPa drop\n70 to 90 percent eta"]
    C --> C1["2 to 5 kPa drop\n85 to 95 percent eta"]
    D --> D1["5 to 15 kPa drop\n95 to 99 percent eta"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (Semrau's Contact Power Theory):** For a given collection efficiency, all scrubber designs require approximately the same **contact power** ($P_T = \Delta P \cdot Q_g$), regardless of geometry. This is a unifying design principle often quoted in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the four primary mechanisms by which particulates are collected on liquid droplets in a wet scrubber.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four primary collection mechanisms are:
1. **Inertial Impaction** — dominant for large particles (>1 µm); particles strike droplets due to inertia.
2. **Brownian Diffusion** — dominant for sub-micron particles (<0.1 µm); particles diffuse to droplets via random molecular motion.
3. **Direct Interception** — particles captured when their trajectory passes within one particle radius of the droplet.
4. **Electrostatic Attraction** — Coulombic forces between charged particles and droplets enhance capture. *(2 marks for naming + 1 mark for the size ranges / explanation)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the Deutsch–Anderson equation and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{n \cdot A \cdot L \cdot E}{Q}\right)$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = fractional collection efficiency
- $n$ = number density of droplets (droplets/m³)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the collector (m²)
- $L$ = effective length of the collector (m)
- $E$ = collection efficiency of a single droplet
- $Q$ = volumetric gas flow rate (m³/s)

**Significance:** It relates scrubbing efficiency to controllable design parameters (droplet density, scrubber geometry, and gas flow), allowing engineers to predict performance. *(1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for defining terms, 1 mark for the significance statement.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **Venturi Scrubber** for particulate matter control. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A venturi scrubber treats 8 m³/s of exhaust gas containing 5 g/m³ of particulate matter. The penetration of the scrubber is 4%. Calculate:
1. The collection efficiency.
2. The mass of particulates emitted per hour.
3. If two identical such scrubbers are placed in series, what is the new overall efficiency? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Working Principle:** A venturi scrubber consists of three sections — a **converging section**, a **throat**, and a **diverging section**. The dirty gas is accelerated through the converging section to high velocities (60–120 m/s) at the throat. Scrubbing liquid is injected at the throat through spray nozzles, atomizing it into millions of fine droplets (50–500 µm). The high relative velocity between the gas and droplets causes particles to **inertially impact** the droplets. The droplet–particle aggregates then enter the diverging section where velocity decreases, and finally pass through a **mist eliminator** to remove entrained droplets. The clean gas exits to the stack.

**Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.1 above for the labelled functional architecture.)

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. Highest single-stage collection efficiency (99%+) among wet scrubbers.
2. Compact design — small footprint.
3. Can handle hot gases (up to 1000°C with proper material).
4. Effective on fine particulates.
5. Low capital cost compared to baghouses or ESPs for high-temperature service.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. High pressure drop → high operating cost.
2. High water consumption.
3. Slurry disposal problem.
4. Corrosion of throat and downstream ducting.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Collection efficiency:**

$$\eta = 1 - P_t = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96 = 96\%$$

**(ii) Mass emitted per hour:**

$$N_{t,in} = 5 \, \text{g/m}^3 \times 8 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 40 \, \text{g/s} = 144 \, \text{kg/h}$$

$$N_{o} = 0.04 \times 144 = 5.76 \, \text{kg/h}$$

**Mass emitted = 5.76 kg/h**

**(iii) Two scrubbers in series:**

For two identical scrubbers, each with $\eta = 0.96$:

$$P_{t,total} = (1 - 0.96)^2 = (0.04)^2 = 0.0016$$

$$\eta_{overall} = 1 - 0.0016 = 0.9984 = 99.84\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing penetration with efficiency** — Many students write $P_t = 1 - \eta$ but then forget that mass emitted = $P_t \times$ mass input. Always re-derive: emission = $(1-\eta) \times$ input.
> 2. **Series efficiency formula** — Don't simply double the efficiency. The correct formula is $\eta_{total} = 1 - (1-\eta)^N$. Marks are awarded for using the correct series form.
> 3. **Unit conversions** — Convert g/s to kg/h by multiplying by 3.6. A common error is to forget this step.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*: `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Contact Power Theory** (Semrau's Correlation) for wet scrubber design. How does it help in selecting between low-energy and high-energy scrubbers? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A packed bed scrubber handles 2 m³/s of gas containing SO₂ at 1500 ppm. The gas–liquid contact achieves 90% absorption of SO₂. The scrubbing liquid (NaOH solution) flows at 0.005 m³/s with concentration 2 mol/L. Calculate:
1. Moles of SO₂ absorbed per second.
2. Stoichiometric NaOH requirement (SO₂ + 2NaOH → Na₂SO₃ + H₂O).
3. Actual NaOH supplied and its excess percentage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Contact Power Theory (Semrau, 1959):**

The Contact Power $P_T$ is defined as the product of gas pressure drop and the gas volumetric flow rate:

$$P_T = \Delta P \cdot Q_g \quad (\text{Watts})$$

Semrau empirically observed that for a **given collection efficiency**, the required contact power is approximately **constant regardless of scrubber geometry**. This led to the **Cut-Height correlation**:

$$\ln P_t = -\alpha \cdot P_T^{\beta}$$

where $P_t$ = penetration, and $\alpha, \beta$ are empirical constants that depend on the scrubber type and dust properties.

**Implications:**
- A **plot of $\ln P_t$ vs $P_T^\beta$** yields a straight line — this is the **master design curve**.
- Engineers can choose the most cost-effective combination of $\Delta P$ and $Q_g$ to achieve the target efficiency.
- A scrubber with higher $\Delta P$ can use lower $Q_g$ (or vice versa) to achieve the same efficiency.
- This unifies spray towers, venturis, and cyclonic scrubbers on a **common design basis**.

**Selection Logic:** If a target penetration of 0.01 is required, one can use either a low-$\Delta P$ large-volume spray tower OR a high-$\Delta P$ compact venturi — the **operating cost differs** (high $\Delta P$ → more fan power), so the choice is economic.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $Q_g = 2 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $C_{SO_2} = 1500 \, \text{ppm}$
- Absorption efficiency = 90%
- $L = 0.005 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $C_{NaOH} = 2 \, \text{mol/L} = 2000 \, \text{mol/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Moles of SO₂ entering per second:**

At NTP, 1 ppm ≈ 1/22.4 × 10⁻⁶ mol/m³ × (P/RT correction) — KTU standard practice:

$$C_{SO_2} = 1500 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{(mole fraction)}$$

$$\dot{n}_{SO_2,in} = \dfrac{P \cdot V}{R \cdot T} = \dfrac{101325 \times 2}{8.314 \times 298} = 81.74 \, \text{mol/s}$$

$$\dot{n}_{SO_2} = 81.74 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1226 \, \text{mol/s}$$

**Step 2 — Moles absorbed (90%):**

$$\dot{n}_{absorbed} = 0.90 \times 0.1226 = 0.1103 \, \text{mol/s}$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric NaOH:**

Reaction: $SO_2 + 2NaOH \rightarrow Na_2SO_3 + H_2O$

$$\dot{n}_{NaOH,stoich} = 2 \times 0.1103 = 0.2206 \, \text{mol/s}$$

**Step 4 — NaOH supplied:**

$$\dot{n}_{NaOH,supplied} = 0.005 \times 2000 = 10 \, \text{mol/s}$$

**Step 5 — Excess NaOH %:**

$$\%\text{ Excess} = \dfrac{10 - 0.2206}{0.2206} \times 100 = 4433\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-B):**
> 1. **ppm-to-mole conversion** — Students frequently treat ppm as a volumetric concentration without converting via the ideal gas law. Use $n = PV/RT$ first.
> 2. **Stoichiometric ratio** — SO₂ requires 2 moles of NaOH, not 1. A common error is to write $SO_2 + NaOH$.
> 3. **Excess calculation** — Excess is calculated relative to stoichiometric demand, not the inlet flow.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Wet scrubbers = **gas–liquid contact devices**; versatile for PM + gas absorption.
- ✅ **Dominant mechanism** shifts with particle size: Impaction (>1 µm), Diffusion (<0.1 µm).
- ✅ **Venturi scrubber** = highest single-stage efficiency, highest pressure drop.
- ✅ **Spray tower** = lowest pressure drop, lowest efficiency on fine PM.
- ✅ **Deutsch–Anderson equation** = $\eta = 1 - e^{-nALE/Q}$ — *must be memorized verbatim*.
- ✅ **Penetration** $P_t = 1 - \eta$ — distinguishes from efficiency in series calculations.
- ✅ **Series efficiency**: $\eta_{total} = 1 - (1-\eta)^N$, **NOT** $N \times \eta$.
- ✅ **L/G ratio** = liquid mass flow / gas mass flow; Venturi typically 0.5–5 L/m³.
- ✅ **Contact Power Theory** unifies all scrubbers: $\ln P_t = -\alpha P_T^\beta$.
- ✅ **Higher pressure drop = higher efficiency**, but = higher operating cost (fan power).
- ✅ Slurry disposal, corrosion, and water treatment are **major practical constraints**.
- ✅ For KTU numericals: always show units, conversions (g↔kg, s↔h), and re-state assumptions.
- ✅ Most-mistaken area: confusing **single-droplet efficiency E** with **overall scrubber efficiency η**.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — "Collection in Fluids"**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020) Outcome-Based Education standards.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrostatic Precipitation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Electrostatic Precipitation (ESP) - Foundations

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP):** A high-efficiency particulate control device that uses the force of an induced electrostatic charge to remove fine particulates (dust, smoke, fumes, mist) from a flowing gas stream. Particles are charged by a high-voltage direct current (HVDC) field and subsequently collected on oppositely charged collection electrodes.

As per the **PEPOT524 - Air Pollution Control (KTU 2024 Scheme)** syllabus under Module 2 (*Particulate Control Devices – Advanced*), ESPs are classified as **high-energy, dry-type, secondary particulate control equipment** with collection efficiencies routinely exceeding **99 %** for sub-micron and micron-sized particulates from industrial flue gases.

**Standard Application Metrics:**
- Particulate size range collected: **0.01 µm to 100 µm**
- Operational efficiency: **95 % – 99.9 %**
- Typical gas temperature handling: **up to 700 K (≈ 427 °C)**
- Pressure drop across unit: **very low (≈ 10–25 mm H₂O)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** ESPs are the only particulate control device that does **not** rely on filtration, impaction, or centrifugal forces. They exploit **electrostatic attraction**, making them the device of choice for power plants, cement kilns, and metallurgical smelters in Kerala and worldwide.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Lightning Rod Café"

Imagine walking into a giant café during a thunderstorm. The café owner installs a lightning rod (the **discharge electrode**) connected to a Van de Graaff generator that creates a strong static field inside the café. When dust particles (smoke, pollen) drift in, they pass through this field and acquire a tiny static charge (just like rubbing a balloon on your sweater). The walls of the café (the **collection electrodes**) carry the opposite charge. As the charged dust particles try to leave, the walls *pull* them in like a magnet, and they stick to the walls. Periodically, the owner taps the walls (the **rapping mechanism**) and the dust cake falls into collection hoppers below.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" behind ESPs:** Mechanical separators (cyclones, scrubbers) lose efficiency dramatically below 5 µm. ESPs excel precisely in the **0.1 – 10 µm** range — the *most penetrating* size range for cyclones, which is why ESPs are indispensable in modern air pollution control.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic)

The ESP operation is governed by a **four-stage sequential mechanism**. Each stage must be optimized for the next to occur efficiently.

### Stage 1 — Corona Discharge Generation
- A high negative DC potential (**–20 kV to –75 kV**) is applied between two asymmetric electrodes.
- The **discharge electrode (DE)** has a small radius of curvature (thin wire or spiked mast); the **collection electrode (CE)** is flat or cylindrical.
- The strong non-uniform field around the DE causes local dielectric breakdown of air → **corona discharge**.
- Negative gas ions (mainly **O₂⁻**, **CO₃⁻**, **OH⁻**) are generated and migrate outward.

### Stage 2 — Particulate Charging
Suspended dust particles enter the ionized field and acquire charge via two simultaneous mechanisms:
- **Field Charging (Boltzmann/Field Mechanism):** Dominant for particles **> 0.5 µm**. Ions follow field lines and deposit on the particle surface. Saturates quickly.
- **Diffusion Charging:** Dominant for particles **< 0.5 µm**. Random Brownian motion of ions causes them to attach even in the absence of a directional field.

### Stage 3 — Particle Migration & Collection
- Charged particles are subjected to the electrostatic force: $F_e = q \cdot E_c$
- A **drag force** opposes motion (Stokes' regime): $F_d = 3\pi \mu d_p w$
- Steady-state velocity is the **migration velocity (drift velocity)**: $w = \dfrac{q E_c C_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$

### Stage 4 — Rapping & Dislodgement
- Collected particulate forms a dust cake on the collection plate.
- Periodic mechanical impact (rapping) dislodges the cake into **hoppers** without shutting down the unit.
- The dislodged dust is removed via screw conveyors or pneumatic systems.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the formulas in the table below — at least 3 of them appear in every KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).**

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Significance / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Deutsch-Anderson Equation** | $\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{A \cdot w}{Q}\right)$ | Overall ESP collection efficiency |
| 2 | **Migration Velocity (Stokes)** | $w = \dfrac{q \, E_c \, C_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$ | Drift velocity of charged particle |
| 3 | **Field Charging (Pauthenier)** | $q_f = \left(\dfrac{3 \varepsilon}{\varepsilon + 2}\right) \pi \varepsilon_0 d_p^{2} E_0$ | Saturation charge on a particle |
| 4 | **Diffusion Charging (White)** | $q_d = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 d_p k T}{e} \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{d_p \, \bar{u} \, e^2 \, N_0 \, t}{8 \varepsilon_0 k T}\right)$ | Charge via random ion motion |
| 5 | **Peek's Law (Corona Onset)** | $E_c = 3.0 \times 10^6 \, m \left(\delta + \dfrac{0.308}{\sqrt{\delta \, r}}\right)$ | Critical electric field for corona |
| 6 | **Sulfur Trioxide Conditioning** | $SO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4$ film | Reduces fly-ash resistivity |
| 7 | **Total Collection Efficiency** | $\eta_{total} = 1 - (1-\eta_1)(1-\eta_2)$ | Multi-field ESP (series sections) |
| 8 | **Specific Collection Area (SCA)** | $SCA = \dfrac{A}{Q} \;\; [m^2/(m^3/s)]$ | Design parameter; $A$ is total plate area |
| 9 | **Ohm's Law for Corona Current** | $I = \dfrac{V - V_c}{R}$ | V-I characteristic of discharge |
| 10 | **Resistivity Threshold (Critical)** | $\rho_{critical} = 10^{10} \, \Omega\text{-cm}$ | Above this, back-corona occurs |

**Where:**
- $\eta$ = collection efficiency (dimensionless)
- $A$ = total collection plate area (m²)
- $w$ = migration / drift velocity (m/s)
- $Q$ = volumetric gas flow rate (m³/s)
- $q$ = particle charge (C)
- $E_c$ = collecting field strength (V/m)
- $C_c$ = Cunningham slip correction factor (dimensionless)
- $\mu$ = gas dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $d_p$ = particle diameter (m)
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- $V_c$ = corona onset voltage
- $m$ = wire surface irregularity factor (0.5 – 1.0)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Thermal Power Plants (coal-fired):** Largest single application — 60–70 % of global ESPs are installed in coal-based thermal plants to capture **fly ash** before stack emission. Indian CPCB mandates **≤ 50 mg/Nm³** PM from plants > 500 MW.
- **Cement Industry:** Pre-heater and kiln exhaust gases containing high-temperature dust.
- **Steel & Ferro-alloy Smelters:** Collection of metal oxide fumes (ZnO, Fe₂O₃, MnO₂).
- **Kerala Context:** Used in Travancore Cochin Chemicals, FACT (Fertilizers and Chemicals Travancore) and rubber processing units.
- **Pulp & Paper:** Recovery boiler particulate control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deutsch-Anderson Equation (Master Derivation)

This is the **single most important derivation** in ESP theory and is a guaranteed question in KTU exams (10–14 marks in Part B).

### Given / Assumed:
- A single dust particle enters the ESP with gas velocity $v_g$ in the $x$-direction.
- The particle drifts toward the collection plate at velocity $w$ in the $y$-direction.
- Gas flow is **plug-flow** (uniform, no back-mixing).
- The collection plate spacing is $b$ (half-distance from DE to CE).
- A particle at height $y$ above the collection plate will be collected in time $t_c = y/w$.

### Step 1 — Set up the time available for collection
The gas spends a **residence time** inside the ESP of length $L$:
$$t_{res} = \frac{L}{v_g}$$

### Step 2 — Set up the time required for collection
A particle entering at the *worst-case* position (midway, $y = b$) requires:
$$t_{req} = \frac{b}{w}$$

### Step 3 — Define fractional collection for a single streamline
A particle is collected **if and only if** $t_{res} \geq t_{req}$. For a particle entering at height $y$ from the collection plate:
$$t_{req}(y) = \frac{y}{w}$$

The fraction of the channel cross-section from which particles will be collected (assuming uniform distribution) is:
$$f = \frac{y}{b} = \frac{w \, t_{res}}{b} = \frac{w \, L}{b \, v_g}$$

### Step 4 — Differentiate to get the differential collection equation
For an infinitesimal element of length $dL$ at position $L$, the fraction of dust *not yet collected* that will be removed is proportional to the remaining dust concentration and the local field strength.

Let $n(x)$ be the number concentration of dust particles at distance $x$ from the inlet. The decrease in concentration:
$$dn = -n(x) \cdot \frac{w}{b \, v_g} \, dL$$

Wait — let us set this up via the **penetration** approach used by Anderson (1919), extending Deutsch's original 1922 work.

### Step 5 — Penetration equation
Penetration $P(x)$ = fraction of particles that *escape* collection up to position $x$:
$$\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{w}{v_g \, b} \, P(x)$$

### Step 6 — Separate variables and integrate
$$\int_{1}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{w}{v_g \, b} \int_{0}^{L} dx$$

$$\ln P = -\frac{w \, L}{v_g \, b}$$

### Step 7 — Express in terms of $A$ and $Q$
The **plate area** is $A = n_s \cdot L \cdot H$ (where $n_s$ is the number of gas passages, $H$ is plate height). The volumetric flow rate is $Q = n_s \cdot b \cdot H \cdot v_g$.

Therefore:
$$\frac{w \, L}{v_g \, b} = \frac{w \cdot A}{Q}$$

### Step 8 — Final Deutsch-Anderson Equation
$$\boxed{P = \exp\left(-\frac{A \, w}{Q}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A \, w}{Q}\right)}$$

**Interpretation:** Doubling the plate area $A$ or doubling the migration velocity $w$ does **not** double the efficiency — efficiency follows a **diminishing returns** exponential law. To go from 90 % to 99 % efficiency, you must roughly *triple* $A$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Migration Velocity in Stokes' Regime

### Given:
- A spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ with charge $q$ in an electric field $E_c$.
- Gas viscosity $\mu$.

### Step 1 — Force balance at terminal velocity
At terminal (steady-state) velocity, the **electrostatic driving force** equals the **Stokes drag force**:
$$q \, E_c = 3 \pi \mu d_p \, w$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $w$
$$w = \frac{q \, E_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Cunningham Slip Correction
For sub-micron particles where the Knudsen number $Kn$ is not negligible, the drag force is reduced because the gas does not behave as a continuum. We multiply the velocity by a correction factor $C_c$:
$$w = \frac{q \, E_c \, C_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$$

where:
$$C_c = 1 + \frac{\lambda}{d_p}\left(2.34 + 1.05 \exp\left(-0.39 \frac{d_p}{\lambda}\right)\right)$$

and $\lambda$ is the **mean free path of gas molecules** (≈ 0.066 µm for air at STP).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Peek's Law (Corona Onset)

The **corona onset gradient** $E_c$ around a clean, smooth wire of radius $r$ in air is empirically given by:
$$E_c = 3.0 \times 10^6 \cdot m \cdot \left(\delta + \frac{0.308}{\sqrt{\delta \cdot r}}\right) \;\; \text{V/m}$$

**Variables:**
- $m$ = wire surface roughness factor (0.5 for smooth, 1.0 for rough/weathered)
- $\delta$ = air density factor:
$$\delta = \frac{3.92 \, B}{T} \quad \text{with} \quad B \text{ in cm Hg},\ T \text{ in K}$$

**Example KTU-style Calculation:**
At $T = 400$ K, $B = 75$ cm Hg, $r = 2$ mm, $m = 0.7$:
$$\delta = \frac{3.92 \times 75}{400} = 0.735$$
$$E_c = 3.0 \times 10^6 \times 0.7 \times \left(0.735 + \frac{0.308}{\sqrt{0.735 \times 0.002}}\right)$$
$$= 2.1 \times 10^6 \times (0.735 + 8.035) = 2.1 \times 10^6 \times 8.77$$
$$\boxed{E_c \approx 1.842 \times 10^7 \;\; \text{V/m}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ESP vs Other Particulate Control Devices

| Parameter | ESP | Fabric Filter (Baghouse) | Wet Scrubber | Cyclone |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Efficiency for 1 µm | **High** | Very High | Low–Medium | Very Low |
| Pressure drop | **Very Low** (10–25 mm H₂O) | Medium (100–150 mm H₂O) | High (250–400 mm H₂O) | Medium (50–150 mm H₂O) |
| Power consumption | High (HV supply) | Medium | Very High (pump) | Low |
| Temperature limit | **~700 K** | ~480 K (filter media) | ~600 K | ~1000 K |
| Capital cost | **Very High** | Medium–High | Medium | Low |
| Operating cost | Low | Medium | High | Very Low |
| Maintenance | Low | High (bag replacement) | Medium (corrosion) | Low |
| Sensitivity to dust resistivity | **Yes (critical)** | No | No | No |

> [!WARNING]
> **Resistivity is the Achilles' Heel of ESPs.** If dust resistivity exceeds $\rho > 10^{10} \, \Omega\text{-cm}$, the collected layer becomes a poor conductor, charge accumulates, and **back-corona** (a secondary discharge from the collection plate) occurs — destroying efficiency. Mitigation: $SO_3$ conditioning, water spray, hot-side ESP placement.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ESP Block Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INLET[Inlet Duct with Turning Vanes] --> GAS_DIST[Gas Distribution Screens]
    GAS_DIST --> CHARGE_ZONE[Charging Zone Field Charging Diffusion Charging]
    CHARGE_ZONE --> COLL_ZONE[Collection Zone]
    COLL_ZONE --> RAPPER[Rapper Mechanism Periodic Impact]
    RAPPER --> HOPPER[Collection Hoppers]
    HOPPER --> CONVEYOR[Screw Conveyor or Pneumatic Conveying]
    CONVEYOR --> DISPOSAL[Ash Disposal Silo]
    COLL_ZONE --> OUTLET[Outlet to Stack]
    
    HV[HVDC Power Supply Rectifier 20kV to 75kV] -.-> CHARGE_ZONE
    HV -.-> COLL_ZONE
    CTRL[Microprocessor Control Unit Voltage Current Rapper Timing] -.-> HV
    CTRL -.-> RAPPER
```

## 4.2 Electrode Configuration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DE[Discharge Electrode Thin Wire Spiked Mast Polarized Negative] -->|High Voltage -50 kV| FIELD[Strong Non-Uniform Field]
    CE[Collection Electrode Flat Plates or Cylinders Grounded] -->|Ground Reference| FIELD
    FIELD --> CORONA[Corona Discharge Negative Ions Generated]
    CORONA --> CHG[Particles Become Negatively Charged]
    CHG --> MIGR[Charged Particles Migrate to Plates]
    MIGR --> DEPOSIT[Deposition on Collection Plates]
    DEPOSIT --> CAKE[Dust Cake Formation]
    CAKE --> RAP[Rapping Cycle Initiated]
    RAP --> HOPPER2[Hopper Discharge]
```

## 4.3 ESP Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ESP[Electrostatic Precipitators] --> T1[Stage Type]
    ESP --> T2[Electrode Geometry]
    ESP --> T3[Temperature Zone]
    ESP --> T4[Application]
    
    T1 --> S1[Single Stage Cottrell Type]
    T1 --> S2[Two Stage Charging and Collection Separate]
    
    T2 --> G1[Wire Plate Type]
    T2 --> G2[Wire Pipe Type]
    T2 --> G3[Plate Plate Type]
    
    T3 --> H1[Hot Side 400 to 700 K]
    T3 --> H2[Cold Side 350 to 420 K]
    
    T4 --> A1[Industrial Power Plants]
    T4 --> A2[Cement and Lime]
    T4 --> A3[Steel Smelters]
    T4 --> A4[Pulp and Paper]
```

## 4.4 Failure & Mitigation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FAIL[ESP Performance Failure] --> F1[High Dust Resistivity]
    FAIL --> F2[Gas Flow Maldistribution]
    FAIL --> F3[Spark Over]
    FAIL --> F4[Wire Failure]
    FAIL --> F5[Rapping Inefficiency]
    
    F1 --> M1[SO3 Conditioning Water Spray Hot Side ESP]
    F2 --> M2[Use Perforated Plates and Turning Vanes]
    F3 --> M3[Automatic Voltage Control AVC Reduce kV During Sparks]
    F4 --> M4[Use Weighted Wires Anti Sway]
    F5 --> M5[Optimize Rapper Frequency and Intensity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** In the block diagrams above, the **solid arrows** denote material/gas flow and **dashed arrows** denote electrical/control signal flow. This convention is consistent with KTU textbook schematics (Wark, Davis, Cooper & Alley).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**State the Deutsch-Anderson equation for ESP collection efficiency. Why is the efficiency-versus-plate-area relationship non-linear?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Deutsch-Anderson equation is:
$$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A \, w}{Q}\right)$$

where $A$ = plate area (m²), $w$ = migration velocity (m/s), $Q$ = gas flow rate (m³/s).

The relationship is **non-linear (exponential)** because the equation arises from a first-order penetration differential equation. As particles are progressively removed, the *remaining* number available for collection decreases. Hence, doubling the plate area does not double the efficiency — it follows a **diminishing-returns** law. *(1 mark: equation, 1 mark: variables, 1 mark: explanation)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Distinguish between field charging and diffusion charging in ESPs. Which one dominates for fine particles (< 0.5 µm)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Field Charging | Diffusion Charging |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Ions follow field lines to particle | Random Brownian motion of ions |
| Dominant size | $d_p > 0.5$ µm | $d_p < 0.5$ µm |
| Time dependence | Saturates rapidly | Slow, time-dependent |
| Governing parameter | Electric field $E_0$ | Temperature $T$, ion density $N_0$ |

**Diffusion charging** dominates for fine particles ($< 0.5$ µm) because field lines diverge around very small particles and field charging saturates weakly, while the relative importance of random ion motion increases. *(1 mark each for distinction + 1 mark for the answer)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a single-stage wire-plate electrostatic precipitator. Discuss the role of (i) corona discharge, (ii) particle charging, and (iii) rapping mechanism. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An ESP handles $Q = 50$ m³/s of flue gas. The total plate area is $A = 8000$ m² and the effective migration velocity is $w = 0.10$ m/s. Calculate: (i) the Deutsch-Anderson collection efficiency, (ii) the SCA, and (iii) the penetration. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
Flue gas laden with particulates enters the ESP through a **gas distribution section** fitted with baffles and perforated plates to ensure uniform flow. It then passes between arrays of **discharge electrodes** (negatively charged thin wires, –30 to –60 kV DC) and grounded **collection electrodes** (flat steel plates, ~10 m tall). A high-voltage rectifier (50/60 Hz step-up transformer + silicon diode bridge) supplies the HVDC. The collected dust cake is dislodged periodically by **rappers** and falls into hoppers.

**Role of Corona Discharge (1.5 marks):**
The intense non-uniform field near the discharge wire (radius 1–3 mm) ionizes surrounding air molecules once the local gradient exceeds the breakdown threshold. This generates a **unipolar cloud of negative ions** that migrates outward and attaches to dust particles, giving them a net negative charge.

**Role of Particle Charging (1.5 marks):**
The negative ions impinge on dust particles via two mechanisms — **field charging** (Pauthenier, dominant > 0.5 µm) and **diffusion charging** (White, dominant < 0.5 µm). The particles acquire charges on the order of $10^4$ to $10^5$ elementary charges.

**Role of Rapping (1 mark):**
An electromagnetic or pneumatic hammer impacts the collection plates at 1–10 minute intervals. The dust cake, which is held by **adhesion** (van der Waals + electrostatic), is detached and falls into the hopper. The hopper is then emptied by screw conveyors.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $Q = 50$ m³/s, $A = 8000$ m², $w = 0.10$ m/s.

**(i) Collection Efficiency (3 marks):**
$$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A \cdot w}{Q}\right) = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{8000 \times 0.10}{50}\right)$$
$$= 1 - \exp(-16)$$
$$= 1 - 1.125 \times 10^{-7}$$
$$\boxed{\eta \approx 99.99999 \, \%}$$

> **Note:** Such a high theoretical efficiency is unattainable in practice due to non-idealities (gas maldistribution, re-entrainment, rapping losses). Practical efficiencies are 99.0 – 99.9 %.

**(ii) Specific Collection Area (2 marks):**
$$SCA = \frac{A}{Q} = \frac{8000}{50} = 160 \;\; \text{s/m}$$

This is a *very* high SCA; typical industrial ESPs operate at SCA = 30–100 s/m, indicating that the given example is over-designed.

**(iii) Penetration (2 marks):**
$$P = \exp\left(-\frac{A \cdot w}{Q}\right) = e^{-16} = 1.125 \times 10^{-7}$$
$$\boxed{P \approx 0.00001125 \;\; \text{(or} \; 1.125 \times 10^{-5} \, \%)}$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the Deutsch-Anderson equation for ESP collection efficiency starting from the penetration balance. State clearly the assumptions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of **dust resistivity** and its effect on ESP performance. What are the methods to control high resistivity? Discuss briefly the phenomenon of **back-corona**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

(See **Section 3.1** above for the full derivation. Key check-points for 7 marks:)

1. **Assumptions (2 marks):** Plug flow, uniform particle distribution, constant $w$, particle charging independent of position.
2. **Set up penetration equation (1.5 marks):** $dP/P = -(w/v_g b) dx$
3. **Integration (1.5 marks):** $\ln P = -(wL)/(v_g b)$
4. **Substitution of $A$ and $Q$ (1 mark):** $(wL)/(v_g b) = (wA)/Q$
5. **Final boxed equation (1 mark):** $\eta = 1 - \exp(-Aw/Q)$

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Dust Resistivity Definition (1.5 marks):**
Dust resistivity $\rho$ is the **electrical resistance** of a unit cube (1 cm × 1 cm × 1 cm) of dust cake, measured in $\Omega$-cm. It quantifies how easily charges flow through the collected particulate layer on the collection plate.

**Effect on ESP Performance (2 marks):**
- **Low resistivity ($\rho < 10^8$ $\Omega$-cm):** Charges leak away too rapidly; particles re-entrain into the gas stream.
- **Optimal ($\rho = 10^8$ – $10^{10}$ $\Omega$-cm):** Holds charge briefly, gets recharged continuously — ideal.
- **High resistivity ($\rho > 10^{10}$ $\Omega$-cm):** Charge accumulates on the dust layer → builds up high potential → causes **back-corona** → efficiency crashes.

**Methods to Control High Resistivity (2 marks):**
1. **$SO_3$ Conditioning:** Inject $SO_3$ upstream of ESP — forms a thin $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ film on particles, reducing $\rho$.
2. **Water Spray Conditioning:** Adiabatic humidification lowers temperature and adds surface moisture.
3. **Hot-Side ESP:** Operate at $T > 600$ K where resistivity of typical fly ash decreases naturally.
4. **Chemical Additives:** Ammonia ($NH_3$) conditioning.
5. **Use of Pulse Energization:** Reduces back-corona intensity.

**Back-Corona Phenomenon (1.5 marks):**
When dust resistivity is too high, the accumulated negative charge on the collection plate creates a positive corona discharge from the plate *toward* the wire. This generates **positive ions** that neutralize the negatively charged particles, dramatically reducing migration velocity and overall efficiency. Sparks become more frequent, and the HVDC supply must throttle voltage — losing 20–60 % efficiency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in ESP Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the Deutsch-Anderson equation: $A$ in m², $w$ in m/s, $Q$ in m³/s. The argument $Aw/Q$ must be dimensionless — a *very common* 0.5 mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing $V_c$ (corona onset voltage) with $V_{op}$ (operating voltage)**. Operating voltage is typically 1.5 – 2× the onset voltage.
> 3. **Mixing up field and diffusion charging dominance ranges** — KTU specifically asks for 0.5 µm as the transition.
> 4. **Omitting the "resistivity" topic** in ESP questions — it is a high-weight topic in Module 2.
> 5. **Neglecting to state assumptions** in the Deutsch-Anderson derivation — KTU awards 2 marks solely for assumptions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **⚡ HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — ELECTROSTATIC PRECIPITATION**

- ✅ **Four operational stages:** Corona → Charging → Migration → Rapping
- ✅ **Deutsch-Anderson equation:** $\eta = 1 - \exp(-Aw/Q)$ — the *single most tested* formula
- ✅ **Diminishing returns:** To go from 90 % to 99 % efficiency, you need to *triple* the plate area
- ✅ **Migration velocity** is governed by charge × field / drag
- ✅ **Field charging** dominates > 0.5 µm; **Diffusion charging** dominates < 0.5 µm
- ✅ **Resistivity sweet spot:** $10^8$ – $10^{10}$ $\Omega$-cm
- ✅ **Back-corona** occurs when $\rho > 10^{10}$ $\Omega$-cm
- ✅ **Typical operating voltage:** –20 to –75 kV DC (negative polarity preferred)
- ✅ **Pressure drop:** only 10–25 mm H₂O (very low — major advantage)
- ✅ **Two-stage ESPs** have separate charging and collection sections (used for air conditioning, clean rooms)
- ✅ **Peek's Law** governs corona onset; depends on wire radius and air density
- ✅ **SCA** = Specific Collection Area = A/Q (typical: 30 – 100 s/m)
- ✅ **Sulfur trioxide conditioning** is the standard industrial fix for high-resistivity fly ash
- ✅ **Industrial applications:** Coal-fired TPPs (60 %), cement, steel, paper
- ✅ **Major limitation:** Cannot effectively collect gases — only particulates

> **📚 End of Module 2 ESP Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme | PEPOT524 | Air Pollution Control**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conversion to Harmless and Useful Products - Meteorology Related to Atmosphere - Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Conversion to Harmless and Useful Products - Meteorology Related to Atmosphere - Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Conversion to Harmless and Useful Products

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> Conversion to harmless and useful products refers to a class of air pollution control strategies in which the captured or generated pollutant stream is chemically, biologically, or physically transformed into a substance that is either non-toxic and environmentally benign, or into a marketable by-product that has commercial utility. This is also termed **"Recovery and Recycling"** or **"Product Recovery"** in the KTU curriculum.

The fundamental philosophy behind this approach is the **3R principle** — **Reduce, Reuse, Recover** — which shifts the design mindset from end-of-pipe treatment to a **Circular Economy** model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Perspective:**
> In KTU examinations, students often confuse "Conversion to harmless products" with "Removal of pollutants." The critical distinction is that conversion implies a **deliberate chemical reaction** that changes the molecular identity of the pollutant (e.g., $SO_2 \rightarrow CaSO_3$), whereas removal merely separates the pollutant from the gas stream (e.g., using a cyclone or baghouse filter).

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen Exhaust Analogy

Imagine a small Indian household kitchen where strong spices (pollutants like VOCs, particulate matter) are released during cooking. There are three ways to handle this:

1. **Dilution (Stack emission)** — Open a window. The smell spreads to neighbors. *(Not a real solution.)*
2. **Removal (Separation)** — A mesh filter catches oil droplets but does not destroy the odour-causing molecules. *(Mechanical removal.)*
3. **Conversion to harmless product** — A catalytic converter (like in automobiles) chemically transforms the odourous hydrocarbons into $CO_2$ and $H_2O$ using a catalyst. *(True conversion — change of identity.)*

This third approach — where the pollutant is **chemically reborn** as a harmless or useful product — is the core subject of this module section.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Examples in Industrial Air Pollution Control:**
> - $SO_2$ from flue gases $\rightarrow$ converted to **sulfuric acid** ($H_2SO_4$) — useful in fertilizer industry.
> - $HCl$ from incinerators $\rightarrow$ recovered as **hydrochloric acid** for reuse.
> - $NO_x$ via **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** $\rightarrow$ reduced to $N_2$ and $H_2O$.
> - $CO_2$ scrubbing $\rightarrow$ recovered for use in carbonated beverages or urea production.
> - Mercury vapors $\rightarrow$ recovered as elemental mercury for sale.

---

### 1.2 Meteorology Related to Atmosphere

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> Meteorology is the interdisciplinary scientific study of the atmosphere, focusing on weather processes and forecasting. In the context of **Air Pollution Control**, atmospheric meteorology focuses on the physical and dynamic behaviour of the lower atmosphere (troposphere), including temperature gradients, wind patterns, turbulence, humidity, and pressure systems, all of which govern the **transport, dispersion, dilution, and accumulation** of air pollutants.

#### The Atmosphere — A Conceptual Overview

The Earth's atmosphere is a **thin gaseous envelope** held by gravitational attraction. Its total mass is approximately **$5.15 \times 10^{18}$ kg**, and **99% of its mass lies below an altitude of 32 km**. For an air pollution engineer, the most critical region is the **mixing layer** — the lower portion of the troposphere where pollutants are released and dispersed.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Blanket and the Chimney

Think of the atmosphere as a **woollen blanket** wrapped around the Earth, and an industrial stack as a **chimney poking a hole through the blanket**. The blanket is not uniform — sometimes it is tight (temperature inversion → pollutants trapped) and sometimes loose (convective mixing → pollutants dispersed). The behaviour of this "blanket" is determined by **meteorology**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is meteorology essential for air pollution control?**
> Without understanding meteorology, an engineer cannot answer:
> - Why do pollutant concentrations peak at certain times of the day?
> - How high should a stack be designed?
> - When should an industry be ordered to shut down?
> - How do pollutants travel from one city to another (e.g., stubble burning in Punjab affecting Delhi)?

---

### 1.3 Pressure — The Central Atmospheric Variable

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Atmospheric pressure is the **force per unit area** exerted by the weight of the column of air directly above a given point, due to the gravitational pull of the Earth on the atmospheric gas molecules. It is a fundamental thermodynamic state variable that decreases with altitude following the hydrostatic equation.

At sea level, the standard atmospheric pressure is:
> **$P_0 = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1013.25 \text{ mbar} = 760 \text{ mm Hg} = 1 \text{ atm}$**

The acceleration due to gravity is taken as **$g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$** at sea level.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Diving Pool Analogy

Imagine standing at the edge of a deep swimming pool. As you dive deeper, you feel greater pressure on your eardrums. The deeper you go, the more water (heavier fluid) is stacked above you. Similarly, **atmospheric pressure is the cumulative weight of all the air molecules stacked vertically above a point**. At sea level, you are at the "bottom" of the air ocean, and at high altitudes (e.g., Shimla, Leh), you are nearer the "surface" of this ocean, so the pressure is lower.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Insight:**
> Air pollution control equipment (blowers, fans, bag filters, ESPs) is **rated for specific pressure drops**. The atmospheric pressure at the plant location affects fan sizing, gas density calculations, and stack design. For every 1000 m rise in altitude, atmospheric pressure drops by approximately **12%**, which directly impacts the performance of pollution control devices.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Methods of Conversion to Harmless and Useful Products

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups these methods into four main categories:

#### A. Chemical Conversion Methods

1. **Catalytic Oxidation**
   - Pollutants (VOCs, CO) are oxidized over a catalyst at lower temperatures.
   - Example: $CO + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \xrightarrow{Pt, Pd} CO_2$
   - $C_xH_y + \left(x + \tfrac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \tfrac{y}{2}H_2O$

2. **Thermal Oxidation (Incineration)**
   - High-temperature combustion ($700°C - 1200°C$) without catalyst.
   - Useful for halogenated VOCs and hazardous air pollutants (HAPs).

3. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)**
   - Reduces $NO_x$ to $N_2$ using ammonia or urea.
   - $4NO + 4NH_3 + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{catalyst}} 4N_2 + 6H_2O$

4. **Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction (SNCR)**
   - Similar to SCR but at higher temperature ($900°C - 1100°C$) without catalyst.

5. **FGD (Flue Gas Desulfurization) — Wet Limestone Process**
   - $SO_2 + CaCO_3 \rightarrow CaSO_3 + CO_2$
   - By-product gypsum ($CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O$) recovered for use in cement/plaster.

#### B. Biological Conversion Methods (Biofiltration)

- Microorganisms (bacteria, fungi) oxidize biodegradable VOCs and odours into $CO_2$, $H_2O$, and biomass.
- Used for low-concentration, high-flow waste gases.
- Example: Biofilter for H$_2$S removal — $H_2S \xrightarrow{\text{Thiobacillus}} S + H_2O$ (elemental sulfur is recovered).

#### C. Physical Conversion / Recovery Methods

1. **Adsorption** — Activated carbon adsorbs VOCs; later desorbed and recovered.
2. **Absorption** — Solvent scrubbing with chemical reaction.
3. **Condensation** — VOCs liquefied by cooling; recovered as liquid product.
4. **Membrane Separation** — Selective permeation to recover $H_2$, $CH_4$, $CO_2$.

#### D. Electrochemical Conversion Methods

- Used for converting $SO_2$ to $H_2SO_4$ via electrolysis.
- Useful for $CO_2$ capture and conversion to fuels.

---

### 2.2 Atmospheric Structure and Meteorology

The atmosphere is divided into concentric layers based on **temperature gradients**:

| Layer | Altitude Range | Temperature Behaviour | Relevance to Air Pollution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Troposphere** | 0 – 11 km (avg.) | Decreases at **6.5 °C/km** (Environmental Lapse Rate) | All pollution occurs here |
| **Stratosphere** | 11 – 50 km | Increases with altitude (ozone layer) | Long-range transport of pollutants |
| **Mesosphere** | 50 – 85 km | Decreases again | Negligible for APC |
| **Thermosphere** | > 85 km | Increases sharply | Negligible for APC |

> **Why the troposphere?**
> Pollution generated by human activity is released near the ground, within the **planetary boundary layer (PBL)** — the lowest 1–2 km of the troposphere — where turbulent mixing, wind, and convection govern dispersion.

#### Key Meteorological Variables

1. **Wind speed and direction** — Determines horizontal transport of pollutants.
2. **Atmospheric stability** — Determines vertical mixing.
3. **Temperature inversions** — Trap pollutants near the ground.
4. **Solar radiation** — Drives photochemical smog formation.
5. **Humidity** — Affects reaction kinetics of $SO_2$, $NO_x$.
6. **Precipitation** — Wet deposition of pollutants (acid rain).

#### Atmospheric Stability Classes (Pasquill-Gifford)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Pasquill-Gifford stability classification is **frequently asked in KTU examinations** as a short note (3 marks).

| Class | Stability | Mixing | Dispersion Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Very Unstable | Strong | Excellent (but high ground concentration near source) |
| **B** | Unstable | Moderate | Good |
| **C** | Slightly Unstable | Weak | Moderate |
| **D** | Neutral | Mechanical turbulence only | Average |
| **E** | Slightly Stable | Very weak | Poor |
| **F** | Stable (Inversion) | Almost none | Very poor (worst case) |

---

### 2.3 Atmospheric Pressure — Detailed Theory

Pressure is one of the **three fundamental thermodynamic state variables** (along with temperature and volume/density). It plays the following critical roles in air pollution:

1. **Gas density calculation** — $\rho = \dfrac{P}{RT}$ (Ideal Gas Law).
2. **Stack design** — Effluent gases must be at sufficient pressure to overcome atmospheric pressure and provide plume rise.
3. **Equipment rating** — Fans, blowers, and ductwork are pressure-rated.
4. **High-altitude corrections** — ESP and bag filter efficiency drop at lower pressure.

#### Barometric Pressure Formula (Hydrostatic)

The variation of pressure with altitude is derived from the **hydrostatic equilibrium**:

$$P = P_0 \cdot e^{-\frac{M g h}{R T}}$$

Where:
- $P$ = pressure at altitude $h$
- $P_0$ = pressure at reference altitude (sea level)
- $M$ = molar mass of air = **0.02896 kg/mol** (or 28.96 g/mol)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (in Kelvin)
- $h$ = altitude in meters

#### Scale Height ($H_s$)

The **scale height** is the altitude at which pressure drops to $\dfrac{1}{e}$ (about 36.8%) of its value at the reference level. It is defined as:

$$H_s = \frac{RT}{Mg}$$

> **For standard atmosphere (T = 288.15 K):**
> $H_s = \dfrac{8.314 \times 288.15}{0.02896 \times 9.81} \approx 8430 \text{ m} \approx 8.4 \text{ km}$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = P_0 e^{-h/H_s}$ | Barometric pressure variation with altitude | Pa, m |
| 2 | $H_s = \dfrac{RT}{Mg}$ | Scale height of the atmosphere | m |
| 3 | $\rho = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$ | Air density from ideal gas law | kg/m³ |
| 4 | $P_0 = 1.01325 \times 10^5$ Pa | Standard atmospheric pressure at sea level | Pa |
| 5 | $\Delta P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure due to a fluid column | Pa |
| 6 | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | Gauge pressure relation | Pa |
| 7 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Absolute pressure relation | Pa |
| 8 | $\dot{n} = \dfrac{PV}{RT}$ | Molar flow rate of gas | mol/s |
| 9 | $v_{exit} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_s - P_a)}{\rho_g}}$ | Stack exit velocity for plume rise | m/s |
| 10 | $T = T_0 - \Gamma \cdot h$ | Temperature at altitude $h$ (linear lapse rate) | K, where $\Gamma$ = lapse rate (K/m) |
| 11 | $\Delta h_{plume} = \dfrac{v_s \cdot d}{u} \left(1.5 + 0.04 \cdot \dfrac{T_s - T_a}{T_s} \cdot \dfrac{d}{u}\right)$ | Holland plume rise formula | m |
| 12 | $E = \dfrac{1000 \cdot C_p \cdot \rho_s \cdot \Delta T}{\rho_a \cdot c_p \cdot T_s} \cdot q$ | Buoyancy flux parameter (Briggs) | m⁴/s³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always express answers in SI units (Pa, m³, kg). For $R$, use **8.314 J/(mol·K)** in pressure-volume problems and **0.287 kJ/(kg·K)** in mass-based specific gas constant problems.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Design of Industrial Stacks** — Minimum stack height (per CPCB/IS 4320 guidelines) is calculated using wind speed, temperature, and pressure data.
2. **ESP Performance** — At high altitudes (e.g., mining sites in Ladakh), reduced gas density decreases ESP migration velocity, requiring larger collection areas.
3. **Meteorological Dispersal Models** — Gaussian Plume Model (the most-tested KTU topic) directly uses pressure and temperature.
4. **Indoor Air Quality** — Pressure differentials between indoor and outdoor drive infiltration of outdoor pollutants.
5. **Scrubber Design** — Gas density ($\rho \propto P$) affects absorption column sizing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Hydrostatic Equation

> **Starting Principle:** Newton's second law applied to a static fluid column. In a static atmosphere, there is no net force on any fluid element, so the pressure gradient force must exactly balance the gravitational force.

#### Step 1 — Set up the free body diagram

Consider a thin horizontal slab of air at altitude $z$ with:
- Cross-sectional area = $A$
- Thickness = $dz$
- Density of air = $\rho(z)$

The mass of this slab = $\rho(z) \cdot A \cdot dz$

The weight (downward force) = $m \cdot g = \rho(z) \cdot A \cdot dz \cdot g$

#### Step 2 — Apply force balance

The pressure at the bottom of the slab is $P(z)$, and at the top is $P(z + dz)$. The net upward force on the slab due to pressure is:

$$F_{net} = P(z) \cdot A - P(z + dz) \cdot A$$

For static equilibrium, this net force must balance the weight:

$$P(z) \cdot A - P(z + dz) \cdot A - \rho(z) \cdot A \cdot g \cdot dz = 0$$

#### Step 3 — Simplify

Divide the entire equation by $A$:

$$P(z) - P(z + dz) - \rho(z) \cdot g \cdot dz = 0$$

Rearrange:

$$P(z + dz) - P(z) = -\rho(z) \cdot g \cdot dz$$

The left side is the change in pressure $dP$ across the slab. So:

$$dP = -\rho(z) \cdot g \cdot dz$$

> **This is the fundamental hydrostatic equation.**

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign indicates that pressure **decreases** as altitude **increases** (since $dz > 0$ corresponds to $dP < 0$). This is consistent with physical intuition: deeper in the air ocean = more weight above = more pressure.

#### Step 4 — Substitute ideal gas law

For an ideal gas, the relationship between density, pressure, and temperature is:

$$\rho = \frac{PM}{RT}$$

Substitute this into the hydrostatic equation:

$$dP = -\left(\frac{PM}{RT}\right) g \, dz$$

Assuming $T$ is constant (isothermal atmosphere approximation, valid for the lower troposphere on average):

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{Mg}{RT} \, dz$$

#### Step 5 — Integrate from sea level to altitude $h$

Integrate both sides:

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{Mg}{RT} \int_{0}^{h} dz$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{Mg}{RT} h$$

Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{P}{P_0} = e^{-\frac{Mg h}{RT}}$$

$$\boxed{P = P_0 \cdot e^{-\frac{h}{H_s}} \quad \text{where} \quad H_s = \frac{RT}{Mg}}$$

> **This is the isothermal barometric formula**, which is a high-yield KTU result.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Scale Height

From the barometric formula:

$$P(h) = P_0 \cdot e^{-h/H_s}$$

By definition, scale height $H_s$ is the altitude at which:

$$P(H_s) = \frac{P_0}{e}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{P_0}{e} = P_0 \cdot e^{-H_s/H_s} = P_0 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{P_0}{e}$$

This is consistent with the definition. Now solve for $H_s$:

Starting from $\ln(P/P_0) = -h/H_s$:

$$H_s = -\frac{h}{\ln(P/P_0)} = \frac{h}{\ln(P_0/P)}$$

For the physical definition, we use dimensional analysis of the hydrostatic equation. From:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{Mg}{RT} dz$$

The exponent must be dimensionless, so $\dfrac{Mg \, h}{RT}$ is dimensionless, which means $\dfrac{RT}{Mg}$ has units of length. Therefore:

$$H_s = \frac{RT}{Mg}$$

#### Numerical Evaluation for Standard Atmosphere

Given:
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T_0 = 288.15$ K (ISA standard)
- $M = 0.02896$ kg/mol
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

Calculate:

$$H_s = \frac{8.314 \times 288.15}{0.02896 \times 9.81}$$

**Step-by-step arithmetic:**

Numerator: $8.314 \times 288.15 = 2396.04$ J/mol

Denominator: $0.02896 \times 9.81 = 0.28411$ kg·m/(mol·s²)

$$H_s = \frac{2396.04}{0.28411} = 8434.2 \text{ m} \approx 8.43 \text{ km}$$

> **Therefore, the scale height of the standard atmosphere is approximately 8.4 km.**

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A monitoring station is located at an altitude of 1500 m above mean sea level. The temperature at this altitude is measured as 15°C. Assuming isothermal conditions, calculate:
> (a) The scale height
> (b) The atmospheric pressure at this location
> (c) The air density at this location

#### Given Data

- $h = 1500$ m
- $T = 15°C = 288.15$ K
- $P_0 = 1.01325 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $M = 0.02896$ kg/mol
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

#### (a) Scale Height

$$H_s = \frac{RT}{Mg} = \frac{8.314 \times 288.15}{0.02896 \times 9.81}$$

Numerator: $8.314 \times 288.15$

$$8 \times 288.15 = 2305.20$$
$$0.3 \times 288.15 = 86.445$$
$$0.01 \times 288.15 = 2.8815$$
$$0.004 \times 288.15 = 1.1526$$
$$\text{Sum} = 2305.20 + 86.445 + 2.8815 + 1.1526 = 2395.68 \text{ J/mol}$$

Denominator: $0.02896 \times 9.81$

$$0.02896 \times 10 = 0.2896$$
$$0.02896 \times 0.19 = 0.0055024$$
$$0.2896 - 0.0055024 = 0.28410 \text{ kg·m/(mol·s²)}$$

$$H_s = \frac{2395.68}{0.28410} = 8432.9 \text{ m} \approx 8.43 \text{ km}$$

#### (b) Atmospheric Pressure

$$P = P_0 \cdot e^{-h/H_s} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \cdot e^{-1500/8432.9}$$

Compute the exponent:

$$\frac{1500}{8432.9} = 0.17787$$

$$e^{-0.17787} = ?$$

Using the Taylor expansion or calculator: $e^{-0.17787} \approx 0.8372$

$$P = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \times 0.8372 = 8.483 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$$

> **Result:** $P \approx 8.48 \times 10^4$ Pa, or **848.3 mbar**.

(This is consistent with the rule of thumb: pressure drops by ~12% per 1000 m of altitude; 1500 m → ~17% drop.)

#### (c) Air Density

$$\rho = \frac{PM}{RT} = \frac{8.483 \times 10^4 \times 0.02896}{8.314 \times 288.15}$$

Numerator: $8.483 \times 10^4 \times 0.02896 = 2456.6$ Pa·kg/mol

Denominator: $8.314 \times 288.15 = 2395.68$ J/mol

$$\rho = \frac{2456.6}{2395.68} = 1.025 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

> **Result:** $\rho \approx 1.025$ kg/m³, which is **lower than sea-level density** ($\approx 1.225$ kg/m³) by ~16%, as expected.

---

### 3.4 Conversion to Useful Product — Stoichiometric Example

> **Problem:** A coal-fired power plant generates 1000 kg/hr of $SO_2$. The flue gas is treated in a wet limestone FGD scrubber. Calculate:
> (a) The mass of limestone ($CaCO_3$, 100% pure) required.
> (b) The mass of gypsum ($CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O$) produced (assuming forced oxidation).

#### Stoichiometric Equations

$$SO_2 + CaCO_3 \rightarrow CaSO_3 + CO_2 \quad \text{(absorption)}$$
$$2CaSO_3 + O_2 + 4H_2O \rightarrow 2CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O \quad \text{(oxidation)}$$

Combined: $SO_2 + CaCO_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O + CO_2$

#### (a) Mass of Limestone

Molar masses:
- $M_{SO_2} = 64$ g/mol
- $M_{CaCO_3} = 100$ g/mol
- $M_{CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O} = 172$ g/mol

Moles of $SO_2$ per hour:

$$n_{SO_2} = \frac{1000 \times 10^3 \text{ g/hr}}{64 \text{ g/mol}} = 15625 \text{ mol/hr}$$

By 1:1 stoichiometry:

$$n_{CaCO_3} = 15625 \text{ mol/hr}$$

$$\dot{m}_{CaCO_3} = 15625 \times 100 = 1.5625 \times 10^6 \text{ g/hr} = 1562.5 \text{ kg/hr}$$

> **Result:** Approximately **1562.5 kg of limestone** is required per hour.

#### (b) Mass of Gypsum Produced

By 1:1 stoichiometry ($SO_2$ to $CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O$):

$$\dot{m}_{gypsum} = 15625 \text{ mol/hr} \times 172 \text{ g/mol} = 2.6875 \times 10^6 \text{ g/hr} = 2687.5 \text{ kg/hr}$$

> **Result:** Approximately **2687.5 kg/hr of gypsum** is recovered as a useful by-product, which can be sold to cement/plasterboard industries — a perfect example of **conversion to a useful product**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Conversion Pathways of Major Air Pollutants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Industrial Air Pollutant Sources] --> B[Source Characterization]
    B --> C{Pollutant Type?}
    C -->|Sulfur Compounds SO2| D1[Scrubbing with Limestone]
    C -->|Oxides of Nitrogen NOx| D2[Selective Catalytic Reduction SCR]
    C -->|Volatile Organics VOC| D3[Catalytic Thermal Oxidation]
    C -->|Particulate Matter PM| D4[Electrostatic Precipitator ESP]
    C -->|Hazardous Air Pollutants HAP| D5[High-Temperature Incineration]
    C -->|CO2 Greenhouse Gas| D6[Carbon Capture and Storage CCS]
    D1 --> E1[Conversion Reaction]
    D2 --> E2[Conversion Reaction]
    D3 --> E3[Conversion Reaction]
    D4 --> E4[Conversion Reaction]
    D5 --> E5[Conversion Reaction]
    D6 --> E6[Conversion Reaction]
    E1 --> F1[CaSO4 Gypsum - Useful]
    E2 --> F2[N2 plus H2O - Harmless]
    E3 --> F3[CO2 plus H2O - Harmless]
    E4 --> F4[Captured PM - Useful in Construction]
    E5 --> F5[CO2 HCl H2O - Harmless or Recovered]
    E6 --> F6[Urea Methanol Food Grade CO2 - Useful]
    F1 --> G[End Product - Useful or Harmless]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    G --> H[Discharge to Atmosphere or Market]
```

> **Interpretation:** The diagram maps the journey of a pollutant from its industrial origin, through the appropriate conversion technology, to a final state that is either environmentally benign or commercially valuable.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Atmospheric Layers and Pressure Variation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Sea Level P equals 1013.25 mbar] --> A1[Troposphere 0 to 11 km]
    A1 --> A2[Stratosphere 11 to 50 km]
    A2 --> A3[Mesosphere 50 to 85 km]
    A3 --> A4[Thermosphere above 85 km]
    A4 --> A5[Exosphere above 500 km]
    A0 -. Pressure drops exponentially .-> A1
    A1 -. Pressure drops exponentially .-> A2
    A2 -. Pressure continues to drop .-> A3
    A3 -. Very low pressure .-> A4
    A4 -. Near vacuum .-> A5
    B1[Temperature decreases at 6.5 C per km] -.-> A1
    B2[Temperature increases ozone layer] -.-> A2
    B3[Temperature decreases again] -.-> A3
    B4[Temperature increases sharply] -.-> A4
    A1 --> Z1[Most Air Pollution Occurs Here]
    Z1 --> Z2[Mixing Layer 0 to 1.5 km]
    Z1 --> Z3[Residual Layer 1.5 to 3 km]
```

> **Interpretation:** The schematic illustrates that as altitude increases, pressure decays exponentially (the troposphere contains virtually all mass and pressure), while temperature behaviour alternates. Air pollution control is overwhelmingly concerned with the **mixing layer** — the lowest 1.5 km of the troposphere.

---

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Role of Pressure in Pollution Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Atmospheric Pressure P_atm] --> Q1[Gas Density Calculation]
    P1 --> Q2[Stack Buoyancy and Plume Rise]
    P1 --> Q3[Fan and Blower Sizing]
    P1 --> Q4[Equipment Pressure Drop Rating]
    P1 --> Q5[High Altitude Correction Factor]
    P1 --> Q6[Indoor-Outdoor Pressure Differential]
    Q1 --> R1[Air Pollution Control Device Sizing]
    Q2 --> R2[Stack Height Design per CPCB]
    Q3 --> R3[Ductwork and ID Fan Selection]
    Q4 --> R4[Bag Filter and ESP Performance]
    Q5 --> R5[Site-Specific Design Adjustments]
    Q6 --> R6[Building Ventilation and IAQ]
    R1 --> S[Optimized Pollution Control System]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
    R5 --> S
    R6 --> S
```

> **Interpretation:** Pressure is a foundational input variable that propagates into nearly every design and operational decision in an air pollution control system. Engineers must always account for local pressure conditions, especially at high-altitude industrial sites.

---

### 4.4 Schematic — Conversion of $SO_2$ to Gypsum in a Wet FGD System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Boiler Flue Gas SO2 rich] --> B[Quench Section Cooling to Saturation]
    B --> C[Absorber Tower Lime Slurry Spray]
    C --> D[Reaction SO2 plus CaCO3 to CaSO3]
    D --> E[Oxidation Tank Forced Air Injection]
    E --> F[Reaction CaSO3 to CaSO4 dot 2H2O Gypsum]
    F --> G[Gypsum Dewatering Centrifuge]
    G --> H[Gypsum Storage Useful Product Sold]
    G --> I[Filtrate Recycle to Slurry Tank]
    C --> J[Clean Flue Gas to Stack]
    J --> K[Stack Discharge to Atmosphere]
```

> **Interpretation:** This schematic traces the end-to-end conversion of an air pollutant ($SO_2$) into a marketable by-product (gypsum) through sequential unit operations in a wet FGD plant.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **"Differentiate between 'Conversion to Harmless Products' and 'Removal of Pollutants' with one example each." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Conversion to Harmless/Useful Products | Removal of Pollutants |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The pollutant undergoes a **chemical reaction** to form a different, less harmful or commercially useful substance. | The pollutant is **physically separated** from the gas stream without changing its molecular identity. |
| **Process Type** | Chemical / Biological / Electrochemical transformation | Mechanical / Physical separation |
| **End State** | New substance produced (e.g., gypsum, $N_2$, $CO_2$, $H_2O$) | Pollutant captured and stored/disposed |
| **Example** | $SO_2$ converted to $H_2SO_4$ in a contact process plant | Particulate matter removed by a **cyclone separator** or **baghouse filter** |
| **Sustainability** | Often aligned with **circular economy** and **zero discharge** philosophy | Pollutant is still a waste, just relocated |

> The above tabulated answer covering the **definition, process type, end state, example, and sustainability aspect** of each approach is the expected KTU 3-mark response.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **"What is atmospheric pressure? State the standard value of atmospheric pressure at sea level in SI units and explain why pressure decreases with altitude." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Atmospheric pressure is the force per unit area exerted on a surface by the weight of the column of air above it, due to Earth's gravitational pull.

**Standard value at sea level:** $P_0 = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1013.25 \text{ mbar} = 760 \text{ mm Hg} = 1 \text{ atm}$

**Why pressure decreases with altitude (3 brief points):**

1. **Reduced air column above:** At higher altitudes, there is less air mass above any given point, hence less weight pressing down, so pressure is lower.
2. **Decreased gas density:** Air density decreases exponentially with altitude (since $\rho \propto P$), reducing the weight contribution of each successive layer.
3. **Hydrostatic equilibrium:** From $dP = -\rho g \, dz$, since $\rho > 0$ and $g > 0$, the pressure gradient $dP/dz$ is negative, confirming pressure decreases with increasing $z$.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** in Part B by not drawing a **neat labelled schematic** of the conversion process. Always include a flow diagram wherever a process is described.
> - For pressure problems, students forget to **convert temperature to Kelvin** before substituting. This costs 1 mark per instance.
> - In SCR/SNCR questions, students confuse the **catalyst** and **temperature ranges**. SCR operates at $300°C - 400°C$ with catalyst; SNCR at $900°C - 1100°C$ without catalyst.
> - For barometric formula problems, students often write the **isothermal version** in questions that specify a **linear temperature lapse rate** — be careful!

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the various methods used for **conversion of air pollutants to harmless and useful products**, with suitable chemical reactions and industrial examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **barometric pressure equation** $P = P_0 e^{-h/H_s}$ and explain the concept of **scale height** with a numerical example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Methods of Conversion (7 marks):**

A comprehensive answer should cover at least **4 of the following 5 categories**, each with an example reaction:

**1. Catalytic Oxidation (2 marks)**
- VOCs, CO, and hydrocarbons are oxidized over Pt, Pd, or metal-oxide catalysts at $200°C - 500°C$.
- Example: $CO + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \xrightarrow{Pt} CO_2$
- Example: $C_6H_6 + \tfrac{15}{2} O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{catalyst}} 6CO_2 + 3H_2O$
- Applications: Automobile catalytic converters, industrial VOC abatement.

**2. Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) for $NO_x$ (2 marks)**
- $NO_x$ reduced to $N_2$ and $H_2O$ using $NH_3$ or urea over a $V_2O_5/TiO_2$ catalyst at $300°C - 400°C$.
- Reaction: $4NO + 4NH_3 + O_2 \rightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O$
- Reaction: $2NO_2 + 4NH_3 + O_2 \rightarrow 3N_2 + 6H_2O$
- Application: Power plants, cement kilns, diesel engines.

**3. Wet Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD) (1 mark)**
- $SO_2$ absorbed in limestone slurry to form gypsum.
- $SO_2 + CaCO_3 \rightarrow CaSO_3 + CO_2$
- $2CaSO_3 + O_2 + 4H_2O \rightarrow 2(CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O)$ — gypsum recovered.
- Application: Coal-fired power plants; gypsum sold to cement industry.

**4. Biological Conversion / Biofiltration (1 mark)**
- Microorganisms degrade biodegradable VOCs and odours.
- Example: $H_2S \xrightarrow{\text{Thiobacillus thiooxidans}} S + H_2O$ — elemental sulfur recovered.
- Example: Toluene $\rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + biomass$
- Application: Wastewater treatment plants, composting facilities, food processing.

**5. Adsorption with Recovery (1 mark)**
- Activated carbon adsorbs VOCs; desorbed with steam/hot nitrogen; recovered as liquid product.
- Application: Solvent recovery industries (paint, pharmaceutical).

> **Schematic (must be drawn for full marks):**
> Source → Capture → Conversion Reactor → Product Recovery → Useful Product / Clean Gas to Stack

**Part (b) — Barometric Equation Derivation (7 marks):**

(Full derivation as given in Section 3.1 above, with the following key checkpoints for marks:)

| Step | Marks Awarded |
|---|---|
| Writing the hydrostatic equilibrium condition: $dP = -\rho g \, dz$ | 1 mark |
| Substituting ideal gas law: $\rho = PM/RT$ | 1 mark |
| Separating variables: $dP/P = -(Mg/RT) dz$ | 1 mark |
| Integration limits and final integrated form: $\ln(P/P_0) = -Mg h/RT$ | 2 marks |
| Final result: $P = P_0 e^{-h/H_s}$ and definition of scale height $H_s = RT/Mg$ | 1 mark |
| Numerical example: $H_s$ for standard atmosphere = 8.4 km | 1 mark |

**Numerical Example Solution:**

At $T = 288.15$ K, $M = 0.02896$ kg/mol, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K):

$$H_s = \frac{8.314 \times 288.15}{0.02896 \times 9.81} = \frac{2395.68}{0.28410} = 8432.9 \text{ m} \approx 8.43 \text{ km}$$

At $h = 2000$ m:

$$P = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \cdot e^{-2000/8432.9} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \cdot e^{-0.2372} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \times 0.7888$$

$$P = 7.993 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} \approx 79.9 \text{ kPa}$$

> **Conclusion:** At 2000 m altitude, atmospheric pressure drops to about **79%** of sea-level value.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 (Alternative)]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **role of atmospheric pressure** in air pollution dispersion with reference to the **hydrostatic equation**, **scale height**, and **pressure corrections for high-altitude pollution control equipment**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A thermal power plant emits flue gas at the rate of $200{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$ at NTP containing $1500 \text{ mg/m}^3$ of $SO_2$. The flue gas is passed through a wet limestone scrubber where 95% of $SO_2$ is removed. Calculate:
> - (i) The hourly emission of $SO_2$ to the atmosphere after treatment.
> - (ii) The mass of limestone ($CaCO_3$) consumed per day (24 hours).
> - (iii) The mass of gypsum ($CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O$) produced per day. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Role of Pressure in Pollution Dispersion (7 marks):**

A well-structured answer should include:

**1. Hydrostatic Equation and Vertical Pressure Gradient (2 marks)**
- $dP/dz = -\rho g$ indicates pressure decreases with height.
- This vertical gradient drives **buoyancy** of hot flue gas plumes.
- The buoyant plume rises until it reaches a layer of equal density (equilibrium level).

**2. Scale Height and Atmospheric Mass Distribution (2 marks)**
- $H_s = RT/Mg \approx 8.4$ km for standard atmosphere.
- Pressure decays exponentially: $P(h) = P_0 e^{-h/H_s}$.
- 75% of atmospheric mass is below ~10 km; pollution is essentially a tropospheric phenomenon.

**3. Impact on Pollution Control Equipment (3 marks)**
- **ESP at high altitudes:** Lower gas density (e.g., at Leh, $\rho \approx 0.75$ kg/m³) reduces the migration velocity of charged particles, decreasing collection efficiency. The Deutsch equation shows $\eta = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$, where $w \propto E \cdot d_p / \mu$ but the lower $\rho$ affects the actual particle charging and drift.
- **Bag filter:** Pressure drop $\Delta P = \xi \cdot (\rho v^2/2)$ decreases at altitude, requiring higher face velocity to maintain cleaning cycles.
