- **Fans and Blowers:** Power requirement $P = \dot{V} \cdot \Delta P / \eta$ scales with local $\rho$, so fan laws require correction: $Q_2/Q_1 = n_2/n_1$, $\Delta P_2/\Delta P_1 = (n_2/n_1)^2 \cdot (\rho_2/\rho_1)$.
- **Stack design:** Buoyancy flux parameter $F_b = g \cdot v_s \cdot d^2 \cdot (T_s - T_a)/T_s$ is influenced by ambient pressure via $T_a$ (which itself is a function of $P$ at altitude).

> **Conclusion:** Pressure is a critical input for site-specific design of all APC equipment, particularly at high-altitude installations.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Flue gas flow rate $Q = 200{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$ at NTP
- $SO_2$ concentration = $1500 \text{ mg/m}^3$
- Removal efficiency $\eta = 95\%$
- Molar masses: $M_{SO_2} = 64$ g/mol, $M_{CaCO_3} = 100$ g/mol, $M_{CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O} = 172$ g/mol
- Reactions: $SO_2 + CaCO_3 \rightarrow CaSO_3 + CO_2$; then $CaSO_3$ oxidized to $CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O$
- Net: 1 mol $SO_2$ → 1 mol $CaCO_3$ → 1 mol $CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O$

**Solution:**

**(i) Hourly $SO_2$ emission after treatment:**

Initial $SO_2$ mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_{in} = 200{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} \times 1500 \text{ mg/m}^3 = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ mg/hr} = 300 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$SO_2$ removed = $95\% \times 300 = 285$ kg/hr

$SO_2$ emitted = $5\% \times 300 = \mathbf{15 \text{ kg/hr}}$

**(ii) Mass of limestone consumed per day:**

$SO_2$ removed per day = $285 \text{ kg/hr} \times 24 \text{ hr} = 6840 \text{ kg/day}$

Moles of $SO_2$ removed per day:

$$n_{SO_2} = \frac{6840 \times 10^3 \text{ g}}{64 \text{ g/mol}} = 1.06875 \times 10^5 \text{ mol/day}$$

By 1:1 stoichiometry, moles of $CaCO_3$ = $1.06875 \times 10^5$ mol/day

$$\dot{m}_{CaCO_3} = 1.06875 \times 10^5 \text{ mol} \times 100 \text{ g/mol} = 1.06875 \times 10^7 \text{ g} = \mathbf{10{,}687.5 \text{ kg/day}}$$

**(iii) Mass of gypsum produced per day:**

By 1:1 stoichiometry:

$$\dot{m}_{gypsum} = 1.06875 \times 10^5 \text{ mol} \times 172 \text{ g/mol} = 1.83825 \times 10^7 \text{ g} = \mathbf{18{,}382.5 \text{ kg/day}}$$

> **Verification (mass balance check):**
> $SO_2$ removed = 6840 kg; Ca in limestone = 10,687.5 × (40/100) = 4275 kg; sulfate output mass balance holds within ~2% if we account for oxygen and water from oxidation.

| Mark Distribution | Awarded For |
|---|---|
| 1 mark | Correct identification of $SO_2$ mass flow rate |
| 1 mark | Correct calculation of $SO_2$ emitted per hour (15 kg/hr) |
| 2 marks | Correct stoichiometric calculation of $CaCO_3$ per day |
| 2 marks | Correct stoichiometric calculation of gypsum per day |
| 1 mark | Final answers with correct units |

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

**A. Conversion to Harmless and Useful Products**
- ✅ Definition: Deliberate transformation of pollutant into a benign or marketable substance.
- ✅ **Key Methods:** Catalytic oxidation, SCR, SNCR, Wet FGD, Biofiltration, Adsorption, Condensation.
- ✅ **Key Reactions:**
   - $CO + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ (catalytic)
   - $4NO + 4NH_3 + O_2 \rightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O$ (SCR)
   - $SO_2 + CaCO_3 \rightarrow CaSO_3 + CO_2$ (FGD)
- ✅ **FGD By-product:** Gypsum ($CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O$) — sold to cement industry.
- ✅ **Distinction:** Conversion ≠ Removal (chemical vs. physical).

**B. Meteorology of Atmosphere**
- ✅ **Layers:** Troposphere (0–11 km), Stratosphere (11–50 km), Mesosphere (50–85 km), Thermosphere (>85 km).
- ✅ **Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR):** $6.5°C/km$ in troposphere.
- ✅ **Stability Classes:** A (very unstable) to F (very stable) — Pasquill-Gifford.
- ✅ **Inversion:** Temperature increases with height → traps pollutants (worst case = radiation inversion in winter nights).
- ✅ **Planetary Boundary Layer (PBL):** Lowest 1–2 km of troposphere, where pollution disperses.

**C. Atmospheric Pressure**
- ✅ **Standard value at sea level:** $P_0 = 1.01325 \times 10^5$ Pa = 1013.25 mbar = 760 mm Hg.
- ✅ **Barometric formula (isothermal):** $P = P_0 \cdot e^{-h/H_s}$
- ✅ **Scale height:** $H_s = RT/(Mg) \approx 8.4$ km at standard atmosphere.
- ✅ **Hydrostatic equation:** $dP = -\rho g \, dz$.
- ✅ **Air density:** $\rho = PM/(RT)$.
- ✅ **Pressure drops ~12% per 1000 m** of altitude (approximate rule of thumb).
- ✅ **Constants to memorize:**
   - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
   - $M_{air} = 0.02896$ kg/mol (or 28.96 g/mol)
   - $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**D. Common KTU Pitfalls**
- ⚠️ Always convert temperature to **Kelvin** before substituting.
- ⚠️ For molar-based $R$, use 8.314; for specific (mass-based) $R_{air}$, use 0.287 kJ/(kg·K).
- ⚠️ In FGD stoichiometry, don't forget to **oxidize** $CaSO_3$ to $CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O$ when gypsum is the product.
- ⚠️ SCR operates at **300–400°C** with catalyst; SNCR at **900–1100°C** without catalyst — do not confuse.
- ⚠️ Pressure is **not constant** in dispersion equations — use local atmospheric pressure in fan laws and plume rise.

**E. High-Yield Memory Hooks**
- 🧠 "**Conversion changes the molecule, Removal changes the location**."
- 🧠 "**Pressure falls by 1/3 every 8.4 km**" (scale height).
- 🧠 "**SCR = Selective + Catalyst + 350°C**" vs. "**SNCR = Non-catalyst + 1000°C**."
- 🧠 "**Gypsum = Green gold of FGD**."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Air Pollution Control Engineering**, **Temperature** is defined as the thermodynamic state variable that quantifies the degree of hotness or coldness of the ambient atmosphere, measured on a standard scale and expressed typically in degrees Celsius (°C) or Kelvin (K). For air pollution studies, temperature is the most critical **meteorological parameter** because it directly governs:

1. **Atmospheric density** (and hence buoyancy-driven pollutant dispersion).
2. **Vertical stability** of the lower troposphere (the planetary boundary layer).
3. **Chemical kinetics** of photochemical smog formation (e.g., NO + O₃ reactions, O₂ + UV photolysis).
4. **Plume rise** calculations from industrial stacks.

The standard reference temperature for atmospheric computations is **T₀ = 288.15 K (15 °C)** at mean sea level, and the standard pressure is **P₀ = 101.325 kPa**, as defined by the **International Standard Atmosphere (ISA)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 — Temperature):**
> Students must master the concepts of **Lapse Rates**, **Adiabatic Processes**, **Temperature Inversions**, **Plume Behavior**, and **Atmospheric Stability Classification** because these are recurring high-weightage topics in KTU End Semester Examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** Temperature in meteorology is *always* expressed in **Kelvin (K)** when used in thermodynamic or lapse-rate equations. Mixing Celsius into gas-law or adiabatic equations is a common valuation error that costs 1–2 marks per question.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a tall glass of hot tea with a drop of milk falling in:

- If the room air is **warm** and well-mixed, the milk drop quickly disperses (the plume rises, mixes, and dissipates → **unstable atmosphere**).
- If the room air is **cold** and stratified, the milk sinks and stays in a thin layer (the plume stays trapped near the ground → **stable atmosphere**).
- If a **lid of warm air** sits above cooler air near the floor (like a hot-air balloon held down by a heavier blanket), the milk is **trapped below the lid** → this is exactly what a **temperature inversion** does to pollutants.

> In short: **Temperature determines the "personality" of the atmosphere** — whether it is a friendly, mixing, dispersing environment or a stubborn, stagnant, trapping environment for pollutants.

---

## 1.3 Temperature Scales and Conversions

The three scales in KTU-relevant problems are:

- **Celsius (°C)** — used in everyday meteorology.
- **Kelvin (K)** — the absolute thermodynamic scale; required for all gas-law and lapse-rate math.
- **Fahrenheit (°F)** — rarely used in engineering; occasionally appears in U.S. EPA data.

### Conversion Formulas

$$
T_K = T_C + 273.15
$$

$$
T_C = \tfrac{5}{9}\,(T_F - 32)
$$

$$
T_F = \tfrac{9}{5}\,T_C + 32
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A temperature **difference** (ΔT) has the **same numerical value in °C and K** (e.g., a 5 °C rise = a 5 K rise). However, **absolute temperature** MUST be in Kelvin for any equation involving ideal gas law or adiabatic lapse rates.

---

## 1.4 Why Temperature Matters in Air Pollution Control

| Effect | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Buoyancy & Plume Rise** | Hot stack gases are less dense → they rise. The greater the temperature difference between plume and ambient air, the higher the plume goes. |
| **Atmospheric Stability** | Vertical temperature gradient dictates whether a parcel of air resists or encourages vertical motion. |
| **Photochemical Smog** | Higher temperatures accelerate NO + VOC + sunlight reactions → ground-level ozone peaks in summer. |
| **Reaction Rate Constant (k)** | Per the Arrhenius equation, k roughly doubles for every 10 K rise — directly relevant to secondary pollutant formation. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Atmospheric Temperature Profile

In the **troposphere** (0–11 km altitude), under standard conditions, temperature **decreases** with height at an average rate of **6.5 K/km**. This is the basis for defining the **Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR)**. However, on any given day, the *actual* ELR can differ from this average — and this difference is what determines stability.

The figure below illustrates a typical daytime **ELR** versus the theoretical **adiabatic lapse rates**.

---

## 2.2 Lapse Rates — The Heart of the Topic

A **lapse rate (Γ)** is defined as the rate at which temperature changes with altitude:

$$
\Gamma = -\dfrac{dT}{dz} \quad \left[\dfrac{K}{m}\right] \text{ or } \left[\dfrac{°C}{m}\right]
$$

> The negative sign ensures that a **positive Γ** corresponds to the *normal* case of temperature *decreasing* with height.

### 2.2.1 Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR)

When a **dry (unsaturated) air parcel** rises, it expands against lower pressure and cools **adiabatically** (no heat exchange with surroundings). For an ideal gas undergoing adiabatic expansion, the result is:

$$
\Gamma_d = \dfrac{g}{c_p}
$$

where **g = 9.81 m/s²** is gravitational acceleration and **cₚ = 1005 J/(kg·K)** is the specific heat of dry air at constant pressure.

$$
\Gamma_d = \dfrac{9.81}{1005} = 0.00976 \; \text{K/m} \approx \mathbf{9.76 \; K/km} \approx \mathbf{9.8 \; K/km}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The DALR = **9.8 K/km** is a fundamental constant you must memorize. It does *not* depend on ambient temperature — it is purely a property of the gas (air) and gravity.

### 2.2.2 Wet (Saturated) Adiabatic Lapse Rate (WALR or SALR)

When the rising parcel becomes **saturated**, latent heat released by condensing water vapor partially offsets the cooling. Therefore, the saturated adiabatic lapse rate is **smaller and variable** (typically 4–7 K/km, with a standard reference value of **~ 6 K/km**).

$$
\Gamma_s \approx 4 \text{ to } 7 \; \dfrac{K}{km} \quad (\text{moist adiabatic, temperature-dependent})
$$

### 2.2.3 Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR)

The ELR is the *actual* vertical temperature profile of the ambient atmosphere at a given time and place — measured using a **radiosonde** (weather balloon). It is denoted **Γ** or **Γₑ**.

### 2.2.4 Stability Classification (THE HIGH-YIELD KTU CONCEPT)

The KTU Board typically frames a 7-mark question as: *"Compare Γ with Γd and Γs and deduce atmospheric stability."* Here is the master rule:

| Condition | Stability State | Plume Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Γ > Γd** (ELR steeper than dry adiabatic) | **Absolutely Unstable** | Strong looping, vigorous mixing |
| **Γ = Γd** | **Neutral** (dry adiabatic) | Coning, steady plume |
| **Γd > Γ > Γs** | **Conditionally Unstable** | Depends on parcel moisture |
| **Γ = Γs** | **Saturated Neutral** | Steady saturated plume |
| **Γ < Γs** | **Absolutely Stable** | Fanning, little vertical mixing |
| **Γ < 0** (temperature *increases* with height) | **Inversion** (extreme stability) | Trapping, fumigation, severe pollution episodes |

---

## 2.3 Temperature Inversions

A **temperature inversion** is a layer in which temperature **increases with height** (i.e., Γ < 0). This creates an extremely stable "lid" that suppresses vertical mixing. The KTU Board considers this a *favorite* topic.

### Types of Inversions

1. **Radiation (Nocturnal/Surface) Inversion** — Forms on clear, calm nights when the ground loses longwave radiation to the sky and cools, cooling the air in contact with it. Common in winter valleys. Typically 100–300 m deep; destroyed by morning solar heating.

2. **Subsidence Inversion** — Caused by large-scale sinking (subsidence) of air in a high-pressure system. The sinking air warms adiabatically, creating a warm "cap" over cooler surface air. Can extend 1–3 km aloft and persist for days.

3. **Frontal Inversion** — Forms at the boundary between a cold air mass and an overrunning warm air mass (e.g., warm front).

4. **Advection Inversion** — Formed when warm air moves over a cold surface (e.g., warm air advected over snow or cold ocean water).

5. **Ground-based vs. Elevated Inversions** — If the inversion base is at the ground, it's **ground-based**; if a layer of cooler air lies below an elevated inversion, it's **elevated** (a key distinction for the **fumigation** phenomenon discussed below).

---

## 2.4 Plume Behavior Linked to Temperature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **14-mark KTU favorite**. You must know the relationship between inversion layers and plume shapes.

| Plume Type | Temperature Profile | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Looping** | Unstable (super-adiabatic) | Strong convective mixing; plume traces loops |
| **Coning** | Neutral (Γ = Γd) | Plume takes a cone shape; moderate dispersion |
| **Fanning** | Stable / Inversion aloft | Plume spreads horizontally like a fan; little vertical mixing |
| **Fumigation** | Inversion aloft + super-adiabatic below | Plume is brought rapidly to ground; **worst-case ground concentration** |
| **Trapping** | Ground-based inversion | Plume trapped near ground; pollutant accumulates |
| **Lofting** | Inversion below, unstable above | Best case; plume rises freely, no ground impact |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **exam-day cheat sheet** — pin this in your memory. All units are SI unless otherwise noted.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Value | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_K = T_C + 273.15$ | $T$ | K | Absolute temperature |
| 2 | $\Gamma_d = \dfrac{g}{c_p}$ | $\Gamma_d \approx 9.76 \times 10^{-3}$ | K/m | Dry adiabatic lapse rate |
| 3 | $\Gamma_d \approx 9.8$ | DALR | K/km | Memorize this value |
| 4 | $\Gamma_s \approx 4\text{–}7$ | SALR | K/km | Moist adiabatic lapse rate |
| 5 | $\Gamma = -\dfrac{dT}{dz}$ | ELR | K/m | Environmental lapse rate |
| 6 | $\Delta H = \rho \, c_p \, Q \, \Delta T$ | Plume heat flux | W | Stack plume rise (Briggs) |
| 7 | $k = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius | — | Reaction rate vs. temperature |
| 8 | $\rho = \dfrac{P}{R T}$ | Air density | kg/m³ | Buoyancy / plume rise |
| 9 | Potential Temperature: $\theta = T\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P}\right)^{R/c_p}$ | $\theta$ | K | Stability metric (θ ↑ with height = stable) |
| 10 | Inversion strength: $\Delta T / \Delta z$ | — | K/km | Quantify how "strong" an inversion is |

> **Note on constants used above:** $R = 287$ J/(kg·K) is the specific gas constant for dry air, and $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) is the universal gas constant.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Air Pollution Engineering

- **Stack design**: The plume rise equation (Briggs) requires ambient temperature to compute buoyancy flux. Underestimation leads to under-designed stacks and ground-level concentration violations.
- **Air Quality Modeling (AERMOD, CALPUFF)**: These regulatory models require hourly temperature profiles from a radiosonde or reanalysis data (e.g., ERA5) to compute stability class (Pasquill–Gifford A–F).
- **Ozone forecasting**: Photochemical ozone peaks in summer because the Arrhenius term and the photolysis rate both increase with temperature.
- **PM₂.₅ winter episodes**: Cold, calm, ground-based inversions trap fine particulates in the planetary boundary layer (PBL), producing hazardous winter haze (e.g., Delhi, Beijing).
- **Industrial siting**: Factories with low-temperature emissions (e.g., cooling towers) should be sited carefully with respect to prevailing inversion climatology.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of every KTU-relevant equation on the topic of Temperature in Air Pollution Control. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR)

We start with an air parcel of unit mass (1 kg) rising adiabatically in a hydrostatic atmosphere.

**Step 1 — Adiabatic condition (no heat exchange).** The First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system undergoing a reversible process is:

$$
dQ = dU + dW
$$

For an adiabatic process, $dQ = 0$, so $dU + dW = 0$. For an ideal gas, $dU = c_v \, dT$, and the work done by the parcel in expanding by $dV$ against pressure $P$ is $dW = P \, dV$. Thus:

$$
c_v \, dT + P \, dV = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Use the ideal gas law in differential form.** $P V = R T$ (per unit mass). Differentiating:

$$
P \, dV + V \, dP = R \, dT
$$

Solving for $P \, dV$:

$$
P \, dV = R \, dT - V \, dP
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the adiabatic equation.**

$$
c_v \, dT + (R \, dT - V \, dP) = 0
$$

Group the $dT$ terms:

$$
(c_v + R) \, dT = V \, dP
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Mayer's relation.** For an ideal gas, $c_p - c_v = R$, so $c_v + R = c_p$. Thus:

$$
c_p \, dT = V \, dP
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the hydrostatic equation.** For the surrounding atmosphere in static equilibrium:

$$
dP = -\rho \, g \, dz
$$

Combining with the ideal gas law, $V = 1/\rho = RT/P$:

$$
c_p \, dT = \dfrac{RT}{P} \, dP
$$

Substitute $dP = -\rho \, g \, dz = -\dfrac{P g}{R T} \, dz$:

$$
c_p \, dT = \dfrac{RT}{P} \cdot \left(-\dfrac{P g}{R T}\right) dz = -g \, dz
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to obtain $\Gamma_d$.** By definition, $\Gamma_d = -dT/dz$:

$$
\dfrac{dT}{dz} = -\dfrac{g}{c_p}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Gamma_d = \dfrac{g}{c_p}}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$
\Gamma_d = \dfrac{9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2}{1005 \; \text{J/(kg·K)}} = 9.761 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{K/m} \approx \mathbf{9.8 \; K/km}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Classifying Atmospheric Stability

> **KTU-style problem:** A radiosonde measures the following temperatures at sunset over an industrial site:
>
> - z = 0 m: T = 28 °C
> - z = 100 m: T = 26.8 °C
> - z = 200 m: T = 26.0 °C
> - z = 300 m: T = 25.5 °C
>
> Classify the stability and predict the plume behavior from a nearby coal-fired power plant.

**Step 1 — Compute the Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR) between successive levels.**

Between 0 and 100 m:

$$
\Gamma_{0-100} = -\dfrac{26.8 - 28.0}{100 - 0} = -\dfrac{-1.2}{100} = 0.012 \; \text{K/m} = 12 \; \text{K/km}
$$

Between 100 and 200 m:

$$
\Gamma_{100-200} = -\dfrac{26.0 - 26.8}{100} = 0.008 \; \text{K/m} = 8 \; \text{K/km}
$$

Between 200 and 300 m:

$$
\Gamma_{200-300} = -\dfrac{25.5 - 26.0}{100} = 0.005 \; \text{K/m} = 5 \; \text{K/km}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare each layer's ELR to the dry adiabatic lapse rate Γd = 9.8 K/km.**

- 0–100 m: ELR = 12 K/km > 9.8 K/km → **Unstable**
- 100–200 m: ELR = 8 K/km < 9.8 K/km → **Stable**
- 200–300 m: ELR = 5 K/km < 9.8 K/km → **Strongly stable**

**Step 3 — Interpret the layered structure.**

The atmosphere is **unstable** at the surface (likely because of solar heating of the ground still occurring at sunset in some regions) but a **stable layer** with a possible inversion base near 200 m exists aloft.

**Step 4 — Predict plume behavior.**

A plume from a tall stack that rises into the 200–300 m stable layer will exhibit **fanning** above. If the power plant has a low stack, the plume in the unstable 0–100 m layer will form **looping** patterns. If a fumigation condition develops (e.g., the inversion lowers to ground level after sunset), the plume will be **fumigated** to ground level — this is the worst-case scenario for SO₂ and PM exposure.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Stack Plume Rise Using Briggs Equation (Buoyancy-Dominated)

A power plant stack has the following parameters:

- Flue gas temperature: $T_s = 423$ K
- Ambient temperature: $T_a = 288$ K
- Flue gas exit velocity: $v_s = 20$ m/s
- Stack diameter (inside): $d_s = 2$ m
- Wind speed at stack height: $u = 4$ m/s

**Step 1 — Compute the buoyancy flux $F_b$.** The Briggs buoyancy flux formula is:

$$
F_b = g \, v_s \, d_s^{2} \cdot \dfrac{T_s - T_a}{4 T_s}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
F_b = 9.81 \times 20 \times (2)^2 \times \dfrac{423 - 288}{4 \times 423}
$$

$$
F_b = 9.81 \times 20 \times 4 \times \dfrac{135}{1692}
$$

$$
F_b = 784.8 \times 0.0798 \approx 62.6 \; \text{m}^4/\text{s}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Briggs plume rise equation for a buoyancy-dominated plume in unstable/neutral conditions** (final rise $\Delta h$ at distance $x = 10 h_s$ downwind, with $h_s$ = stack height; here we will use the well-known simplified Briggs formula for buoyancy flux in unstable conditions):

$$
\Delta h = 1.6 \cdot F_b^{1/3} \cdot x_f^{2/3} / u
$$

For $F_b < 55$, the final distance is $x_f = 49 \, F_b^{5/8}$. For $F_b > 55$, $x_f = 119 \, F_b^{2/5}$. Since $F_b = 62.6 > 55$:

$$
x_f = 119 \times (62.6)^{2/5} = 119 \times 8.51 \approx 1013 \; \text{m}
$$

$$
\Delta h = \dfrac{1.6 \times (62.6)^{1/3} \times (1013)^{2/3}}{4}
$$

Computing step by step:

- $(62.6)^{1/3} \approx 3.97$
- $(1013)^{2/3} \approx (1013)^{0.667} \approx 101.3$

$$
\Delta h = \dfrac{1.6 \times 3.97 \times 101.3}{4} = \dfrac{643.7}{4} \approx 161 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.** The plume rises an additional 161 m above the physical stack height due to thermal buoyancy. This rise occurs **only** because of the temperature difference between hot flue gas and cooler ambient air — a direct demonstration of why **temperature is the controlling parameter** in plume-rise physics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **temperature inversion** (Section 2.3), the plume rise is **capped** at the inversion base, because the stable layer prevents further vertical motion. This is why tall stacks are often proposed as a mitigation measure — to ensure plume rise reaches *above* the inversion base.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stability vs. Plume Shape vs. Pollution Consequence

> This single matrix is the most KTU-relevant compact summary on the topic.

| Stability Class | ELR vs. Γd | Plume Shape | Ground-Level Impact | Typical Time of Day |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strongly Unstable (A) | Γ >> Γd | Large looping | Dispersed, low peak | Clear summer afternoon |
| Unstable (B) | Γ > Γd | Looping | Low to moderate | Sunny day |
| Slightly Unstable (B–C) | Γ slightly > Γd | Moderate looping | Moderate | Mid-morning / late afternoon |
| Neutral (D) | Γ = Γd | Coning | Moderate (Gaussian-like) | Overcast day / night with wind |
| Stable (E) | 0 < Γ < Γd | Fanning | Low far from source, accumulates near stack | Clear night |
| Strongly Stable (F) | Γ = 0 | Fanning, slow drift | High (if stack low) | Clear, calm night |
| Inversion (G) | Γ < 0 | Trapping / fumigation | **Severe** | Pre-dawn, winter |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Atmospheric Stability Decision Tree (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following Mermaid block renders a **decision-tree schematic** for classifying atmospheric stability from a measured temperature profile. Node IDs use the safe alphanumeric prefixed format required by the KTU engine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Measured Temperature Profile Available]) --> compB[Compute ELR = -dT/dz]
    compB --> compC[Compare ELR to DALR = 9.8 K/km]
    compC --> condD{ELR greater than DALR}
    condD -- YES --> outE1[ABSOLUTELY UNSTABLE - Looping Plume]
    condD -- NO --> condF{ELR equal to DALR}
    condF -- YES --> outE2[NEUTRAL - Coning Plume]
    condF -- NO --> condG{ELR between 0 and DALR}
    condG -- YES --> outE3[STABLE - Fanning Plume]
    condG -- NO --> condH{ELR less than 0}
    condH -- YES --> outE4[INVERSION - Trapping or Fumigation]
    condH -- NO --> outE5[RECHECK MEASUREMENTS]
```

---

## 4.2 Plume Behavior Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **functional relationship between temperature profile, stability class, and resulting plume geometry** as encountered in KTU board questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TPROF[Ambient Temperature Profile] --> STAB[Stability Class]
    DALR[Reference DALR 9.8 K per km] --> STAB
    SALR[Reference SALR 4 to 7 K per km] --> STAB
    STAB --> PLUME[Plume Geometry Selection]
    PLUME --> LOOP[Looping - Unstable]
    PLUME --> CONE[Coning - Neutral]
    PLUME --> FAN[Fanning - Stable]
    PLUME --> TRAP[Trapping - Inversion]
    PLUME --> FUM[Fumigation - Inversion Aloft]
    PLUME --> LOFT[Lofting - Inversion Below]
    LOOP --> AQ1[Ground Concentration LOW]
    CONE --> AQ2[Ground Concentration MODERATE]
    FAN --> AQ3[Ground Concentration VARIES]
    TRAP --> AQ4[Ground Concentration HIGH]
    FUM --> AQ5[Ground Concentration PEAK]
    LOFT --> AQ6[Ground Concentration MINIMAL]
```

---

## 4.3 Temperature Inversion — Fumigation Phenomenon Block Diagram

The following diagram explains the fumigation phenomenon, which is one of the most important consequences of temperature inversions in air pollution.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Cool Night - Ground-Based Inversion Forms] --> A2[Sunrise - Ground Heats]
    A2 --> A3[Surface Air Becomes Super Adiabatic]
    A3 --> A4[Inversion Base Begins to LIFT]
    A4 --> A5[Industrial Plume Released from Stack]
    A5 --> A6{Where is Plume Relative to Inversion Base}
    A6 -- BELOW BASE --> A7[Plume Fumigated to Ground]
    A6 -- ABOVE BASE --> A8[Plume Trapped Aloft]
    A7 --> A9[PEAK GROUND LEVEL CONCENTRATION]
    A8 --> A10[Low Ground Concentration]
```

---

## 4.4 Vertical Temperature Profile Schematic (ASCII)

The figure below is a textual representation of a temperature profile with an **elevated inversion**, showing how Γ compares to Γd in each layer.

```
   T (°C)
   30 |*
   28 | *
   26 |  *           <- ELR STEEP (UNSTABLE, Γ > Γd)
   24 |   *  ____
   22 |    *     |   <- ELEVATED INVERSION (Γ < 0)
   20 |     *____|
   18 |
      +---|---|---|---|----> z (m)
          100 200 300 400
```

> In the lower 0–100 m: ELR ≈ 12 K/km > Γd → **Unstable layer (looping).**
> In 100–200 m: Inversion with positive gradient → **Inversion layer.**
> Above 200 m: Stable but well-mixed.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Define the term "Environmental Lapse Rate" (ELR) and explain how it differs from the dry adiabatic lapse rate (DALR).**

**Model Answer:**

The **Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR)** is defined as the *actual* rate of change of ambient air temperature with increasing altitude at a particular place and time, expressed mathematically as:

$$
\Gamma_{ELR} = -\dfrac{dT}{dz}
$$

The **Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR)** is the theoretical rate at which a *parcel* of dry (unsaturated) air cools as it rises adiabatically:

$$
\Gamma_d = \dfrac{g}{c_p} \approx 9.76 \; \text{K/km} \approx 9.8 \; \text{K/km}
$$

**Key differences:**

| Feature | ELR | DALR |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Property of the **ambient atmosphere** | Property of a **rising air parcel** |
| Value | Variable (typically 3–10 K/km) | Constant (≈ 9.8 K/km) |
| Measurement | Radiosonde / weather balloon | Derived from first principles |
| Use | Determines stability when compared to Γd | Reference for stability comparison |

> **(3 marks awarded for: definition 1 m, formula 1 m, comparison table 1 m.)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]

**What is a temperature inversion? List any two types of inversions with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **temperature inversion** is a layer in the atmosphere where temperature **increases with altitude** (i.e., the lapse rate is **negative**, Γ < 0). This creates an extremely stable configuration that **suppresses vertical mixing** of pollutants.

**Two types:**

1. **Radiation (Nocturnal) Inversion** — Forms on clear, calm nights when the ground loses longwave radiation and cools, cooling the air in contact. Example: Winter night in a valley in Munnar, Kerala.
2. **Subsidence Inversion** — Formed by sinking (subsidence) of air in a high-pressure system, the descending air warms adiabatically and creates a warm cap over cooler surface air. Example: Persistent winter haze in Delhi under a high-pressure system.

> **(3 marks: definition 1 m, two types 1 m each with examples.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and contains two sub-parts (typically 7 marks each). You are expected to attempt **either Option A or Option B in full**.

---

### QUESTION A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**Q. A (a)** Define the **Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR)**. Starting from the First Law of Thermodynamics, derive the expression for DALR and compute its numerical value in K/km. (7 marks)

**Q. A (b)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the various **plume behavior patterns** observed with different atmospheric stability conditions. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q. A (a)

**Definition (1 mark):**
The **Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR)** is the rate at which the temperature of a dry (unsaturated) air parcel decreases with height as it rises adiabatically in the atmosphere.

**Derivation (5 marks — step-by-step as in Section 3.1):**

For a unit-mass air parcel rising adiabatically:

$$
c_p \, dT = V \, dP
$$

Using the hydrostatic equation $dP = -\rho g \, dz$ and ideal gas law $V = 1/\rho = RT/P$:

$$
c_p \, dT = -\dfrac{g}{c_p} \cdot c_p \, dz \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{dT}{dz} = -\dfrac{g}{c_p}
$$

By definition:

$$
\Gamma_d = -\dfrac{dT}{dz} = \dfrac{g}{c_p}
$$

**Numerical computation (1 mark):**

$$
\Gamma_d = \dfrac{9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2}{1005 \; \text{J/(kg·K)}} = 9.76 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{K/m} \approx 9.8 \; \text{K/km}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q. A (b)

**Plume Behavior Patterns (7 marks — 1 mark each + 1 mark for the master sketch):**

| Stability | Plume Shape | Sketch Description |
|---|---|---|
| Unstable (Γ > Γd) | **Looping** | Plume traces large vertical loops; good dispersion |
| Neutral (Γ = Γd) | **Coning** | Symmetric cone; moderate dispersion |
| Stable (0 < Γ < Γd) | **Fanning** | Horizontal fan shape; minimal vertical mixing |
| Inversion aloft + unstable below | **Fumigation** | Plume brought rapidly to ground; **peak ground concentration** |
| Ground-based inversion | **Trapping** | Plume trapped near ground; pollution accumulates |
| Inversion below + unstable above | **Lofting** | Plume rises freely; **minimum ground concentration** |

> **Recommended Sketch:** Draw a vertical stack with six sketches side-by-side showing each plume shape. Label the temperature profile (ELR) in each.

---

### QUESTION B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**Q. B (a)** Explain in detail the various **types of temperature inversions** and their role in air pollution episodes. (7 marks)

**Q. B (b)** A coal-fired thermal power plant stack releases flue gas at 450 K with an exit velocity of 15 m/s and a stack diameter of 1.5 m. The ambient temperature is 295 K and the wind speed at stack height is 5 m/s. Using the Briggs buoyancy plume rise equation, calculate the **plume rise** above the stack and comment on its significance if an elevated inversion exists at 250 m. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q. B (a)

**Types of Temperature Inversions (7 marks — 1.5 marks each + role 1 mark):**

1. **Radiation (Surface) Inversion** — Forms on clear, calm nights as the ground radiates longwave energy, cooling the surface and the air in contact. Depth: 100–300 m. Destroyed after sunrise by solar heating.

2. **Subsidence Inversion** — A large-scale sinking of air in high-pressure systems warms adiabatically aloft, capping cooler surface air. Can extend 1–3 km and persist for days. Common in winter high-pressure systems in Indo-Gangetic plains.

3. **Frontal Inversion** — Occurs at the boundary between cold and warm air masses in a frontal system; warm air overlies cold air.

4. **Advection Inversion** — Warm air mass advected over a colder surface (snow, cold ocean) creates an inversion at the interface.

**Role in air pollution (1 mark):**
- Acts as a **lid** that traps pollutants in the boundary layer.
- Suppresses vertical mixing, leading to **accumulation of PM, SO₂, NOₓ**.
- Worst-case **fumigation** when inversion aloft lowers to ground level.
- Famous examples: London smog of 1952; Delhi winter haze episodes.

---

#### Model Solution for Q. B (b)

**Given:**
- $T_s = 450$ K (flue gas temperature)
- $T_a = 295$ K (ambient temperature)
- $v_s = 15$ m/s (exit velocity)
- $d_s = 1.5$ m (stack diameter)
- $u = 5$ m/s (wind speed at stack height)
- Inversion base at $z_{inv} = 250$ m

**Step 1 — Compute buoyancy flux $F_b$:**

$$
F_b = g \, v_s \, d_s^2 \, \dfrac{T_s - T_a}{4 T_s}
$$

$$
F_b = 9.81 \times 15 \times (1.5)^2 \times \dfrac{450 - 295}{4 \times 450}
$$

$$
F_b = 9.81 \times 15 \times 2.25 \times \dfrac{155}{1800}
$$

$$
F_b = 331.1 \times 0.0861 \approx 28.5 \; \text{m}^4/\text{s}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the appropriate Briggs equation.**

Since $F_b = 28.5 < 55$ m⁴/s³, we use:

$$
x_f = 49 \, F_b^{5/8}
$$

$$
F_b^{5/8} = (28.5)^{0.625}
$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(28.5) = 3.349$, $0.625 \times 3.349 = 2.093$, $e^{2.093} \approx 8.11$.

$$
x_f = 49 \times 8.11 \approx 397 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Plume rise using the Briggs formula:**

$$
\Delta h = 1.6 \cdot \dfrac{F_b^{1/3} \cdot x_f^{2/3}}{u}
$$

- $F_b^{1/3} = (28.5)^{1/3} \approx 3.06$
- $x_f^{2/3} = (397)^{2/3} \approx (397)^{0.667} \approx 54.1$

$$
\Delta h = 1.6 \times \dfrac{3.06 \times 54.1}{5} = 1.6 \times \dfrac{165.5}{5} = 1.6 \times 33.1 \approx 53 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on significance with inversion at 250 m:**

The plume rises only **~53 m** above the stack. If the stack physical height is, say, 200 m, the plume centerline reaches only 253 m — just barely above the inversion base at 250 m. **Implication:**

- If the stack is *shorter* than 200 m, the plume will be **trapped below** the inversion → severe ground-level pollution.
- If the stack is *taller* than 200 m, the plume penetrates the inversion → reduced ground concentration.
- **Design recommendation:** Increase stack height to ensure plume clears the inversion base, with a safety margin.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Temperature Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit mix-up (–1 mark):** Using °C in gas-law or lapse-rate equations. Always convert to K.
> 2. **Sign confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** The lapse rate is defined with a *negative sign* ($\Gamma = -dT/dz$). Forgetting it flips the stability classification entirely.
> 3. **Confusing Γd with ELR (–2 marks):** Γd ≈ 9.8 K/km is a constant of *dry air*. ELR is what the *radiosonde* measures. Comparing them is the entire basis of stability classification.
> 4. **Forgetting latent heat in WALR (–1 mark):** Saturated parcels cool more slowly because of latent heat release; the SALR is therefore *less* than DALR.
> 5. **In Briggs equation, mixing $F_b < 55$ and $F_b > 55$ formulas (–2 marks):** Use the correct branch based on the numerical value of $F_b$.
> 6. **In plume behavior questions, omitting sketches (–1 to –2 marks):** Always include a labeled diagram of the plume shape — it is a "must-have" for full marks on stability questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Temperature**

- **Standard reference temperature** = 288.15 K (15 °C); standard pressure = 101.325 kPa.
- **Always convert °C to K** before using lapse-rate or gas-law equations.
- **DALR = g/cₚ ≈ 9.8 K/km** is a *constant* — derived from first principles; memorize the formula and the numerical value.
- **SALR ≈ 4–7 K/km** — temperature-dependent; lower than DALR due to latent heat release.
- **Stability rule (master):** Compare ELR (Γ) to Γd (≈ 9.8 K/km):
  - Γ > Γd → **Unstable** (looping)
  - Γ = Γd → **Neutral** (coning)
  - 0 < Γ < Γd → **Stable** (fanning)
  - Γ < 0 → **Inversion** (trapping / fumigation)
- **Six plume shapes** to memorize: **L**ooping, **C**oning, **F**anning, **F**umigation, **T**rapping, **L**ofting. (Mnemonic: **"LCFFT L"**).
- **Inversion types** — Radiation (night), Subsidence (high-pressure), Frontal, Advection; plus ground-based vs. elevated.
- **Fumigation** = inversion aloft + unstable surface layer = **worst-case ground pollution**.
- **Lofting** = inversion below + unstable layer above = **best-case** (plume escapes upward).
- **Briggs plume rise** uses the **buoyancy flux $F_b$** which is directly proportional to $(T_s - T_a)$ — emphasizing the role of temperature difference.
- **Arrhenius implication:** Higher ambient temperature → faster secondary pollutant (e.g., ozone) formation → summer smog peaks.
- **Potential temperature** $\theta = T(P_0/P)^{R/c_p}$ is a more robust stability indicator: $\theta$ increasing with height = stable.
- **Industrial design rule:** Stack height must be chosen so that the **maximum plume rise** clears the **maximum climatological inversion base** with a safety margin.
- **Photochemical smog** episodes correlate strongly with high temperatures, intense sunlight, and stagnant high-pressure systems with subsidence inversions.
- **Always draw a labeled plume diagram** in any stability or plume-behavior question — it is worth 1–2 marks.
- For an inversion to be classified as "strong," $\Delta T / \Delta z$ should be on the order of several K per 100 m or more (e.g., 10 K in 200 m).
- **High-pressure systems** (anticyclones) are statistically the most common cause of prolonged, severe pollution episodes due to subsidence inversions and calm winds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lapse Rates - Humidity - Condensation - Wind Direction and Velocity - Effects of Meteorological Parameters on Transport and Diffusion - Atmospheric Electricity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 2 — METEOROLOGICAL FOUNDATIONS OF AIR POLLUTION

## 1.1 Lapse Rate — Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Lapse Rate** is defined as the rate of change of atmospheric temperature with respect to height (elevation) in the troposphere. Mathematically, $\Gamma = -\dfrac{dT}{dz}$ where $T$ is absolute temperature (K) and $z$ is geometric height (m). The negative sign ensures a **positive lapse rate** when temperature decreases with altitude (the normal tropospheric condition).

It is expressed in units of **°C per 100 m** or **K per km**. The standard reference value adopted worldwide is **Γ_st = 6.5 K/km** (the U.S. Standard Atmosphere mean tropospheric lapse rate).

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of the atmosphere as a stack of invisible "thermal layers" — like a layered cake. A lapse rate is simply *how quickly the cake cools as you walk up the staircase from the ground floor to the terrace*. If the cake cools fast, the lapse rate is high (lapse rate = steep cooling). If the cake stays warm, the lapse rate is low or even negative (inversion — temperature *rises* with height, like an upside-down cake).

### Classification of Lapse Rates (KTU High-Yield)

| Lapse Rate Type | Symbol | Typical Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR)** | $\Gamma_{env}$ | Variable (often 6.5 K/km average) | Actual measured vertical temperature profile |
| **Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR)** | $\Gamma_d$ | **9.8 K/km** (≈ 1 °C / 100 m) | Cooling rate of unsaturated rising parcel |
| **Wet / Saturated Adiabatic Lapse Rate (SALR / WALR)** | $\Gamma_s$ | **≈ 5–6 K/km** (variable) | Cooling rate of saturated rising parcel |
| **Auto-convective Lapse Rate** | — | **> 9.8 K/km** (≈ 34.1 K/km for surface air) | Atmosphere absolutely unstable |

---

## 1.2 Humidity — Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Atmospheric Humidity** is the measure of water vapour present in the air. It is the most variable gaseous component of the atmosphere (0–4 % by volume) and is the master variable that controls condensation, cloud formation, precipitation, and the wet-removal of air pollutants.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Imagine the air as a **sponge** for water. Humidity measures *how soaked* that sponge is. A *dry sponge* (low humidity) can absorb more water; a *soaked sponge* (high humidity) cannot — leading to condensation (droplets forming on a cold glass). The exact point at which the sponge is fully soaked corresponds to the **dew point**.

### Key Humidity Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vapour Pressure** | $e$ | Partial pressure exerted by water vapour | hPa or Pa |
| **Saturation Vapour Pressure** | $e_s$ | Maximum $e$ at given $T$ before condensation | hPa or Pa |
| **Absolute Humidity** | $\rho_v$ | Mass of water vapour per unit volume | g/m³ |
| **Specific Humidity** | $q$ | Mass of water vapour per unit mass of moist air | g/kg (dimensionless) |
| **Mixing Ratio** | $w$ | Mass of water vapour per unit mass of **dry** air | g/kg |
| **Relative Humidity** | $\phi$ | $\phi = \dfrac{e}{e_s} \times 100$ | % |
| **Dew Point Temperature** | $T_d$ | Temperature at which saturation occurs (cooling at constant $e$) | K or °C |

---

## 1.3 Condensation — Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Condensation** is the phase transition of atmospheric water vapour into liquid water (or ice). It occurs when a moist air parcel is cooled to (or below) its dew-point temperature, causing the partial pressure of water vapour to equal the saturation vapour pressure ($\phi = 100\%$). Condensation nuclei (CCN — cloud condensation nuclei, typically 0.1–1 µm aerosols) are essential; without them, supersaturation can persist.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hold a cold Coca-Cola bottle in humid weather — droplets form on its surface. The bottle wall is colder than the dew point of the surrounding air, so vapour *condenses* on it. The atmosphere does the same thing on a giant scale: dust particles, soot, and sea-salt aerosols act as the "bottle wall", and rising/cooling air provides the cold surface — producing clouds, fog, mist, dew, and precipitation.

### Forms of Atmospheric Condensation (KTU High-Yield)

| Form | Scale / Location | Visibility | Air Pollution Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dew** | Surface (night radiative cooling) | — | Wet deposition of soluble gases |
| **Frost** | Surface (sub-zero cooling) | — | — |
| **Fog** | Surface (≤ 1 km altitude) | < 1 km | Traps pollutants; smog episodes |
| **Mist** | Surface | 1–2 km | — |
| **Cloud** | Aloft | Variable | Long-range transport, wet removal |
| **Smog (London-type)** | Urban surface layer | Very low | $SO_2$ + fog droplets → acid mist |

---

## 1.4 Wind Direction and Velocity — Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Wind** is the horizontal motion of air relative to the Earth's surface, driven by the **pressure-gradient force**, modified by the **Coriolis force**, **frictional force** (near surface), and **centripetal acceleration** (curved flow). By meteorological convention, **wind direction is named by the direction FROM which it blows** (e.g., a "north wind" blows from north to south).

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of the atmosphere as a vast ocean of air. **Wind is the current** in that ocean. **Pressure differences** are the *slope* of the sea bed — air flows from high pressure (the "hill" of dense, cool air) to low pressure (the "valley" of warm, rising air), just as water flows downhill. The **Coriolis effect** is like the swirling vortex you see when water drains from a bathtub — it deflects the path.

### Wind Velocity Components (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Wind Speed (u)** | Scalar magnitude of horizontal wind | 0–20 m/s (surface) |
| **Wind Direction (θ)** | Compass bearing (0–360°) from which wind blows | — |
| **Geostrophic Wind ($V_g$)** | Balance of pressure-gradient and Coriolis (frictionless, straight flow) | 5–50 m/s |
| **Gradient Wind ($V_{gr}$)** | Balance including centripetal force (curved isobars) | — |
| **Surface Wind** | Wind measured at 10 m height (WMO standard) | — |
| **Anemometer Height Wind** | Wind at any height $z$ | — |

---

## 1.5 Effects of Meteorological Parameters on Transport and Diffusion — Overview

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Atmospheric transport and diffusion** describe how pollutants released from a stack are carried downwind (advective transport) and spread laterally and vertically (turbulent diffusion). The governing parameters are: (i) **wind speed & direction** (advection), (ii) **atmospheric stability** (lapse rate profile), (iii) **turbulence intensity** (mechanical + thermal), and (iv) **mixing layer height** ($H$). These are encapsulated in the Pasquill–Gifford stability classification and the Gaussian Plume Model.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pour a drop of ink into a still glass of water — it spreads slowly by molecular diffusion. Now pour ink into a fast-flowing river — it is whisked downstream (advection) and spreads widely (turbulent diffusion). Pollutants behave identically: the "river" is the wind, and the "turbulence" is the atmospheric stability. On a sunny afternoon, strong turbulence mixes the plume vertically (good dispersion). On a clear, calm night, a stable layer caps the plume — trapping pollution near the ground (the *Inversion Smog* scenario).

---

## 1.6 Atmospheric Electricity — Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Atmospheric Electricity** is the study of electrical phenomena in the atmosphere, dominated by the **global atmospheric electric circuit**: a continuous, fair-weather vertical current of ≈ **1–3 pA/m²** flowing from the positively-charged ionosphere (~ 250 kV potential) to the negatively-charged Earth's surface, maintained globally by ~ 2000 thunderstorms acting as DC generators. The classic fair-weather parameters are: **Potential Gradient (PG)**, **Air-Earth Current Density ($J$)**, **Ionic Conductivity ($\lambda$)**, and **Space Charge**.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Picture the Earth as a giant negatively-charged sphere, with the ionosphere above acting as a positively-charged shell — together they form a *spherical capacitor*. This capacitor is continuously "leaking" current through the atmosphere (a fair-weather trickle of ions). Thunderstorms are the "battery chargers" that re-supply the leaking current. Aerosol pollution disturbs this by capturing ions, increasing PG, and reducing conductivity — a phenomenon KTU examiners love to link with industrial pollution.

### Key Atmospheric Electrical Quantities (KTU High-Yield)

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Fair Weather) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Potential Gradient (Electric Field)** | $E$ or PG | **100–150 V/m** at surface (1 m elevation) | V/m |
| **Air-Earth Current Density** | $J$ | **2–4 pA/m²** | A/m² |
| **Ionic Conductivity** | $\lambda$ | **1–5 fS/m** | S/m |
| **Total Air-Earth Resistance** | $R_{AE}$ | ≈ **1.3 × 10¹⁷ Ω·m²** | Ω·m² |
| **Ionospheric Potential** | $V_i$ | **≈ 250 kV** | V |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Lapse Rates — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR) — Derivation Logic
A dry (unsaturated) air parcel rising expands and cools adiabatically. The first law of thermodynamics for an adiabatic process gives:
$$dQ = 0 = c_p \, dT - \dfrac{RT}{p}\,dp$$

Applying the hydrostatic equation $dp = -\rho g\,dz$ and the ideal gas law $\rho = p/(RT)$:

$$\dfrac{dT}{dz} = -\dfrac{g}{c_p} = \Gamma_d$$

> Substituting **g = 9.81 m/s²** and **c_p = 1005 J/(kg·K)**:
> $$\Gamma_d = \dfrac{9.81}{1005} = 9.76 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K/m} \approx 9.8\ \text{K/km}$$

### 2.1.2 Wet (Saturated) Adiabatic Lapse Rate (SALR) — Derivation Logic
For a saturated rising parcel, latent heat is released upon condensation:
$$\Gamma_s = \Gamma_d \cdot \dfrac{1 + \dfrac{L_v \, w_s}{R T}}{1 + \dfrac{L_v^2 \, w_s \, \varepsilon}{c_p \, R \, T^2}}$$

where $L_v = 2.5 \times 10^6$ J/kg (latent heat of vaporization), $w_s$ = saturation mixing ratio, $\varepsilon = 0.622$ (ratio of gas constants). $\Gamma_s$ ranges **5–9 K/km**, typically ≈ **6 K/km**.

### 2.1.3 Atmospheric Stability Classes (Pasquill–Gifford–Turner)

| Stability Class | $\Gamma_{env}$ vs $\Gamma_d$ | Daytime Insolation / Night-time Cloud Cover |
|---|---|---|
| **A — Very Unstable** | $\Gamma_{env} \gg \Gamma_d$ (super-adiabatic) | Strong sunshine, low wind |
| **B — Unstable** | $\Gamma_{env} > \Gamma_d$ | Moderate sunshine |
| **C — Slightly Unstable** | $\Gamma_{env}$ slightly > $\Gamma_d$ | Slight sunshine / overcast |
| **D — Neutral** | $\Gamma_{env} \approx \Gamma_d$ | Overcast / high wind |
| **E — Slightly Stable** | $\Gamma_{env} < \Gamma_d$ | Slight overcast, low wind |
| **F — Stable (Inversion)** | $\Gamma_{env} \le 0$ (inversion) | Clear night, low wind |

### 2.1.4 Temperature Inversions — The Pollution Trap
An **inversion** is a layer in which temperature *increases* with height ($\Gamma_{env} \le 0$). It acts as a "lid" suppressing vertical mixing. Types:

1. **Radiation Inversion** (most common) — surface radiative cooling on clear, calm nights
2. **Subsidence Inversion** — descending air in high-pressure systems warms adiabatically
3. **Frontal Inversion** — warm air overriding cold air mass
4. **Advection Inversion** — warm air advected over cold surface

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** Classic air-pollution disasters — Bhopal (1984), London Smog (1952), Donora (1948), Delhi winter smog — all coincided with **temperature inversions + low wind + high emissions**.

### 2.1.5 Mixing Layer Height ($H$)
The depth of the well-mixed boundary layer is critical:
- **Daytime convective BL:** $H \approx 1–2$ km
- **Nighttime stable BL:** $H \approx 100–300$ m
- Holzworth formula: $H = 121 \cdot (6 - P_1) \cdot \dfrac{T_{1} - T_2}{T_1} + x$, where $P_1$ is the 6-h forecast afternoon temperature, $T_1, T_2$ are noon and sunset temperatures.

---

## 2.2 Humidity — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Saturation Vapour Pressure (Clausius–Clapeyron)
$$e_s(T) = 6.112 \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.67\, T}{T + 243.5}\right)\ \text{hPa} \quad (T \text{ in } °\text{C})$$

### 2.2.2 Mixing Ratio and Specific Humidity
$$w = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{e}{p - e}, \quad w_s = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{e_s}{p - e_s}$$

$$q = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{e}{p - 0.378 e}$$

### 2.2.3 Relative Humidity → Dew Point
$$T_d = \dfrac{243.5 \cdot \left[\ln(e/6.112)\right]}{17.67 - \ln(e/6.112)}\ °\text{C}$$

### 2.2.4 Psychrometric Equation (Wet-Dry Bulb)
$$e = e_s(T_w) - \gamma \cdot p \cdot (T_d - T_w)$$

where $\gamma$ = psychrometric constant $\approx$ **0.000665 °C⁻¹** (at standard conditions), $T_d$ = dry-bulb, $T_w$ = wet-bulb.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Humidity

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $\phi = e / e_s \times 100$ | Relative humidity |
| $q = 0.622 e / (p - 0.378 e)$ | Specific humidity |
| $w = 0.622 e / (p - e)$ | Mixing ratio |
| $e_s = 6.112 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.67 T}{T+243.5}\right)$ | Saturation vapour pressure (hPa) |
| $T_d = \dfrac{243.5\, \alpha}{17.67 - \alpha}$ where $\alpha = \ln(e/6.112)$ | Dew-point temperature |
| $\gamma = c_p \cdot p / (0.622 \cdot L_v)$ | Psychrometric constant |

---

## 2.3 Condensation & Precipitation — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Lifting Condensation Level (LCL)
The height at which a rising parcel becomes saturated:
$$z_{LCL} = 123 \cdot (T_0 - T_{d,0})\ \text{m}, \quad (T_0, T_{d,0} \text{ in } °\text{C at surface})$$

### 2.3.2 Wet Removal of Pollutants
- **Rainout (in-cloud scavenging):** Pollutant incorporated into cloud droplets as CCN.
- **Washout (below-cloud scavenging):** Falling raindrops absorb gaseous / particulate pollutants.
$$\Lambda = \int_0^\infty \pi r^2 \, E(r, R) \, v_t(r) \, N(r)\, dr$$
where $\Lambda$ = scavenging coefficient, $E$ = collection efficiency, $v_t$ = terminal velocity of drops, $N(r)$ = raindrop size distribution.

Typical values: $\Lambda \approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ s⁻¹ for particulates; lower for gases.

### 2.3.3 Acid Rain Formation
$$SO_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{catalyst, H}_2\text{O}} SO_3 \xrightarrow{H_2O} H_2SO_4$$
$$2\,NO_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O \rightarrow 2\,HNO_3$$

---

## 2.4 Wind Direction and Velocity — Theoretical Framework

### 2.4.1 Geostrophic Wind (Straight Isobars, No Friction)
$$V_g = \dfrac{1}{\rho f} \cdot \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n}$$
where $f = 2\Omega \sin\phi$ = Coriolis parameter, $\Omega$ = 7.292 × 10⁻⁵ rad/s, $\phi$ = latitude.

### 2.4.2 Gradient Wind (Curved Isobars)
$$V_{gr} = \dfrac{r}{\rho f}\left[-\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial r} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial r}\right)^2 + \dfrac{4 \rho f^2 V_g^2}{r}}\right]$$

### 2.4.3 Power-Law Wind Profile (Surface Layer)
$$u(z) = u_{ref}\left(\dfrac{z}{z_{ref}}\right)^p$$
where exponent $p$ depends on stability:
- A: 0.07 | B: 0.07 | C: 0.10 | **D: 0.15** | E: 0.35 | F: 0.55

### 2.4.4 Logarithmic Wind Profile (Neutral Conditions)
$$u(z) = \dfrac{u_*}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{z}{z_0}\right)$$
where $u_*$ = friction velocity, $k$ = von Kármán constant **≈ 0.41**, $z_0$ = roughness length (≈ 0.1 m for rural, 1 m for urban).

### 2.4.5 Wind Rose
A polar histogram showing the **frequency** of wind direction AND the **wind speed distribution** per sector. Used in KTU problems for predicting:
- Downwind impact of stacks
- Airport / industrial siting
- Dust dispersion in mining

---

## 2.5 Transport and Diffusion — Theoretical Framework

### 2.5.1 Gaussian Plume Model (Steady-State, Elevated Source)
The cornerstone of KTU Module 2. Concentration at point $(x, y, z)$ downwind of an elevated source:
$$C(x, y, z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u\, \sigma_y\, \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\left[\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

where:
- $Q$ = emission rate (g/s or mg/s)
- $u$ = mean wind speed at stack height
- $H$ = effective stack height (physical + plume rise)
- $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ = horizontal and vertical dispersion coefficients (functions of $x$ and stability)

### 2.5.2 Maximum Ground-Level Concentration (GLMC)
Setting $z = 0$ and optimizing w.r.t. $x$:
$$C_{max} = \dfrac{2 Q}{e \pi u H^2}\left(\dfrac{\sigma_z}{\sigma_y}\right) \quad \text{at} \quad x_{max} = \dfrac{H}{\sqrt{2}}$$
(for neutral stability, with $\sigma_z = 0.08 x^{0.75}$, $\sigma_y = 0.16 x^{0.75}$).

### 2.5.3 Plume Rise (Briggs Formulas) — KTU Favourite
- **Buoyancy-dominated (stable / neutral):**
  $$\Delta h = 2.6 \left(\dfrac{F_b}{u \, s}\right)^{1/3}$$
- **Buoyancy-dominated (unstable / convective):**
  $$\Delta h = 1.6 \, F_b^{1/3} \, x^{2/3} / u$$
- **Momentum-dominated:** $\Delta h = 1.5 \dfrac{(F_m)^{1/3}}{u}$ with $F_m = v_s^2 r_s^2 T_a / T_s$

where buoyancy flux $F_b = g v_s r_s^2 (T_s - T_a)/T_s$.

### 2.5.4 Pasquill–Gifford Dispersion Coefficients (Briggs Formulas)
For **rural** conditions:
$$\sigma_y(x) = \dfrac{0.08 x}{\sqrt{1 + 0.0001 x}} \cdot f_y$$
$$\sigma_z(x) = \dfrac{0.06 x}{\sqrt{1 + 0.0015 x}} \cdot f_z$$
where $f_y, f_z$ are stability-dependent correction factors (A: 0.22, 0.20; B: 0.16, 0.12; C: 0.11, 0.08; D: 0.08, 0.06; E: 0.06, 0.03; F: 0.04, 0.016).

### KTU Formula Sheet — Transport and Diffusion

| Formula | Application |
|---|---|
| $C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2})[\exp(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}) + \exp(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2})]$ | Gaussian Plume (elevated source, reflection) |
| $C_{max} = \dfrac{2Q}{e \pi u H^2}\left(\dfrac{\sigma_z}{\sigma_y}\right)$ | Max ground-level concentration |
| $H_{eff} = h_s + \Delta h$ | Effective stack height |
| $\Delta h = 2.6 (F_b/(u s))^{1/3}$ | Briggs plume rise (stable) |
| $\sigma_y = 0.08 x (1+0.0001 x)^{-1/2}$ | Lateral dispersion (rural D) |
| $\sigma_z = 0.06 x (1+0.0015 x)^{-1/2}$ | Vertical dispersion (rural D) |

---

## 2.6 Atmospheric Electricity — Theoretical Framework

### 2.6.1 Ohm's Law for the Atmosphere
$$J = \lambda \cdot E$$
where $J$ = current density (A/m²), $\lambda$ = conductivity (S/m), $E$ = electric field / PG (V/m).

### 2.6.2 Air-Earth Current Density
The vertical conduction current in fair weather:
$$J \approx 2\text{–}4\ \text{pA/m}^2$$
Globally, integrating over the Earth's surface gives $I_{AE} \approx 1350$ A. This is replenished by ~ 2000 thunderstorms acting as generators.

### 2.6.3 Global Resistance
$$R_{AE} = \int_0^h \dfrac{dz}{\lambda(z)} \approx 1.3 \times 10^{17}\ \Omega \cdot m^2$$
with $V_i = I_{AE} \cdot R_{AE} \approx 2.5 \times 10^5$ V.

### 2.6.4 Effects of Pollution on Atmospheric Electricity
- **Aerosols attach ions** → $\lambda \downarrow$ → $E \uparrow$ (PG rises)
- **Fog and high humidity** → ions grow, mobility falls → $\lambda \downarrow$
- **Industrial plumes** measurably enhance PG by 30–100 % over background
- **Smoke from major fires** (e.g., forest fires) alters the global circuit — a 2020s research hotspot

### 2.6.5 Air Ion Mobility
$$\mu = \dfrac{v_d}{E} \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2/(\text{V·s})\ \text{(small ions)}$$

### KTU Formula Sheet — Atmospheric Electricity

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $J = \lambda \cdot E$ | Ohm's law for air |
| $V_i = I_{AE} \cdot R_{AE} \approx 250$ kV | Ionosphere potential |
| $I_{AE} \approx 1350$ A | Global air-earth current |
| $\lambda = n_+ e \mu_+ + n_- e \mu_-$ | Conductivity (bipolar ions) |
| $E = V_i / H$ (approx.) | Potential gradient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these topics matter to an air-pollution engineer:**
> 1. **Stability** determines whether stack emissions stay aloft or get trapped near the ground (Bhopal-type disasters).
> 2. **Wind speed** is inversely proportional to ground-level concentration ($C \propto 1/u$) for a given emission rate.
> 3. **Humidity & condensation** drive wet deposition (rain-out, wash-out) — the *natural cleansing* of the atmosphere.
> 4. **Atmospheric electricity** is a sensitive indicator of aerosol loading and is being researched for pollution monitoring using field mills and balloon-borne electrometers.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR)

**Starting Equations (1st Law + Hydrostatic + Ideal Gas):**

$$dQ = c_p\,dT - \dfrac{p}{\rho}\,d\rho = 0 \quad (\text{adiabatic, dry})$$

$$\dfrac{dp}{dz} = -\rho g \quad (\text{hydrostatic})$$

$$p = \rho R T \quad (\text{ideal gas})$$

**Step 1** — Differentiate the 1st law:
$$0 = c_p\,dT - \dfrac{p}{\rho}\,d\rho \Rightarrow c_p\,dT = \dfrac{p}{\rho}\,d\rho$$

**Step 2** — Differentiate the ideal gas law logarithmically:
$$\dfrac{dp}{p} = \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} + \dfrac{dT}{T} \Rightarrow \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = \dfrac{dp}{p} - \dfrac{dT}{T}$$

**Step 3** — Substitute Step 2 into Step 1:
$$c_p\,dT = p \left(\dfrac{dp}{p} - \dfrac{dT}{T}\right) = dp - \dfrac{p}{T}\,dT$$

**Step 4** — Rearrange:
$$c_p\,dT + \dfrac{p}{T}\,dT = dp \Rightarrow dT\left(c_p + \dfrac{p}{T}\right) = dp$$

Using hydrostatic $dp = -\rho g\,dz$ and $\rho = p/(RT)$:
$$dp = -\dfrac{pg}{RT}\,dz$$

**Step 5** — Substitute and solve for $dT/dz$:
$$dT\left(c_p + \dfrac{p}{T}\right) = -\dfrac{pg}{RT}\,dz$$
$$dT \cdot \dfrac{c_p T + p}{T} = -\dfrac{pg}{RT}\,dz$$
$$dT \cdot \dfrac{R(c_p T/R + p/R)}{T} = -\dfrac{pg}{RT}\,dz$$

Since $c_p - c_v = R$ and for air $c_p T \gg p$:
$$\dfrac{dT}{dz} \approx -\dfrac{g}{c_p}$$

**Step 6** — Numerical evaluation:
$$\Gamma_d = -\dfrac{dT}{dz} = \dfrac{g}{c_p} = \dfrac{9.81}{1005} = 9.76 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K/m}$$
$$\boxed{\Gamma_d \approx 9.8\ \text{K/km} \approx 1\ °\text{C}/100\ \text{m}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Pasquill Stability from Lapse Rate Data

**Problem:** A balloon sounding at 5:00 AM yields the following temperatures:

| Height (m) | Temperature (°C) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 12 |
| 100 | 14 |
| 200 | 15 |
| 300 | 14 |
| 500 | 11 |

**Find:** Stability classification, mixing height, and expected plume behaviour.

**Solution Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Compute ELR for each layer:**
- 0–100 m: $\Gamma_{env} = (14-12)/0.1 = +20$ K/km (super-adiabatic, unstable)
- 100–200 m: $\Gamma_{env} = (15-14)/0.1 = +10$ K/km (≈ DALR, slightly unstable)
- 200–300 m: $\Gamma_{env} = (14-15)/0.1 = -10$ K/km (INVERSION — temperature rises with height)
- 300–500 m: $\Gamma_{env} = (11-14)/0.2 = -15$ K/km (strong inversion)

**Step 2 — Identify inversion base and top:**
Inversion base: 100 m, top: 300 m. This is a **radiation inversion** (early morning).

**Step 3 — Mixing height:**
With inversion base at 100 m, the **mixing layer height H = 100 m** (pollutants are trapped in this thin layer).

**Step 4 — Stability class:**
Surface layer (0–100 m) is unstable, but the layer above is highly stable. For plume dispersion, the **capping inversion dominates** → **Stability Class E or F (Stable)**.

**Step 5 — Plume behaviour:**
Smoke from a stack will rise to the inversion base, then spread laterally in a "fanning" pattern, NOT reaching the ground if $H_{eff}$ < inversion base. If $H_{eff}$ > 100 m, pollutants will be trapped and fumigation may occur at sunrise when the inversion lifts.

> **Conclusion:** Nocturnal radiation inversion; Pasquill Class F; mixing height ≈ 100 m; plume will undergo **fanning** (no vertical mixing above inversion).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Gaussian Plume Ground-Level Concentration

**Problem:** A power plant stack releases $SO_2$ at $Q = 800$ g/s, $H_{eff} = 100$ m, $u = 5$ m/s. Stability class D. Find ground-level concentration at $x = 500$ m downwind on the centreline ($y = 0$).

**Solution Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Compute dispersion coefficients (rural, Class D):**
$$\sigma_y(500) = \dfrac{0.08 \times 500}{\sqrt{1+0.0001 \times 500}} = \dfrac{40}{\sqrt{1.05}} = \dfrac{40}{1.0247} = 39.04\ \text{m}$$
$$\sigma_z(500) = \dfrac{0.06 \times 500}{\sqrt{1+0.0015 \times 500}} = \dfrac{30}{\sqrt{1.75}} = \dfrac{30}{1.3229} = 22.68\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Gaussian Plume (centreline, $y = 0$, $z = 0$):**
$$C(x,0,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

$$C = \dfrac{800}{\pi \times 5 \times 39.04 \times 22.68}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{100^2}{2 \times 22.68^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute pre-exponential:**
$$\pi \times 5 \times 39.04 \times 22.68 = 3.1416 \times 5 \times 39.04 \times 22.68$$
$$= 15.708 \times 39.04 \times 22.68 = 15.708 \times 885.4 = 13,907$$
$$\text{Pre-exp} = \dfrac{800}{13,907} = 0.05753\ \text{g/m}^3 = 57.53\ \text{mg/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute exponent:**
$$-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} = -\dfrac{10,000}{2 \times 514.4} = -\dfrac{10,000}{1028.8} = -9.72$$
$$\exp(-9.72) = 6.03 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**
$$C = 57.53 \times 6.03 \times 10^{-5} = 3.47 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mg/m}^3 = 3.47\ \mu\text{g/m}^3$$

> **Result:** $C(500,0,0) \approx 3.5\ \mu\text{g/m}^3$ of $SO_2$ at 500 m downwind. This is well below the NAAQS 24-hour standard of 365 µg/m³, indicating acceptable local air quality at that distance.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Plume Behaviour vs. Stability Class

| Stability Class | $\Gamma_{env}$ vs $\Gamma_d$ | Plume Shape | Plume Name | Vertical Spread | Dilution |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A (Very Unstable)** | $\Gamma_{env} \gg \Gamma_d$ | Looping | Looping Plume | Very high (both up & down) | Maximum |
| **B (Unstable)** | $\Gamma_{env} > \Gamma_d$ | Coning with loops | Coning (with loops) | High | High |
| **C (Slightly Unstable)** | $\Gamma_{env}$ slightly > $\Gamma_d$ | Coning | Coning Plume | Moderate | Moderate |
| **D (Neutral)** | $\Gamma_{env} \approx \Gamma_d$ | Symmetric cone | Coning Plume | Moderate | Moderate |
| **E (Slightly Stable)** | $\Gamma_{env} < \Gamma_d$ | Fanning | Fanning Plume | Limited | Low |
| **F (Stable / Inversion)** | $\Gamma_{env} \le 0$ | Flat horizontal spread | Fanning Plume | Minimal | Very low |
| **Special: Inversion Below Stack + Convection Above** | Mixed | Lofting + fumigation | **Fumigation** | Reaches ground | Worst case (high concentration) |
| **Special: Inversion Above + Convection Below** | Mixed | Trapped below lid | **Lofting** | Capped | Best case (no ground impact) |

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Geostrophic Wind

**Starting from horizontal momentum equations (steady, frictionless, no acceleration):**
$$\dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} = f v \quad \text{(x-momentum)}$$
$$-\dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial y} = f u \quad \text{(y-momentum)}$$

**Step 1** — Cross-multiply and add in quadrature:
$$V_g^2 = u^2 + v^2 = \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho f}\right)^2 \left[\left(\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 2** — Define pressure gradient normal to isobars:
$$\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right)^2}$$

**Step 3** — Substituting:
$$\boxed{V_g = \dfrac{1}{\rho f}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n} = \dfrac{1}{2\rho\Omega\sin\phi}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n}}$$

where $f = 2\Omega\sin\phi$, $\Omega$ = 7.292 × 10⁻⁵ rad/s, $\phi$ = latitude.

**Step 4 — Sample numerical value:**
At $\phi = 30°$, $p$ gradient = 1 hPa / 100 km, $\rho$ = 1.2 kg/m³:
$$V_g = \dfrac{1}{1.2 \times 2 \times 7.292 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.5} \times \dfrac{100}{100,000}$$
$$= \dfrac{1}{8.75 \times 10^{-5}} \times 10^{-3} = 11.4\ \text{m/s}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Air-Earth Current and PG

**Problem:** During fair weather, atmospheric conductivity $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-14}$ S/m, $J = 3$ pA/m². Find the potential gradient and discuss implications for pollution.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Ohm's law for air:**
$$E = \dfrac{J}{\lambda} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-14}} = 150\ \text{V/m}$$

**Step 2 — Interpret:**
- 150 V/m is a typical fair-weather PG, confirming data.
- This is a strong electric field at Earth's surface — sufficient to drive small ion transport.
- If pollution doubles aerosol load, $\lambda$ drops by ~ 50 % → $E$ doubles to 300 V/m.
- This is why atmospheric electricity measurements are **sensitive indicators of aerosol pollution** — a key link between Module 2 and Module 5 (pollution monitoring).

> **Result:** PG = 150 V/m. Doubling aerosols → PG → 300 V/m, doubling the field strength.

---

## 3.7 Psychrometric Calculation — Determining Humidity

**Problem:** Dry-bulb $T_d = 30$ °C, wet-bulb $T_w = 22$ °C, pressure $p = 1000$ hPa. Find relative humidity and dew point.

**Solution Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Saturation vapour pressure at wet-bulb (22 °C):**
$$e_s(22) = 6.112\exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.67 \times 22}{22+243.5}\right) = 6.112\exp(1.463) = 6.112 \times 4.32 = 26.40\ \text{hPa}$$

**Step 2 — Psychrometric constant:**
$$\gamma = 0.000665\ ·^{-1}\text{C} \cdot p = 0.000665 \times 1000 = 0.665\ \text{hPa/°C}$$

**Step 3 — Vapour pressure:**
$$e = e_s(T_w) - \gamma (T_d - T_w) = 26.40 - 0.665 \times (30-22)$$
$$= 26.40 - 5.32 = 21.08\ \text{hPa}$$

**Step 4 — Saturation vapour pressure at dry-bulb (30 °C):**
$$e_s(30) = 6.112 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.67 \times 30}{30+243.5}\right) = 6.112\exp(1.935) = 6.112 \times 6.92 = 42.32\ \text{hPa}$$

**Step 5 — Relative humidity:**
$$\phi = \dfrac{e}{e_s(30)} \times 100 = \dfrac{21.08}{42.32} \times 100 = 49.8\%$$

**Step 6 — Dew point** (using $e = 21.08$ hPa):
$$\alpha = \ln(21.08/6.112) = \ln(3.449) = 1.238$$
$$T_d = \dfrac{243.5 \times 1.238}{17.67 - 1.238} = \dfrac{301.5}{16.43} = 18.35\ \text{°C}$$

> **Result:** $\phi \approx 50\%$, dew point ≈ 18.4 °C. (Indicates moderately dry air; condensation would begin if air cooled below 18.4 °C.)
