# MODULE 3 — METHODS OF MEASUREMENT OF VISIBILITY

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** *Visibility* in the context of air pollution engineering is defined as the maximum distance at which an object of specified characteristics (typically a dark object against the horizon sky) can be seen and identified by an observer under prevailing atmospheric conditions. The standard reference object defined by the **WMO (World Meteorological Organization)** and adopted by the **U.S. EPA** is a dark, ideally black object of subtended angular size greater than **0.5°**, viewed against the horizon sky.

The quantitative metric used universally is the **Visual Range (V)**, sometimes denoted as the **Meteorological Range (MR)**. The standard value adopted internationally for "daytime visibility impairment" is the distance at which the **contrast ratio** of a black object against the sky background drops to **0.02 (i.e., 2%)** — this threshold corresponds to what the average human eye can just barely discern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Visual Range ≠ Sight Distance. Visual Range is a physical/optical property of the atmosphere, whereas Sight Distance (used in transportation engineering) is a geometric property of road alignment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Stadium Fog":** Imagine sitting in the top tier of a packed football stadium on a foggy evening. The players on the far side of the field look "washed out" or completely invisible, while the players in the foreground are crystal clear. The "fog" here is essentially a suspension of tiny water droplets and pollutant particles that **scatter and absorb** the light traveling from the players to your eyes. As the optical path (distance) increases, more photons are lost to scattering/absorption, reducing the contrast between the dark jersey (object) and the bright floodlights (background sky). The distance at which the dark jersey *just blends* with the floodlit sky is the **Visual Range**. Heavy PM₂.₅, SO₂ haze, or photochemical smog literally "shrinks" the stadium by increasing this loss of contrast.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Contrast Threshold (ε):** **0.02** (dimensionless, also expressed as 2%)
- **Koschmieder Constant (K):** **3.912** (dimensionless, derived as -ln(0.02))
- **Wavelength of Reference Light (λ):** **550 nm** (green-yellow, peak photopic response of human eye)
- **Standard Observer Threshold:** Photopic (day) vision
- **Key Metric:** Extinction Coefficient (**b_ext**) in units of **Mm⁻¹** or **km⁻¹** or **m⁻¹**

### 1.4 Why Visibility Matters in Air Pollution

- It is the **most direct, citizen-observable indicator** of air quality
- It is governed primarily by **fine particulate matter (PM₂.₅)** and **NO₂** (which absorbs light in the visible spectrum)
- Aesthetics of national parks, urban skylines, and aviation safety
- Regulatory trigger in many U.S. states (e.g., **Class I Visibility Protection Areas** under the 1977 Clean Air Act Amendments)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Physics of Atmospheric Extinction

When light travels from an object to an observer, it undergoes three competing processes:

1. **Absorption (b_abs):** Photons are captured by gas molecules (NO₂, O₃) or particles (soot/black carbon) and converted to other energy forms (heat).
2. **Scattering (b_sca):** Photons are redirected out of the line-of-sight by molecules (**Rayleigh scattering**) or particles (**Mie scattering**).
3. **Total Extinction (b_ext):** Sum of both.

$$
b_{ext} = b_{abs} + b_{sca}
$$

### 2.2 The Contrast Reduction Law — Koschmieder's Equation

The fundamental relationship between visual range and the atmospheric extinction coefficient was derived by **Horst Koschmieder (1924)**. For a black object viewed against a uniform horizon sky at distance **x**, the apparent contrast **C** follows:

$$
C = C_0 \cdot e^{-b_{ext} \cdot x}
$$

where:
- $C$ = apparent contrast at distance $x$ (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- $C_0$ = intrinsic contrast of object against background (≈ 1.0 for an ideal black object against white sky)
- $b_{ext}$ = atmospheric extinction coefficient (length⁻¹)
- $x$ = distance between object and observer (length)

**Definition of Visual Range (V):** The distance at which the apparent contrast equals the threshold contrast $\epsilon = 0.02$:

$$
\epsilon = C_0 \cdot e^{-b_{ext} \cdot V}
$$

Solving for $V$ with $C_0 = 1$ and $\epsilon = 0.02$:

$$
V = \frac{-\ln(\epsilon)}{b_{ext}} = \frac{3.912}{b_{ext}}
$$

This is the **iconic Koschmieder equation** used in virtually every air quality regulation involving visibility.

### 2.3 Beer's Law (Lambert-Beer-Bouguer Law)

The basis for **transmissometer** measurements. The transmittance $T$ over a path length $L$ is:

$$
T = \frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-b_{ext} \cdot L}
$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident light intensity
- $I$ = transmitted light intensity after path $L$

Rearranging:

$$
b_{ext} = -\frac{1}{L} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)
$$

### 2.4 Rayleigh vs. Mie Scattering Regimes

| Parameter | Rayleigh Scattering | Mie Scattering |
|---|---|---|
| Particle size ($d_p$) | $d_p \ll \lambda$ (< 0.1 µm) | $d_p \approx \lambda$ (0.1–10 µm) |
| Cause | Air molecules (N₂, O₂) | Suspended particulate matter (PM) |
| Wavelength dependence | $\propto \lambda^{-4}$ (strong) | Weak / $\lambda^{-1}$ to $\lambda^{0}$ |
| Angular distribution | Symmetric (forward = backward) | Strongly forward-peaked |
| Color effect | Blue sky / red sunsets | White haze |
| Dominant in | Clean atmosphere | Polluted atmosphere |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mie Scattering Mastery Point:** For PM₂.₅ particles (typical in urban haze), the particle diameter is comparable to the wavelength of visible light. Hence Mie scattering dominates, and the haze appears **white/grayish** rather than blue.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \frac{3.912}{b_{ext}}$ | Koschmieder Equation | $V$ in km, $b_{ext}$ in km⁻¹ | Visual range from extinction |
| 2 | $b_{ext} = b_{abs} + b_{sca}$ | Total extinction | m⁻¹ or km⁻¹ | Conservation of optical losses |
| 3 | $T = e^{-b_{ext} \cdot L}$ | Beer-Lambert transmittance | dimensionless | Transmissometer |
| 4 | $b_{ext} = -\frac{\ln(T)}{L}$ | Extinction from transmittance | m⁻¹ | Transmissometer inversion |
| 5 | $C = C_0 \cdot e^{-b_{ext} \cdot x}$ | Contrast attenuation law | dimensionless | Object visibility theory |
| 6 | $b_{sca,Rayleigh} \propto \lambda^{-4}$ | Molecular scattering | m⁻¹ | Clean sky analysis |
| 7 | $\sigma_s = \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \cdot Q_{sca}$ | Single-particle scattering | m²/particle | Mie theory |
| 8 | $b_{sca} = N \cdot \sigma_s$ | Bulk scattering coefficient | m⁻¹ | From particle count $N$ |
| 9 | $V_{day} = \frac{3.912}{b_{ext}}$ | Daytime visual range | km | Standard regulation metric |
| 10 | $V_{night} = \frac{2.996}{b_{ext}}$ | Nighttime visual range | km | For illuminated point sources |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to memorize for KTU exams:**
> - $3.912 = -\ln(0.02)$ — Koschmieder constant
> - $2.996 = -\ln(0.05)$ — used for **nighttime** visual range (threshold = 0.05 since eyes use scotopic vision)
> - Reference wavelength: **550 nm**

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation:** Runway Visual Range (RVR) is computed using transmissometers installed alongside runways. Below 600 m RVR, instrument landing becomes mandatory.
- **Environmental Regulation:** U.S. EPA's **Regional Haze Rule** requires tracking of deciview (dv) — a logarithmic transform of extinction — for Class I federal areas (national parks).
- **Climate Studies:** Visibility is a proxy for **Direct Aerosol Radiative Forcing** in climate models.
- **Industrial Hygiene:** Workplace visibility near stacks is used to gauge particulate emissions during opacity tests (USEPA Method 9).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Koschmieder Visual Range Equation

### Given:
- A black object with intrinsic contrast $C_0$ against a uniform horizon sky
- An observer at distance $x$ from the object
- Atmospheric extinction coefficient $b_{ext}$ (assumed uniform along the path)

### Assumption:
The sky luminance $B_{sky}$ and object luminance $B_{obj}$ are related by the object's intrinsic contrast:

$$
C_0 = \frac{B_{sky} - B_{obj}}{B_{sky}}
$$

For a perfectly black object, $B_{obj} = 0$, so $C_0 = 1$.

### Step 1: Apparent Contrast at Distance x
As light from the object travels through the atmosphere, it is attenuated, and scattered skylight is added to the line of sight. The apparent (perceived) contrast becomes:

$$
C(x) = C_0 \cdot e^{-b_{ext} \cdot x}
$$

> **Logic:** Each differential slice of atmosphere of thickness $dx$ reduces the beam intensity by the factor $e^{-b_{ext} \cdot dx}$. Integrating over distance $x$ yields the exponential decay.

### Step 2: Apply the Threshold Condition
By definition, Visual Range $V$ is the distance at which the apparent contrast equals the **minimum perceptible contrast** $\epsilon$:

$$
\epsilon = C_0 \cdot e^{-b_{ext} \cdot V}
$$

### Step 3: Solve for V
Divide both sides by $C_0$ and take the natural logarithm:

$$
\frac{\epsilon}{C_0} = e^{-b_{ext} \cdot V}
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{\epsilon}{C_0}\right) = -b_{ext} \cdot V
$$

$$
V = \frac{\ln(C_0) - \ln(\epsilon)}{b_{ext}} = \frac{\ln(C_0/\epsilon)}{b_{ext}}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute Standard Daytime Values
For a black object ($C_0 = 1$) and the standard threshold ($\epsilon = 0.02$):

$$
V = \frac{\ln(1/0.02)}{b_{ext}} = \frac{\ln(50)}{b_{ext}}
$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the Constant

$$
\ln(50) = \ln(5 \times 10) = \ln 5 + \ln 10 = 1.6094 + 2.3026 = 3.9120
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{V = \frac{3.912}{b_{ext}}}
$$

This is the **foundational equation** of visibility science. ∎

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** The atmospheric extinction coefficient at a polluted urban site is measured as $b_{ext} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{-1}$. Calculate the daytime visual range.

### Step 1: Identify the formula

$$
V = \frac{3.912}{b_{ext}}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute and compute

$$
V = \frac{3.912}{4.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{-1}}
$$

$$
V = \frac{3.912}{0.00045} \text{ m} = 8693.3 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to kilometers

$$
V = 8.69 \text{ km}
$$

**Interpretation:** Visual range of about 8.7 km — moderately impaired (clean air visual range can exceed 100 km).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Transmissometer Inversion

**Problem:** A long-path transmissometer uses a baseline path length of $L = 1.5$ km. The measured intensity ratio is $I/I_0 = 0.35$. Compute the visual range.

### Step 1: Compute $b_{ext}$ from Beer-Lambert law

$$
b_{ext} = -\frac{1}{L} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)
$$

### Step 2: Substitute

$$
b_{ext} = -\frac{1}{1.5 \text{ km}} \cdot \ln(0.35)
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate logarithm

$$
\ln(0.35) = -1.0498
$$

### Step 4: Compute $b_{ext}$

$$
b_{ext} = -\frac{-1.0498}{1.5} = 0.6999 \text{ km}^{-1}
$$

### Step 5: Compute visual range

$$
V = \frac{3.912}{0.6999} = 5.59 \text{ km}
$$

**Answer: $V \approx 5.59$ km** — typical of a hazy urban afternoon. ∎

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Single-Particle Scattering

**Problem:** Air contains $N = 1.2 \times 10^{9}$ particles per m³. Each particle has diameter $d_p = 0.5$ µm and scattering efficiency $Q_{sca} = 2.0$ (Mie regime). Find the bulk scattering coefficient and visual range (assume $b_{abs} \approx 0$).

### Step 1: Single-particle scattering cross-section

$$
\sigma_s = \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \cdot Q_{sca}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute

$$
\sigma_s = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.5 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4} \cdot 2.0
$$

$$
\sigma_s = \frac{\pi \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-13}}{4} \cdot 2.0
$$

$$
\sigma_s = 3.927 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^2/\text{particle}
$$

### Step 3: Bulk scattering coefficient

$$
b_{sca} = N \cdot \sigma_s = (1.2 \times 10^9) \cdot (3.927 \times 10^{-13})
$$

$$
b_{sca} = 4.71 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

### Step 4: Visual range

$$
V = \frac{3.912}{4.71 \times 10^{-4}} = 8306 \text{ m} = 8.31 \text{ km}
$$

∎

---

## 3.5 Nighttime Visual Range Derivation

For a point light source at night, the threshold is $\epsilon = 0.05$ (eye is more sensitive in dark, but the source must be 5% above background):

$$
V_{night} = \frac{-\ln(0.05)}{b_{ext}} = \frac{2.996}{b_{ext}}
$$

> **Logic:** At night, a *light* source is seen against a dark background. The contrast is inverted (source brighter than background), but the threshold is less strict.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Visibility Measurement Methodology

The following Mermaid block maps the entire taxonomy of visibility measurement techniques as required by the KTU syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Visibility Measurement] --> B[Direct Optical Methods]
    A --> C[Indirect / Remote Sensing]
    A --> D[Chemical / Particulate Proxies]
    A --> E[Human Observer Methods]

    B --> B1[Transmissometer]
    B --> B2[Telephotometer]
    B --> B3[Nephelometer]
    B --> B4[Integrating Sphere]

    C --> C1[LIDAR]
    C --> C2[Satellite Aerosol Optical Depth]
    C --> C3[Solar Radiometer]

    D --> D1[Optical Particle Counter]
    D --> D2[PM2.5 Mass Concentration]
    D --> D3[Black Carbon Analyzer]

    E --> E1[Trained Human Observer]
    E --> E2[Visibility Marker Targets]
    E --> E3[All-Weather Camera Systems]

    B1 --> F1[Output: Extinction Coefficient]
    B3 --> F2[Output: Scattering Coefficient]
    C1 --> F3[Output: Range-Resolved Profile]
    D2 --> F4[Output: Correlation with Visual Range]

    F1 --> G[Apply Koschmieder Eqn]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Visual Range V in km]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Transmissometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Light Source 550nm LED] --> S2[Collimating Optics]
    S2 --> S3[Open Atmosphere Path L=100m to 10km]
    S3 --> S4[Receiver Telescope]
    S4 --> S5[Photodetector]
    S5 --> S6[Signal Processor]
    S6 --> S7[Display or Data Logger]

    REF[Reference Detector] --> S6

    S6 --> CALC[Compute T = I divided by I0]
    CALC --> EXT[Compute bext = minus ln T divided by L]
    EXT --> VR[Compute V = 3.912 divided by bext]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Integrating Nephelometer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Sample Air Inlet] --> A2[Optical Chamber with Scatter Volume]
    A3[Lambertian Light Source] --> A2
    A2 --> A4[Detector at 90 degrees to Beam]
    A2 --> A5[Backscatter Detector at 135 degrees]
    A4 --> A6[Signal Amplifier and ADC]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Microcontroller]
    A7 --> A8[Output bsca in per meter]
    A7 --> A9[Assume babs from NO2 BC data]
    A9 --> A10[Compute bext = bsca plus babs]
    A10 --> A11[Apply Koschmieder for Visual Range]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Visibility Reduction Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Emission Source Stack] --> P2[Primary Pollutants PM SO2 NOx]
    P2 --> P3[Atmospheric Dispersion]
    P3 --> P4[Secondary Aerosol Formation]
    P4 --> P5[Increased bsca and babs]
    P5 --> P6[Increased bext]
    P6 --> P7[Reduced Visual Range V]
    P7 --> P8[Observer Reports Haze Smog]
    P8 --> P9[Regulatory Action]
```

## 4.5 Concept Map — Linking Light, Particles, and Visibility

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LM[Molecules N2 O2] -->|Rayleigh bsca lambda to power minus 4| BE[bext]
    PM[Fine Particles PM2.5] -->|Mie bsca weakly wavelength dependent| BE
    BC[Black Carbon] -->|Strong babs across spectrum| BE
    NO2[Nitrogen Dioxide] -->|Selective babs in blue 400 to 500nm| BE
    BE --> K[Kosmieder V = 3.912 over bext]
    K --> VR[Visual Range V in km]
    VR --> OBS[Human Perception of Haze]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Visual Range" as applied in air pollution monitoring. State the standard contrast threshold and the reference wavelength used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Visual Range* is the maximum distance at which a standard dark object can be discerned against the horizon sky under prevailing atmospheric conditions (1 mark).
- It is defined as the distance at which the apparent contrast of the object falls to the **threshold contrast** $\epsilon = 0.02$ (i.e., 2%) (1 mark).
- The reference wavelength used is $\lambda = 550$ nm, corresponding to the peak photopic (daytime) response of the human eye (1 mark).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Rayleigh scattering and Mie scattering. Which is more relevant in a polluted urban atmosphere?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Rayleigh scattering* occurs when particle diameter is much smaller than the wavelength of light ($d_p \ll \lambda$); it is caused by air molecules and has a strong wavelength dependence ($\propto \lambda^{-4}$), producing blue skies (1 mark).
- *Mie scattering* occurs when particle size is comparable to the wavelength ($d_p \approx \lambda$); caused by suspended PM; weakly wavelength-dependent; produces white/gray haze (1 mark).
- In a polluted urban atmosphere with high PM₂.₅ concentrations, **Mie scattering** dominates (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the Koschmieder equation relating visual range to the atmospheric extinction coefficient. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the working principle, components, and applications of a **transmissometer** for measuring visibility. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — State Koschmieder's assumptions (2 marks):**
- The atmosphere is horizontally homogeneous (uniform $b_{ext}$)
- The sky is a uniform luminance background
- The object is a perfect black body ($C_0 = 1$)
- The observer's eye threshold contrast is $\epsilon = 0.02$

**Step 2 — Set up the contrast attenuation equation:**

$$
C(x) = C_0 \cdot e^{-b_{ext} \cdot x}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of visual range** at $x = V$ when $C = \epsilon$:

$$
\epsilon = C_0 \cdot e^{-b_{ext} \cdot V}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for V:**

$$
V = \frac{\ln(C_0/\epsilon)}{b_{ext}} = \frac{\ln(1/0.02)}{b_{ext}} = \frac{3.912}{b_{ext}}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on physical meaning (2 marks):**
- Visual range is inversely proportional to extinction coefficient
- Higher $b_{ext}$ (more pollution) → lower visual range
- A 10% increase in $b_{ext}$ reduces visibility by ~9%

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A transmissometer measures the fraction of light transmitted across a known horizontal path length $L$ through the atmosphere. The transmittance $T = I/I_0$ is related to $b_{ext}$ via Beer's law. The instrument essentially provides a real-time measurement of $b_{ext}$, from which visual range is computed using Koschmieder's equation.

**Major Components (2 marks):**
- **Transmitter:** A stabilized light source (LED or incandescent), typically at 550 nm, with collimating optics
- **Receiver:** A photodetector with telescope optics, aligned to the transmitter
- **Path length $L$:** Typically 100 m to 10 km (long-path transmissometers can use retroreflectors)
- **Signal processor:** Computes $T$, $b_{ext}$, and $V$

**Procedure (1 mark):**
1. Calibrate with clean air reference ($I_0$)
2. Measure transmitted intensity $I$
3. Compute $b_{ext} = -\frac{1}{L}\ln(I/I_0)$
4. Compute $V = 3.912/b_{ext}$

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Airport Runway Visual Range (RVR) systems
- Highway fog-warning systems
- Long-term visibility monitoring in Class I areas (national parks)
- Compliance with EPA Regional Haze Rule

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of an **integrating nephelometer**. (7 marks)

**(b)** The atmospheric extinction coefficient at an industrial site is $b_{ext} = 2.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{-1}$. Calculate the visual range. If a transmissometer of path length 2 km measures $I/I_0 = 0.50$, find the visual range from this measurement and comment on the agreement. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Principle (2 marks):**
The integrating nephelometer measures the **light scattering coefficient** $b_{sca}$ of the ambient aerosol by illuminating a sample air volume and measuring the total light scattered in all directions (integrated using a Lambertian light source and integrating chamber walls).

**Sketch description (2 marks):**
- A sealed optical chamber
- Sample air drawn continuously through the chamber
- A diffuse light source (incandescent lamp with opal glass diffuser) illuminates the sample volume
- A photomultiplier tube (PMT) detector is positioned at 90° to the beam
- Optional backscatter detector at 135°
- Output signal $\propto b_{sca}$

**Working (2 marks):**
- Clean (particle-free) air is used to set zero
- Span gas (e.g., CO₂ or known aerosol) used for calibration
- Sample air continuously drawn; scattered light at 90° integrated by chamber geometry
- The PMT output voltage is directly proportional to $b_{sca}$ in m⁻¹

**Advantages / Applications (1 mark):**
- Provides real-time, in-situ $b_{sca}$ data
- Used in EPA's IMPROVE network for regional haze studies
- Combined with $b_{abs}$ (from aethalometer), gives total $b_{ext}$ → visual range

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Part (i) — Visual range from given $b_{ext}$ (3 marks):**

$$
V = \frac{3.912}{b_{ext}} = \frac{3.912}{2.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{-1}}
$$

$$
V = 13{,}971 \text{ m} \approx 13.97 \text{ km}
$$

**Part (ii) — Visual range from transmissometer (3 marks):**

$$
b_{ext} = -\frac{1}{L} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\frac{1}{2 \text{ km}} \cdot \ln(0.50)
$$

$$
b_{ext} = -\frac{-0.6931}{2} = 0.3466 \text{ km}^{-1}
$$

Converting to m⁻¹: $b_{ext} = 3.466 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{-1}$

$$
V = \frac{3.912}{3.466 \times 10^{-4}} = 11{,}287 \text{ m} \approx 11.29 \text{ km}
$$

**Part (iii) — Comment (1 mark):**
The two values differ slightly because the first uses a single-point extinction estimate (possibly from a nephelometer that misses $b_{abs}$), while the second uses a long-path transmissometer that captures both scattering and absorption along 2 km. The agreement within ~20% is reasonable for field measurements.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in $b_{ext}$:** Mixing km⁻¹ and m⁻¹ in the Koschmieder equation. If $V$ is in km, use $b_{ext}$ in km⁻¹; if $V$ is in m, use $b_{ext}$ in m⁻¹. The constant 3.912 is dimensionless.
> 2. **Using 3.912 for nighttime visual range:** Examiners often give a question on night visibility to test if students know the threshold is 0.05 → constant is **2.996**, not 3.912.
> 3. **Confusing Rayleigh and Mie regimes:** The "blue sky" question is a favorite. Always quote $\lambda^{-4}$ for Rayleigh.
> 4. **Skipping the assumption that $C_0 = 1$:** A 1-mark deduction if you don't state it explicitly in the Koschmieder derivation.
> 5. **Transmissometer sketch missing the retroreflector or the alignment** between transmitter and receiver.
> 6. **Forgetting the integrating nephelometer measures ONLY $b_{sca}$, not $b_{ext}$.** You must add $b_{abs}$ separately.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorize):**
> - **Koschmieder equation:** $V = 3.912 / b_{ext}$ (day) and $V = 2.996 / b_{ext}$ (night)
> - **Threshold contrasts:** 0.02 (day, black object) and 0.05 (night, point source)
> - **Beer-Lambert Law:** $T = \exp(-b_{ext} \cdot L)$, $b_{ext} = -\ln(T)/L$
> - **Three extinction components:** $b_{ext} = b_{abs} + b_{sca}$
> - **Rayleigh scattering:** $\propto \lambda^{-4}$, dominates for clean air / blue sky
> - **Mie scattering:** Weakly wavelength dependent, dominates for polluted air / white haze
> - **Single-particle cross-section:** $\sigma_s = (\pi d_p^2 / 4) \cdot Q_{sca}$
> - **Bulk scattering:** $b_{sca} = N \cdot \sigma_s$
> - **Reference wavelength:** 550 nm
> - **Six methods of measurement (must list all in 14-mark questions):**
>   1. Transmissometer (long-path)
>   2. Telephotometer
>   3. Integrating Nephelometer
>   4. LIDAR (remote sensing)
>   5. Optical Particle Counter (indirect)
>   6. Human Observer / Visibility Markers
> - **Transmissometer measures total $b_{ext}$** (both absorption and scattering)
> - **Nephelometer measures only $b_{sca}$**
> - **Visual range decreases as $b_{ext}$ increases** — pollution shrinks visibility
> - **PM₂.₅ is the dominant pollutant** affecting urban visibility; **NO₂ and black carbon** are the dominant absorbers
> - **Deciview (dv)** is a log-scale visibility metric used by EPA Regional Haze Rule: $dv = 10 \cdot \ln(b_{ext,0}/b_{ext})$ where $b_{ext,0} = 10 \text{ Mm}^{-1}$
> - **Standard path length for airport RVR transmissometers:** 30 m to 300 m (baseline) and forward-scatter sensors are also used
> - **Class I areas** (US National Parks) require visibility tracking under the 1977 Clean Air Act Amendments

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Noise Pollution

### Formal Definition
**Noise pollution** is defined as any unwanted, unpleasant, or harmful sound that disrupts normal human activities, harms psychological or physiological wellbeing, or damages the ecological balance of an environment. From a KTU engineering perspective, it is treated as an environmental stressor characterised by fluctuating pressure waves propagating through an elastic medium (usually air) with intensity, frequency, and duration sufficient to cause adverse effects on human or animal life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Definition (CPCB/IS 1417):** "Noise is defined as any disagreeable or undesired sound which is loud, unpleasant, unexpected, or otherwise undesirable."

### Quantitative Standard Metrics
The physical quantity that describes noise is **Sound Pressure (P)** measured in **Pascals (Pa)**. However, because the human ear responds to an enormous range of pressures (from 20 μPa to 200 Pa), a logarithmic scale is used:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Sound Pressure:** $P_{ref} = 20 \, \mu Pa = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, N/m^2$. This is the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz.
>
> **Reference Sound Intensity:** $I_{ref} = 10^{-12} \, W/m^2$.
>
> **Reference Sound Power:** $W_{ref} = 10^{-12} \, W$.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Whisper vs. The Rock Concert"
Imagine standing in a library (a quiet whisper at 30 dB) versus a rock concert (110 dB). The actual air pressure variation in a rock concert is not "four times" louder than a whisper — it is **one million times greater in pressure** and **one trillion times greater in intensity**. Because these numbers are impossible to use directly, we compress them into a logarithmic unit called the **decibel (dB)**. The decibel is a *ratio*, not an absolute unit, which is why you will always see a reference attached (dB SPL, dB(A), dBW, etc.).

### Common Sound Sources & Their Levels

| Source | Approx. Level (dB) | Effect on Humans |
|---|---|---|
| Rustling leaves | 10 | Barely audible |
| Whisper | 20–30 | Quiet |
| Office / Library | 40–50 | Comfortable |
| Normal conversation | 60 | Intrusive at night |
| Heavy traffic | 80–85 | Long-term hearing damage |
| Subway / Power tools | 90–100 | Hearing damage in 8 hrs |
| Rock concert | 110–120 | Pain threshold (~120 dB) |
| Jet engine (close) | 130–140 | Immediate pain/damage |

> [!NOTE]
> **A-weighting (dB(A)):** The human ear is not equally sensitive to all frequencies. It is most sensitive near 1–4 kHz and less sensitive to very low and very high frequencies. The **A-weighting filter** mimics this response so sound level meters can report a single "perceived" level — **dB(A)**. This is the metric mandated by the **CPCB Noise Rules (2000)** and **IS 4954**.

---

## 1.2 Photochemical Reactions of the Atmosphere

### Formal Definition
**Photochemical reactions** are light-initiated (photon-driven) chemical reactions occurring in the troposphere in which primary emissions of nitrogen oxides (NOx) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) react under solar ultraviolet radiation to form secondary pollutants, primarily **ozone (O₃)**, **peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN)**, **aldehydes**, and other oxidants. The resulting mixture of oxidising pollutants and visibility-reducing aerosols is termed **photochemical smog**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Atmospheric Reactor"
Think of a polluted city basin (Delhi, Los Angeles, Athens) on a hot, sunny, windless afternoon as a giant chemical reactor. The raw ingredients (NOx from car exhausts, VOCs from fuel evaporation, solvents) are continuously fed in. **Sunlight acts as the catalyst/activator** — without sunlight, the mixture would simply sit there. Once the UV photons arrive, a chain reaction begins producing **ozone (O₃)**, the main toxicant in photochemical smog. The "products" (O₃, PAN) are not emitted by any single source — they are *generated in situ*, which is why this type of pollution is called a **secondary pollutant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary vs. Secondary Pollutants:**
> - **Primary Pollutants** — Emitted directly (CO, SO₂, NO, particulates).
> - **Secondary Pollutants** — Formed *in the atmosphere* by chemical reactions (O₃, PAN, HNO₃, smog aerosols).
>
> Photochemical smog is a classic **secondary pollution** phenomenon.

### Why It Matters for Engineers
Photochemical reactions govern:
- Tropospheric ozone build-up, which damages crops, materials, and lungs.
- Formation of acid rain precursors (HNO₃, H₂SO₄).
- Visibility degradation through light-scattering aerosols.
- Greenhouse gas (O₃) forcing.

---

## 1.3 Sampling & Measurement Instruments — Quick Definitions

| # | Instrument | What It Measures | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **High Volume Sampler (HVS)** | Total Suspended Particulates (TSP) and/or PM₁₀/PM₂.₅ in ambient air | IS 5182 (Part XIV), CPCB |
| 2 | **Exhaust Gas Analyser (Petrol)** | CO, HC, CO₂, O₂, NOx, λ (lambda) in petrol vehicle tailpipe | BIS 14654, AIS-137 |
| 3 | **Exhaust Gas Analyser (Diesel)** | Smoke opacity / smoke density (% opacity, Bosch, Hartridge units) | BIS 14654, AIS-137 |
| 4 | **Stack Sampler** | Particulate matter & velocity from industrial stacks | IS 11255 (Parts 1–3), USEPA Method 5/17 |
| 5 | **Sound Level Meter (SLM)** | Equivalent continuous SPL, Leq, in dB(A) | IEC 61672, IS 1417, IS 15086 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each of these instruments is a *measuring transducer system* — they convert a physical/chemical quantity into an electrical signal that is processed and displayed in engineering units.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Noise Pollution — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 The Physics of Sound
Sound is a **mechanical longitudinal wave** requiring a medium. It propagates as alternating compressions and rarefactions. The measurable parameters are:

- **Sound Pressure (P)** in Pa — RMS value of pressure fluctuation.
- **Sound Intensity (I)** in W/m² — sound energy per unit area per second.
- **Sound Power (W)** in W — total acoustic energy radiated per second.
- **Frequency (f)** in Hz — number of pressure oscillations per second.
- **Wavelength (λ)** in m — spatial period of the wave.

The speed of sound in air is governed by:
$$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$$
where $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats (1.4 for air), $R$ = universal gas constant, $T$ = absolute temperature, $M$ = molar mass.

### 2.1.2 Decibel Scales Used in Noise Pollution

| Scale | Formula | Reference | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sound Pressure Level (SPL) | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_{ref}}\right)$ dB | $P_{ref} = 20\,\mu Pa$ | Raw microphone reading |
| Sound Intensity Level (SIL) | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{ref}}\right)$ dB | $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}\,W/m^2$ | Energy-based measure |
| Sound Power Level (PWL) | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_{ref}}\right)$ dB | $W_{ref} = 10^{-12}\,W$ | Source rating |

> [!NOTE]
> Because intensity is proportional to the *square* of pressure, the pressure-level formula carries a factor of 20 while the intensity/power-level formulas carry a factor of 10.

### 2.1.3 Noise Addition — "The Dumbbell Rule"
When *n* identical sound sources operate simultaneously, total level is:
$$L_{total} = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}(n)$$
For **two unequal sources** with level difference $\Delta L = L_1 - L_2$ (where $L_1 > L_2$):
$$L_{total} = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + 10^{-\Delta L / 10}\right)$$

A simple field shortcut: **when two sources differ by ≥ 10 dB, the quieter source adds less than 0.5 dB and is essentially negligible.**

### 2.1.4 Equivalent Continuous Level (Leq)
The most important descriptor in KTU/CPCB standards is:
$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt\right)$$
where $L(t)$ is the instantaneous A-weighted SPL and $T$ is the averaging time (usually 8 hr or 24 hr).

### 2.1.5 Ambient Noise Standards (CPCB)

| Zone | Day (6 AM–10 PM) dB(A) | Night (10 PM–6 AM) dB(A) |
|---|---|---|
| Industrial | 75 | 70 |
| Commercial | 65 | 55 |
| Residential | 55 | 45 |
| Silence Zone | 50 | 40 |

> Silence zones include hospitals, educational institutions, courts, and religious places (100 m buffer).

---

## 2.2 Photochemical Smog Chemistry — Step-by-Step

### 2.2.1 The Initiation Step (Photolysis of NO₂)
$$NO_2 + h\nu \xrightarrow{\lambda < 430\,nm} NO + O^{\bullet}$$
A photon of UV/visible light dissociates nitrogen dioxide. The atomic oxygen produced is highly reactive.

### 2.2.2 Ozone Formation
$$O^{\bullet} + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$$
where **M** is a third body (usually N₂ or O₂) that absorbs excess vibrational energy and stabilises the newly formed ozone molecule.

### 2.2.3 The Null Cycle (No Net O₃ Without VOCs)
$$O_3 + NO \rightarrow NO_2 + O_2$$
This reaction rapidly destroys ozone and reforms NO₂, creating a **photo-stationary state** with little net O₃ accumulation. **The system is "stuck" until VOCs intervene.**

### 2.2.4 The Radical Chain — VOCs as the Key
Hydroxyl radicals (•OH) are the atmosphere's "detergent" — generated primarily by ozone photolysis and HONO/nitrate photolysis:
$$O_3 + h\nu \rightarrow O_2 + O^{\bullet} \quad (\text{at } \lambda < 320\,nm)$$
$$O^{\bullet} + H_2O \rightarrow 2\,^{\bullet}OH$$
$$^{\bullet}OH + RH \rightarrow R^{\bullet} + H_2O$$
$$R^{\bullet} + O_2 \rightarrow RO_2^{\bullet}$$
$$RO_2^{\bullet} + NO \rightarrow RO^{\bullet} + NO_2$$
**The regenerated NO₂ again photolyses, making more O₃ → this is the net O₃ production cycle.**

### 2.2.5 PAN Formation (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate)
$$CH_3CHO + ^{\bullet}OH \rightarrow CH_3CO^{\bullet} + H_2O$$
$$CH_3CO^{\bullet} + O_2 \rightarrow CH_3C(O)OO^{\bullet}$$
$$CH_3C(O)OO^{\bullet} + NO_2 \leftrightarrow CH_3C(O)OONO_2 \;(\text{PAN})$$
**PAN** is a powerful lachrymator and phytotoxin, and is a key marker of photochemical smog.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The VOC-to-NOx ratio determines the regime of O₃ formation. In **VOC-limited** regimes, reducing VOCs is more effective. In **NOx-limited** regimes, reducing NOx is more effective. This is why O₃ control strategies must be region-specific.

### 2.2.6 Photochemical Smog — Favourable Conditions
- Sunny days (high UV flux).
- Temperature inversion trapping pollutants.
- High vehicle density.
- Low wind (stagnation).
- Topography that traps air (basins, valleys).

---

## 2.3 Measurement Instruments — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.3.1 High Volume Sampler (HVS) — Theory

**Principle:** Ambient air is drawn through a pre-weighed **glass-fibre filter** at a controlled high flow rate (typically **1.1–1.7 m³/min**) for a fixed duration (usually **24 hours**). The mass of particulates collected, divided by the volume of air sampled, gives the **Total Suspended Particulate (TSP)** concentration.

**Mass Concentration Formula:**
$$C_{TSP} = \dfrac{(W_f - W_i)}{V}$$
$$V = Q \times t \times 10^{-3} \quad (\text{volume in m}^3 \text{ at ambient conditions})$$

Where:
- $W_f$ = final mass of filter (g or mg)
- $W_i$ = initial mass of filter (g or mg)
- $Q$ = average flow rate (L/min)
- $t$ = sampling duration (min)

**Correction to Standard Conditions (25 °C, 101.325 kPa):**
$$V_{std} = V \times \dfrac{P_{ambient}}{P_{std}} \times \dfrac{T_{std}}{T_{ambient}}$$

### 2.3.2 Exhaust Gas Analyser — Theory

#### (a) Petrol (Gasoline) Exhaust
- **CO & CO₂** → **Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR)** analyser.
- **HC** → Flame Ionisation Detector (FID) or NDIR.
- **O₂** → Electrochemical / Paramagnetic sensor.
- **NOx** → Chemiluminescent analyser (NO + O₃ → NO₂* → hν).
- **Lambda (λ)** = (Actual A/F) / (Stoichiometric A/F).

#### (b) Diesel Exhaust
Diesel engines run lean with excess O₂, so NDIR for CO is less critical. The main concern is **smoke/particulates** (the "black soot"), measured by an **opacimeter (smoke meter)**.

- **Principle:** A light beam is transmitted through a fixed length of exhaust. The attenuation is reported as **% opacity**.
- **Scales:**
  - **Hartridge scale** (0–100) — used in India.
  - **Bosch scale** (0–10) — European.
  - **Bacharach scale** (0–9) — older.
- **Conversion:** Bosch = 10 × (1 − transmittance); Hartridge ≈ 10 × Bosch.

### 2.3.3 Stack Sampler — Theory
- **Purpose:** Determine the concentration (mg/Nm³) and emission rate (kg/hr) of particulates and gaseous pollutants from industrial stacks.
- **Sampling principle:** **Isokinetic sampling** — the gas velocity at the probe inlet must equal the stack gas velocity, otherwise oversized or undersized particles are preferentially sampled, leading to error.
- **Isokinetic Velocity Condition:**
$$V_{probe} = V_{stack} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{stack}}$$
$$Q_{sampling} = V_{stack} \times A_{probe}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Isokinetic sampling is non-negotiable for accurate PM measurement per IS 11255 (Part 1).** A ±20% deviation in velocity causes significant error in measured mass.

### 2.3.4 Sound Level Meter (SLM) — Theory

**Block-level principle:** Sound → Microphone (transducer) → Preamplifier → A-weighting filter → RMS detector → Integrator (for Leq) → Display.

**SLM Classes (IEC 61672-1):**
- **Class 1 (Type 1)** — Precision (±0.7 dB), laboratory/field reference.
- **Class 2 (Type 2)** — General purpose (±1.5 dB), used for most CPCB surveys.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU board examinations frequently ask the difference between Type 1 and Type 2 SLMs and why A-weighting is used (mimics human ear response at 40-phon).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Topic | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(P / P_{ref})$ | $P$ in Pa, $P_{ref} = 20\,\mu Pa$ |
| 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_{ref})$ | $I$ in W/m², $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ |
| 3 | Sound Power Level | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W / W_{ref})$ | $W$ in W, $W_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ |
| 4 | n identical sources | $L_{tot} = L + 10 \log_{10}(n)$ | — |
| 5 | Two unequal sources | $L_{tot} = L_1 + 10\log_{10}(1+10^{-\Delta L/10})$ | $\Delta L = L_1 - L_2$ |
| 6 | Equivalent Level | $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\int 10^{L(t)/10}dt\right)$ | in dB(A) |
| 7 | TSP from HVS | $C = (W_f - W_i) / V$ | $C$ in $\mu g/m^3$, $V$ in m³ |
| 8 | Standard volume | $V_{std} = V \times (P_a / P_s) \times (T_s / T_a)$ | in Nm³ |
| 9 | Stack velocity | $V_{stack} = Q / A_{stack}$ | $V$ in m/s, $Q$ in m³/s |
| 10 | Isokinetic condition | $V_{probe} = V_{stack}$ | m/s |
| 11 | Emission rate | $E = C \times Q \times 10^{-6}$ | $E$ in kg/s, $C$ in mg/m³ |
| 12 | Bosch ↔ Opacity | $Bosch = 10 \times (1 - e^{-kL})$ | $k$ = absorption coeff., $L$ = path length |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sound Pressure Level (SPL) Formula

**Given:** Sound pressure $P$ in Pa, reference pressure $P_{ref} = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa.

**Step 1 — Define the ratio:**
We seek a logarithmic scale that maps the enormous range of human hearing (20 μPa to 200 Pa) to a manageable range (~0 to 140).

$$R = \dfrac{P}{P_{ref}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply base-10 logarithm:**
A multiplicative factor of 10 in pressure becomes "1 unit" on the log scale.

$$\log_{10}(R) = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_{ref}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Define the Bel:**
One Bel corresponds to a 10:1 power ratio (or 10^0.5:1 pressure ratio).

$$L_{Bel} = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_{ref}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Convert Bel to decibel (1 Bel = 10 dB):**
$$L_{dB} = 10 \times L_{Bel} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_{ref}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**
For $P = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa, $L = 10 \log_{10}(1) = 0$ dB (threshold of hearing).
For $P = 20$ Pa, $L = 10 \log_{10}(10^6) = 60$ dB.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor 10 (not 20) is used because the original Bel definition is based on **power ratios**, and power is proportional to the *square* of pressure: $I \propto P^2$. The conversion to a **pressure** ratio inserts a factor of 2 in the exponent, hence the standard SPL formula $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(P/P_{ref})$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Isokinetic Sampling Velocity

**Step 1 — Define volumetric flow at the stack:**
$$Q_{stack} = V_{stack} \times A_{stack}$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow of particulates (true):**
$$\dot{m}_{true} = C \times Q_{stack}$$

**Step 3 — Sampled mass (if non-isokinetic):**
A probe sampling at velocity $V_{probe} \neq V_{stack}$ draws gas that does not faithfully represent streamlines, biasing the particle size captured.

**Step 4 — Condition for representative sampling:**
For particles to enter the probe with the same inertia distribution as in the free stream, the streamline entering the probe must be parallel to the probe axis. This requires:

$$V_{probe} = V_{stack}$$

**Step 5 — Probe flow rate:**
$$Q_{probe} = V_{stack} \times A_{probe}$$

If $A_{probe}$ is fixed, the sampling pump is throttled until $Q_{probe}$ satisfies the above.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Noise Addition

**Problem:** Two machines produce 85 dB(A) and 82 dB(A) respectively. What is the combined level?

**Step 1 — Identify the louder source:**
$L_1 = 85$ dB, $L_2 = 82$ dB, so $\Delta L = 3$ dB.

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**
$$L_{total} = 85 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + 10^{-3/10}\right)$$
$$= 85 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + 10^{-0.3}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $10^{-0.3}$:**
$$10^{-0.3} = 0.5012$$

**Step 4 — Add 1:**
$$1 + 0.5012 = 1.5012$$

**Step 5 — Logarithm:**
$$\log_{10}(1.5012) = 0.1764$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by 10:**
$$10 \times 0.1764 = 1.764 \, dB$$

**Step 7 — Add to $L_1$:**
$$L_{total} = 85 + 1.76 = 86.76 \approx 86.8 \, dB(A)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** Never just *add* dB values. Always use the logarithmic addition. Two sources differing by ≤ 3 dB add ~3 dB maximum. Two equal sources add exactly 3 dB.

## 3.4 Worked Example — High Volume Sampler Concentration

**Problem:** A high volume sampler is operated for 24 hours at an average flow of 1.2 m³/min. The initial filter mass is 4.2672 g, and the final mass is 4.3145 g. Find the TSP concentration in μg/m³ and the ambient air quality status with respect to NAAQS (CPCB NAAQS for TSP = 200 μg/m³ for 24-hour average).

**Step 1 — Mass of particulates:**
$$\Delta W = 4.3145 - 4.2672 = 0.0473 \, g = 47.3 \, mg = 47{,}300 \, \mu g$$

**Step 2 — Total volume sampled:**
$$V = 1.2 \times 24 \times 60 = 1728 \, m^3$$

**Step 3 — Concentration:**
$$C_{TSP} = \dfrac{47{,}300}{1728} = 27.37 \, \mu g/m^3$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with NAAQS:**
$$27.37 \, \mu g/m^3 \ll 200 \, \mu g/m^3 \;\;\Rightarrow \text{Within limit, acceptable air quality.}$$

## 3.5 Worked Example — Stack Emission Rate

**Problem:** A cement stack has flue gas flow $Q = 25 \, m^3/s$ and PM concentration $C = 150 \, mg/Nm^3$. Find the emission rate in kg/hr.

**Step 1 — Convert concentration to kg/m³:**
$$C = 150 \, mg/m^3 = 150 \times 10^{-6} \, kg/m^3 = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \, kg/m^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute mass flow:**
$$\dot{m} = C \times Q = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 = 3.75 \times 10^{-3} \, kg/s$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kg/hr:**
$$\dot{m} = 3.75 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 13.5 \, kg/hr$$

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Four Instruments

| Parameter | HVS | EGA (Petrol) | EGA (Diesel) | Stack Sampler | SLM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pollutant | TSP/PM | CO, HC, NOx, O₂ | Smoke (Opacity) | PM, SO₂, NOx | Sound Pressure |
| Sensor Principle | Gravimetric | NDIR / Chemiluminescent | Opacimeter | Gravimetric + Gas analyser | Microphone |
| Sample Form | Ambient air | Tailpipe gas | Tailpipe gas | Hot stack gas | Air pressure wave |
| Flow / Sampling Rate | 1.1–1.7 m³/min | ~1 L/min | Continuous | 10–30 L/min | N/A (acoustic) |
| Standard | IS 5182 XIV | AIS-137 / BIS 14654 | AIS-137 | IS 11255 | IEC 61672 |
| Output Unit | μg/m³ | %, ppm, λ | % opacity, Bosch | mg/Nm³, kg/hr | dB(A), Leq |
| Critical Requirement | Constant flow | Lambda / Warm-up | Heated sample line | Isokinetic velocity | A-weighting, calibration |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High Volume Sampler — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ambient Air Inlet] --> B[Sieve Plate Protector]
    B --> C[Filter Holder with Glass Fibre Filter]
    C --> D[Flow Measurement Orifice + Manometer]
    D --> E[High Capacity Blower Motor 1.1-1.7 m3 per min]
    E --> F[Rotameter or Digital Flow Readout]
    F --> G[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    C --> H[Pre-weighed Filter Wi grams]
    C --> I[Post-sampling Filter Wf grams]
    H --> J[Mass Difference Calculation]
    I --> J
    J --> K[TSP Concentration microgram per m3]
    F --> L[Sampling Time t hours]
    L --> M[Total Volume V equals Q times t]
    M --> N[C equals Wf minus Wi over V]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Air enters via the inlet, is filtered through a pre-weighed glass-fibre filter, and the flow rate is monitored by an orifice/manometer combination. Mass gained on the filter divided by total volume sampled gives the TSP.

## 4.2 Exhaust Gas Analyser (Petrol) — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Petrol Vehicle Tailpipe] --> B[Sample Probe and Heated Line]
    B --> C[Particulate Filter and Water Trap]
    C --> D[Sample Pump]
    D --> E1[NDIR Cell for CO]
    D --> E2[NDIR Cell for HC]
    D --> E3[Electrochemical O2 Sensor]
    D --> E4[Chemiluminescent NOx Module]
    E1 --> F[Microprocessor Signal Processing]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Digital Display ppm percent lambda]
    F --> H[Data Logger and Printer]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Diesel Vehicle Tailpipe] --> B[Opacimeter Sampling Head]
    B --> C[Light Source Stabilised]
    C --> D[Sample Chamber of Fixed Path Length L]

    E --> F[Comparator with Reference Beam]

```

## 4.4 Stack Sampler — Isokinetic Sampling Train

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Industrial Stack with Hot Flue Gas] --> B[Stack Gas Velocity Probe Pitot]
    B --> C[Determine Vstack in m per s]
    C --> D[Select Probe Diameter and Set Vprobe equals Vstack]
    D --> E[Sampling Nozzle Inserted into Stack]
    E --> F[Heated Filter Holder with Glass Fibre Filter]
    F --> G[Cold Trap and Impingers for SO2 NOx Absorption]
    G --> H[Condensate Removal and Silica Drying Tube]
    H --> I[Orifice or Dry Gas Meter for Volume Measurement]
    I --> J[Vacuum Pump Throttled to Maintain Isokinetic Flow]
    J --> K[Post-test Weighing of Filter and Reagents]
    K --> L[Compute Concentration in mg per Nm3]
    L --> M[Compute Emission Rate in kg per hr]
```

## 4.5 Photochemical Smog Formation — Reaction Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NOx from Vehicles and Industry] --> B[NO2 plus hv gives NO plus O radical]
    C[VOCs from Fuels and Solvents] --> D[OH radical Attack on VOCs]
    B --> E[O plus O2 plus M gives O3 plus M]
    E --> F[O3 plus NO gives NO2 plus O2 Null Cycle]
    D --> G[RO2 radical plus NO gives RO radical plus NO2]
    G --> H[New NO2 Photolyses gives NO plus O]
    H --> E
    F -.->|Without VOCs no net O3| I[Photo Stationary State Low O3]
    G -.->|With VOCs net O3 builds| J[Photochemical Smog High O3 PAN Aldehydes]
    E --> J
    D --> K[PAN Formation CH3CHO plus OH gives CH3CO plus H2O]
    K --> L[CH3CO plus O2 plus NO2 gives PAN]
    L --> J
```

