# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Set A]**
**Define noise pollution. What is A-weighting and why is it preferred over linear (unweighted) measurements in environmental noise assessment?**

**Model Answer:**
Noise pollution is any unwanted sound that adversely affects human health, comfort, or environment. **A-weighting** is an electronic filter built into a sound level meter that mimics the frequency response of the human ear at moderate sound levels (40-phon contour). The ear is most sensitive near 1–4 kHz and less sensitive at very low and very high frequencies. Because the human auditory system responds non-uniformly, A-weighting converts the raw SPL into a single number — **dB(A)** — that correlates well with perceived loudness and subjective annoyance. CPCB and ISO standards mandate A-weighted measurements (dB(A)) for all ambient noise compliance testing, which is why it is universally preferred over linear (unweighted) dB measurements for environmental noise.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently write "A-weighting reduces the effect of low frequencies" without explaining *why* the human ear behaves that way. Always link A-weighting to the 40-phon Fletcher-Munson equal-loudness contour.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the term 'photochemical smog'. List the four main primary pollutants that lead to its formation and the three main secondary pollutants produced.**

**Model Answer:**
Photochemical smog is a mixture of oxidising pollutants formed in the lower atmosphere through a series of light-driven chemical reactions involving primary emissions.

**Primary Pollutants (emitted directly):**
1. **NOx** (mainly NO and NO₂) — from vehicle exhausts and combustion.
2. **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** — from fuel evaporation, solvents, industrial processes.
3. **CO** — from incomplete combustion.
4. **Particulate matter** — from diesel and industrial stacks.

**Secondary Pollutants (formed in the atmosphere):**
1. **Ozone (O₃)** — the dominant toxicant, formed via NO₂ photolysis.
2. **Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN)** — lachrymator, marker of photochemical smog.
3. **Aldehydes (e.g., acetaldehyde, formaldehyde)** — irritants and carcinogens.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students list ozone as a primary pollutant. **O₃ in the troposphere is always secondary** — it is not directly emitted in significant quantities. Only stratospheric O₃ is "natural"; tropospheric O₃ is anthropogenic and photochemically produced.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note on KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice between two questions on the same module topic is provided. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** as full alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **principle, construction, and working of a High Volume Sampler (HVS)**. Discuss the role of the glass-fibre filter and the importance of the 24-hour sampling period. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **isokinetic sampling**? Why is it essential in stack sampling? Derive the condition for isokinetic sampling and explain what happens if the sampling velocity is greater than or less than the stack velocity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) High Volume Sampler (HVS):**

The High Volume Sampler is a standard instrument used to measure **Total Suspended Particulate Matter (TSP)** in ambient air. Its principle is **gravimetric** — particulates are collected on a filter and weighed.

**Construction (Block Diagram — refer Section 4.1):**
1. **Inlet/Sieve Plate:** Located at the top, prevents large debris from entering.
2. **Filter Holder:** Holds a circular **glass-fibre filter** (typically 8″ × 10″ or 20 cm × 25 cm) of high collection efficiency (≥ 99% for 0.3 μm particles).
3. **Flow Measurement System:** An orifice plate connected to a U-tube manometer, or a digital mass flow controller, ensures a known flow rate (typically 1.1–1.7 m³/min).
4. **Blower/Motor Assembly:** A high-capacity suction pump that draws air through the filter.
5. **Timer & Volume Totaliser:** Records total sampling duration and integrated volume.

**Working:**
- A pre-weighed filter ($W_i$) is placed in the holder.
- The blower is switched on; ambient air is drawn through the filter for 24 hours.
- Flow rate, ambient temperature, and barometric pressure are noted.
- After 24 hours, the filter is removed, conditioned in a desiccator, and reweighed ($W_f$).
- Mass gain = $W_f - W_i$; total volume = $Q \times t$.
- TSP = $(W_f - W_i)/V$ in μg/m³.

**Role of Glass-Fibre Filter:** It has high mechanical strength, can withstand high flow without tearing, has high collection efficiency for sub-micron particulates, and is stable at ambient humidity.

**Why 24 hours?** A 24-hour integrated sample smooths out diurnal variations in PM levels and provides a representative daily average, allowing direct comparison with CPCB **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** which are defined for 24-hour averaging times.

> **Key formula:** $C_{TSP} = (W_f - W_i)/V$ with $V$ corrected to standard temperature and pressure.

**(b) Isokinetic Sampling:**

**Definition:** Isokinetic sampling is the condition in which the velocity of gas entering the sampling probe is **exactly equal** to the velocity of the gas stream in the stack at the point of sampling. The word "iso" (equal) + "kinetic" (motion) means "equal motion".

**Why it is essential:**
Particulate matter in a stack has a distribution of sizes and inertia. The sampling probe draws a small portion of the stack gas into the measuring train. If the gas velocities are mismatched:
- The streamlines around the probe tip will curve, causing **selective sampling bias**.
- Large particles (high inertia) cannot follow curved streamlines and may either overshoot or undershoot the probe inlet.
- This leads to **over-estimation or under-estimation** of actual PM concentration.

**Derivation of the isokinetic condition:**

Let $V_s$ = stack gas velocity (m/s), $A_s$ = stack cross-section (m²).
Volumetric flow in stack: $Q_s = V_s A_s$.

Let the probe have cross-section $A_p$ and draws gas at velocity $V_p$ such that $Q_p = V_p A_p$.

For the gas entering the probe to faithfully represent the local gas composition, the streamline pattern must remain undisturbed. This is possible **only if** the velocity of gas in the probe inlet matches the free-stream velocity:

$$V_p = V_s$$

The required probe flow rate is:

$$Q_p = V_s \times A_p$$

**Consequences of Non-Isokinetic Sampling:**

| Condition | Effect on Measured PM |
|---|---|
| $V_p > V_s$ (super-isokinetic) | Streamlines converge into the probe; large particles (high inertia) bypass the inlet → **under-sampling of large particles → under-estimation of total mass** |
| $V_p < V_s$ (sub-isokinetic) | Streamlines diverge away from the probe; large particles overshoot and enter the inlet → **over-sampling of large particles → over-estimation of total mass** |
| $V_p = V_s$ | Faithful sampling → correct mass concentration |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (b):** Students often confuse which condition causes over- or under-estimation. Memorise: **Super-isokinetic = small particles sampled (mass under-estimated); Sub-isokinetic = large particles oversampled (mass over-estimated).** Also, the isokinetic condition applies at *each sampling traverse point*, not just on average — IS 11255 mandates multi-point sampling across the stack cross-section.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question Paper — Alternative Set]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **principle and working of a Sound Level Meter (SLM)**. Distinguish between **Type 1 and Type 2** instruments and explain the concept of **equivalent continuous sound level (Leq)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **photochemical reactions** responsible for the formation of **ozone and PAN** in the troposphere. Discuss the role of **VOCs as the rate-limiting precursor** in net O₃ production. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Sound Level Meter (SLM):**

**Principle:** A SLM converts sound pressure variations in air into an electrical signal using a precision microphone, processes this signal through frequency-weighting filters, time-averaging circuits, and displays the result as a single decibel value (dB SPL or dB(A)).

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.6):**
1. **Microphone** — 1/2-inch condenser (electret) microphone; converts acoustic pressure to voltage. Sensitivity typically 50 mV/Pa.
2. **Preamplifier** — matches the high-impedance microphone output to the input of the next stage.
3. **A-Weighting Filter** — analogue or digital filter that emulates the 40-phon equal-loudness contour of the human ear.
4. **RMS Detector & Integrator** — computes the RMS value of the A-weighted signal and integrates it over the measurement period.
5. **Display** — digital LCD in dB(A); also provides slow/fast time weighting (1 s or 125 ms).
6. **AC/DC Outputs** — for recorders, data loggers, and computer interfaces.
7. **Calibrator Interface** — acoustic calibrator (94 dB at 1 kHz) for field verification.

**Working:** Sound pressure → microphone → preamplifier → A-weighting filter → RMS detector → integrator → display. The instrument is calibrated before and after each measurement session using an acoustic calibrator.

**Type 1 vs Type 2 (per IEC 61672-1):**

| Feature | Type 1 (Precision) | Type 2 (General Purpose) |
|---|---|---|
| Tolerance | ±0.7 dB | ±1.5 dB |
| Use | Laboratory, reference, judicial | Field surveys, general compliance |
| Cost | High | Moderate |
| Frequency range | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | 20 Hz – 8 kHz (wider tolerance) |
| Examples | Brüel & Kjær 2236, CESVA SC-310 | Brüel & Kjær 2231, generic CPCB units |

**Equivalent Continuous Sound Level (Leq):**
$$L_{eq,T} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt\right)$$

Leq is the constant sound level that, if maintained for the entire period $T$, would deliver the same acoustic energy as the actual fluctuating noise. It is the **single most important descriptor** used by CPCB and ISO 1996 for environmental noise compliance, and is the basis for $L_{day}$, $L_{night}$, and $L_{den}$ (day-evening-night) indices.

**(b) Photochemical Reactions — Ozone and PAN Formation:**

Photochemical smog forms through a sequence of light-initiated radical chain reactions. The detailed mechanism (refer Section 4.5 flow chart) is as follows:

**Step 1 — Initiation (Photolysis of NO₂):**
$$NO_2 + h\nu \xrightarrow{\lambda < 430\,nm} NO + O^{\bullet}\quad (1)$$

**Step 2 — Ozone Formation:**
$$O^{\bullet} + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M\quad (2)$$

**Step 3 — The Null Cycle:**
$$O_3 + NO \rightarrow NO_2 + O_2\quad (3)$$
Equations (1) + (2) + (3) form a closed cycle: NO₂ is photolysed, O₃ is formed, O₃ reacts with NO to reform NO₂. **At steady state, [O₃] remains low** because the rate of (3) equals the rate of (1). No *net* ozone is produced.

**Step 4 — Radical Attack on VOCs (Breaking the Null Cycle):**
The •OH radical (the atmosphere's primary oxidant) is generated by:
$$O_3 + h\nu \rightarrow O_2 + O^{\bullet} \quad (4)$$
$$O^{\bullet} + H_2O \rightarrow 2\,^{\bullet}OH \quad (5)$$

The •OH attacks a hydrocarbon RH:
$$^{\bullet}OH + RH \rightarrow R^{\bullet} + H_2O \quad (6)$$
The alkyl radical $R^{\bullet}$ rapidly combines with O₂:
$$R^{\bullet} + O_2 \rightarrow RO_2^{\bullet} \quad (7)$$
**The peroxy radical $RO_2^{\bullet}$ is the key intermediate** — it reacts with NO to convert it into NO₂ *without* consuming ozone:
$$RO_2^{\bullet} + NO \rightarrow RO^{\bullet} + NO_2 \quad (8)$$

**Step 5 — Net Ozone Production:**
The NO₂ regenerated in (8) again undergoes photolysis (1) to form O₃. Now, because NO has been "shunted" to NO₂ by the VOC pathway (8) — bypassing the null cycle (3) — ozone **accumulates**. Each $RO_2^{\bullet}$ generated results in the net production of one O₃ molecule. This is the **net photochemical O₃ production cycle**.

**Step 6 — PAN Formation:**
When the VOC is acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO), the sequence is:
$$CH_3CHO + ^{\bullet}OH \rightarrow CH_3CO^{\bullet} + H_2O \quad (9)$$
$$CH_3CO^{\bullet} + O_2 \rightarrow CH_3C(O)OO^{\bullet} \quad (10)$$
$$CH_3C(O)OO^{\bullet} + NO_2 \leftrightarrow CH_3C(O)OONO_2 \quad (\text{PAN})\quad (11)$$

PAN is thermally unstable and decomposes back at higher temperatures, but at the cool conditions of the upper troposphere, it acts as a **reservoir for NOx** that can be transported over long distances.

**Role of VOCs as Rate-Limiting Precursor:**
The rate of net O₃ production is given by:
$$P_{O_3} = k_1 [RO_2^{\bullet}][NO]$$
Since $RO_2^{\bullet}$ is generated only via OH-initiated VOC oxidation, the VOC concentration controls $RO_2^{\bullet}$ concentration. In a **VOC-limited regime** (low VOC/NOx), increasing VOCs increases O₃, while reducing NOx has little effect. In a **NOx-limited regime** (high VOC/NOx), reducing NOx is more effective. This regime dependence is the basis for differentiated O₃ control strategies in cities like Los Angeles (VOC-limited) versus Mexico City (NOx-limited).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (b):** Do **not** list photochemical reactions without stating the role of VOCs. Examiners specifically test whether the student understands *why* the null cycle alone produces no net O₃ and *how* VOCs break the cycle. Also, do not confuse **PAN (peroxyacetyl nitrate)** with **PNA or nitrate** — PAN is a specific organic compound, $CH_3C(O)OONO_2$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Topic-Wise Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Module 3:**
> 1. **Photochemistry:** Writing "ozone is a primary pollutant" or forgetting to mention that **VOCs are needed to break the NO–NO₂–O₃ null cycle**.
> 2. **HVS:** Not converting flow rate and time correctly into the **same units** (e.g., mixing L/min with hours). Always convert to **m³** before computing TSP.
> 3. **Stack Sampler:** Confusing the direction of bias in isokinetic deviation. Memorise: **Super-isokinetic → under-estimate mass; Sub-isokinetic → over-estimate mass.**
> 4. **SLM:** Saying "A-weighting removes low frequencies" — it actually *attenuates* them according to the 40-phon curve; the wording matters.
> 5. **Diesel EGA:** Writing "smoke is measured in ppm" — diesel smoke is **particulate** and is measured in **% opacity** or **Bosch/Hartridge units**, *not* ppm.
> 6. **Decibel arithmetic:** Adding dB values directly (a common error). Always use the **10 log₁₀ (Σ 10^(L/10))** form.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Noise Pollution:** Unwanted sound harmful to humans, measured logarithmically in dB. Reference pressure = 20 μPa. Reference intensity = 10⁻¹² W/m².
- **dB scale:** Pressure-based uses 20 log; intensity/power-based uses 10 log. Always use **log addition**, never arithmetic addition.
- **A-weighting:** Mimics the human ear's 40-phon response. **dB(A)** is the global standard for environmental noise.
- **Leq:** Single number that represents total acoustic energy over a period $T$. Foundation of CPCB noise rules.
- **CPCB Ambient Standards (dB(A)):** Industrial 75/70, Commercial 65/55, Residential 55/45, Silence 50/40 (Day/Night).
- **Photochemical Smog:** A **secondary pollution** phenomenon driven by sunlight, NOx, and VOCs. **O₃** is the main toxicant; **PAN** is a marker; **aldehydes** are irritants.
- **Null Cycle:** NO₂ + hv → NO + O; O + O₂ → O₃; O₃ + NO → NO₂. By itself, this produces *no net O₃*. **VOCs break the cycle** by converting NO to NO₂ via RO₂• radicals.
- **PAN** forms from acetaldehyde (or other aldehydes) + •OH + O₂ + NO₂.
- **High Volume Sampler (HVS):** Gravimetric, 1.1–1.7 m³/min flow, 24-hr sampling, glass-fibre filter. $C = (W_f - W_i)/V$.
- **Petrol EGA:** NDIR for CO, HC, CO₂; chemiluminescent for NOx; electrochemical for O₂. **Lambda** = actual A/F ÷ stoichiometric A/F.
- **Diesel EGA / Opacimeter:** Measures **% opacity** (smoke) in Bosch (0–10) or Hartridge (0–100) scales.
- **Stack Sampler:** Used for industrial emissions, must follow **isokinetic sampling** ($V_{probe} = V_{stack}$). Standards: IS 11255 (Parts 1–3).
- **Sound Level Meter:** Type 1 (±0.7 dB precision) vs Type 2 (±1.5 dB general). Microphone → A-weighting → RMS → Integrator → Display in dB(A).
- **SLM Standards:** IEC 61672 (international), IS 1417, IS 15086 (Indian).
- **A-weighting Reference Curve:** 40-phon Fletcher-Munson equal-loudness contour.
- **NAAQS for TSP (24 hr):** 200 μg/m³ (Industrial, Residential, Rural); 500 μg/m³ (Ecologically Sensitive, with 75 μg/m³ PM₂.₅ cap).
- **Photochemical Smog — Favourable Conditions:** Sunny, hot, stagnant air, topographical basin, high traffic.
- **Isokinetic deviation penalty:** ±20% velocity deviation → significant PM error.
- **PAN reservoir effect:** Stable in cool upper troposphere, allows long-range transport of NOx.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain with a diagram", always draw the block diagram first, then label each component, then explain the working principle in 3–4 sentences. Examiners reward labelled diagrams generously. For photochemistry, the equation sequence (1)→(11) above is the most-tested derivation — practice writing it without looking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Hygiene and In-Plant Safety to Workers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Industrial Hygiene and In-Plant Safety to Workers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Hygiene — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Hygiene (Occupational Hygiene)** is the science and art devoted to the **anticipation, recognition, evaluation, and control** of those environmental factors or stresses arising in or from the workplace that may cause sickness, impaired health and well-being, or significant discomfort and inefficiency among workers.

This is the universally accepted definition given by the **American Industrial Hygiene Association (AIHA)** and the **American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH)**, which is also adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus framework. Industrial hygiene forms the *anticipatory and preventive arm* of occupational health, sitting one layer upstream of clinical medicine.

## 1.2 In-Plant Safety — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **In-Plant Safety** refers to the integrated set of engineering controls, administrative policies, work practices, and personal protective equipment (PPE) deployed *inside the plant boundary* to protect workers from accidental injury, exposure to hazardous agents, fire, explosion, and occupational illness during routine, abnormal, and emergency operations.

The term is often used interchangeably with **Occupational Health and Safety (OHS)** at the operational level, but in the strict KTU air pollution context, it emphasizes the *engineering and ventilation-based interventions* applied at the source of pollutant generation.

## 1.3 The Four Pillars of Industrial Hygiene (Recognition–Evaluation–Control Cycle)

| Pillar | What it does | Practical example |
|---|---|---|
| **Anticipation** | Predict hazards *before* a process starts | Reviewing an MSDS before commissioning a new reactor |
| **Recognition** | Identify the hazard actually present in the workplace | Spotting solvent fumes, noise, or silica dust |
| **Evaluation** | Quantify the hazard — concentration, intensity, frequency | Air sampling, noise dosimetry, TLV comparison |
| **Control** | Eliminate or reduce the hazard to acceptable levels | Local exhaust ventilation, PPE, job rotation |

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Smoke Detector + Mechanic + Doctor" Model:**
> Think of industrial hygiene as a four-stage process for your workplace. *Anticipation* is like a smoke detector — it warns you of danger before it happens. *Recognition* is the mechanic inspecting the wiring. *Evaluation* is the doctor measuring your blood pressure. *Control* is the lifestyle change (medicine + diet + exercise) the doctor prescribes. The workplace, just like a human body, needs all four stages to stay healthy.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics and Organizations

- **TLV = Threshold Limit Value** (set by **ACGIH**) — airborne concentration to which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed without adverse health effects.
- **PEL = Permissible Exposure Limit** (set by **OSHA** — legally enforceable in the USA).
- **REL = Recommended Exposure Limit** (set by **NIOSH**).
- **TWA = 8-hour Time-Weighted Average** (standard reference workday = **8 hours** or **480 minutes**).
- **STEL = Short-Term Exposure Limit** (15-minute time-weighted average, max 4 exposures/day, ≥ 60 min apart).
- **IDLH = Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** TLVs are *not* legal limits — they are *advisory* values. The KTU board may ask you to distinguish between TLV and PEL. Always state that **TLV is advisory (ACGIH)** while **PEL is legally enforceable (OSHA)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — The Recognition → Evaluation → Control Pipeline

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats industrial hygiene as a **sequential, decision-based process**. The reasoning behind each step is explained below:

### Step 1 — Anticipation (Pre-Process Stage)
- Conducted during **process design and plant layout planning**.
- Tools used: **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)**, **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**, **Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS / SDS)**.
- **Why this step?** ~70% of occupational diseases have origins that were *predictable* at the design stage. Anticipation shifts the cost from "treatment" (downstream) to "prevention" (upstream).

### Step 2 — Recognition (Walk-Through Survey)
- **Why?** A hazard not recognized is a hazard not controlled.
- Methods: **Walk-through surveys**, checklists, employee interviews, dust fall observations, odor surveys.
- Categories of stressors recognized:
  - **Chemical** — dusts, fumes, mists, vapors, gases.
  - **Physical** — noise, vibration, radiation (ionizing & non-ionizing), temperature extremes.
  - **Biological** — bacteria, viruses, fungi, bioaerosols.
  - **Ergonomic** — repetitive motion, awkward posture, manual lifting.
  - **Psychosocial** — stress, shift work, monotony.

### Step 3 — Evaluation (Quantitative Measurement)
- **Why?** Recognition alone tells you *what* is there; evaluation tells you *how much* is there and *how dangerous*.
- Equipment: **Air sampling pumps** (high-volume, low-volume), **impingers**, **filter cassettes**, **gas detector tubes**, **sound level meters**, **vibration dosimeters**, **direct-reading instruments (DRIs)**.
- Result is compared to **TLV-TWA, TLV-STEL, TLV-C**.

### Step 4 — Control (Hierarchy of Controls)
The internationally accepted hierarchy, from most effective to least effective:

1. **Elimination** — remove the hazard entirely (best).
2. **Substitution** — replace with a less hazardous material/process.
3. **Engineering Controls** — ventilation, isolation, enclosure, automation.
4. **Administrative Controls** — job rotation, training, work-rest cycles.
5. **PPE** — respirators, ear plugs, gloves, safety goggles (last line of defense).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** When asked to "design a control strategy," the examiner expects the answer in the **decreasing order of effectiveness** (Elimination → PPE). Many students list PPE first and lose marks.

## 2.2 In-Plant Safety — Engineering Control Architecture

In-plant safety relies on three integrated engineering subsystems:

1. **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** — captures contaminants at the source.
2. **General (Dilution) Ventilation** — dilutes contaminants below TLV in the breathing zone.
3. **Process Enclosure & Isolation** — physically separates worker from hazard.

Each is governed by quantitative design equations, given in the formula sheet.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **TLV-TWA (Time-Weighted Average)** | $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i T_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i}$ | $C_i$ in ppm or mg/m³, $T_i$ in hours. Standard workday = **8 h** |
| 2 | **TLV-STEL** | 15-min TWA, max 4 exposures/day, ≥ 60 min apart | Cannot exceed TLV × √(8/0.25) ≈ 5.66 × TLV-TWA in some cases |
| 3 | **TLV-C (Ceiling)** | Instantaneous concentration that must never be exceeded | Single absolute value, no time averaging |
| 4 | **Equivalent TWA for variable exposure** | $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{C_1 T_1 + C_2 T_2 + \cdots + C_n T_n}{8\text{ h}}$ | Used in KTU numerical problems |
| 5 | **Total Hood Flow (LEV)** | $Q = V_c \times A$ | $V_c$ = capture velocity (m/s), $A$ = cross-section of hood (m²), $Q$ in m³/s |
| 6 | **Static Pressure in Duct** | $\Delta P_s = \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{K_e} + f\dfrac{L}{D} + \sum K_m\right)$ | $\rho$ = air density (**1.2 kg/m³** at 20°C), $v$ = duct velocity, $f$ = friction factor |
| 7 | **Capture Velocity $V_c$** (empirical) | $V_c = \dfrac{V_x \cdot 10 x}{10 x + A}$ | $V_x$ = centerline velocity at distance $x$, $A$ = hood face area |
| 8 | **Dilution Ventilation Flow** | $Q_d = \dfrac{G \times 10^6}{K \cdot \text{TLV}}$ | $G$ = contaminant generation rate (g/s), $K$ = mixing factor (typically **5–10**), $Q_d$ in m³/s |
| 9 | **Hood Entry Loss Coefficient** | $K_e = 0.25 \text{ to } 1.78$ (depends on hood type) | Flanged, plain, bell-mouth, tapered |
| 10 | **Noise: Sound Pressure Level Addition** | $L_{T} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | $L_i$ in dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for TWA:** If the sum of exposure times ≠ 8 h, the **denominator must still be 8 h** (because TLV-TWA is normalized to a standard workday). This is a classic KTU examiner trap.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — TWA for Variable-Shift Worker (Classic KTU Numericals)

**Problem Statement (Worked Example):**
A worker is exposed to the following concentrations of toluene during an 8-hour shift:

- 0 to 2 hours: 100 ppm
- 2 to 4 hours: 150 ppm
- 4 to 6 hours: 50 ppm
- 6 to 7 hours: 200 ppm
- 7 to 8 hours: 0 ppm (no exposure — clean room task)

The TLV-TWA for toluene = **50 ppm**. Determine whether the worker's exposure is in compliance, and if not, compute the *equivalent compliance concentration*.

### Step 1 — Tabulate Exposure Data

| Interval $i$ | Duration $T_i$ (h) | Concentration $C_i$ (ppm) | $C_i \times T_i$ (ppm·h) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 100 | 200 |
| 2 | 2 | 150 | 300 |
| 3 | 2 | 50 | 100 |
| 4 | 1 | 200 | 200 |
| 5 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| **Total** | **8** | — | **800** |

### Step 2 — Apply the TWA Formula

$$\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i T_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i}$$

Substituting the summed values:

$$\text{TWA} = \dfrac{200 + 300 + 100 + 200 + 0}{2 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 1} = \dfrac{800 \text{ ppm·h}}{8 \text{ h}}$$

$$\text{TWA} = 100 \text{ ppm}$$

### Step 3 — Compare with TLV-TWA

$$\text{TWA}_{\text{calc}} = 100 \text{ ppm} \quad ; \quad \text{TLV-TWA}_{\text{toluene}} = 50 \text{ ppm}$$

$$\text{Exceedance Factor} = \dfrac{\text{TWA}_{\text{calc}}}{\text{TLV-TWA}} = \dfrac{100}{50} = 2.0$$

> [!WARNING]
> Since exceedance factor > 1, the worker's exposure **violates** the ACGIH recommendation. Engineering or administrative controls are required.

### Step 4 — Compute Equivalent Compliance Concentration

The equivalent concentration $C_{eq}$ that the worker can be exposed to for the *entire* 8 hours without exceeding the TLV:

$$C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 T_1 + C_2 T_2 + \cdots}{8 \text{ h}} = 50 \text{ ppm (per definition of TLV-TWA)}$$

So the worker must never be exposed to more than a constant 50 ppm throughout the 8-h day. Hence peak spikes (e.g., 200 ppm for 1 h) must be brought down via **local exhaust ventilation** at the source.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Capture Velocity for a Flanged Circular Hood

A round flanged hood of diameter $D = 0.30$ m must capture a contaminant generated at a centerline distance $x = 0.50$ m from the hood face. The desired centerline velocity at distance $x$ is $V_x = 0.75$ m/s. The hood face area is:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.30)^2}{4}$$

$$A = \dfrac{0.0707}{4} = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2 \quad (\text{rounded})$$

Using the empirical hood capture-velocity relation:

$$V_c = \dfrac{V_x \cdot 10 x}{10 x + A}$$

Substituting $V_x = 0.75$ m/s, $x = 0.50$ m, $A = 0.0707$ m²:

$$V_c = \dfrac{(0.75)(10 \times 0.50)}{10 \times 0.50 + 0.0707}$$

$$V_c = \dfrac{0.75 \times 5.0}{5.0 + 0.0707} = \dfrac{3.75}{5.0707}$$

$$V_c = 0.7395 \text{ m/s}$$

This is the **required face velocity at the hood opening**. The volumetric flow rate through the hood is then:

$$Q = V_c \times A = 0.7395 \times 0.0707$$

$$Q = 0.0523 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 188.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** A typical **paint spray booth** LEV uses 0.5–1.0 m/s capture velocity; 0.74 m/s is consistent with a **low-velocity, low-toxicity** application such as soldering fumes.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Control Selection

| Measured Hazard Level | Action Triggered | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{\text{measured}} < 0.1 \times \text{TLV}$ | **No action** | Insignificant exposure; routine monitoring only |
| $0.1 \times \text{TLV} \le C_{\text{measured}} < 0.5 \times \text{TLV}$ | **Periodic monitoring + record keeping** | Action level per OSHA; verify trend |
| $0.5 \times \text{TLV} \le C_{\text{measured}} < \text{TLV}$ | **Engineering control evaluation** | Approaching regulatory limit; preventive action |
| $C_{\text{measured}} \ge \text{TLV}$ | **Mandatory engineering controls + PPE** | Statutory violation; immediate intervention required |
| $C_{\text{measured}} \ge \text{STEL or Ceiling}$ | **Stop operation / emergency control** | Acute health effects possible within minutes |
| $C_{\text{measured}} \ge \text{IDLH}$ | **Evacuation + SCBA** | Immediately dangerous to life or health |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Action Level (AL) = 0.5 × TLV-TWA** is a concept borrowed from OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1000 and is sometimes tested as a 3-mark KTU question. Memorize the **five tier thresholds**: 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, STEL, IDLH.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation — Dilution Ventilation Flow Rate

A factory generates toluene vapor at a rate $G = 0.50$ g/s. The TLV-TWA for toluene = 50 ppm ≈ **188 mg/m³** (using MW = 92 g/mol and 24.45 L/mol at 25°C, 1 atm).

The required dilution flow rate is:

$$Q_d = \dfrac{G \times 10^6}{K \cdot \text{TLV}_{\text{mg/m}^3}}$$

With mixing factor $K = 10$ (poor mixing, conservative):

$$Q_d = \dfrac{0.50 \times 10^6}{10 \times 188} = \dfrac{500{,}000}{1880}$$

$$Q_d = 265.96 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 266 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This extremely high flow rate (≈ 9.6 lakh m³/h) shows why **dilution ventilation alone is rarely economical for toxic gases**. This is the engineering justification for preferring **local exhaust ventilation (LEV)** at the source, which is the cornerstone of in-plant safety.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Industrial Hygiene Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Design Stage] --> B{Hazard Anticipation via HAZOP and FMEA}
    B -- Predicted Hazard --> C[Engineering Design Modification]
    B -- No Hazard --> D[Proceed to Commissioning]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Walk Through Survey and Recognition]
    E --> F[Identify Stressor Type]
    F --> G1[Chemical]
    F --> G2[Physical]
    F --> G3[Biological]
    F --> G4[Ergonomic]
    G1 --> H[Quantitative Air Sampling]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Compute TWA and Compare with TLV TWA]
    I --> J{Exceeds TLV?}
    J -- No --> K[Periodic Monitoring every 6 months]
    J -- Yes --> L[Apply Hierarchy of Controls]
    L --> L1[Elimination]
    L1 --> L2[Substitution]
    L2 --> L3[Engineering Controls LEV]
    L3 --> L4[Administrative Controls]
    L4 --> L5[PPE as Last Resort]
    L5 --> M[Medical Surveillance and Record Keeping]
    K --> M
```

## 4.2 In-Plant Safety — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOURCE_LAYER
        A1[Process Equipment Reactor, Mixer, Spray Booth]
    end
    subgraph CAPTURE_LAYER
        B1[Local Exhaust Hood]
        B2[Ductwork with Static Pressure Losses]
    end
    subgraph TREATMENT_LAYER
        C1[Cyclone Separator for Particulates]
        C2[Wet Scrubber for Gases]
        C3[Activated Carbon Adsorber for VOCs]
        C4[HEPA Filter for Fine Dust]
    end
    subgraph MONITORING_LAYER
        D1[Stack Emission Monitor]
        D2[In Plant Air Quality Sensor]
        D3[Personal Exposure Monitor on Worker]
    end
    subgraph PROTECTION_LAYER
        E1[PPE Respirator, Gloves, Goggles]
        E2[Emergency Eyewash and Shower]
        E3[SCBA for IDLH Zones]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B2 --> C3
    B2 --> C4
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1
    D3 --> E2
    D3 --> E3
```

## 4.3 Control Strategy Selection Matrix (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Contaminant Identified] --> Q1{Source Eliminated by Process Change?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> DONE1[Eliminate Source]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Less Hazardous Substitute Available?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> DONE2[Substitution]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{High Toxicity or Carcinogen?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> LEV[Total Enclosure and LEV with Backup PPE]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Worker Near Source?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> LEV
    Q4 -- No --> DIL[General Dilution Ventilation]
    LEV --> ADM[Administrative Controls Shift Rotation Training]
    DIL --> ADM
    ADM --> PPE[PPE as Final Line of Defense]
    PPE --> REC[Health Surveillance and Medical Records]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams for the exam:** The arrows show the *direction of contaminant flow* (left to right) and the *logical decision flow* (top to bottom). During the 14-mark KTU question, the examiner will reward candidates who can verbally walk through this flow while explaining the design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define Threshold Limit Value (TLV). Differentiate between TLV-TWA, TLV-STEL, and TLV-Ceiling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**TLV Definition (1 mark):**
TLV refers to airborne concentrations of substances under which it is believed that **nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed, day after day, without adverse health effects**. TLVs are advisory values developed by the **American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH)**.

**Differences (2 marks):**

| Type | Full Form | Averaging Period | Key Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TLV-TWA** | Time-Weighted Average | **8 h** workday | Standard daily exposure metric |
| **TLV-STEL** | Short-Term Exposure Limit | **15 min** | Max 4 per day, separated by ≥ 60 min |
| **TLV-C** | Ceiling Limit | **Instantaneous** | Must never be exceeded at any moment |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook:** TWA = "**T**otal **W**orkday **A**verage", STEL = "**S**hort **T**erm **E**xcursion **L**imit", C = "**C**eiling (no time averaging)".

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] State and explain the Hierarchy of Controls used in industrial hygiene. Why is PPE considered the last line of defense?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Hierarchy of Controls** is the internationally accepted priority order for controlling occupational hazards, arranged from **most effective to least effective** (1 mark):

1. **Elimination** — physically remove the hazard.
2. **Substitution** — replace with a less hazardous material or process.
3. **Engineering Controls** — ventilation, isolation, automation.
4. **Administrative Controls** — job rotation, training, work schedules.
5. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — respirators, gloves, ear plugs.

**PPE is the last line of defense (2 marks):** PPE depends on **worker compliance, correct fit, and proper maintenance**. Unlike engineering controls, PPE **does not eliminate the hazard**; it merely places a *barrier* between the worker and the hazard. If the respirator is damaged, ill-fitting, or not worn, exposure continues unabated. Engineering controls (e.g., local exhaust ventilation) operate continuously and independently of human behavior, making them inherently more reliable and effective.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and contains **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. The student must answer **either OR** the alternative. Both alternatives are given below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.A (a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024] A worker in a pesticide formulation plant is exposed to methyl isocyanate (MIC) under the following shift pattern:**

| Time | 08:00–10:00 | 10:00–12:00 | 12:00–13:00 | 13:00–15:00 | 15:00–17:00 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| MIC (ppm) | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0 (lunch break) | 0.08 | 0.06 |

**The TLV-TWA for MIC = 0.02 ppm. Compute the 8-h TWA and comment on the worker's exposure status. Suggest one engineering and one administrative control measure.**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate (1 mark):**

| Interval | $T_i$ (h) | $C_i$ (ppm) | $C_i T_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 08:00–10:00 | 2 | 0.05 | 0.10 |
| 10:00–12:00 | 2 | 0.10 | 0.20 |
| 12:00–13:00 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 13:00–15:00 | 2 | 0.08 | 0.16 |
| 15:00–17:00 | 2 | 0.06 | 0.12 |
| **Sum** | **9 h** | — | **0.58** |

**Step 2 — Apply TWA formula with 8 h denominator (2 marks):**

$$\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum C_i T_i}{8 \text{ h}} = \dfrac{0.58}{8} = 0.0725 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with TLV-TWA (1 mark):**

$$\text{Exceedance} = \dfrac{0.0725}{0.02} = 3.625$$

The TWA exceeds the TLV by a factor of **3.6** — the worker's exposure is **grossly non-compliant**.

**Step 4 — Engineering and Administrative Controls (3 marks):**

- **Engineering Control:** Install a **closed-system MIC transfer and metering unit** with local exhaust ventilation and a **real-time MIC sensor with automatic shutoff**. The hood should be designed for a **capture velocity of 0.5–1.0 m/s** at the transfer point.
- **Administrative Control:** **Reduce shift duration** to 4 h/day in MIC zones, enforce **medical surveillance** (lung function tests, blood cyanide levels) every 3 months, and provide **SCBA training** for emergency response.

---

#### **Q.A (b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024] With a neat sketch, describe the components and working of a Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) system for a welding shop. State the design capture velocity range and the role of each component.**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (2 marks):** A LEV system **captures the contaminant at the source** before it spreads into the breathing zone, transports it through ducts, removes it via air-cleaning devices, and discharges the cleaned air to the atmosphere.

**Components (4 marks):**

1. **Hood** — The capture device placed near the welding arc. A **flanged slot hood** or **movable fume extractor arm** is typical.
2. **Ductwork** — Transports contaminated air from hood to cleaner. Designed for **duct velocity = 15–20 m/s** for welding fumes to prevent particle settling.
3. **Air-cleaning device** — A **dry filter cartridge** or **electrostatic precipitator (ESP)** to capture metal fumes and fine particulates.
4. **Fan (Blower)** — Provides motive power; sized using the **total static pressure equation**.
5. **Stack** — Discharges cleaned air at a height satisfying **EPA stack height guidelines** (typically ≥ 1.5× the building height).

**Design Capture Velocity (1 mark):**
- For welding fumes: $V_c = \mathbf{0.5 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ m/s}}$ (low-velocity thermal updraft applications).

**Schematic (description only, since not all components are listed as a figure here):** Hood → Duct → Filter → Fan → Stack, with a by-pass damper for flow balancing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Welding LEV:** Students often quote capture velocity = 10 m/s for welding, confusing it with **duct transport velocity**. The **capture velocity** (at the hood face) is 0.5–1.0 m/s; the **transport velocity** (inside the duct) is 15–20 m/s. Conflating the two is a **2-mark deduction** in the KTU valuation key.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q.B (a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Explain in detail the "Recognition" stage of industrial hygiene. List the categories of workplace stressors and give two examples of each.**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Recognition — Definition (1 mark):** Recognition is the *qualitative identification* of an occupational hazard present in the workplace, performed through **walk-through surveys, checklists, employee interviews, and review of process flow diagrams**.

**Categories of Workplace Stressors (6 marks):**

| # | Category | Definition | Two Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Chemical** | Airborne dusts, fumes, vapors, gases, mists | (i) Silica dust in quarrying, (ii) Toluene vapor in paint shops |
| 2 | **Physical** | Energy forms that can harm the body | (i) Noise from crushers, (ii) UV radiation from welding arc |
| 3 | **Biological** | Living organisms or their by-products | (i) Legionella in cooling towers, (ii) Mold spores in food processing |
| 4 | **Ergonomic** | Workstation design and posture-related stressors | (i) Repetitive motion in assembly line, (ii) Heavy manual lifting |
| 5 | **Psychosocial** | Mental and emotional stressors | (i) Shift work sleep disorder, (ii) Workplace bullying / high-stress deadlines |

**Note for KTU 2024:** The 5-category classification (chemical, physical, biological, ergonomic, psychosocial) is the ACGIH/National Safety Council taxonomy. Some textbooks use a 4-category version (excluding psychosocial), but the 5-category version is preferred in NEP 2020-aligned syllabi.

---

#### **Q.B (b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] A grinding shop uses a circular flanged hood of diameter 0.40 m to capture metal dust at a centerline distance of 0.60 m. The required centerline velocity at that distance is 1.20 m/s. Compute (i) the hood face capture velocity, (ii) the volumetric flow rate, and (iii) the minimum duct transport velocity to keep the dust airborne.**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
- $D = 0.40$ m, $x = 0.60$ m, $V_x = 1.20$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Hood Face Area (1 mark):**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.40)^2}{4} = 0.1257 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Capture Velocity (2 marks):**

$$V_c = \dfrac{V_x \cdot 10 x}{10 x + A} = \dfrac{1.20 \times 10 \times 0.60}{10 \times 0.60 + 0.1257}$$

$$V_c = \dfrac{7.20}{6.1257} = 1.175 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric Flow Rate (1 mark):**

$$Q = V_c \times A = 1.175 \times 0.1257 = 0.1477 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q = 0.1477 \times 3600 = 531.7 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Duct Transport Velocity (2 marks):** For metal dust, the **minimum transport velocity** must prevent particle settling. From standard LEV design tables:
- For **grinding dust**: $V_{\text{duct, min}} = \mathbf{20 \text{ m/s}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** Duct transport velocity > particle settling velocity, otherwise the duct will clog. The "rule of thumb" minimum transport velocities for various dusts:
> - **Light dry dusts** (cotton, wood): 15–18 m/s
> - **Welding fumes**: 15–20 m/s
> - **Metallic grinding dust**: 20–25 m/s
> - **Heavy moist dusts**: 25 m/s

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Loss of Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting to use 8 h as the denominator** in TWA even when total exposure time ≠ 8 h. This is the **#1 mistake** in 7-mark numerics.
> 2. **Confusing capture velocity (at hood face) with transport velocity (in duct)** — these are two different quantities used for two different purposes.
> 3. **Listing PPE first in the Hierarchy of Controls** — PPE is the *last* line of defense, not the first.
> 4. **Quoting TLV as a legal limit** — TLV is *advisory* (ACGIH), not legally enforceable. The legally enforceable limit in the US is **PEL (OSHA)**.
> 5. **Mixing up IDLH with TLV** — IDLH is for respiratory protection selection, TLV is for routine exposure.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Industrial Hygiene** = Anticipation + Recognition + Evaluation + Control (the 4-pillar cycle).
- [ ] **In-Plant Safety** = Engineering + Administrative + PPE controls, applied *at source*.
- [ ] **TLV-TWA** is normalized to **8 h**; **TLV-STEL** to **15 min**; **TLV-C** is instantaneous.
- [ ] **TLV** (ACGIH) is **advisory**; **PEL** (OSHA) is **legally enforceable**.
- [ ] **Hierarchy of Controls** in strict order: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE**.
- [ ] **PPE is the LAST line of defense** — never the first.
- [ ] **TWA formula:** $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum C_i T_i}{8 \text{ h}}$ (denominator is ALWAYS 8 h, even if exposure time is less).
- [ ] **Capture velocity (m/s)** ≠ **Transport velocity (m/s)** — hood face vs inside duct.
- [ ] **Dilution ventilation** is **uneconomical** for highly toxic gases — prefer **LEV** at source.
- [ ] **Action Level (AL) = 0.5 × TLV** triggers engineering control evaluation (OSHA concept).
- [ ] **5 categories of stressors**: Chemical, Physical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial.
- [ ] **Standard air density** for duct design: $\rho = \mathbf{1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ at 20°C.
- [ ] **Minimum duct transport velocity**: 15–20 m/s (welding), 20–25 m/s (grinding), ≥ 25 m/s (heavy moist dust).
- [ ] **IDLH** triggers **SCBA** use; **STEL exceedance** triggers emergency control.
- [ ] **HAZOP and FMEA** are the two most important **anticipation** tools.
- [ ] **Personal Exposure Monitor** is the only direct way to assess what a worker actually breathes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ventilation and Indoor Air Quality Control - An Overview of Indoor Air Quality, Basics of HVAC Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ventilation and Indoor Air Quality Control - An Overview of Indoor Air Quality, Basics of HVAC Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Indoor Air Quality (IAQ) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)** is defined as the quality of air inside and around buildings and structures, especially as it relates to the health and comfort of building occupants. It is quantified by the concentration of pollutants, thermal comfort parameters (temperature, humidity), and the effectiveness of ventilation in diluting and removing contaminants.

According to the **American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE) Standard 62.1**, IAQ is formally expressed as *"air in which there are no known contaminants at harmful concentrations as determined by cognizant authorities and with which a substantial majority (≥80%) of the people exposed do not express dissatisfaction."*

### Key Parameters Defining IAQ
- **Particulate Matter (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅)** concentrations
- **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** such as formaldehyde, benzene
- **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** as a tracer gas for ventilation adequacy
- **Bioaerosols** — bacteria, fungi, viruses, pollen
- **Thermal comfort** — dry-bulb temperature, relative humidity, air velocity
- **Radon (²²²Rn)** — radioactive pollutant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A CO₂ concentration of **≤ 700 ppm above outdoor air** is the ASHRE-recommended threshold indicating adequate ventilation in occupied spaces.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lungs of a Building"

Think of a building as a **living organism**:
- The **walls and roof** are its "skin" that traps air inside.
- The **HVAC system** acts as the building's **lungs** — constantly inhaling fresh outdoor air and exhaling stale, contaminated indoor air.
- The **occupants, equipment, and furnishings** are like **metabolic processes**, continuously generating "pollutants" (CO₂, VOCs, heat, moisture).

If the lungs (HVAC) are weak, pollutants accumulate — analogous to how a person feels sick in a poorly ventilated room. The term **Sick Building Syndrome (SBS)** is literally used by environmental engineers to describe buildings that fail to "breathe" properly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sick Building Syndrome (SBS):** A condition in which occupants experience acute health/comfort effects that appear linked to time spent inside a building, but no specific illness or cause can be identified. Building-related illnesses (BRIs), in contrast, have diagnosable symptoms with identified causes (e.g., Legionnaires' disease, asbestos exposure).

---

## 1.3 Sources of Indoor Air Pollutants

| Source Category | Examples | Primary Pollutants |
|---|---|---|
| **Outdoor sources** | Vehicle exhaust, industrial emissions, pollen | PM, SO₂, NOₓ, O₃ |
| **Building materials** | Plywood, adhesives, paints, insulation | Formaldehyde, VOCs, asbestos fibers |
| **Occupant activities** | Smoking, cooking, cleaning, printing | CO, CO₂, PM, VOCs |
| **HVAC systems** | Moldy ducts, dirty filters, humidifiers | Bioaerosols, dust, microbes |
| **Office equipment** | Printers, copiers, computers | Ozone, toner particles, VOCs |
| **Combustion appliances** | Gas stoves, heaters, fireplaces | CO, NO₂, particulate matter |
| **Soil & groundwater** | Underground intrusion | Radon, VOCs, sewer gas |
| **Bioaerosols** | Pets, pests, humans | Bacteria, fungi, dander |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** According to the **U.S. EPA**, indoor air pollutant levels are typically **2 to 5 times higher** than outdoor levels. In some cases (e.g., during cooking or smoking), indoor concentrations can exceed outdoor levels by **100×**.

---

## 1.4 HVAC — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning)** is the integrated engineering system used to provide thermal comfort and acceptable indoor air quality by controlling the temperature, humidity, airflow, and air cleanliness of an enclosed space.

The three primary functions are:
1. **Heating** — Raising indoor air temperature during cold conditions.
2. **Ventilation** — Supplying fresh outdoor air and removing stale indoor air.
3. **Air Conditioning** — Cooling, dehumidifying, filtering, and circulating air.

A fourth implicit function is **air cleaning / filtration**, which has gained prominence in the post-COVID-19 era.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ventilation — The Core Mechanism for IAQ Control

Ventilation is the **intentional introduction of outdoor air** into a space to dilute and remove indoor-generated pollutants while maintaining thermal comfort. It is the single most important engineering control for IAQ.

### 2.1.1 Types of Ventilation

**(a) Natural Ventilation**
- Driven by **wind pressure** and **stack effect (buoyancy)**.
- Depends on openings such as windows, doors, vents, chimneys.
- No mechanical energy input.
- **Limitation:** Cannot be reliably controlled; highly weather-dependent.

**(b) Mechanical Ventilation**
- Uses **fans, ducts, blowers** to force air movement.
- Provides **controlled, predictable air exchange rates**.
- Enables **filtration, heating, cooling, and humidity control** before air is supplied.

**(c) Hybrid (Mixed-Mode) Ventilation**
- Combines natural and mechanical systems.
- Operates naturally when conditions permit; switches to mechanical when outdoor conditions are unfavorable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** *Infiltration* refers to uncontrolled air leakage through cracks and gaps; *exfiltration* is the reverse. These are NOT considered ventilation by ASHRAE standards since they are not intentional.

---

## 2.2 Ventilation Metrics — The Quantitative Backbone

The performance of a ventilation system is quantified by three key metrics:

### 2.2.1 Air Changes Per Hour (ACH)

$$\text{ACH} = \frac{Q}{V}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate of supply (or exhaust) air, in **m³/hr** or **ft³/min (CFM)**
- $V$ = volume of the room/space, in **m³** or **ft³**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Reference Values (from ASHRAE 62.1):**
> - **Offices:** 4–6 ACH
> - **Classrooms:** 6–8 ACH
> - **Hospitals (operating rooms):** 15–25 ACH
> - **Cleanrooms:** 100–600 ACH
> - **Residential (minimum):** 0.35–1.0 ACH

### 2.2.2 Ventilation Effectiveness ($\varepsilon_v$)

This describes how efficiently outdoor air reaches the breathing zone.

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{C_{in,exhaust} - C_{outdoor}}{C_{breathing\,zone} - C_{outdoor}}$$

Where $C$ denotes the concentration of a tracer gas (often CO₂ or SF₆). An ideal system has $\varepsilon_v = 1.0$.

### 2.2.3 Outdoor Air Fraction ($Z$)

The fraction of supply air that is outdoor air:

$$Z = \frac{C_{return} - C_{supply}}{C_{return} - C_{outdoor}}$$

Where $C$ refers to a contaminant concentration. For a 100% outdoor air system, $Z = 1$.

---

## 2.3 IAQ Mass Balance — The Steady-State Equation

For a well-mixed room with a contaminant being generated at rate $G$ (mg/s) and removed by ventilation at flow rate $Q$ (m³/s):

$$V \frac{dC}{dt} = G - Q(C - C_{out})$$

At **steady state** ($\frac{dC}{dt} = 0$):

$$C_{ss} = C_{out} + \frac{G}{Q}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equation is the **single most-tested formula in KTU exams** for IAQ/ventilation problems. The pollutant concentration is *inversely proportional* to the ventilation rate — a 2× increase in $Q$ halves the indoor excess concentration.

### 2.3.1 Time to Reach Steady State

The characteristic time constant is $\tau = \frac{V}{Q}$ (seconds). At time $t = \tau$, the concentration reaches $\approx 63.2\%$ of its steady-state value; at $t = 4.6\tau$, it is effectively at steady state.

$$C(t) = C_{out} + \frac{G}{Q}\left(1 - e^{-Qt/V}\right)$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{ACH} = \dfrac{Q}{V}$ | Air changes per hour | hr⁻¹ | Ventilation design |
| $C_{ss} = C_{out} + \dfrac{G}{Q}$ | Steady-state indoor pollutant concentration | mg/m³ | IAQ problems |
| $C(t) = C_{out} + \dfrac{G}{Q}\left(1 - e^{-Qt/V}\right)$ | Transient indoor concentration | mg/m³ | Time-dependent buildup |
| $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Ventilation time constant | seconds | Response time |
| $Q_{req} = \dfrac{G}{C_{limit} - C_{out}}$ | Required ventilation flow for compliance | m³/s | Design calculations |
| $Z = \dfrac{C_{r} - C_{s}}{C_{r} - C_{o}}$ | Outdoor air fraction | dimensionless | Recirculation systems |
| $\varepsilon_v$ | Ventilation effectiveness | dimensionless | Air distribution design |
| $h_s = 1.005T + 2500 \cdot W$ | Moist air enthalpy (kJ/kg dry air) | kJ/kg | HVAC psychrometrics |
| $\dot{Q}_{heat} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat load | W or kW | Heating/cooling load |
| $\dot{m}_{latent} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{latent}}{h_{fg}}$ | Latent (moisture) removal | kg/s | Dehumidification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** Standard temperature for HVAC calculations is **ASHRAE Standard**: **20°C (68°F)** indoor in winter, **24°C (75°F)** indoor in summer. Standard pressure: **101.325 kPa (1 atm)**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mass balance equation drives several real engineering decisions:
- **Designing cleanroom ventilation** for semiconductor fabs (requires ACH of several hundred).
- **Sizing exhaust hoods** in chemical laboratories to keep VOC concentrations below TLV (Threshold Limit Value).
- **Estimating required outdoor air** in commercial buildings to maintain CO₂ below 1000 ppm.
- **Indoor radon mitigation** via sub-slab depressurization systems.
- **Hospital infection control** — designing isolation rooms with negative pressure and ≥12 ACH.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Indoor Pollutant Concentration

### Problem Setup
A small office of volume $V = 150$ m³ has an occupant generating CO₂ at a rate $G = 0.005$ L/s. The outdoor CO₂ concentration is $C_{out} = 400$ ppm, and the ventilation system supplies fresh air at $Q = 0.05$ m³/s. Find the steady-state indoor CO₂ concentration.

### Step 1: Write the General Mass Balance
The general mass balance for a well-mixed room is:

$$V \frac{dC}{dt} = G + Q \cdot C_{out} - Q \cdot C$$

The terms represent:
- Accumulation in the room
- Generation inside the room
- Inflow of pollutant from outdoor air
- Outflow of pollutant from indoor air

### Step 2: Apply the Steady-State Condition
At steady state, $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = 0$:

$$0 = G + Q \cdot C_{out} - Q \cdot C_{ss}$$

### Step 3: Solve for $C_{ss}$
Rearranging:

$$Q \cdot C_{ss} = G + Q \cdot C_{out}$$

$$C_{ss} = C_{out} + \frac{G}{Q}$$

### Step 4: Substitute Numerical Values

$$C_{ss} = 400 + \frac{0.005}{0.05}$$

$$C_{ss} = 400 + 0.10$$

$$C_{ss} = 400.1 \text{ ppm}$$

### Step 5: Interpretation
The indoor CO₂ is only marginally above outdoor — indicating **adequate ventilation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Practice:** In KTU-style problems, $G$ is often given in different units (mg/min, L/hr, g/s). Always convert $G$ and $C$ to **consistent units** before applying the formula.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Ventilation Flow Rate

### Problem Setup
A laboratory has a formaldehyde (HCHO) source emitting $G = 2$ mg/s. The OSHA permissible exposure limit is $C_{limit} = 0.75$ ppm. The outdoor air concentration is negligible ($C_{out} \approx 0$). Find the required ventilation rate $Q$ in m³/s.

### Step 1: Convert Units
- $C_{limit}$ in mg/m³: Using molar mass of HCHO = 30 g/mol and 1 ppm = 1 mL/m³ at STP:

$$C_{limit} = 0.75 \times \frac{30}{24.45} = 0.92 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

(using 24.45 L/mol as molar volume at 25°C, 1 atm)

### Step 2: Apply Steady-State Equation
Setting $C_{ss} = C_{limit}$:

$$C_{limit} = C_{out} + \frac{G}{Q}$$

$$0.92 = 0 + \frac{2}{Q}$$

### Step 3: Solve for $Q$

$$Q = \frac{2}{0.92}$$

$$Q = 2.17 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 4: Convert to Common Ventilation Units
- $Q = 2.17 \times 3600 = 7812$ m³/hr
- $Q = 2.17 \times 2118.88 = 4598$ CFM (cubic feet per minute)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert $G$ and $C$ to consistent units. **Always work in SI (mg/m³ and m³/s) for KTU exam problems**, then convert to the requested final unit.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transient Buildup of a Pollutant

### Problem Setup
A closed room (no ventilation initially) of volume $V = 50$ m³ contains a person generating CO₂ at $G = 0.004$ L/s. The room is suddenly ventilated at $Q = 0.025$ m³/s with outdoor air at $C_{out} = 400$ ppm. Find the CO₂ concentration after 30 minutes.

### Step 1: Governing Equation
The general unsteady-state mass balance:

$$V \frac{dC}{dt} = G + Q(C_{out} - C)$$

### Step 2: Rearrange to Standard Form

$$\frac{dC}{dt} + \frac{Q}{V}C = \frac{G + Q \cdot C_{out}}{V}$$

### Step 3: Solve the Linear ODE
The integrating factor is $e^{(Q/V)t}$. The solution (assuming $C(0) = C_{out}$):

$$C(t) = C_{out} + \frac{G}{Q}\left(1 - e^{-Qt/V}\right)$$

### Step 4: Compute Time Constant

$$\tau = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{50}{0.025} = 2000 \text{ s} = 33.33 \text{ min}$$

### Step 5: Substitute $t = 30$ min = 1800 s

$$C(1800) = 400 + \frac{0.004}{0.025}\left(1 - e^{-1800/2000}\right)$$

$$C(1800) = 400 + 0.16 \times \left(1 - e^{-0.9}\right)$$

$$C(1800) = 400 + 0.16 \times (1 - 0.4066)$$

$$C(1800) = 400 + 0.16 \times 0.5934$$

$$C(1800) = 400 + 0.095$$

$$C(1800) \approx 400.1 \text{ ppm}$$

### Step 6: Compare to Steady State

$$C_{ss} = 400 + \frac{0.004}{0.025} = 400.16 \text{ ppm}$$

The room has reached ~59% of steady-state, consistent with $t/\tau = 1800/2000 = 0.9$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ACH Standards by Space Type

| Space Type | Recommended ACH (ASHRAE 62.1) | Pressure Relationship | Filtration Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Office | 4–6 | Neutral | MERV 8–13 |
| Classroom | 6–8 | Neutral | MERV 8–13 |
| Hospital corridor | 2–4 | Neutral | MERV 13 |
| Patient isolation room | 12–15 | **Negative** | MERV 14 + HEPA |
| Operating room | 15–25 | **Positive** | MERV 14 + HEPA |
| Cleanroom (Class 100) | 100–600 | **Positive** | ULPA |
| Residential | 0.35–1.0 | Neutral | MERV 6–8 |
| Smoking lounge | 15–30 | **Negative** | MERV 14 |
| Industrial paint booth | 100–200 | **Negative** | MERV 14 |
| Laboratory (chemical) | 8–12 | **Negative** | MERV 14 + carbon |

> [!NOTE]
> **MERV** = Minimum Efficiency Reporting Value (ASHRAE 52.2). **HEPA** = High-Efficiency Particulate Air filter (≥99.97% at 0.3 μm). **ULPA** = Ultra-Low Penetration Air filter (≥99.999% at 0.12 μm).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IAQ Control Hierarchy — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Outdoor Air] --> B[Air Intake]
    B --> C[Pre-Filter MERV 8]
    C --> D[Heating or Cooling Coil]
    D --> E[Humidifier or Dehumidifier]
    E --> F[Fine Filter MERV 13 to 14]
    F --> G[HEPA or Carbon Filter Optional]
    G --> H[Supply Fan]
    H --> I[Supply Ductwork]
    I --> J[Diffusers to Occupied Zone]
    J --> K[Indoor Space Occupants Equipment Materials]
    K --> L[Return Air Grille]
    L --> M[Return Ductwork]
    M --> N{Recirculation Decision}
    N -->|Yes| O[Mix with Outdoor Air]
    N -->|No| P[Exhaust to Outside]
    O --> B
    P --> Q[Exhaust Fan]
    Q --> R[Outdoor Air Polluted]
    K --> S[CO2 VOCs PM Bioaerosols Radon]
    S --> T[IAQ Monitoring Sensors]
    T --> U[Building Management System BMS]
    U --> N
    U --> H
    U --> Q
```

## 4.2 HVAC System Classification — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HVAC[HVAC System Classification] --> A1[Centralized Systems]
    HVAC --> A2[Decentralized Systems]
    HVAC --> A3[Hybrid Systems]
    A1 --> B1[Single Duct CAV]
    A1 --> B2[Single Duct VAV]
    A1 --> B3[Dual Duct]
    A1 --> B4[Multizone]
    A2 --> C1[Unitary AC Units]
    A2 --> C2[Split Systems]
    A2 --> C3[Window Units]
    A2 --> C4[PTAC Units]
    A3 --> D1[Chilled Beam Systems]
    A3 --> D2[Radiant Floor Cooling]
    A3 --> D3[Variable Refrigerant Flow VRF]
    B1 --> E1[Constant Air Volume]
    B2 --> E2[Variable Air Volume Energy Efficient]
    D3 --> E3[Inverter Driven Heat Pump]
```

## 4.3 IAQ Assessment & Control Flow — Process Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1[Step 1 Identify Pollutant Sources] --> ST2[Step 2 Quantify Generation Rates]
    ST2 --> ST3[Step 3 Define Exposure Limits]
    ST3 --> ST4[Step 4 Calculate Ventilation Requirements]
    ST4 --> ST5[Step 5 Select HVAC System Type]
    ST5 --> ST6[Step 6 Design Air Distribution Layout]
    ST6 --> ST7[Step 7 Specify Filtration Levels]
    ST7 --> ST8[Step 8 Install Monitoring Sensors]
    ST8 --> ST9[Step 9 Commissioning and Testing]
    ST9 --> ST10[Step 10 Operations and Maintenance]
    ST10 -->|Periodic| ST1
    ST9 -->|Validation| ST11[Compliance with ASHRAE 62.1]
    ST11 -->|Yes| ST12[Occupied Building]
    ST11 -->|No| ST4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** The IAQ/HVAC system operates as a **closed-loop control system** similar to a process control loop. The Building Management System (BMS) continuously receives feedback from CO₂, temperature, humidity, and PM sensors, and modulates supply/exhaust fan speeds, damper positions, and cooling/heating coil valves — analogous to a PID controller maintaining a setpoint.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Indoor Air Quality (IAQ). List any four major categories of indoor air pollutants.**

**Model Answer:**

**IAQ Definition:** Indoor Air Quality refers to the air quality within and around buildings and structures, especially as it relates to the health, comfort, and well-being of building occupants. It is determined by the concentrations of various pollutants, thermal parameters, and ventilation effectiveness, as codified in **ASHRAE Standard 62.1**.

**Four Major Categories of Indoor Air Pollutants:**

1. **Particulate Matter (PM)** — fine particles (PM₂.₅, PM₁₀) from smoking, cooking, dust.
2. **Gaseous Pollutants** — CO, CO₂, NO₂, O₃, SO₂ from combustion and infiltration.
3. **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** — formaldehyde, benzene, toluene from paints, adhesives, furnishings.
4. **Biological Pollutants (Bioaerosols)** — bacteria, fungi, viruses, pollen, pet dander.
5. **Radon (²²²Rn)** — radioactive gas from soil intrusion.
6. **Asbestos fibers** — from old insulation and ceiling tiles.

*(Any four accepted)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is Sick Building Syndrome (SBS)? How does it differ from Building-Related Illness (BRI)?**

**Model Answer:**

**Sick Building Syndrome (SBS):** A condition in which occupants of a building experience acute health and comfort effects (e.g., headaches, eye/nose/throat irritation, fatigue, dizziness) that appear to be linked to time spent inside the building, but **no specific cause or diagnosable illness** can be identified.

**Building-Related Illness (BRI):** An illness with **identifiable symptoms, diagnosable causes, and a known etiology**, traceable to airborne building contaminants. Examples include Legionnaires' disease (from *Legionella* in cooling towers), hypersensitivity pneumonitis, and asbestos-related diseases.

| Feature | SBS | BRI |
|---|---|---|
| Identifiable cause | No | Yes |
| Symptoms resolve on leaving building | Yes | Often persist |
| Diagnostic test | None specific | Specific (e.g., blood test) |
| Example | General office fatigue | Legionnaires' disease |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — 7 Marks
**Explain the major sources of indoor air pollution in residential and commercial buildings. Discuss the health effects of formaldehyde exposure in detail.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Sources of Indoor Air Pollution in Residential Buildings:**

1. **Combustion sources** — gas stoves, wood-burning fireplaces, kerosene heaters → release CO, NO₂, PM.
2. **Building materials** — pressed wood products, plywood, particleboard → emit formaldehyde.
3. **Furnishings and fabrics** — carpets, upholstery, drapes → emit VOCs.
4. **Cleaning products and pesticides** — release ammonia, bleach, chlorinated compounds.
5. **Occupant activities** — cooking, smoking, personal care products.
6. **Soil gas intrusion** — radon, VOCs from contaminated soil.
7. **Bioaerosols** — pets, mold in damp areas, pests.

**(ii) Sources in Commercial Buildings:**

1. **Office equipment** — printers, copiers (ozone, toner particles).
2. **Adhesives and sealants** — used in modern construction.
3. **HVAC systems** — microbial growth in ducts, dirty filters.
4. **Personal care products** — perfumes, deodorants from occupants.
5. **Environmental tobacco smoke (ETS)**.

**(iii) Health Effects of Formaldehyde (HCHO):**

- **Concentration threshold:** OSHA PEL = 0.75 ppm (8-hr TWA); NIOSH REL = 0.016 ppm.
- **Acute effects:** Eye, nose, and throat irritation; coughing; headaches.
- **Chronic effects:** Allergic contact dermatitis, asthma exacerbation, respiratory sensitization.
- **Long-term exposure:** Classified as a **Group 1 human carcinogen** by the **IARC (International Agency for Research on Cancer)** — linked to nasopharyngeal cancer and leukemia.
- **Sensory perception:** Smell detected as low as 0.06–0.5 ppm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse **OSHA PEL**, **NIOSH REL**, and **ACGIH TLV**. Remember: **PEL = legally enforceable; REL = NIOSH recommended; TLV = ACGIH recommended.** KTU typically expects OSHA values for problems.

---

#### Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — 7 Marks
**Derive the steady-state mass balance equation for a pollutant in a well-mixed indoor space. A laboratory has dimensions 10 m × 8 m × 4 m. A chemical process releases formaldehyde at 1.2 mg/s. The outdoor concentration is 0.02 mg/m³. The recommended indoor limit is 0.1 mg/m³. Calculate the minimum ventilation rate required.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Consider a well-mixed room of volume $V$ (m³) with a contaminant generated at rate $G$ (mg/s) and removed by ventilation air at rate $Q$ (m³/s).

**Mass balance:**

$$V \frac{dC}{dt} = G + Q \cdot C_{out} - Q \cdot C$$

**At steady state** ($\frac{dC}{dt} = 0$):

$$0 = G + Q \cdot C_{out} - Q \cdot C_{ss}$$

**Solving for $C_{ss}$:**

$$C_{ss} = C_{out} + \frac{G}{Q}$$

**Solving for $Q$:**

$$Q = \frac{G}{C_{ss} - C_{out}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

- Room volume: $V = 10 \times 8 \times 4 = 320$ m³
- Generation rate: $G = 1.2$ mg/s
- Outdoor concentration: $C_{out} = 0.02$ mg/m³
- Limit: $C_{limit} = 0.1$ mg/m³

**Apply the formula:**

$$Q = \frac{1.2}{0.1 - 0.02} = \frac{1.2}{0.08}$$

$$Q = 15 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Convert to ACH:**

$$\text{ACH} = \frac{Q \times 3600}{V} = \frac{15 \times 3600}{320} = 168.75 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

**Convert to CFM:**

$$Q = 15 \times 2118.88 = 31{,}783 \text{ CFM}$$

**Answer:** Minimum ventilation rate = **15 m³/s** (≈ 168.75 ACH, ≈ 31,783 CFM).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 7 Marks
**Describe the basic components and working principles of a centralized HVAC system. Differentiate between single-duct CAV and VAV systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Basic Components of a Centralized HVAC System:**

1. **Air Handling Unit (AHU)** — central unit containing filters, cooling/heating coils, fans, dampers, and humidifiers.
2. **Supply ductwork** — distributes conditioned air to occupied zones via diffusers.
3. **Return ductwork** — collects stale air from occupied zones back to AHU.
4. **Exhaust system** — removes a portion of return air to outdoors.
5. **Outdoor air intake** — introduces fresh air with control dampers.
6. **Mixed air plenum** — mixes outdoor and return air before conditioning.
7. **Cooling plant** — chiller/condenser provides chilled water.
8. **Heating plant** — boiler/heat exchanger provides hot water.
9. **Control system (BMS)** — sensors, actuators, and controllers.
10. **Terminal devices** — VAV boxes, diffusers, grilles.

**Working Principle:**
Return air + outdoor air → mixing → filtering → cooling/heating → humidification → supply fan → ductwork → diffusers → occupied zone → return grille → return ductwork → (part exhausted, part recirculated).

**Difference between CAV and VAV:**

| Feature | CAV (Constant Air Volume) | VAV (Variable Air Volume) |
|---|---|---|
| Airflow rate | Fixed | Variable |
| Temperature control | By varying supply air temperature | By varying airflow rate |
| Energy efficiency | Lower | Higher (15–30% savings) |
| Duct sizing | Larger | Smaller |
| Complexity | Simpler | More complex (with VAV boxes) |
| Initial cost | Lower | Higher |
| Best suited for | Spaces with constant load | Spaces with variable load |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write "CAV is better" or "VAV is better" without context. KTU expects a **comparative table** with both advantages and disadvantages of each. Marks are awarded for **engineering justification**, not personal opinion.

---

#### Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 7 Marks
**A 50 m³ room contains a person who exhales CO₂ at a rate of 0.005 m³/h. The room is initially free of CO₂ (C₀ = 0). A mechanical ventilation system supplies outdoor air (containing 400 ppm CO₂) at a rate of 75 m³/h. Determine: (i) the steady-state CO₂ concentration in the room, (ii) the time required to reach 90% of the steady-state value, and (iii) the CO₂ concentration after 1 hour.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 50$ m³
- $G = 0.005$ m³/h = 5 L/h of CO₂ at exhaled concentration (assumed 4% = 40,000 ppm)
- $Q = 75$ m³/h
- $C_{out} = 400$ ppm
- $C_0 = 0$

**Note on units:** $G$ is a *volumetric* generation rate, but for ppm-based problems we use the **mass generation rate** in ppm·m³/h:

$$G = 0.005 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \times 40{,}000 \text{ ppm} = 200 \text{ ppm·m}^3/\text{h}$$

*(Alternatively, $G$ in mg/s if concentrations are in mg/m³.)*

**(i) Steady-State Concentration:**

$$C_{ss} = C_{out} + \frac{G}{Q} = 400 + \frac{200}{75}$$

$$C_{ss} = 400 + 2.667 = 402.67 \text{ ppm}$$

**(ii) Time to Reach 90% of Steady State:**

For 90% of steady state: $C(t) = C_{out} + 0.9 \times \dfrac{G}{Q}$

The time constant:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{50}{75} = 0.667 \text{ h} = 40 \text{ min}$$

General solution: $C(t) = C_{out} + \dfrac{G}{Q}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

Setting $C(t) - C_{out} = 0.9 \times \dfrac{G}{Q}$:

$$1 - e^{-t/\tau} = 0.9$$

$$e^{-t/\tau} = 0.1$$

$$t = -\tau \ln(0.1) = 2.303 \times \tau$$

$$t = 2.303 \times 0.667 = 1.535 \text{ h} \approx 92.1 \text{ min}$$

**(iii) CO₂ Concentration after 1 hour:**

$$C(1) = 400 + \frac{200}{75}(1 - e^{-1/0.667})$$

$$C(1) = 400 + 2.667 \times (1 - e^{-1.5})$$

$$C(1) = 400 + 2.667 \times (1 - 0.2231)$$

$$C(1) = 400 + 2.667 \times 0.7769$$

$$C(1) = 400 + 2.072$$

$$C(1) \approx 402.07 \text{ ppm}$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Steady-state CO₂ | 402.67 ppm |
| Time to 90% of $C_{ss}$ | 92.1 min |
| CO₂ after 1 hour | 402.07 ppm |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is using $G$ in L/h directly with $C$ in ppm — this is a **unit mismatch**. Always express generation rate in **ppm·m³/h** (i.e., multiply exhaled volume by exhaled concentration) when concentrations are in ppm. Alternatively, convert all to **mg/m³** with $G$ in **mg/s**.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **IAQ** — Quality of indoor air concerning health and comfort of occupants (ASHRAE 62.1).
- **SBS** — Acute symptoms without identifiable cause; symptoms resolve on leaving.
- **BRI** — Diagnosable illness with identified airborne cause.
- **HVAC** — Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning integrated system.
- **ACH** — Air Changes Per Hour; the principal design metric.
- **MERV** — Filter efficiency rating (1–16).

### 🧮 Must-Know Formulas
1. **Steady-state indoor concentration:** $C_{ss} = C_{out} + \dfrac{G}{Q}$
2. **Air changes per hour:** $\text{ACH} = \dfrac{Q}{V}$
3. **Transient concentration:** $C(t) = C_{out} + \dfrac{G}{Q}\left(1 - e^{-Qt/V}\right)$
4. **Time constant:** $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ (in **seconds** if Q is in m³/s, in **hours** if Q is in m³/h)
5. **Required ventilation:** $Q_{req} = \dfrac{G}{C_{limit} - C_{out}}$
6. **Time to 90% steady state:** $t_{90\%} = 2.303 \tau$

### 📏 Key Numerical Standards
- **ASHRAE recommended indoor CO₂:** ≤ 700 ppm above outdoor.
- **ASHRAE 62.1 outdoor air (offices):** 8.5 L/s per person + 0.6 L/s per m².
- **Residential minimum:** 0.35 ACH (very tight houses need mechanical ventilation).
- **Hospital OR:** 15–25 ACH, **positive** pressure.
- **TB isolation:** ≥ 12 ACH, **negative** pressure, exhaust to outside.
- **OSHA Formaldehyde PEL:** 0.75 ppm (8-hr TWA).
- **EPA Radon action level:** 4 pCi/L (148 Bq/m³).

### ⚠️ Common KTU Pitfalls
1. **Unit mismatches** in $G$ and $C$ — always use SI (mg/m³, m³/s).
2. **Forgetting the outdoor air term** $C_{out}$ in the mass balance.
3. **Confusing ventilation, infiltration, and exfiltration**.
4. **Conflating CAV and VAV** without comparing both.
5. **Using ACH in s⁻¹** — it is by definition per **hour**.

### 🏗️ HVAC System Types (Rapid Revision)
- **Centralized:** AHU + ductwork; suited for large buildings.
- **Decentralized:** Unitary ACs, split systems; suited for small spaces.
- **CAV:** Constant airflow; modulates temperature.
- **VAV:** Variable airflow; constant temperature; more energy-efficient.
- **VRF:** Variable Refrigerant Flow; modern inverter-based heat pump technology.

### 🔬 Major Indoor Pollutants to Remember
- **CO, CO₂, NO₂** (combustion)
- **PM₁₀, PM₂.₅** (smoking, cooking, dust)
- **VOCs** (formaldehyde, benzene)
- **Radon (²²²Rn)** (soil gas)
- **Bioaerosols** (mold, bacteria, viruses)
- **O₃** (printers, photocopiers, outdoor infiltration)
- **Asbestos fibers** (legacy building materials)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** For numerical problems, **always draw the system schematic first** (room + source + supply/exhaust), then write the **mass balance**, then **substitute** values with units. This three-step structure is what KTU examiners expect and is exactly how the model solutions above are organized.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
