topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IAQ Issues and Impacts on Occupants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)

> [!NOTE]
> **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)** is formally defined as the quality of air inside buildings, especially as it relates to the health, comfort, and well-being of building occupants. It is determined by a complex interplay of physical, chemical, and biological parameters, including the concentration of contaminants, temperature, humidity, and air movement, as established by global standards such as **ASHRAE Standard 62.1** and the **World Health Organization (WHO) guidelines**.

The American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE) defines acceptable IAQ as **"air in which there are no known contaminants at harmful concentrations as determined by cognizant authorities, and with which a substantial majority (80% or more) of the people exposed do not express dissatisfaction."**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Aquarium Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Aquarium Analogy:** Imagine a modern office building as a large, sealed glass aquarium. The fish (occupants) inside continually release CO₂, moisture, and bioeffluents (ammonia equivalent). If the aquarium's filter (HVAC system) and fresh water supply (ventilation) are inadequate, the water becomes toxic, and the fish become sick. Similarly, modern energy-efficient buildings with tightly sealed envelopes trap pollutants, leading to degraded IAQ unless proper ventilation and filtration are engineered in.

## 1.3 Why IAQ Matters — A Critical Engineering Concern

Modern construction has shifted toward **tight, well-insulated buildings** to reduce heating/cooling loads. While this improves energy efficiency, it inadvertently reduces the natural air-change rate from historical levels of **1–2 ACH (Air Changes per Hour)** down to as low as **0.1–0.3 ACH** in some buildings. This stagnation of indoor air causes pollutant concentrations to rise well above outdoor levels.

**Key Performance Metrics for IAQ Assessment:**

| Metric | Standard Value | Reference Authority |
|---|---|---|
| **CO₂ Concentration (limit)** | **1000 ppm** (8-hour average) | ASHRAE 62.1 |
| **CO₂ Concentration (limit)** | **1500 ppm** (short-term) | WHO |
| **Minimum Ventilation Rate (Offices)** | **8.5 L/s·person + 0.6 L/s·m²** | ASHRAE 62.1-2019 |
| **Recommended ACH (Office)** | **4–6 ACH** | ASHRAE |
| **Indoor Temperature (Winter)** | **20–24 °C** | ASHRAE 55 |
| **Indoor Temperature (Summer)** | **23–26 °C** | ASHRAE 55 |
| **Relative Humidity (Comfort Range)** | **30–60 %** | ASHRAE 55 |
| **PM2.5 (24-hr)** | **15 µg/m³** | WHO |
| **Total VOCs (TVOC)** | **< 500 µg/m³** | WHO |

> [!NOTE]
> **Carbon dioxide (CO₂)** is the most widely used surrogate indicator for IAQ assessment because it has a strong, predictable indoor source (human respiration) and negligible indoor sinks, making its concentration a direct measure of ventilation adequacy.

## 1.4 Classification of Indoor Air Pollutants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Indoor pollutants are broadly classified into three categories: **Biological**, **Chemical**, and **Particulate**. Each category interacts with occupants through inhalation, dermal contact, or ingestion.

**Category A — Biological Pollutants:**
- Bacteria (*Legionella*, *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*)
- Viruses (influenza, SARS-CoV-2)
- Mold spores (*Stachybotrys chartarum*, *Aspergillus*)
- Dust mites, pet dander, pollen
- Biological aerosols (cat allergens Fel d 1)

**Category B — Chemical Pollutants:**
- **Combustion by-products:** CO, NO₂, SO₂, PM2.5, PAHs
- **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs):** Formaldehyde (HCHO), Benzene (C₆H₆), Toluene (C₇H₈), Xylene
- **Semi-VOCs (SVOCs):** Phthalates, flame retardants (PBDEs)
- **Radon (²²²Rn):** Radioactive noble gas from soil
- **Ozone (O₃):** From photocopiers, air purifiers
- **Asbestos fibers** (legacy buildings)

**Category C — Particulate Matter:**
- **PM₁₀** (coarse): Pollen, dust, skin flakes
- **PM₂.₅** (fine): Smoke, combustion aerosols
- **Ultrafine (< 0.1 µm):** Printer emissions, cooking aerosols

## 1.5 The Concept of "Sick Building Syndrome" (SBS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sick Building Syndrome (SBS)** refers to a set of non-specific, building-related symptoms reported by occupants that resolve upon leaving the building, but cannot be traced to a specific identifiable cause or illness. It is a **diagnosis of exclusion** and indicates inadequate IAQ, poor ventilation, or psychosocial stressors.

**Distinguishing SBS from Building-Related Illness (BRI):**

| Feature | Sick Building Syndrome (SBS) | Building-Related Illness (BRI) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause** | Unidentifiable, multifactorial | Identifiable contaminant/agent |
| **Symptoms** | Non-specific, vague | Specific clinical signs |
| **Resolution** | Symptoms clear after leaving | Symptoms persist; medical treatment required |
| **Examples** | Headache, fatigue, eye irritation | Legionnaires' disease, asthma, hypersensitivity pneumonitis |
| **Affected Group** | Majority of occupants | Few specific individuals |
| **Diagnostic Markers** | None | Positive clinical/lab findings |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Indoor Air Pollution — Mass Balance Framework

The behavior of any indoor pollutant is governed by a **mass balance equation** applied to a well-mixed room (the "well-mixed compartment model"):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Well-Mixed Compartment Model Assumption:** The indoor air is assumed to be instantaneously and uniformly mixed. This is valid for mechanically ventilated spaces with supply diffusers providing good air distribution, but breaks down in spaces with poor air circulation, thermal stratification, or localized sources.

The general mass balance for an indoor contaminant is:

$$\frac{dC_{in}}{dt} = \frac{Q}{V} \cdot (C_{out} - C_{in}) + \frac{S}{V} - k \cdot C_{in}$$

Where each term represents:
- $\frac{Q}{V} \cdot (C_{out} - C_{in})$ — **Ventilation term** (outdoor air exchange)
- $\frac{S}{V}$ — **Source term** (indoor generation rate per unit volume)
- $k \cdot C_{in}$ — **Removal/sink term** (deposition, filtration, chemical decay)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Decoding of the Mass Balance

**Step 1: The Ventilation Term (Q/V)**
- $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate of outdoor air entering the space (m³/s)
- $V$ is the volume of the room (m³)
- The ratio $Q/V$ defines the **Air Change per Hour (ACH)**:
$$ACH = \frac{Q}{V} \times 3600 \quad \text{[h}^{-1}\text{]}$$

**Step 2: The Source Term (S/V)**
- $S$ is the mass generation rate of the pollutant (µg/s)
- For human respiration of CO₂: $S \approx 0.30$ L/min per person (sedentary office worker)

**Step 3: The Sink Term (k·C_in)**
- $k$ is the first-order removal rate constant (s⁻¹)
- Includes surface deposition, filtration, and chemical reactions
- For CO₂, $k \approx 0$ (no significant indoor removal)

**Step 4: Steady-State Simplification**
At steady state, $\frac{dC_{in}}{dt} = 0$, and for CO₂ (where $k=0$):

$$C_{in}^{ss} = C_{out} + \frac{S}{Q}$$

This is the **fundamental equation** that demonstrates how ventilation rate $Q$ directly controls indoor pollutant levels.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation a KTU student must memorize for IAQ problems. Pay special attention to units — **most marks in KTU exams are lost on unit conversion errors**.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $ACH = \frac{Q}{V} \times 3600$ | Air changes per hour from volumetric flow | h⁻¹ |
| 2 | $C_{in}^{ss} = C_{out} + \frac{S}{Q}$ | Steady-state indoor pollutant concentration | µg/m³ or ppm |
| 3 | $C(t) = C_{in}^{ss} - (C_{in}^{ss} - C_0)e^{-\lambda t}$ | Transient concentration after step change | µg/m³ or ppm |
| 4 | $\lambda = \frac{Q}{V} + k$ | Total removal rate constant | s⁻¹ or h⁻¹ |
| 5 | $Q_{req} = \frac{S_{CO_2}}{C_{limit} - C_{out}}$ | Ventilation required to keep CO₂ below limit | m³/s |
| 6 | $V_{breath} = \frac{M \cdot RQ}{0.0404}$ | CO₂ exhaled per person (Brody's equation) | L/s |
| 7 | $S_{per\,person} = 0.005 \text{ L/s} \cdot (C_{room} - 400)$ | Respiration-based CO₂ generation (Rule of thumb) | L/s |
| 8 | $C_{CO_2} = 700 \, \text{ppm} + \frac{1.8 \times 10^7 \cdot N}{Q}$ | Simplified office CO₂ prediction | ppm |
| 9 | $E = \frac{\text{Mass emitted}}{\text{Mass fuel burned}}$ | Emission factor for combustion | dimensionless |
| 10 | $t_{1/e} = \frac{V}{Q + kV}$ | e-folding decay time | s or h |

**Note on Brody's Equation (Row 6):**
- $M$ = metabolic rate (W or met; 1 met = 58.2 W/m²)
- $RQ$ = respiratory quotient (0.83 typical, 1.0 for pure carbohydrate)
- Constant 0.0404 includes oxygen volume conversion and surface area assumptions

## 2.4 Health Impact Severity Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Health effects of indoor pollutants are classified by the **time-scale of exposure** and the **reversibility** of the resulting condition.

**Tier 1 — Acute Effects (Reversible, Short-Term Exposure):**
- Eye, nose, and throat irritation
- Headaches, dizziness, fatigue
- Nausea, cognitive impairment
- Examples: VOC exposure, CO₂ > 1000 ppm

**Tier 2 — Chronic Effects (Long-Term, Partially Reversible):**
- Asthma induction and exacerbation
- Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
- Cardiovascular disease (PM₂.₅)
- Lung cancer (Radon, asbestos, ETS)
- Allergic sensitization

**Tier 3 — Immediate-Life-Threatening (Acute High-Dose):**
- Carbon monoxide poisoning (CO binds to hemoglobin; affinity is **~240× that of O₂**)
- Acute respiratory failure (Cl₂, NH₃ leaks)
- Legionella pneumonia
- Carbon dioxide asphyxiation (> 5% CO₂)

## 2.5 The CO₂-to-Ventilation Diagnostic Procedure

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Whenever a problem gives a CO₂ concentration and asks about ventilation adequacy, follow this three-step diagnostic:
> 1. Compare to **ASHRAE limit of 1000 ppm** (700 ppm above outdoor ≈ 400 ppm).
> 2. Calculate required outdoor air using $Q_{req} = S_{CO_2} / (C_{limit} - C_{out})$.
> 3. Compare with the actual supply flow rate to determine pass/fail.

## 2.6 ASHRAE Standard 62.1 — Ventilation Rate Procedure

The standard prescribes a **two-component ventilation requirement**:

$$Q_{total} = R_p \cdot P_z + R_a \cdot A_z$$

Where:
- $R_p$ = **Per-person outdoor air rate** (L/s·person)
- $P_z$ = **Number of occupants** in the zone
- $R_a$ = **Per-area outdoor air rate** (L/s·m²)
- $A_z$ = **Zone floor area** (m²)

**Common values from ASHRAE 62.1-2019:**

| Space Type | $R_p$ (L/s·person) | $R_a$ (L/s·m²) |
|---|---|---|
| Office | 8.5 | 0.6 |
| Conference room | 3.0 | 0.6 |
| Classroom (5–17 yr) | 5.0 | 0.6 |
| Hotel guest room | 5.0 | 0.6 |
| Retail (mall) | 3.0 | 0.6 |
| Restaurant dining | 3.0 | 0.9 |
| Auditorium (seats) | 2.5 | 0.3 |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The IAQ framework is applied in:
- **HVAC design** for green buildings (LEED, BREEAM, GRIHA certification)
- **Hospital isolation rooms** (negative pressure, HEPA filtration)
- **Cleanrooms** (semiconductor, pharmaceutical) — ISO Class 1–9
- **Underground/subway ventilation** systems
- **Industrial workplace** compliance with OSHA permissible exposure limits (PELs)
- **Post-pandemic ventilation retrofits** (post-COVID-19 airborne infection control)

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Indoor CO₂ Concentration

**Problem Setup:**
A conference room has dimensions **10 m × 8 m × 3 m** (height). It is occupied by **30 people** engaged in light office work. The outdoor air supply rate is **300 m³/h**, and the outdoor CO₂ concentration is **400 ppm**. Each person exhales CO₂ at a rate of **0.30 L/min (at STP)**. Calculate the steady-state indoor CO₂ concentration. Is the IAQ acceptable per ASHRAE 62.1?

**Given Data:**
- Room volume: $V = 10 \times 8 \times 3 = 240 \text{ m}^3$
- Number of occupants: $N = 30$
- Outdoor air flow: $Q = 300 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 0.0833 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Outdoor CO₂: $C_{out} = 400 \text{ ppm}$
- Per-person CO₂ generation: $S_p = 0.30 \text{ L/min} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Total CO₂ Source Rate:**
$$S = N \times S_p = 30 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to volumetric concentration units:**
For an ideal gas, the volumetric fraction is identical to the mole fraction. The CO₂ generation is essentially a volumetric flow of pure CO₂ added to the room. So at steady state, the increment in CO₂ fraction is:
$$\Delta C = \frac{S}{Q} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0833} = 1.80 \times 10^{-3} \text{ (volume fraction)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to ppm:**
$$1.80 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^6 = 1800 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 4 — Total Indoor CO₂ at Steady State:**
$$C_{in}^{ss} = C_{out} + \Delta C = 400 + 1800 = 2200 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Steady-State Formula Directly:**
$$C_{in}^{ss} = C_{out} + \frac{S}{Q} = 400 + \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0833} = 400 + 1800 = 2200 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 6 — Diagnostic Conclusion:**
Since **2200 ppm > 1000 ppm (ASHRAE 8-hr limit)**, the IAQ is **NOT acceptable**. The room is severely under-ventilated.

**Step 7 — Required Ventilation for Compliance:**
$$Q_{req} = \frac{S}{C_{limit} - C_{out}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{(1000 - 400) \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$Q_{req} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 900 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

The current supply of 300 m³/h must be tripled. **ACH check:**
$$ACH = \frac{900}{240} = 3.75 \text{ h}^{-1} \quad \text{(vs. current 1.25 h}^{-1}\text{)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Transient Response After Pollutant Release

**Problem Setup:**
A laboratory of volume $V = 100 \text{ m}^3$ has a ventilation rate of $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. Initially, the formaldehyde concentration is $C_0 = 0$ (the experiment is about to start). At $t = 0$, a chemistry experiment begins, releasing formaldehyde at a constant rate $S = 5 \text{ mg/s}$. The decay constant is negligible ($k = 0$). Determine the formaldehyde concentration after **10 minutes**. Use the transient solution.

**Step 1 — Define the Decay Constant:**
$$\lambda = \frac{Q}{V} + k = \frac{0.05}{100} + 0 = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Steady-State Concentration:**
$$C_{in}^{ss} = \frac{S}{\lambda V} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 100} = 0.10 \text{ mg/m}^3 = 100 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

*(Note: We used $\frac{S}{Q}$ here since $k=0$, giving $\frac{5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.05} = 0.10 \text{ mg/m}^3$. Consistent.)*

**Step 3 — Apply the Transient Solution:**
$$C(t) = C_{in}^{ss} \left(1 - e^{-\lambda t}\right) + C_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$
With $C_0 = 0$:
$$C(t) = C_{in}^{ss} \left(1 - e^{-\lambda t}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at t = 600 s:**
$$\lambda t = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 600 = 0.30$$
$$e^{-0.30} = 0.7408$$
$$C(600) = 100 \times (1 - 0.7408) = 100 \times 0.2592 = 25.92 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Diagnostic Conclusion:**
WHO guideline for formaldehyde is **100 µg/m³ (30-min average)**. The value of 25.92 µg/m³ is well within the limit, so the laboratory is operating safely. However, it will asymptotically approach 100 µg/m³, so prolonged experiments require monitoring.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: CO₂ as Ventilation Indicator — Direct Calculation

**Problem Setup:**
A classroom has 40 students. Outdoor CO₂ is 400 ppm. Indoor CO₂ is measured at **1200 ppm**. The room volume is 250 m³. Calculate:
(a) The actual ventilation rate $Q$ assuming average CO₂ generation of 4.0 × 10⁻⁶ m³/s per student.
(b) The air changes per hour (ACH).
(c) The per-person outdoor air supplied.

**Part (a):** Use $C_{in}^{ss} = C_{out} + \frac{N \cdot S_p}{Q}$:
$$1200 = 400 + \frac{40 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-6}}{Q}$$
$$800 \text{ ppm} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ (volume fraction)}$$
$$8.0 \times 10^{-4} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-4}}{Q}$$
$$Q = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-4}}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.20 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 720 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Part (b):** 
$$ACH = \frac{720}{250} = 2.88 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Part (c):**
$$Q_{per\,person} = \frac{0.20 \times 1000}{40} = 5.0 \text{ L/s·person}$$

ASHRAE 62.1 specifies **5.0 L/s·person** for classrooms — this room is **exactly meeting** the standard. Engineering judgment: this is borderline; design margin of 10–20% is recommended.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — IAQ Improvement Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked "how to improve IAQ," do not list measures randomly. Organize them in the **hierarchy of control effectiveness** (analogous to industrial hygiene):

| Priority | Control Strategy | Effectiveness | Cost | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1 (Highest)** | **Elimination / Source Removal** | 100% | Variable | Ban smoking; remove asbestos |
| **2** | **Source Substitution** | 70–90% | Low | Low-VOC paints; composite wood with low formaldehyde |
| **3** | **Ventilation (Dilution)** | 50–80% | High | Increase outdoor air fraction; natural ventilation |
| **4** | **Local Exhaust Ventilation** | 80–95% | Medium | Kitchen hoods, laboratory fume hoods |
| **5** | **Filtration/Air Cleaning** | 40–90% | Medium | HEPA, activated carbon, UVGI |
| **6 (Lowest)** | **Administrative Controls / PPE** | 10–30% | Low | Cleaning schedules, respirators |

## 3.5 Health Impact Quantification — DALY Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs)** are used by WHO to quantify the burden of indoor air pollution. DALY = YLL (Years of Life Lost) + YLD (Years Lived with Disability). Globally, indoor air pollution is responsible for an estimated **3.2 million premature deaths annually**, primarily from cooking with solid fuels in developing nations (household air pollution, HAP).

The WHO comparative burden of indoor vs. outdoor air pollution:

$$\text{Global IAQ-attributable deaths} \approx 3.2 \text{ million/year (mostly LMICs)}$$
$$\text{Global outdoor AQ-attributable deaths} \approx 4.2 \text{ million/year}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A answer should be structured as: (1) one-line crisp definition, (2) 2–3 supporting technical points, (3) one practical example or numerical value. Total answer length: **roughly 5–8 lines**.

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define Sick Building Syndrome (SBS). List five common symptoms and two causes.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Sick Building Syndrome** is a condition in which a substantial proportion of building occupants (typically > 20%) report non-specific symptoms of ill health during their presence in the building, which resolve upon leaving, with no identifiable specific cause or disease.

**Five common symptoms:**
1. Headache and heaviness in the head
2. Eye, nose, and throat irritation (mucous membrane irritation)
3. Fatigue and lethargy
4. Dry skin and rashes
5. Nausea and dizziness; difficulty concentrating

**Two major causes:**
1. **Inadequate ventilation** — outdoor air supply below ASHRAE 62.1 minimum (often < 5 L/s·person)
2. **Chemical contamination** — VOCs from paints, adhesives, furniture, and cleaning agents

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Explain why CO₂ is used as the primary indicator for indoor air quality assessment. State its ASHRAE limit value.**

**Model Answer:**

CO₂ is used as the IAQ indicator because:
1. **Predictable indoor source** — exclusively from human respiration (and combustion); no other significant indoor sources
2. **Negligible sinks** — chemically inert in occupied spaces; does not deposit on surfaces
3. **Easy and inexpensive measurement** — non-dispersive infrared (NDIR) sensors are highly accurate
4. **Strong correlation with bioeffluents** — concentrations track other occupant-generated contaminants (body odor, perfumes, pathogens)
5. **Direct measure of ventilation adequacy** — a high CO₂ reading indicates insufficient outdoor air supply

**ASHRAE 62.1 limit:** **1000 ppm** (8-hour weighted average, with outdoor baseline ≈ 400 ppm, allowing a 600 ppm increment from occupants).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part B question features two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Follow the KTU valuation pattern: clear diagram/equation, stepwise solution, boxed final answer.

---

### Part B — Option A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Question:** (a) Derive the steady-state mass balance equation for an indoor contaminant. Explain the physical significance of each term. (7 marks)
(b) An office space of dimensions 15 m × 10 m × 3.5 m is occupied by 25 employees working at sedentary tasks. The HVAC system supplies 600 m³/h of outdoor air, and the outdoor CO₂ concentration is 380 ppm. Each person exhales CO₂ at 0.005 L/s. Calculate the steady-state indoor CO₂ concentration and comment on whether the IAQ is acceptable as per ASHRAE 62.1. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Mass Balance Equation:

**Step 1:** Consider a room of volume $V$ (m³) with a well-mixed indoor air assumption. Let $C_{in}(t)$ be the indoor concentration (µg/m³ or ppm), $Q$ (m³/s) be the outdoor air flow rate, and $C_{out}$ be the outdoor concentration.

**Step 2:** Apply conservation of mass over a time interval $dt$:

$$V \cdot \frac{dC_{in}}{dt} = Q \cdot C_{out} - Q \cdot C_{in} + S - V \cdot k \cdot C_{in}$$

**Step 3:** Divide throughout by $V$ to obtain the **mass balance equation**:

$$\frac{dC_{in}}{dt} = \frac{Q}{V}(C_{out} - C_{in}) + \frac{S}{V} - k \cdot C_{in}$$

**Physical significance of each term:**

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\frac{dC_{in}}{dt}$ | Rate of change of indoor concentration with time |
| $\frac{Q}{V}(C_{out} - C_{in})$ | **Ventilation term** — net mass flux from outdoor air (dilution) |
| $\frac{S}{V}$ | **Source term** — rate of pollutant generation inside the room per unit volume |
| $k \cdot C_{in}$ | **Sink term** — first-order removal by deposition, filtration, reaction |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:

**Given:**
- $V = 15 \times 10 \times 3.5 = 525 \text{ m}^3$
- $N = 25$ employees
- $Q = 600 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 0.1667 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $C_{out} = 380 \text{ ppm}$
- $S_p = 0.005 \text{ L/s}$ per person

**Step 1:** Total CO₂ source rate:
$$S = N \cdot S_p = 25 \times 0.005 = 0.125 \text{ L/s} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply steady-state equation (with $k = 0$ for CO₂):
$$C_{in}^{ss} = C_{out} + \frac{S}{Q} = 380 + \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{0.1667}$$
$$C_{in}^{ss} = 380 + 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ (volume fraction)}$$
$$C_{in}^{ss} = 380 + 750 = 1130 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 3:** Compare with ASHRAE 62.1 limit of 1000 ppm:
$$1130 \text{ ppm} > 1000 \text{ ppm} \quad \Rightarrow \text{IAQ is NOT acceptable}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the required ventilation:
$$Q_{req} = \frac{S}{C_{limit} - C_{out}} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{(1000 - 380) \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{6.2 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.2016 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$Q_{req} = 726 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 5:** ACH analysis:
$$ACH_{actual} = \frac{600}{525} = 1.14 \text{ h}^{-1} \quad (\text{too low})$$
$$ACH_{req} = \frac{726}{525} = 1.38 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Final Answer:** Indoor CO₂ = **1130 ppm**, **IAQ is unacceptable**; ventilation must be increased to **726 m³/h**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Unit Conversion:** The most common error in this problem is forgetting to convert **L/s to m³/s** (divide by 1000). If you keep $S$ in L/s but $Q$ in m³/s, the resulting concentration will be **1000× off**, leading to a grossly incorrect answer of 1130000 ppm. Always state units explicitly in every calculation step.

---

### Part B — Option B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Question:** (a) Explain the term "Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)" in the context of IAQ. List six common indoor VOCs and their typical sources. (7 marks)
(b) Discuss the various health effects of indoor air pollution on building occupants, with specific reference to (i) Sick Building Syndrome, (ii) Carbon Monoxide poisoning, and (iii) Radon exposure. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs):

> **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** are organic chemicals that have a high vapor pressure at ordinary room temperature, causing them to evaporate readily from solids or liquids into the surrounding air. VOCs are quantified as **Total VOCs (TVOC)** in µg/m³, with the WHO guideline recommending TVOC < **500 µg/m³**.

**Six common indoor VOCs and their sources:**

| # | VOC | Chemical Formula | Typical Indoor Sources |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Formaldehyde** | HCHO | Particle board, plywood, urea-formaldehyde insulation, new carpets |
| 2 | **Benzene** | C₆H₆ | Cigarette smoke, paints, detergents, vehicle exhaust infiltrating indoors |
| 3 | **Toluene** | C₇H₈ | Solvents, paints, adhesives, nail polish removers |
| 4 | **Xylene** | C₈H₁₀ | Printing inks, rubber, leather, paint thinners |
| 5 | **Styrene** | C₈H₈ | Polystyrene products, plastic cups, insulation |
| 6 | **Tetrachloroethylene** | C₂Cl₄ | Dry-cleaned clothing, spot removers |
| 7 | **Methylene Chloride** | CH₂Cl₂ | Paint strippers, aerosol propellants |

#### Part (b) — Health Effects Discussion:

**(i) Sick Building Syndrome (SBS):**
- **Definition:** Non-specific symptom complex reported by ≥ 20% of occupants, with no identifiable single cause; symptoms resolve after leaving the building.
- **Common symptoms:** Headache, fatigue, eye/nose/throat irritation, dry skin, nausea, difficulty concentrating.
- **Causative factors:** Inadequate ventilation (CO₂ > 1000 ppm), high TVOC, low humidity (< 30%), psychosocial stress, poor lighting, noise.
- **Mechanism:** Bioeffluents and VOCs accumulate above sensory irritation thresholds; combined effect of multiple sub-threshold contaminants.

**(ii) Carbon Monoxide (CO) Poisoning:**
- **Source:** Incomplete combustion of carbonaceous fuels (gas heaters, fireplaces, blocked chimneys, vehicle exhaust in attached garages).
- **Mechanism:** CO binds to hemoglobin (Hb) with an affinity **~240 times greater than O₂**, forming **carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)**, which:
  - Reduces blood oxygen carrying capacity
  - Shifts the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve to the **left**, impairing tissue O₂ release
  - Inhibits cytochrome oxidase in mitochondria
- **Symptoms by COHb level:**
  - 10% — headache, breathlessness on exertion
  - 20–30% — throbbing headache, irritability
  - 40–50% — confusion, syncope
  - > 60% — coma, seizures, death
- **Treatment:** 100% oxygen or hyperbaric oxygen therapy.

**(iii) Radon (²²²Rn) Exposure:**
- **Source:** Naturally occurring radioactive gas from the decay of uranium-238 in soil and rocks; seeps into buildings through foundation cracks.
- **Mechanism:** Radon decays to **Polonium-218** and **Polonium-214**, which are alpha-emitting solid isotopes. When inhaled, these deposit on bronchial epithelium, delivering high-LET radiation to lung tissue.
- **Health effect:** **Lung cancer** — second leading cause after smoking. EPA estimates **15,000–22,000 lung cancer deaths/year in the US** from radon.
- **Risk model:** Synergistic with smoking; combined risk is multiplicative (not additive).
- **EPA action level:** **4 pCi/L** (148 Bq/m³) in indoor air.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Naming Conventions:** Students often confuse "VOCs" with "SVOCs" (Semi-VOCs like phthalates) and "VOCs" with "Particulate VOCs" (like PAHs). In KTU answers, **always write the chemical formula in brackets** after the compound name (e.g., Benzene C₆H₆). Examiners award bonus marks for chemical specificity. Also, never write "carbon monoxide and CO₂" as if they are the same — they have completely different toxicity profiles.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Last-Minute Revision Points** — read this list three times before entering the exam hall.

✅ **Core Definition:** IAQ = quality of indoor air with respect to occupant health, comfort, and well-being (ASHRAE 62.1).

✅ **Key Indicator:** **CO₂ at 1000 ppm (8-hr limit)** is the master diagnostic surrogate for ventilation adequacy.

✅ **SBS vs. BRI:** SBS = no specific cause, symptoms reversible; BRI = specific cause, requires treatment (e.g., Legionnaires').

✅ **Sick Building Triangle:** (1) Source of pollutants, (2) HVAC/ventilation system inadequacy, (3) Susceptible occupants.

✅ **Mass Balance Equation:** $C_{in}^{ss} = C_{out} + \frac{S}{Q}$ — memorize and be able to derive.

✅ **ACH Formula:** $ACH = \frac{Q}{V} \times 3600$ (h⁻¹) — minimum recommended: **4–6 ACH** for offices.

✅ **ASHRAE 62.1 Office:** **8.5 L/s·person + 0.6 L/s·m²** of outdoor air.

✅ **Sources of CO:** Incomplete combustion (gas stoves, vehicle exhaust in garages, blocked chimneys); **NOT** from human respiration.

✅ **CO Toxicity:** Affinity for Hb is **~240× that of O₂**; forms COHb, shifts ODC to the left.

✅ **Radon:** ²²²Rn, soil-derived, second leading cause of lung cancer; EPA limit **4 pCi/L**.

✅ **VOC Indoor Sources:** Formaldehyde (HCHO) — furniture/plywood; Benzene — tobacco smoke; Toluene — paints.

✅ **PM Categories:** PM₁₀ (thoracic), PM₂.₅ (respirable, alveolar), ultrafine (< 0.1 µm, systemic).

✅ **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Ventilation → Local Exhaust → Filtration → PPE.

✅ **Bioeffluents:** CO₂, body odor, ammonia, hydrogen sulfide, methane, acetone — all generated by human metabolism.

✅ **Legionella:** Grows in **warm water systems (25–45°C)**, cooling towers, hot water tanks; causes **Legionnaires' disease**.

✅ **Control Technologies:** HEPA (PM), Activated Carbon (VOCs/odors), UVGI (bioaerosols), Bipolar Ionization (PM, some bio).

✅ **Ventilation Types:** Natural, Mechanical (Supply/Exhaust/Balanced), Hybrid (Mixed-mode), Displacement (low-turbulence floor supply).

✅ **Air Distribution Patterns:** Mixing (overhead supply), Displacement (floor supply, top return), Underfloor (UFAD).

✅ **Health Metrics:** DALYs for IAQ ≈ 3.2 million deaths/year globally; 50% of pneumonia in children < 5 in LMICs is from indoor smoke.

✅ **Engineering Targets:** TVOC < **500 µg/m³** (WHO), PM₂.₅ < **15 µg/m³** (24-hr WHO), HCHO < **100 µg/m³** (30-min WHO).

✅ **Famous Case Study:** Bhopal (1984) — MIC gas leak; SARS (2003) — Amoy Gardens Hong Kong apartment block sewage aerosol spread; COVID-19 (2020) — airborne transmission via poor ventilation.

✅ **KTU-Specific Point:** Always state the **equation, substitute values with units, and box the final numerical answer**. Marks are lost when units are missing in intermediate steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Audits to Developing an IAQ Profile, Developing Management Plans, IAQ Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Application of Audits to Developing an IAQ Profile, Developing Management Plans, IAQ Problems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an IAQ Audit? An **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ) Audit** is a systematic, documented, and periodic evaluation process used to characterize the physical, chemical, biological, and ergonomic conditions of air within a built environment. According to KTU 2024 syllabus conventions aligned with ASHRAE Standard 62.1 and EPA guidelines, an IAQ audit is the diagnostic engine that feeds data into an **IAQ Profile** — a quantitative and qualitative "fingerprint" of the air in a structure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Board Standard):** > An IAQ Audit is a multi-disciplinary investigation that integrates (i) walk-through visual inspection, (ii) measurement of physical parameters (T, RH, CO₂, PM), (iii) chemical sampling (VOCs, CO, NO₂, O₃, HCHO), (iv) biological sampling (fungi, bacteria, endotoxins), and (v) HVAC performance review — the combined output of which is used to construct a holistic **IAQ Profile** of a building. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Building is a Patient" Think of a building exactly like a human patient visiting a doctor. The IAQ auditor is the **pulmonologist** of the building: - **Walk-through inspection** = the doctor's physical examination (looking, listening, smelling). - **Instrumental sampling** = blood tests and X-rays (CO₂ = respiratory rate, PM = chest congestion, VOCs = metabolic toxins). - **HVAC review** = checking the lungs and circulatory system. - **IAQ Profile** = the patient's medical chart — a single document holding all diagnostic data. - **Management Plan** = the prescribed treatment and lifestyle change program. Just as a doctor cannot prescribe medicine without diagnosis, an IAQ management plan **cannot be designed without a completed audit and a developed IAQ Profile**. ### 1.2 The IAQ Profile — What It Contains The IAQ Profile is a structured database/dossier containing: | Profile Component | Description | |---|---| | Building Inventory | Age, area, occupancy, construction materials, renovations | | HVAC Inventory | Type, age, MERV rating, outdoor air fraction, filter condition | | Contaminant Map | Spatial distribution of measured pollutants | | Occupant Symptom Survey | Self-reported SBS/BRI symptoms | | Time-Series Trends | Diurnal/seasonal patterns of key pollutants | | Benchmark Comparison | Measured values vs. ASHRAE/NAAQMS/WHO limits | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Benchmarks Used in KTU Solutions:** > - **ASHRAE 62.1** Ventilation Rate: **8 L/s per person** + **0.6 L/s per m²** of floor area (office default). > - **CO₂ threshold**: **< 700 ppm above outdoor** (~1000 ppm indoor absolute). > - **PM₂.₅ (24-hr)**: **< 35 µg/m³** (NAAQS) / **< 15 µg/m³** (WHO 2021). > - **Formaldehyde**: **< 100 µg/m³** (0.08 ppm) — 30-min average. ### 1.3 IAQ Management Plan — The Outcome of an Audit A **Management Plan** is the corrective and preventive action document generated from the IAQ Profile. It specifies source control, ventilation upgrades, operational policies, and occupant communication protocols. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hierarchy of Controls (used in every KTU Management Plan answer):** > 1. **Source Elimination** (most effective) > 2. **Source Substitution** (e.g., low-VOC paints) > 3. **Engineering Controls** (HVAC upgrade, local exhaust) > 4. **Administrative Controls** (smoking bans, scheduling) > 5. **PPE / Personal Action** (least preferred) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of an IAQ Audit The KTU syllabus expects students to describe an audit as a **sequential, five-phase protocol**: ### Phase 1 — Pre-Audit Planning & Document Review - Gather building floor plans, HVAC as-built drawings. - Review previous occupant complaint logs. - Identify high-risk zones (basements for radon, cafeterias for CO, copy rooms for O₃/VOCs). ### Phase 2 — Walk-Through Inspection (Qualitative) - Visual checks: water stains (mold indicator), blocked diffusers, dirty filters. - Olfactory checks: musty odors (microbial VOCs), sweet odors (formaldehyde). - Ventilation check: measure supply/return airflow with **anemometer** at grilles. ### Phase 3 — Quantitative Measurement - Continuous monitors for **CO₂, T, RH** over at least one working day (8 hrs minimum, ideally one week per ASHRAE). - Grab sampling for VOCs (Tenax tubes + GC-MS), aldehydes (DNPH cartridges + HPLC), bioaerosols (Anderson impactor). ### Phase 4 — HVAC Performance Verification - Measure **Outdoor Air (OA) intake** vs. design value. - Calculate **Air Changes per Hour (ACH)**. - Inspect condensate pans, cooling coils for biofilm. ### Phase 5 — Profile Generation & Reporting - Tabulate all findings. - Cross-reference with standards. - Flag exceedances and root causes. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why continuous CO₂ monitoring?** Because CO₂ at ~1000 ppm is a proxy for bioeffluent accumulation and a marker of ventilation adequacy — a cheap, real-time surrogate for human comfort and dilution ventilation. - **Why both supply and return airflow?** The **ventilation effectiveness** is meaningless without knowing how much outdoor air actually reaches the breathing zone. - **Why one full working day minimum?** Diurnal occupancy patterns mean pollutant peaks (CO₂ at 11 AM, VOCs from cleaning at 6 PM) must be captured. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Air Changes per Hour | $\mathrm{ACH = \dfrac{Q \times 3600}{V}}$ | hr⁻¹ | Ventilation adequacy; IAQ target ACH ≥ **4 to 6** for offices | | Ventilation Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{supply}}{C_{breathing} - C_{supply}}$ | dimensionless | Ranges 0–1; ≥ 1.0 ideal per ASHRAE 129 | | CO₂-based OA Fraction | $\mathrm{OA\% = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{sup}}{C_{in,design} - C_{sup}} \times 100}$ | % | Verifies HVAC delivers design OA | | Mass Balance (Steady) | $C = \dfrac{G}{Q}$ (well-mixed) | mg/m³ | Used for single-zone IAQ audits | | Contaminant Generation Rate | $G = \rho \cdot \dot{V} \cdot C_s$ | mg/s | For material emission testing (e.g., formaldehyde from furniture) | | Decay Equation (Ventilation) | $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\left(\dfrac{Q}{V} + k\right) t}$ | mg/m³ | Used to model pollutant decay after source removal | | Toluene Conc. (mass) | $C = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | mg/m³ | Lab VOC calculation | | Particulate Settling (Stokes) | $v_s = \dfrac{\rho_p \, g \, d_p^2}{18 \mu}$ | m/s | Used to estimate PM₂.₅ vs PM₁₀ persistence | | Air Quantity (HVAC) | $Q = A \cdot v_{avg}$ | m³/s | Duct airflow measurement | | Heat–Mass analogy (ACH from T tracer) | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{ACH}}$ | hr | Tracer gas decay method | | ASHRAE 62.1 OA Rate | $V_{ot} = R_p \cdot P_z + R_a \cdot A_z$ | L/s | $R_p$ = per-person, $R_a$ = per-m² | | Indoor CO₂ Differential | $\Delta C_{CO_2} = C_{in} - C_{out}$ | ppm | Should be **< 700 ppm** | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Exam Note:** In any KTU 14-mark Part B question, you must use ASHRAE 62.1 ventilation equation $V_{ot} = R_p P_z + R_a A_z$ at least once. It is a high-weight formula. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Commercial buildings** use IAQ audits for **LEED-EQ Credit** compliance and WELL Building certification. - **Hospitals** require audits under **ASHRAE 170** (Health Care Ventilation Standard) for infection control. - **Schools** use audits under the **EPA IAQ Tools for Schools** framework — a directly tested KTU topic. - **Industrial cleanrooms** use audits per **ISO 14644** for particulate classification. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Air Changes per Hour (ACH) from Airflow The most fundamental IAQ audit calculation. We start from the volumetric flow rate definition. **Given:** - Volumetric airflow supplied to a room, $Q$ (in m³/s). - Room volume, $V$ (in m³). **Step 1 — Convert per-second flow to per-hour flow.** The total volume of air entering the room in one hour is: $$V_{hour} = Q \times 3600 \;\; (\mathrm{m^3/hr})$$ **Step 2 — Define ACH as the number of times the room's air is replaced per hour.** $$\mathrm{ACH} = \dfrac{\text{Volume of air supplied per hour}}{\text{Room volume}}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the expression.** $$\mathrm{ACH} = \dfrac{Q \times 3600}{V}$$ **Step 4 — Units check.** - Numerator: m³/s × 3600 s/hr = m³/hr. - Denominator: m³. - Result: 1/hr (i.e., hr⁻¹). ✔ ### Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-mark style) > **Problem:** An office room is $6 \text{ m} \times 5 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m}$. The supply diffuser delivers $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. Calculate ACH. **Step 1 — Volume of room.** $$V = 6 \times 5 \times 3 = 90 \; \mathrm{m^3}$$ **Step 2 — Per-hour supply.** $$Q_{hr} = 0.05 \times 3600 = 180 \; \mathrm{m^3/hr}$$ **Step 3 — Apply ACH formula.** $$\mathrm{ACH} = \dfrac{180}{90} = 2.0 \; \mathrm{hr^{-1}}$$ **Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation.** $$\mathrm{ACH} = 2.0 \; < \; 4 \text{ (recommended for offices)}$$ **Conclusion:** The room is **under-ventilated**. A 2× upgrade to 0.1 m³/s supply (giving ACH = 4) is recommended in the management plan. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Mass Balance for a Contaminant This derivation is the **theoretical backbone of the IAQ Profile**. **Setup:** A well-mixed room of volume $V$ with steady contaminant generation rate $G$ (mg/s) and ventilation rate $Q$ (m³/s). **Step 1 — Write the conservation of mass equation.** $$\dfrac{dC}{dt} \cdot V = G - Q \cdot C$$ The rate of accumulation in the room equals generation minus removal by ventilation (assuming no internal sinks). **Step 2 — At steady state, $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = 0$.** $$0 = G - Q \cdot C_{ss}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for the steady-state indoor concentration $C_{ss}$.** $$C_{ss} = \dfrac{G}{Q}$$ **Step 4 — Add the effect of outdoor background concentration $C_{out}$.** If outdoor air with concentration $C_{out}$ also enters, the balance becomes: $$V \dfrac{dC}{dt} = G - Q \cdot C + Q \cdot C_{out} - Q \cdot C$$ Simplifying at steady state: $$C_{ss} = C_{out} + \dfrac{G}{Q}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** $C_{ss} = G/Q$ is the most frequently tested equation in Module 4. Always state "assuming well-mixed conditions and steady state." ### Numerical Worked Example > **Problem:** A copy room has $V = 30 \text{ m}^3$, $Q = 0.01 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. A photocopier emits ozone at $G = 0.05 \text{ mg/s}$. Outdoor ozone $C_{out} = 0.02 \text{ mg/m}^3$. Find $C_{ss}$ and compare with NAAQS (O₃ 8-hr = 0.10 mg/m³). **Step 1 — Apply the mass balance.** $$C_{ss} = C_{out} + \dfrac{G}{Q}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute.** $$C_{ss} = 0.02 + \dfrac{0.05}{0.01} = 0.02 + 5.0 = 5.02 \; \mathrm{mg/m^3}$$ **Step 3 — Compare with standard.** $$5.02 \; \mathrm{mg/m^3} \gg 0.10 \; \mathrm{mg/m^3}$$ **Conclusion:** Concentration is **~50× the NAAQS**. Management action: install **local exhaust ventilation** above the copier, switch to toner-only models, and isolate the room with dedicated exhaust. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: CO₂ as a Ventilation Tracer CO₂ exhaled by humans is a tracer for **bioeffluent dilution**. **Step 1 — Generation rate of CO₂ per person.** A sedentary office worker exhales approximately $G_p = 0.0056 \text{ L/s}$ of CO₂ ≈ **0.31 L/min per person** (≈ 18,000 ppm·L/s). **Step 2 — Mass balance for CO₂ in a zone with $P$ persons.** $$V \dfrac{dC_{in}}{dt} = P \cdot G_p - Q \cdot (C_{in} - C_{out})$$ **Step 3 — Steady state.** $$C_{in} = C_{out} + \dfrac{P \cdot G_p}{Q}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the required OA flow $Q$ given a target $C_{in}$.** $$Q = \dfrac{P \cdot G_p}{C_{in} - C_{out}}$$ ### Numerical Example > **Problem:** Conference room with 20 persons, target indoor CO₂ = 800 ppm, outdoor CO₂ = 400 ppm. Find required outdoor air $Q$. **Step 1 — Substitute.** $$Q = \dfrac{20 \times 0.0056 \text{ L/s}}{(800 - 400) \times 10^{-6}}$$ **Step 2 — Compute.** $$Q = \dfrac{0.112}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 280 \; \mathrm{L/s}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to L/s per person.** $$Q/P = 280/20 = 14 \; \mathrm{L/s per person}$$ This satisfies **ASHRAE 62.1** (default 8 L/s per person for offices, 10 L/s per person for meeting rooms). ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Contaminant Decay After Source Removal **Scenario:** Source removed at $t = 0$, $C(0) = C_0$. **Step 1 — Mass balance with no source.** $$V \dfrac{dC}{dt} = -Q \cdot C - k \cdot V \cdot C$$ where $k$ is a first-order decay constant (e.g., for surface deposition, reaction). **Step 2 — Separate variables.** $$\dfrac{dC}{C} = -\left(\dfrac{Q}{V} + k\right) dt$$ **Step 3 — Integrate from $C_0$ to $C(t)$.** $$\int_{C_0}^{C(t)} \dfrac{dC}{C} = -\left(\dfrac{Q}{V} + k\right) \int_0^t dt$$ **Step 4 — Solution.** $$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\left(\dfrac{Q}{V} + k\right) t} = C_0 \cdot e^{-(\mathrm{ACH}/3600 + k) t}$$ ### Numerical Example > **Problem:** After removing a smoker, $C_0 = 5 \text{ ppm}$ of ETS tracer. $\mathrm{ACH} = 2$, $k = 0$ (no deposition). Find $C$ at $t = 1$ hr. **Step 1 — Compute decay rate.** $$\lambda = \dfrac{\mathrm{ACH}}{3600} = \dfrac{2}{3600} = 5.56 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $C$ at $t = 3600$ s.** $$C(3600) = 5 \cdot e^{-5.56 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600} = 5 \cdot e^{-2} = 5 \times 0.1353$$ **Step 3 — Final.** $$C(3600) = 0.677 \; \mathrm{ppm}$$ **Conclusion:** Even after 1 hour, ~13% of original tracer remains. Management plan: **purge ventilation at ACH = 6+** for 1 hour to reach < 5% of original. ## 3.5 Derivation 5: ASHRAE 62.1 Outdoor Air Rate **Step 1 — Total OA requirement for a zone.** $$V_{ot} = R_p \cdot P_z + R_a \cdot A_z$$ Where: - $R_p$ = outdoor air rate per person (L/s) - $R_a$ = outdoor air rate per unit floor area (L/s per m²) - $P_z$ = zone population - $A_z$ = zone floor area (m²) **Step 2 — For a default office (ASHRAE 62.1 Table 6.5.1).** $$R_p = 2.5 \text{ L/s per person}, \quad R_a = 0.6 \text{ L/s per m}^2$$ ### Numerical Example > **Problem:** Office zone, $P_z = 25$ people, $A_z = 200 \text{ m}^2$. Find $V_{ot}$. **Step 1 — Per-person component.** $$V_{ot,p} = 2.5 \times 25 = 62.5 \text{ L/s}$$ **Step 2 — Per-area component.** $$V_{ot,a} = 0.6 \times 200 = 120 \text{ L/s}$$ **Step 3 — Sum.** $$V_{ot} = 62.5 + 120 = 182.5 \text{ L/s}$$ **Convert to m³/s:** $0.1825 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. > [!NOTE] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often use *either* the per-person *or* per-area value. ASHRAE 62.1 requires **both components summed** — not a maximum, not a minimum. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete IAQ Audit → Profile → Management Plan Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Building Under Audit] --> B[Pre-Audit Document Review] B --> C[Phase 1 Walk-Through Inspection] C --> D[Phase 2 Continuous Monitoring
CO2, T, RH, PM] C --> E[Phase 3 Grab Sampling
VOCs, HCHO, Bioaerosols] C --> F[Phase 4 HVAC Performance Test
ACH, OA Fraction, Filter Status] D --> G[Data Compilation and Tabulation] E --> G F --> G G --> H[IAQ Profile Generation
Contaminant Map and Symptom Survey] H --> I{Exceedance vs Standard?} I -->|Yes| J[Root Cause Analysis
Source Identification] I -->|No| K[Preventive Maintenance Schedule] J --> L[IAQ Management Plan Design] K --> L L --> M[Implementation of Controls] M --> N[Re-Audit at 6 to 12 Months] N --> A ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of IAQ Controls ```mermaid flowchart TD L1[Most Effective Layer 1 Source Elimination] --> L2[Layer 2 Source Substitution
Low VOC Paints, Asbestos Abatement] L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Engineering Controls
Local Exhaust, HEPA Filters, UVGI] L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Administrative Controls
No Smoking, Cleaning Schedule, IPM] L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Least Effective Personal Action
PPE, Relocation, Time Limits] L1 -.applies first.-> L2 L2 -.applies next.-> L3 L3 -.applies next.-> L4 L4 -.applies last.-> L5 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Major IAQ Problems and Their Sources ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SBS[Sick Building Syndrome] S1[Headache, Fatigue, Eye Irritation] S2[Symptom Relieved Outside Building] end subgraph BRI[Building Related Illness] B1[Legionnaires Disease] B2[Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis] B3[Asbestosis] end subgraph SRC[Common IAQ Sources] P1[CO2 from Occupants] P2[VOCs from Furniture Paints] P3[Formaldehyde from Particle Board] P4[Mold from Water Intrusion] P5[Asbestos from Old Insulation] P6[Radon from Soil] P7[ETS from Smoking] P8[CO from Gas Stoves] end P1 --> S1 P2 --> S1 P3 --> S1 P4 --> S1 P7 --> S1 P4 --> B2 P5 --> B3 P6 --> B1 P8 --> S1 ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — IAQ Management Plan Components ```mermaid flowchart TD MP[IAQ Management Plan] --> SC[Source Control Module] MP --> VE[Ventilation Engineering Module] MP --> OP[Operations and Maintenance Module] MP --> OC[Occupant Communication Module] MP --> EM[Emergency Response Module] SC --> SC1[Material Substitution Policy] SC --> SC2[Moisture Control Program] SC --> SC3[Smoking Ban Enforcement] VE --> VE1[HVAC Upgrades and Filter Replacement] VE --> VE2[Local Exhaust Installation] VE --> VE3[Demand Controlled Ventilation] OP --> OP1[Scheduled Filter Change at 90 Days] OP --> OP2[Coil and Duct Cleaning Log] OP --> OP3[IAQ Walkthrough Every Quarter] OC --> OC1[New Employee IAQ Briefing] OC --> OC2[Complaint Hotline] OC --> OC3[Annual IAQ Report Distribution] EM --> EM1[Mold Spill Response Protocol] EM --> EM2[Chemical Spill Procedure] EM --> EM3[Asbestos Abatement Trigger Levels] ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram — IAQ Audit Decision Matrix for Management Triggers ```mermaid flowchart TD R1[Measurement Initiated] --> R2{Contaminant Level} R2 -->|Below 50 percent of Standard| R3[Green: Routine Monitoring] R2 -->|Between 50 and 100 percent| R4[Yellow: Investigate and Track] R2 -->|Exceeds Standard| R5[Red: Immediate Corrective Action] R3 --> R6[Annual Re-Audit] R4 --> R7[Monthly Tracking and Source Hunt] R5 --> R8[Engineering Intervention within 30 Days] R8 --> R9[Verify with Retest] R6 --> R2 R7 --> R2 R9 --> R2 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** Define an IAQ audit and list its four main phases. **Model Answer:** An **IAQ (Indoor Air Quality) Audit** is a systematic evaluation of the air in a building using visual inspection, instrumental monitoring, and HVAC performance review to produce an IAQ Profile. The four main phases are: 1. **Pre-audit planning** — document and complaint review. 2. **Walk-through inspection** — visual, olfactory, and airflow check. 3. **Quantitative measurement** — CO₂, PM, VOCs, bioaerosols. 4. **Profile generation and reporting** — benchmark against ASHRAE/WHO/NAAQS. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** What is the difference between Sick Building Syndrome (SBS) and Building Related Illness (BRI)? Give one example of each. **Model Answer:** | Feature | SBS | BRI | |---|---|---| | Cause | Multiple, often unidentified | Identifiable contaminant or pathogen | | Diagnosis | Non-specific symptoms | Specific clinical diagnosis | | Resolution | Symptoms vanish on leaving building | Symptoms persist, may require treatment | | Example | Headache from poor ventilation | Legionnaires' disease from cooling tower | - **SBS Example:** Eye irritation and fatigue in a sealed AC office. - **BRI Example:** Legionellosis from a contaminated cooling tower. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for conducting an **IAQ audit in a commercial office building**. List the instruments used and the contaminants measured. *(7 marks)* **(b)** An office has dimensions **10 m × 8 m × 3 m**, with **30 occupants**. The supply air volume is **0.18 m³/s** and outdoor CO₂ is **400 ppm**. The average CO₂ generation per person is **0.31 L/min**. Calculate: *(i)* the **ACH**, *(ii)* the **steady-state indoor CO₂ concentration**, and *(iii)* comment on whether the ventilation is adequate per ASHRAE 62.1. *(7 marks)* ### Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — IAQ Audit Procedure (7 marks) **Step 1 — Pre-Audit Preparation (1 mark):** - Collect building floor plans, HVAC as-built drawings, occupancy schedule, past complaint logs, and material safety data sheets (MSDS). - Identify high-risk zones (basements for radon, copy rooms for O₃, cafeterias for CO, basements for mold). **Step 2 — Walk-Through Visual Inspection (1 mark):** - Inspect for water damage, visible mold, stained ceiling tiles, blocked supply diffusers, dirty return grilles, and unvented combustion sources. - Olfactory assessment for musty, sweet, or pungent odors. - Verify that the HVAC system is operating and that thermostats are functional. **Step 3 — Continuous Monitoring (1.5 marks):** - Use a **multi-gas monitor** to log **CO₂, CO, temperature, and relative humidity** continuously for at least 8 hours (one full working day) — preferably one week per ASHRAE guidelines. - Use an **optical particle counter** for **PM₁, PM₂.₅, PM₁₀**. **Step 4 — Grab Sampling (1.5 marks):** - **VOCs (Toluene, Xylene, Benzene):** Collected on **Tenax-TA tubes** and analyzed by **GC-MS**. - **Aldehydes (Formaldehyde, Acetaldehyde):** Collected on **DNPH-coated silica cartridges** and analyzed by **HPLC-UV**. - **Bioaerosols (Fungi, Bacteria):** Collected using an **Anderson six-stage impactor** on agar plates (e.g., **MEA** for fungi, **TSA** for bacteria). - **Radon:** Measured using **alpha-track detectors** over 3 months. **Step 5 — HVAC Performance Test (1 mark):** - Use a **hot-wire anemometer** or **balometer** to measure supply and return airflow at each grille. - Calculate **ACH** and compare with design and standard values. - Inspect filters for loading, condensate pans for biofilm, and ducts for cleanliness. **Step 6 — Reporting and IAQ Profile (1 mark):** - Tabulate all measured values. - Cross-reference with ASHRAE 62.1, WHO, NAAQS, and OSHA standards. - Produce a spatial contaminant map, occupant symptom survey, and prioritized recommendations. #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** - Room volume: $V = 10 \times 8 \times 3 = 240 \text{ m}^3$. - Occupants: $P = 30$. - Supply airflow: $Q = 0.18 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. - Outdoor CO₂: $C_{out} = 400 \text{ ppm}$. - CO₂ generation: $G_p = 0.31 \text{ L/min per person} = 0.31/60 = 5.167 \times 10^{-3} \text{ L/s per person}$. **(i) Air Changes per Hour (3 marks):** $$\mathrm{ACH} = \dfrac{Q \times 3600}{V}$$ $$\mathrm{ACH} = \dfrac{0.18 \times 3600}{240}$$ $$\mathrm{ACH} = \dfrac{648}{240} = 2.7 \; \mathrm{hr^{-1}}$$ **(ii) Steady-State Indoor CO₂ (3 marks):** $$C_{in} = C_{out} + \dfrac{P \cdot G_p}{Q}$$ $$C_{in} = 400 + \dfrac{30 \times 5.167 \times 10^{-3}}{0.18}$$ $$C_{in} = 400 + \dfrac{0.155}{0.18}$$ $$C_{in} = 400 + 0.861 \; (\mathrm{as\ volume\ fraction})$$ Convert to ppm: $0.861 \times 10^6 \approx 861 \text{ ppm}\ (\text{using air density method})$ — more accurately, treating L/L as direct ppm: $$C_{in} = 400 + 861{,}000 = 861{,}400 \text{ ppm} \text{ (this is wrong; use mole ratio)}$$ **Corrected method (using mass balance with ppm directly):** CO₂ generation per person ≈ **0.0056 L/s per person** (standard ASHRAE 62.1 value). $$C_{in} - C_{out} = \dfrac{P \cdot G_p}{Q} \times 10^6 \; \mathrm{ppm}$$ $$C_{in} - C_{out} = \dfrac{30 \times 0.0056}{0.18} \times 10^6$$ $$C_{in} - C_{out} = 0.9333 \times 10^6 \text{ (volume ratio)}$$ Better formulation: $\Delta C (\text{ppm}) = \dfrac{P \cdot G_p \,(\text{L/s}) \times 10^6}{Q \,(\text{L/s})}$ $$C_{in} = 400 + \dfrac{30 \times 0.0056 \times 10^6}{0.18 \times 10^3} = 400 + \dfrac{168{,}000}{180} = 400 + 933 \; \mathrm{ppm}$$ $$C_{in} \approx 1333 \; \mathrm{ppm}$$ **(iii) Adequacy Check (1 mark):** ASHRAE recommended: $\Delta C_{CO_2} \leq 700$ ppm above outdoor. $$\Delta C = 1333 - 400 = 933 \; \mathrm{ppm} > 700 \; \mathrm{ppm}$$ **Conclusion:** **Inadequate ventilation.** The management plan must mandate an OA upgrade to at least: $$Q_{req} = \dfrac{30 \times 0.0056 \times 10^6}{700} \times 10^{-3} = 0.24 \; \mathrm{m}^3/\text{s}$$ **ASHRAE 62.1 check:** $V_{ot} = 2.5 \times 30 + 0.6 \times 80 = 75 + 48 = 123$ L/s = 0.123 m³/s. Current supply 0.18 m³/s is above this, but the calculated $\Delta C$ is high because of the low 30-person density; hence per-person demand is high. A **demand-controlled ventilation (DCV)** system using CO₂ sensors is recommended. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks (Alternative) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **(a)** Describe the **Sick Building Syndrome (SBS)** and list the major indoor pollutants responsible for it. Also explain the **hierarchy of controls** used in an IAQ management plan. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A newly painted office has $V = 200 \text{ m}^3$, $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. The paint emits total VOC (TVOC) at a steady rate of $G = 1.2 \text{ mg/s}$. Outdoor TVOC is negligible. Calculate the **steady-state TVOC concentration** and the **time required to reach 90% of steady state**. If the WHO guideline for TVOC is **0.3 mg/m³**, recommend corrective actions. *(7 marks)* ### Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — SBS and Hierarchy of Controls (7 marks) **Sick Building Syndrome (2 marks):** SBS refers to a set of non-specific, generalized symptoms reported by occupants of a building, which improve when the affected person leaves the building. Symptoms include: - Headache, dizziness, fatigue. - Eye, nose, and throat irritation. - Dry skin, cough, hoarseness. - Difficulty concentrating, nausea. **Major Indoor Pollutants Causing SBS (3 marks):** 1. **Carbon dioxide (CO₂)** — excess indicates poor ventilation. 2. **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** — from paints, adhesives, cleaning agents, furniture, carpets. 3. **Formaldehyde (HCHO)** — from particle board, UF foams, fabrics. 4. **Particulate matter (PM₂.₅, PM₁₀)** — from printers, copiers, outdoor infiltration. 5. **Bioaerosols (mold, bacteria, endotoxins)** — from water-damaged materials. 6. **Environmental Tobacco Smoke (ETS)** — from indoor smoking. 7. **Ozone (O₃)** — from photocopiers, printers, air purifiers. 8. **Carbon monoxide (CO)** and **NO₂** — from gas stoves, unvented heaters. **Hierarchy of Controls (2 marks):** 1. **Source Elimination** (e.g., remove asbestos). 2. **Source Substitution** (e.g., use low-VOC paint). 3. **Engineering Controls** (HVAC upgrade, local exhaust, HEPA). 4. **Administrative Controls** (smoking bans, cleaning schedules). 5. **PPE / Personal Action** (least preferred; rarely practical for IAQ). #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** - $V = 200 \text{ m}^3$, $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $G = 1.2 \text{ mg/s}$, $C_{out} = 0$. **(i) Steady-State TVOC Concentration (2 marks):** $$C_{ss} = C_{out} + \dfrac{G}{Q}$$ $$C_{ss} = 0 + \dfrac{1.2}{0.05}$$ $$C_{ss} = 24 \; \mathrm{mg/m^3}$$ **(ii) Time to reach 90% of steady state (3 marks):** The transient concentration is: $$C(t) = C_{ss} \left(1 - e^{-Q t / V}\right)$$ For 90% of steady state: $C(t) = 0.9 \, C_{ss}$ $$0.9 = 1 - e^{-Q t / V}$$ $$e^{-Q t / V} = 0.1$$ $$-\dfrac{Q t}{V} = \ln(0.1) = -2.3026$$ $$t = \dfrac{2.3026 \, V}{Q} = \dfrac{2.3026 \times 200}{0.05}$$ $$t = \dfrac{460.52}{0.05}$$ $$t = 9210 \; \mathrm{s} \approx 2.56 \; \mathrm{hours}$$ **(iii) Compare with WHO TVOC guideline (1 mark):** $$C_{ss} = 24 \; \mathrm{mg/m^3} \gg 0.3 \; \mathrm{mg/m^3}$$ The concentration is **80× the WHO guideline** — completely unacceptable. **Recommended Corrective Actions (1 mark):** - **Halt occupancy** of the freshly painted office for at least **72 hours** (10× the time constant τ = V/Q = 4000 s ≈ 1.1 hr) before re-entry. - **Increase ventilation** to $Q \geq 4$ m³/s temporarily (purge mode) to drop $C_{ss}$ to ≤ 0.3 mg/m³ — that is, $G/Q \leq 0.3$ → $Q \geq 4$ m³/s. - **Substitute** with low-VOC (< 50 g/L) water-based paints in future. - **Source substitution** (Hierarchy Level 2) is the most sustainable fix. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Module 4 IAQ Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting $C_{out}$** in the mass balance: $C_{ss} = C_{out} + G/Q$ — examiners specifically check this. Half-mark deduction. > 2. **Using ACH = 1–2 as "good"** — offices need **ACH ≥ 4 to 6**, schools need **ACH = 6 to 8**, hospitals need **ACH ≥ 12**. Know the ranges. > 3. **Confusing SBS with BRI** — SBS has no identifiable cause, BRI has a clinical diagnosis. > 4. **Skipping the Hierarchy of Controls** — every management plan question expects 1–2 marks for mentioning the 5-tier hierarchy. > 5. **Forgetting to convert L/min to L/s** in CO₂ generation problems. Standard value is **0.31 L/min/person = 0.00517 L/s/person** (or use 0.0056 L/s as ASHRAE's sedentary office rate). ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4: IAQ Audits, Profiles, Management):** > - [ ] **IAQ Audit** = systematic indoor air evaluation with walk-through + instrumental + HVAC review. > - [ ] **IAQ Profile** = the diagnostic dossier of a building — contaminant map, HVAC inventory, symptom survey, benchmark comparison. > - [ ] **Five Phases** = pre-audit, walk-through, continuous monitoring, HVAC test, profile reporting. > - [ ] **Key Instruments** = multi-gas monitor, optical particle counter, GC-MS, HPLC, Anderson impactor, hot-wire anemometer, balometer. > - [ ] **ACH Formula** = $\mathrm{ACH} = Q \times 3600 / V$; target ACH = 4–6 for offices, 6–8 for schools. > - [ ] **Steady-state mass balance** = $C_{ss} = C_{out} + G/Q$. > - [ ] **ASHRAE 62.1 OA** = $V_{ot} = R_p P_z + R_a A_z$ — both components summed. > - [ ] **CO₂ target** = $C_{in} - C_{out} \leq 700$ ppm. > - [ ] **SBS vs BRI** = SBS is non-specific, BRI is clinically diagnosed. > - [ ] **Hierarchy of Controls** = Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE. > - [ ] **Top 8 Pollutants** = CO₂, VOCs, HCHO, PM, Bioaerosols, ETS, O₃, CO/NO₂. > - [ ] **Decay equation** = $C(t) = C_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ with $\lambda = Q/V + k$. > - [ ] **Radon mitigation** = sub-slab depressurization (active soil depressurization). > - [ ] **Asbestos abatement** = encapsulation vs enclosure vs removal — only licensed contractors. > - [ ] **Mold remediation** = moisture control first, then removal; HEPA + negative pressure. > - [ ] **Management plan must include** = source control, ventilation, O&M, occupant communication, emergency response.

topics.title: Control, Quantification and Measurement, Air Pollution Dispersion - Dispersion Theory Basics - Air Quality Impact of Stationary Sources - Models and Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT524 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control, Quantification and Measurement, Air Pollution Dispersion - Dispersion Theory Basics - Air Quality Impact of Stationary Sources - Models and Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Air Pollution Dispersion – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Air Pollution Dispersion** is the physical process by which airborne contaminants released from a source (point, line, area, or volume) are transported, diffused, and diluted in the atmosphere under the combined action of **advection** (mean wind transport) and **turbulent diffusion** (random eddies), eventually reaching ground-level receptors at concentrations governed by the **Gaussian Plume Model** assumptions of steady-state, homogeneous, turbulent flow.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, dispersion modeling forms the analytical bridge between **emission inventory (source strength Q)** and **ambient air quality (ground-level concentration χ)**. The topic integrates:

- **Source Characterization** – stack height (h), exit velocity (v_s), gas temperature (T_s), and diameter (d).
- **Atmospheric Stability Classification** – Pasquill–Gifford classes A through F.
- **Meteorological Inputs** – wind speed (u), wind direction, mixing layer height (L), solar radiation, cloud cover.
- **Receptor Analysis** – ground-level concentrations at specific downwind distances (x, y, z).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Smoke Ring" Analogy:** Imagine blowing cigarette smoke through a chimney on a windy day. Far away, the smoke spreads thinly and is barely noticeable. Close to the chimney, it is concentrated and visible. The farther downwind you go, the wider the smoke cone (plume) becomes, but the more diluted it gets. **Dispersion mathematics simply quantifies this "spreading + thinning" phenomenon** using statistics of turbulence.

- **Plume rise (Δh)** is like the initial upward "kick" from hot exhaust gases rising before bending over.
- **Stability class** is like the "calmness" of the air. On a sunny afternoon (unstable, class A), the plume wiggles violently and dilutes fast. On a clear night (stable, class F), it travels almost in a straight, narrow ribbon.
- **Effective stack height (H = h + Δh)** is the virtual height from which the plume begins its horizontal journey.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Standard reference values in KTU board problems:
> - **Standard temperature:** **T₀ = 288.15 K (15 °C)**
> - **Standard pressure:** **P₀ = 101.325 kPa**
> - **Gas constant for air:** **R = 287 J/(kg·K)**
> - **Gravitational acceleration:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **Concentration unit:** **μg/m³** (regulatory) or **ppm/ppb** (volume-based)
> - **Emission rate Q:** **g/s** (preferred SI unit in KTU numericals)

## 1.4 Quantification vs. Measurement – The Distinction

| Aspect | Quantification | Measurement |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Estimating pollutant release rate from source | Determining actual ambient concentration |
| **Tools** | Emission factors, material balance, stoichiometry | Sampling instruments (impingers, analysers) |
| **Output** | Source strength (g/s) | Concentration (μg/m³ or ppm) |
| **Stage** | Pre-construction / permit stage | Post-operation / compliance stage |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Gaussian Plume Model – Step-by-Step Logic

The **Gaussian Plume Model** (Pasquill, 1961; Gifford, 1961) is the cornerstone of KTU Module 4 and forms the basis of regulatory models (ISC, AERMOD).

### 2.1.1 Assumptions Behind the Model

1. **Steady-state emissions** – source strength Q is constant in time.
2. **Homogeneous, isotropic turbulence** – dispersion coefficients σ_y, σ_z depend only on downwind distance, not on coordinates.
3. **No chemical reaction or deposition** – pollutant is conservative (a key limitation).
4. **Coordinate system:** x is downwind, y is crosswind, z is vertical (ground at z = 0).
5. **Reflection at the ground** – the ground acts as a perfect absorber; mathematically, an "image source" is placed at –H.
6. **Uniform wind speed u** along x-direction with no wind shear.
7. **Total reflection at the mixing layer top L** (modification for elevated inversions).

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic – How the Model Works

- **Step 1:** Pollutants leave the stack at **effective height H = h + Δh**.
- **Step 2:** The plume is advected downwind at mean wind speed **u**.
- **Step 3:** Turbulent eddies cause spread in y (lateral) and z (vertical), characterized by **σ_y(x)** and **σ_z(x)**.
- **Step 4:** The concentration at any point (x, y, z) follows a **bivariate Gaussian distribution**.
- **Step 5:** Ground reflection doubles the concentration at z = 0 (and odd multiples of H).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for Part B (14-mark) numericals. Every KTU air pollution dispersion problem reduces to one or more of these equations.**

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\chi(x,y,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | **Ground-level concentration** (Gaussian plume, z=0, with ground reflection) | $\mu g/m^3$ |
| 2 | $\chi(x,0,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | **Centerline (y=0) ground-level concentration** | $\mu g/m^3$ |
| 3 | $\sigma_y = a \cdot x^b$ | Lateral dispersion coefficient (Pasquill–Gifford curves fit) | m |
| 4 | $\sigma_z = c \cdot x^d$ | Vertical dispersion coefficient | m |
| 5 | $\Delta h = \dfrac{v_s d}{u} \left(1.5 + 0.04 P_s\right)$ | **Briggs plume rise (buoyancy-dominated)** | m |
| 6 | $\Delta h = \dfrac{v_s d}{u}$ | **Momentum plume rise** (simple form) | m |
| 7 | $H = h + \Delta h$ | **Effective stack height** | m |
| 8 | $u_z = u_r \left(\dfrac{z}{z_r}\right)^p$ | **Power-law wind profile** | m/s |
| 9 | $x_{max} = \dfrac{H}{\sigma_z/x \cdot \text{slope at } \sigma_z = H/\sqrt{2}}$ | **Distance of maximum ground-level concentration** | m |
| 10 | $\chi_{max} = \dfrac{2Q}{e \pi u H^2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_z}{\sigma_y}\bigg\vert_{x_{max}}$ | **Maximum ground-level concentration** | $\mu g/m^3$ |
| 11 | $p = \dfrac{0.37 - 0.088 \ln u_{10}}{1 - 0.088 \ln(z/10)}$ | **Wind profile exponent (Irwin)** | – |
| 12 | $Q = C \cdot V \cdot \dot{m}$ | **Emission quantification (mass balance)** | g/s |
| 13 | $\text{Conversion: } 1 \text{ ppm} = \dfrac{M}{24.45} \text{ mg/L (at 25 °C)}$ | **ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversion** | – |

### 2.2.1 Pasquill–Gifford Stability Classes

| Class | Description | σ_y at 1 km (m) | σ_z at 1 km (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Very unstable (strong insolination) | ~245 | ~5000 |
| **B** | Moderately unstable | ~202 | ~720 |
| **C** | Slightly unstable | ~156 | ~340 |
| **D** | **Neutral** (overcast, high wind) | ~122 | ~148 |
| **E** | Slightly stable | ~95 | ~75 |
| **F** | Moderately stable (clear night) | ~68 | ~37 |

> [!NOTE]
> Class **D (neutral)** is the most common assumption in default KTU problems unless wind speed and insolation are given.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Regulatory permitting:** Industries must demonstrate that ground-level χ < NAAQS (National Ambient Air Quality Standards) at the property boundary.
- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA):** Mandatory for thermal power plants, cement kilns, and steel plants.
- **Urban planning:** Siting of stacks relative to residential zones.
- **Emergency response:** Predicting plume trajectory during chemical accidents (with modification for dense-gas dispersion).
- **Climate science:** Forms the input to chemistry-transport models (WRF-Chem, CMAQ).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gaussian Plume Equation

We begin with the **advection–diffusion equation** for a conservative pollutant in steady, homogeneous turbulent flow:

$$u \frac{\partial \chi}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial z^2}$$

where $K_y$ and $K_z$ are the turbulent eddy diffusivities in y and z directions.

### Step 1 – Coordinate Translation

For a plume released from effective height H moving with mean wind u, define a translating frame:

$$u \frac{\partial \chi}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial z^2}$$

Boundary conditions:
- $\chi \to 0$ as $x, y, z \to \infty$
- $\chi = 0$ at $z = 0$ (perfect ground absorption for an elevated source)
- Mass conservation: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u \chi\, dy\, dz = Q$

### Step 2 – Gaussian Solution in y and z

For a continuous point source at $(0, 0, H)$, the solution takes the **bivariate Gaussian form**:

$$\chi(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

> The **second exponential term** is the contribution from the **image source at z = –H** which enforces the perfect-reflection boundary condition at the ground. Mathematically this converts the absorbing condition into a reflecting one because the real image cancels the normal gradient at z = 0.

### Step 3 – Specialization to Ground Level (z = 0)

Setting $z = 0$:

$$\chi(x,y,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

The factor **2** in the numerator is the result of adding the two equal exponentials at z = 0:

$$\exp(0) + \exp(0) = 2$$

### Step 4 – Centerline Concentration (y = 0)

Setting $y = 0$:

$$\chi(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

This is the **most tested formula** in KTU board exams. Every dispersion numerical reduces to this single line.

### Step 5 – Finding Maximum Ground-Level Concentration

The maximum concentration $\chi_{max}$ occurs at the downwind distance $x_{max}$ where the function $\chi(x,0,0)$ peaks.

Differentiate with respect to x and set to zero:

$$\frac{d\chi}{dx} = 0 \implies \frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{1}{\sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right] = 0$$

Using the **Briggs approximation** for $\sigma_z/x$ (valid in the range where σ_z ≈ 0.5H):

$$x_{max} = \frac{H^2}{\sigma_z^2}\bigg\vert_{x_{max}} \cdot \text{correction factor}$$

In practice (KTU level), we use the **standard result** derived from $d\sigma_z/dx = \sigma_z/x$ in the regime $\sigma_z = 0.5H$:

$$\chi_{max} = \frac{2Q}{e \pi u H^2}\left(\frac{\sigma_z}{\sigma_y}\right)\bigg\vert_{x_{max}}$$

where $e = 2.718$ is Euler's number, arising from $e^{-1/2} \cdot 2 = 2/e$ in the simplified Briggs formula.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board expects students to **state and use** the standard $\chi_{max}$ formula without re-deriving it, but the derivation logic must be understood for 14-mark questions.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Power-Law Wind Profile

Starting from the **logarithmic wind profile** in the surface layer:

$$u_z = \frac{u_*}{\kappa} \ln\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)$$

where $u_*$ is friction velocity, $\kappa = 0.4$ is von Kármán constant, and $z_0$ is roughness length. The power law is an **empirical approximation** valid in the lower 100 m of the atmosphere:

$$u_z = u_r \left(\frac{z}{z_r}\right)^p$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exponent $p$ depends on stability and surface roughness. Typical values:
> - **Unstable (A, B, C):** $p = 0.02$ to $0.07$
> - **Neutral (D):** $p = 0.10$ to $0.15$
> - **Stable (E, F):** $p = 0.20$ to $0.40$

The Irwin formula relating p to reference wind speed $u_{10}$ at 10 m:

$$p = \frac{0.37 - 0.088 \ln u_{10}}{1 - 0.088 \ln(z/10)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Briggs Buoyancy Plume Rise (Holland 1953 modified by Briggs 1969, 1971)

For a **buoyancy-dominated** plume (hot gas exit, typical of power stations):

$$\Delta h = \frac{v_s d}{u}\left(1.5 + 0.04 P_s\right)$$

where $P_s$ is a **stability parameter**:
- $P_s = \dfrac{T_s - T_a}{T_s}$ (unstable/neutral)
- $P_s = -\dfrac{T_s - T_a}{T_a}$ (stable)

For typical KTU problems with hot flue gas ($T_s ≈ 400$ K) and ambient air ($T_a ≈ 300$ K):

$$P_s = \frac{400 - 300}{400} = 0.25$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 1 – Complete KTU-Style Numerical

> **Problem (KTU 2019 Pattern):** A coal-fired power plant emits SO₂ at a rate of Q = 500 g/s from a stack of height h = 100 m, exit diameter d = 3 m, exit velocity $v_s$ = 20 m/s, gas temperature $T_s$ = 400 K. Ambient temperature $T_a$ = 300 K, wind speed at 10 m u = 5 m/s, neutral stability (D class). For downwind distance x = 2 km on the centerline, calculate:
> (a) Effective stack height H
> (b) Plume rise Δh
> (c) Ground-level concentration

### Solution

**Step (a) – Plume Rise Δh:**

$$\Delta h = \frac{v_s d}{u}\left(1.5 + 0.04 P_s\right)$$

Compute $P_s$:

$$P_s = \frac{T_s - T_a}{T_s} = \frac{400 - 300}{400} = 0.25$$

Substitute:

$$\Delta h = \frac{20 \times 3}{5}\left(1.5 + 0.04 \times 0.25\right) = 12 \times 1.51 = 18.12 \text{ m}$$

**Step (b) – Effective Stack Height:**

$$H = h + \Delta h = 100 + 18.12 = 118.12 \text{ m}$$

**Step (c) – Ground-Level Concentration at x = 2 km:**

For D-class stability at x = 2000 m, use **standard Pasquill–Gifford curves** (interpolation):

$$\sigma_y \approx 122 \text{ m} \quad \text{(at 1 km, scaled as } x^{0.894}\text{)}$$

More accurately using the power-law fit $\sigma_y = a \cdot x^b$ for D class, where a = 68.28, b = 0.9024:

$$\sigma_y(2000) = 68.28 \times 2000^{0.9024} = 68.28 \times 1196 \approx 81.7 \text{ m}$$

$$\sigma_z(2000) = 33.96 \times 2000^{0.8948} = 33.96 \times 1133 \approx 38.5 \text{ m}$$

(Values for D class from standard tables)

Apply the centerline ground-level formula:

$$\chi = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

Compute the exponent:

$$\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} = \frac{118.12^2}{2 \times 38.5^2} = \frac{13952}{2964.5} = 4.71$$

Compute the exponential:

$$\exp(-4.71) = 0.00898$$

Compute the prefactor:

$$\frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} = \frac{500}{\pi \times 5 \times 81.7 \times 38.5} = \frac{500}{49{,}407} = 0.01012 \text{ g/m}^3$$

Multiplying:

$$\chi = 0.01012 \times 0.00898 = 9.09 \times 10^{-5} \text{ g/m}^3 = 90.9 \text{ μg/m}^3$$

> **Comparison with NAAQS:** 24-hour SO₂ standard = **80 μg/m³**. The plant **exceeds** the standard at 2 km. The student must check against relevant regulation.

## 3.5 Worked Example 2 – Maximum Concentration

Using the same parameters, find $\chi_{max}$ and $x_{max}$:

At $x_{max}$, the rule of thumb $\sigma_z = 0.5 H$ is invalid for D class; we use tabulated values. Iterating:

$$x_{max} \approx 2.5 \text{ km, where } \sigma_z \approx 0.4H = 47.2 \text{ m, } \sigma_y \approx 92 \text{ m}$$

$$\chi_{max} = \frac{2Q}{e \pi u H^2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_z}{\sigma_y}\bigg\vert_{x_{max}}$$

$$\chi_{max} = \frac{2 \times 500}{2.718 \times \pi \times 5 \times 118.12^2} \times \frac{47.2}{92}$$

$$\chi_{max} = \frac{1000}{598{,}037} \times 0.513 = 1.672 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.513 = 8.58 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g/m}^3 = 858 \text{ μg/m}^3$$

> This is a **centerline** value, much higher than the 2 km off-centerline value above. Validation of regulation should be at the property boundary, not centerline.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gaussian Plume Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[Stationary Source Q g per s] --> STK[Stack h m diameter d m]
    STK --> RISE[Briggs Plume Rise deltaH]
    RISE --> EFF[Effective Height H equals h plus deltaH]
    EFF --> ADV[Advected by wind u m per s]
    ADV --> YSPREAD[Lateral Spread sigmaY x]
    ADV --> ZSPREAD[Vertical Spread sigmaZ x]
    YSPREAD --> GAUS[Gaussian Plume Equation]
    ZSPREAD --> GAUS
    GAUS --> GLC[Ground Level Concentration chi]
    GLC --> CMP[Compare with NAAQS]
    CMP --> DEC{Compliant?}
    DEC -->|Yes| OK[Permit Issued]
    DEC -->|No| MOD[Modify Design: taller stack, scrubbers, or fuel switch]
```
